$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$

- **Three regimes (2 marks):**

| Regime | Œª Value | Sensor Voltage (Zirconia) |
|---|---|---|
| Rich (excess fuel) | Œª < 1 | ‚âà 0.8 ‚Äì 0.9 V |
| Stoichiometric | Œª = 1 | ‚âà 0.45 V |
| Lean (excess air) | Œª > 1 | ‚âà 0.1 ‚Äì 0.2 V |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2022 Model]** State the **Nernst Equation** for a lambda sensor. Define each term and indicate its role in determining the sensor's output EMF.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)$$

- **R** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**
- **T** = Absolute temperature of the ZrO‚ÇÇ cell **[K]** (must be ‚â• 350 ¬∞C for ionic conductivity)
- **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- **4** = number of electrons transferred per O‚ÇÇ molecule
- **p_{O‚ÇÇ,ref}** = Partial pressure of oxygen in reference air (constant ‚âà 20.9 kPa)
- **p_{O‚ÇÇ,exh}** = Partial pressure of oxygen in exhaust gas (variable with mixture)

The **output EMF E** is therefore directly proportional to the natural log of the oxygen partial-pressure ratio; richer exhaust (lower p_{O‚ÇÇ,exh}) produces a higher voltage, leaner exhaust produces a lower voltage.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE ‚Äî Answer ANY ONE of the following**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working principle of a Zirconia-based narrowband lambda sensor**. Explain how the sensor signal is used by the ECU for closed-loop air-fuel ratio control. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the lambda sensor from fundamental electrochemical principles and compute the EMF when the exhaust gas contains **2 % O‚ÇÇ by volume** at **T = 800 K**, with reference air at **20.9 % O‚ÇÇ**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3.5 marks):**

1. **Thimble-shaped Zirconia (ZrO‚ÇÇ) ceramic element** doped with Yttria (Y‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ) for oxygen-ion conductivity.
2. **Platinum electrodes** coated on both inner and outer surfaces act as catalysts.
3. **Inner cavity** is vented to ambient air through small holes in the steel housing ‚Üí forms the **reference chamber** (p_{O‚ÇÇ,ref} = constant).
4. **Outer surface** is exposed to hot exhaust gas (p_{O‚ÇÇ,exh} = variable).
5. **Heater element** wound inside the housing, supplied with 12 V from ECU; pre-heats the thimble to operating temperature in < 15 s.
6. **Steel threaded housing** for screwing into the exhaust pipe; **slotted protective shield** covers the tip.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

- At T ‚â• 350 ¬∞C, O¬≤‚Åª ions migrate from the high-oxygen side to the low-oxygen side.
- A Nernst EMF is generated across the electrodes proportional to the log of the O‚ÇÇ ratio.
- Rich mixture ‚Üí low p_{O‚ÇÇ,exh} ‚Üí EMF ‚âà 0.8 V
- Lean mixture ‚Üí high p_{O‚ÇÇ,exh} ‚Üí EMF ‚âà 0.1 V

**ECU Closed-Loop Control (1.5 marks):**

- ECU samples the sensor voltage every ~10 ms.
- Voltage > 0.45 V ‚Üí RICH detected ‚Üí **shortens injector pulse width** (less fuel).
- Voltage < 0.45 V ‚Üí LEAN detected ‚Üí **lengthens injector pulse width** (more fuel).
- The system intentionally **oscillates** around Œª = 1 at 0.5‚Äì2 Hz; this toggling is required for the **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** to maintain peak HC, CO, and NO‚Çì conversion simultaneously.

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Half-reactions:
- Cathode (exhaust): $O_{2,\text{exh}} + 4e^{-} \to 2O^{2-}$
- Anode (reference): $2O^{2-} \to O_{2,\text{ref}} + 4e^{-}$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Gibbs free energy per mole of O‚ÇÇ:
$$\Delta G = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Electrical work per mole: $W = -nFE$, n = 4

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equate: $-4FE = RT\ln(\ldots)$, solve for E:
$$E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2,\text{exh}}}\right)$$

**Step 5 (3 marks):** Numerical evaluation at T = 800 K, p_{ref} = 0.209, p_{exh} = 0.02:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{0.02}\right) = \ln(10.45) = 2.346$$

$$\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{(8.314)(800)}{4(96485)} = \frac{6651.2}{385940} = 0.01723 \; V$$

$$E = 0.01723 \times 2.346 = 0.0404 \; V \approx \mathbf{40 \; mV}$$

This is consistent with a **lean** reading (sensor output 0.1‚Äì0.2 V range with electrode overpotentials considered).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Narrowband** and **Wideband** lambda sensors with respect to construction, output signal, and engine-management application. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** List and explain the **common failure modes** of a lambda sensor. State the typical OBD-II diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs) associated with sensor malfunction. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Question B(a) [7 marks]

| Feature | Narrowband (Switching) | Wideband (Planar Pump Cell) |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | ZrO‚ÇÇ thimble, 1‚Äì4 wires, single chamber | Planar ZrO‚ÇÇ with **pump cell + Nernst cell**, 5‚Äì6 wires |
| **Output Signal** | Voltage: 0.1 V (lean) ‚Üî 0.9 V (rich) | **Current** (mA) in pump circuit maintains Nernst cell at 0.45 V |
| **Range** | Œª = 0.97 ‚Äì 1.03 only (binary) | **Œª = 0.7 ‚Äì ‚àû** (continuous, full range) |
| **Accuracy** | ¬± 2 % | ¬± 1 % |
| **ECU Use** | On/off switching around Œª = 1 | Continuous feedback for stratified, lean-burn, GDI |
| **Cost** | Low (‚Çπ 1,500‚Äì3,000) | High (‚Çπ 8,000‚Äì20,000) |
| **Application** | Older MPFI, BS4 vehicles | Modern BS6, Euro 6, GDI, lean-burn diesels |
| **Switching Freq.** | 0.5 ‚Äì 2 Hz (slow dither) | Near-instantaneous tracking |

> **Memory trick:** Narrowband = **"**N**arrow = only tells 1"**; Wideband = **"W**ide range of measurement.

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Question B(b) [7 marks]

| # | Failure Mode | Cause | Symptom | OBD-II DTC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Contamination** by lead, silicone, oil ash | Leaded fuel, coolant leak, oil burning | Slow response, fixed voltage | P0133 |
| 2 | **Open circuit** | Broken internal wire | Signal stuck at ~0.45 V | P0134 |
| 3 | **Shorted circuit** | Wiring damage | Signal stuck at 0 V or 5 V | P0131 / P0132 |
| 4 | **Heater failure** | Open heater coil, blown fuse | Cold sensor, no closed loop for 2‚Äì5 min | P0135 |
| 5 | **Carbon fouling** | Rich running, misfire | Low voltage (false lean) | P0131 |
| 6 | **Aging / Slow response** | Thermal cycling > 100,000 km | ECU fails active air-fuel test | P0133 |
| 7 | **Tip cracking** | Thermal shock | Erratic voltage spikes | P0134 / P0135 |

**Diagnostic Approach (2 marks):**

- **Step 1:** Scan for DTCs and freeze-frame data.
- **Step 2:** Inspect wiring, connectors, and heater resistance (2‚Äì13 Œ© cold).
- **Step 3:** Live data ‚Äî voltage should oscillate 0.1 ‚Äì 0.9 V at idle in closed loop.
- **Step 4:** Graph response during snap-throttle; response time should be < 300 ms (heated).
- **Step 5:** Replace if any of the above failures are confirmed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Lambda Sensor Questions**
> 1. **Mixing up "rich/lean voltage"** ‚Äî Remember: RICH ‚Üí 0.8 V (low O‚ÇÇ in exhaust), LEAN ‚Üí 0.1 V (high O‚ÇÇ in exhaust). The voltage is *inversely* related to the oxygen concentration on the exhaust side.
> 2. **Forgetting "n = 4"** ‚Äî Students often use n = 2 (per O atom) instead of n = 4 (per O‚ÇÇ molecule). The Nernst equation is per O‚ÇÇ gas molecule.
> 3. **Missing the operating temperature condition** ‚Äî The ZrO‚ÇÇ sensor is non-functional below 350 ¬∞C; always mention this in answers.
> 4. **Confusing narrowband vs wideband** ‚Äî Wideband sensors output *current* through a pump cell, not voltage directly. Many students incorrectly say wideband gives a 0‚Äì5 V analog.
> 5. **Sign error in Nernst derivation** ‚Äî Be careful with the sign convention; the standard automotive form puts reference pressure in the numerator.
> 6. **Omitting closed-loop oscillation** ‚Äî Examiners often give 1 mark for stating that the ECU intentionally dithers at 0.5‚Äì2 Hz around Œª = 1 for TWC efficiency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision ‚Äî Pin This on Your Wall**

- ‚òê Lambda (Œª) = (A/F)_actual / (A/F)_stoich ‚Üí **14.7 : 1 for gasoline**
- ‚òê **Œª < 1** ‚Üí RICH ‚Üí Sensor **0.8 V**; **Œª = 1** ‚Üí **0.45 V**; **Œª > 1** ‚Üí LEAN ‚Üí **0.1 V**
- ‚òê **Nernst Eq:** $E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln(p_{\text{ref}}/p_{\text{exh}})$; **R = 8.314, F = 96,485**
- ‚òê Sensor active only above **350 ¬∞C** ‚Üí heater mandatory in modern cars
- ‚òê **Three types:** Zirconia (switching), Titania (resistive), Wideband (pump-cell)
- ‚òê Wideband measures current (mA), narrowband measures voltage (V)
- ‚òê ECU dithers at **0.5 ‚Äì 2 Hz** around Œª = 1 ‚Üí required for **TWC > 95 %** efficiency
- ‚òê Common DTCs: **P0131** (low V), **P0132** (high V), **P0133** (slow response), **P0134** (no activity), **P0135** (heater fault)
- ‚òê Failure causes: **lead/silicone contamination, oil ash, carbon fouling, thermal shock**
- ‚òê Heater resistance: **2 ‚Äì 13 Œ©** cold
- ‚òê Response time: **< 300 ms (heated)**; several seconds if cold
- ‚òê Sensor location: **upstream of TWC** (sensor 1) ‚Äî sometimes a second sensor downstream for catalyst monitoring
- ‚òê Nernst cell ‚Üí n = 4 electrons **per O‚ÇÇ molecule** (not per atom)
- ‚òê Reference gas: ambient air, **20.9 % O‚ÇÇ** (by volume, dry)
- ‚òê Closed-loop fuel trim is a **dynamic ¬± 1‚Äì3 % correction** applied every ~10 ms
- ‚òê In a **wideband** sensor, the ECU forces a pump current that keeps the Nernst cell at 0.45 V; the magnitude of that current is proportional to (1 ‚àí Œª)
- ‚òê The oxygen ion **O¬≤‚Åª** is the charge carrier in ZrO‚ÇÇ, not electrons
- ‚òê Sensor tip temperature during normal operation: **600 ‚Äì 800 ¬∞C**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detonation** in an internal combustion engine is the **uncontrolled, spontaneous, and instantaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas (air-fuel mixture) ahead of the advancing flame front**, occurring simultaneously at multiple points in the combustion chamber. This rapid volumetric combustion generates a sharp, localized pressure rise and high-frequency **shock waves** that strike the cylinder walls, producing the audible metallic sound known as **engine knock**.

In KTU 2024 Autotronics terminology, detonation is classified as an **abnormal combustion phenomenon** in Spark-Ignition (SI) engines, governed by the auto-ignition chemistry of the end-gas and the thermodynamic state of the cylinder charge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are lighting a long trail of gunpowder with a match at one end (this is **normal flame propagation**). The flame travels smoothly and predictably.

Now imagine that the gunpowder gets so hot, just before the flame reaches it, that the entire unburned portion **explodes on its own all at once** (this is **detonation**).

Think of it like this: a controlled campfire is normal combustion, but detonation is like throwing a whole box of fireworks into the campfire at once ‚Äî a sudden, violent, and chaotic release of energy that the engine was not designed to handle smoothly.

## 1.3 Distinction from Related Abnormal Combustion

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly differentiate between **Pre-Ignition**, **Knock**, and **Detonation**, as KTU questions frequently test this distinction for 3-mark short answers.

| Phenomenon | Trigger Point | Cause | Effect |
|------------|--------------|-------|--------|
| **Pre-Ignition** | Before spark | Hot spot (overheated spark plug, carbon deposit, glowing valve) ignites mixture | Power loss, possible engine damage |
| **Knock (Audible Symptom)** | After spark | Result of detonation producing audible sound | Distinct metallic pinging sound |
| **Detonation** | After spark (toward end of combustion) | End-gas auto-ignites due to high T & P | Pressure waves, knock sound, mechanical stress |

> **Key Insight:** *Knock is the SOUND, Detonation is the CAUSE.* Detonation and pre-ignition are different abnormal combustion events.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Octane Number (RON / MON):** Measure of fuel's resistance to detonation.
- **Pressure Rise Rate (dP/dŒ∏):** During normal combustion ~ 2‚Äì4 bar/deg; during detonation, it can spike to **>10 bar/deg**.
- **Knock Limited Spark Advance (KLSA):** Maximum ignition advance before knock occurs.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) of gasoline:** Approximately **370 ¬∞C ‚Äì 430 ¬∞C** under normal pressure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Detonation ‚Äî Stepwise Mechanism

The development of detonation in an SI engine follows a deterministic sequence of thermodynamic and chemical events:

- **Step 1 ‚Äî Flame Initiation:** The spark plug ignites the air-fuel mixture near the spark gap at the prescribed ignition timing angle.
- **Step 2 ‚Äî Flame Propagation:** A smooth, laminar (then turbulent) flame kernel grows outward, typically consuming the mixture at a flame speed of **15‚Äì25 m/s** in normal operation.
- **Step 3 ‚Äî End-Gas Compression:** The unburned mixture trapped between the flame front and the cylinder walls/piston (the *end-gas*) is compressed and heated by the rising cylinder pressure and temperature.
- **Step 4 ‚Äî Pre-Combustion Reactions:** Slow oxidation reactions occur in the end-gas, forming peroxides and other intermediate species. These are **chemical precursors** to knock.
- **Step 5 ‚Äî Auto-Ignition Threshold Breach:** If the end-gas temperature exceeds its **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** for a long enough *ignition delay period*, the entire end-gas self-ignites spontaneously.
- **Step 6 ‚Äî Shock Wave Generation:** The volumetric, near-instantaneous combustion creates a high-pressure pulse that propagates as a shock wave through the cylinder, striking the walls at supersonic speed.
- **Step 7 ‚Äî Audible Knock:** The shock wave reflects off cylinder walls, producing the characteristic metallic "ping."

## 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Engine Management Systems (EMS):** Modern ECUs use knock sensors to detect detonation and **retard ignition timing** in real time, maximizing efficiency while preventing damage.
- **Fuel Design:** Refineries blend gasoline with **iso-octane, ethanol, MTBE, toluene** to raise octane rating.
- **Combustion Chamber Design:** Engineers use **pent-roof, hemispherical, or Heron-type** combustion chambers with central spark plug placement to reduce knock tendency.
- **Materials Selection:** Knock-resistant engines use forged pistons, reinforced connecting rods, and strengthened bearings.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the core analytical toolkit for solving numerical problems on detonation and related SI engine combustion topics.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description of Variables | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| Pressure Rise Rate | $\dfrac{dP}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot P}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{d\theta}$ | $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio, $P$ = pressure, $V$ = volume, $\theta$ = crank angle | Used to compare normal vs. knock combustion |
| Auto-Ignition Delay (œÑ) | $\tau = A \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right) \cdot [O_2]^{-m} [Fuel]^{-n}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = temperature | Arrhenius-type; shorter œÑ ‚Üí higher knock tendency |
| Octane Number Correlation | $ON \approx 100 \cdot \dfrac{R_{iso}}{R_{iso} + R_n}$ | $R_{iso}$ = iso-octane fraction, $R_n$ = n-heptane fraction | Empirical blending rule |
| Mean Effective Pressure Knock Limit | $IMEP_{knock} \approx f(ON,\, CR,\, \lambda,\, T_{intake})$ | $CR$ = compression ratio, $\lambda$ = air-fuel ratio, $T_{intake}$ = intake air temp | Higher ON ‚Üí higher knock-limited IMEP |
| Knock Sensor Output | $V_{out} = K \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}P}{d\theta^{2}}$ | $K$ = piezo constant | Piezo sensor detects vibration from knock pulse |
| KLSA Margin | $\theta_{KLSA} = \theta_{MBT} - \Delta\theta_{safety}$ | $\theta_{MBT}$ = minimum advance for best torque, $\Delta\theta$ = safety margin | Typically 2¬∞‚Äì5¬∞ in modern ECUs |

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step ‚Äî Connecting Physics to Symptoms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The most common 14-mark question in Module 1 asks: *"Explain the mechanism of detonation and the role of end-gas."* The examiner awards 4 marks specifically for correctly identifying the **end-gas** and explaining **why** it auto-ignites (chemistry + thermodynamics).

- **Why does temperature rise in the end-gas?** Because it is **adiabatically compressed** by the expanding burned gases.
- **Why is the chemistry important?** Because gasoline is a **para-olefinic hydrocarbon blend** with varying AITs; longer-chain paraffins (e.g., n-heptane) have lower AIT ‚Üí more knock-prone.
- **Why is knock self-limiting?** Detonation releases energy so fast that cylinder pressure overshoots the normal cycle, causing the piston to decelerate ‚Äî reducing the next cycle's compression temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-Gas Temperature During Flame Propagation

We will derive the end-gas temperature as a function of crank angle to determine when it exceeds the AIT ‚Äî the critical condition for detonation.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption: Adiabatic Compression of End-Gas

Consider the end-gas as a fixed mass trapped between the flame front and the cylinder walls. The end-gas undergoes an **adiabatic compression** process from the time the flame front passes until auto-ignition occurs.

### 3.1.2 Applying the Adiabatic Process Equation

For an ideal gas undergoing an adiabatic (reversible, no heat transfer) process:

$$P_1 \cdot V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{\gamma}$$

where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ is the specific heat ratio of the unburned mixture (typically **Œ≥ ‚âà 1.35** for gasoline-air mixture at engine conditions).

### 3.1.3 Temperature-Volume Relationship

Dividing the adiabatic relation by the ideal gas equation $P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$, we obtain:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

This is the fundamental relation governing end-gas temperature rise.

### 3.1.4 Expressing Volume Ratio via Crank Angle

For a slider-crank mechanism, the instantaneous volume above the piston is given by:

$$V(\theta) = \dfrac{V_s}{2} \left[ (1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r}{L}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}\right) \right] + V_c$$

where $V_s$ is the swept volume, $r$ is the crank radius, $L$ is the connecting rod length, and $V_c$ is the clearance volume.

For a simplified analysis near TDC, we use the small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ and $1 - \cos\theta \approx \dfrac{\theta^2}{2}$:

$$V(\theta) \approx V_c \left(1 + \dfrac{CR - 1}{2}\,\theta^2\right)$$

where $CR = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c}$ is the compression ratio.

### 3.1.5 End-Gas Temperature Expression

Substituting into the adiabatic temperature relation:

$$T_{end}(\theta) = T_0 \left(\dfrac{V_0}{V_{end}(\theta)}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

As the piston moves toward TDC ($\theta \to 0$ after TDC), $V_{end}$ decreases, so $T_{end}$ **increases sharply**.

### 3.1.6 Condition for Detonation Onset

Detonation occurs when:

$$T_{end}(\theta_{det}) \geq T_{AIT}$$

where $T_{AIT}$ is the **auto-ignition temperature** of the end-gas (‚âà 400 ¬∞C for typical gasoline).

### 3.1.7 Combining into a Practical Criterion

Substituting the volume approximation:

$$T_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{CR - 1}{2}\theta_{det}^2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \geq T_{AIT}$$

Solving for the critical crank angle:

$$\theta_{det} \leq \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{CR - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{T_0}{T_{AIT}}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}} - 1\right]}$$

> **Interpretation:** Higher $CR$ ‚Üí smaller $\theta_{det}$ ‚Üí detonation occurs **earlier** (closer to TDC), confirming the well-known rule that high compression ratio engines are more knock-prone.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Octane Number and Compression Ratio

The knock-limited compression ratio for a given fuel is empirically related to its octane number. The semi-empirical relation is:

$$CR_{max} = 1 + \left(ON \cdot k_1 - k_2\right)$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are constants depending on engine geometry. For a typical small gasoline engine:

- $k_1 \approx 0.012$
- $k_2 \approx 0.5$

Therefore:

$$CR_{max} = 1 + 0.012 \cdot ON - 0.5$$

For ON = 95 (Euro-grade petrol): $CR_{max} = 1 + 1.14 - 0.5 = 12.64$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is consistent with modern high-performance engines that achieve CR ‚âà 12‚Äì14 with premium fuel (ON ‚â• 95).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Detonation

The table below classifies the key factors that **increase** or **decrease** detonation tendency. This matrix appears frequently in KTU 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | Effect on Detonation | Engineering Control |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| ‚Üë Compression Ratio | ‚Üë Increases | Limit to fuel octane |
| ‚Üë Intake Air Temperature | ‚Üë Increases | Intercooler, charge cooling |
| ‚Üë Spark Advance | ‚Üë Increases | ECU retards timing on knock |
| ‚Üë Combustion Chamber Deposits | ‚Üë Increases | Fuel additives, decarbonization |
| ‚Üë End-Gas Temperature | ‚Üë Increases | Coolant, oil temperature control |
| ‚Üë Fuel Octane Number | ‚Üì Decreases | Use premium fuel |
| ‚Üë Air-Fuel Ratio (lean) | ‚Üì Decreases (slightly) | Lean-burn calibration |
| ‚Üë Tumble / Swirl | ‚Üì Decreases | Combustion chamber design |
| ‚Üë Knock Sensor Retard | ‚Üì Decreases | Active EMS control |
| EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) | ‚Üì Decreases | ECU-controlled EGR valve |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Detonation Mechanism in SI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spark Plug Fires at Ignition Timing] --> B[Flame Kernel Formation]
    B --> C[Flame Front Propagates Across Chamber]
    C --> D[End-Gas Trapped Between Flame and Walls]
    D --> E[Adiabatic Compression of End-Gas]
    E --> F{End-Gas Temperature Exceeds AIT?}
    F -- No --> G[Smooth Combustion - No Knock]
    F -- Yes --> H[Auto-Ignition of End-Gas]
    H --> I[Rapid Volumetric Energy Release]
    I --> J[Shock Wave Generation]
    J --> K[Knock Sound at Cylinder Walls]
    K --> L[Engine Damage Risk: Piston, Bearing, Head Gasket]
    L --> M[Knock Sensor Detects Vibration]
    M --> N[ECU Retards Ignition Timing]
    N --> O[Combustion Returns to Normal]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: ECU Knock Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Sensors
        KS[Knock Sensor Piezoelectric]
        CKP[Crankshaft Position Sensor]
        MAP[Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor]
        CT[Coolant Temperature Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ECU_Processing
        SIG[Signal Conditioning and Filtering]
        WIN[Window Comparator for Knock Frequency Band]
        DEC[Knock Detection Algorithm]
        MAP2[Knock Map Lookup Table]
        CALC[Ignition Timing Correction Calculator]
    end
    subgraph Output_Actuators
        IGN[Ignition Coil Driver]
        INJ[Injector Driver]
    end
    KS --> SIG --> WIN --> DEC --> MAP2 --> CALC
    CKP --> CALC
    MAP --> MAP2
    CT --> MAP2
    CALC --> IGN
    CALC --> INJ
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Combustion Chamber Showing End-Gas Zone

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cylinder_Head
        SP[Spark Plug]
        IN[Intake Valve]
        EX[Exhaust Valve]
    end
    subgraph Combustion_Chamber
        FF[Flame Front - Expanding Spherical Surface]
        EG[End-Gas Zone - Unburned Mixture]
        BB[Burned Gas Region - High T and P]
        P[Piston Crown]
        W[Cylinder Walls]
    end
    SP --> FF
    FF --> EG
    EG --> W
    EG --> P
    BB --> FF
    EG -->|Auto-Ignition Risk| SHOCK[Detonation Shock Wave]
    SHOCK --> W
    SHOCK --> P
```

> **Diagram Interpretation Notes for KTU Answers:**
> - The flame front moves outward from the spark plug.
> - End-gas is compressed into a smaller volume as the flame advances.
> - When the end-gas temperature exceeds AIT, auto-ignition occurs spontaneously.
> - The shock wave reflects off cylinder walls, producing audible knock.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
Define **detonation** in a spark-ignition engine. How is it different from **pre-ignition**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Detonation is the **uncontrolled, instantaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas mixture** ahead of the advancing flame front in a spark-ignition engine, resulting in a rapid pressure rise and audible knock. (1.5 marks)

It differs from pre-ignition in that:
- **Pre-ignition** occurs **before the spark** due to a hot spot (overheated spark plug, carbon deposit, or glowing valve) igniting the mixture early. (0.75 marks)
- **Detonation** occurs **after the spark** due to end-gas auto-ignition from compression heating. (0.75 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the role of **octane number** in controlling detonation? Name two fuels with high octane rating.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **octane number** is a measure of a fuel's resistance to auto-ignition (knock resistance). Higher octane fuels resist detonation better, allowing higher compression ratios and more advanced ignition timing without knock. (2 marks)

Two high-octane fuels: **iso-octane (ON = 100)** and **toluene (ON ‚âà 114)**; also valid: ethanol (ON ‚âà 108), methanol, premium gasoline (RON 95/98). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of detonation** in a spark-ignition engine with a neat sketch of the combustion chamber showing the end-gas zone. Discuss the role of the end-gas in initiating detonation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the **factors affecting detonation**. Discuss how an electronic engine control unit (ECU) detects and prevents detonation using a **knock sensor**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

The mechanism of detonation involves the following sequential steps:

1. **Spark Ignition (1 mark):** The spark plug initiates combustion at the prescribed ignition timing, forming a small flame kernel.
2. **Flame Propagation (1 mark):** The flame kernel grows into a spherical/turbulent flame front that consumes the air-fuel mixture at 15‚Äì25 m/s.
3. **End-Gas Formation (1.5 marks):** The unburned mixture trapped between the flame front and the cylinder walls/piston is called the **end-gas**. It occupies a shrinking volume as the flame advances.
4. **Adiabatic Compression and Heating (1.5 marks):** The end-gas is compressed by the rising pressure of the burned gases. Its temperature rises sharply according to $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma - 1}$.
5. **Auto-Ignition (1 mark):** When the end-gas temperature exceeds its **auto-ignition temperature (AIT ‚âà 400 ¬∞C)** for a sufficient ignition delay, the entire end-gas self-ignites spontaneously.
6. **Shock Wave and Knock (1 mark):** The instantaneous energy release creates a high-pressure shock wave that strikes the cylinder walls, producing the characteristic metallic "ping."

> **Role of End-Gas:** The end-gas is the location and source of detonation. Its high temperature, high pressure, and long ignition delay make it chemically prone to auto-ignition. The detonation severity is directly proportional to the **mass and temperature** of the end-gas.

**Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Factors Affecting Detonation (4 marks):**

- **Compression Ratio:** Higher CR ‚Üí higher end-gas temperature ‚Üí more knock.
- **Spark Advance:** More advance ‚Üí more time for end-gas heating ‚Üí more knock.
- **Intake Air Temperature:** Hotter air ‚Üí higher end-gas T ‚Üí more knock.
- **Combustion Chamber Deposits:** Carbon deposits act as hot spots and increase compression.
- **Air-Fuel Ratio:** Rich or very lean mixtures are less knock-prone; stoichiometric is most knock-prone.
- **Fuel Octane Number:** Higher ON ‚Üí higher resistance.
- **Engine Speed:** Higher speed ‚Üí less time for end-gas reactions ‚Üí less knock (within limits).
- **EGR and Tumble:** Both suppress knock.

**Knock Sensor Operation (3 marks):**

A **piezoelectric knock sensor** is mounted on the cylinder block. When detonation occurs, the characteristic vibration in the **5‚Äì15 kHz** frequency band is detected by the sensor. The ECU:

- Filters the signal to isolate the knock frequency.
- Compares the signal amplitude against a **knock threshold map** stored in memory.
- **Retards ignition timing** by 1¬∞‚Äì3¬∞ at a time (incremental retard).
- Gradually advances timing back toward MBT after the knock subsides.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "knock sensor location" ‚Äî it is on the cylinder BLOCK, not the cylinder HEAD.** Many students lose 1 mark here.
> - **Do NOT confuse the knock frequency band (5‚Äì15 kHz) with engine vibration frequencies (< 1 kHz).** This is a classic KTU trap.
> - **Always mention "ignition timing RETARD" ‚Äî never say "advance"** as the ECU's response to knock. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if inverted.
> - For Part (a), **sketching the end-gas zone is mandatory** ‚Äî KTU examiners award 1.5 marks for the labeled diagram; missing it caps your score at 5.5/7.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1]**

**(a)** What is **knock** in SI engines? Derive the relationship between end-gas temperature and the crank angle at which detonation occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **knock sensor** with a block diagram. Discuss the role of **KLSA (Knock Limited Spark Advance)** in modern engine management. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Knock Definition (1 mark):** Knock is the audible metallic sound produced when the end-gas in a spark-ignition engine undergoes **detonation** (spontaneous auto-ignition), generating shock waves that strike the cylinder walls.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Consider the end-gas undergoing adiabatic compression. Using the small-angle crank-slider approximation near TDC:

$$V(\theta) \approx V_c \left[ 1 + \dfrac{CR - 1}{2}\theta^2 \right]$$

Applying adiabatic relation $T \cdot V^{\gamma - 1} = \text{const}$:

$$T_{end}(\theta) = T_0 \left[ \dfrac{V_0}{V(\theta)} \right]^{\gamma - 1} = T_0 \left[ \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{CR-1}{2}\theta^2} \right]^{\gamma - 1}$$

For detonation: $T_{end}(\theta_{det}) = T_{AIT}$

Solving for $\theta_{det}$:

$$\theta_{det} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{CR-1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T_{AIT}}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{\gamma-1}} - 1 \right]}$$

**Conclusion:** Higher CR and higher $T_0$ ‚Üí smaller $\theta_{det}$ ‚Üí detonation occurs earlier (closer to TDC).

**Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks**

**Knock Sensor Working (3 marks):**

A knock sensor uses a **piezoelectric crystal** bonded to a mass. When engine vibration (specifically in the 5‚Äì15 kHz knock band) reaches the sensor, the mass exerts a force on the crystal, producing a voltage proportional to the vibration. The ECU's knock detection IC filters this signal and compares it to a calibrated threshold.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Knock Vibration ‚Üí Piezo Element ‚Üí Charge Amplifier ‚Üí
Band-Pass Filter (5‚Äì15 kHz) ‚Üí Window Comparator ‚Üí
Knock Detection IC ‚Üí Ignition Timing Retard Command
```

**KLSA ‚Äî Knock Limited Spark Advance (2 marks):**

KLSA is the **maximum ignition advance** at which the engine can operate without knock under given operating conditions (load, speed, temperature). The ECU continuously computes KLSA from pre-stored 3D maps and **clamps the actual spark advance to KLSA ‚àí safety margin** (typically 2¬∞‚Äì5¬∞). This maximizes torque output (closer to MBT) while protecting the engine from knock damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question B:**
> - **The derivation marks are heavily weighted (5‚Äì6 out of 7).** Skipping steps will cost you severely.
> - Always state the **assumptions** of the derivation explicitly: ideal gas, adiabatic process, small-angle approximation. Each assumption is worth 0.25 marks.
> - For KLSA, do **not** confuse with **MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque)**. MBT is the ideal timing; KLSA is the *limit* imposed by knock.
> - **Include the typical 5‚Äì15 kHz frequency band** in your knock sensor answer ‚Äî a frequently missed specification worth 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- [x] **Detonation** = auto-ignition of **end-gas** after spark; **Knock** = the *sound* it produces.
- [x] **Pre-ignition** happens *before* the spark (hot spot); **Detonation** happens *after* the spark (end-gas chemistry).
- [x] End-gas is the unburned mixture between flame front and cylinder walls.
- [x] Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) of gasoline ‚âà **370‚Äì430 ¬∞C**.
- [x] **Octane Number** measures knock resistance; iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0.
- [x] Higher **compression ratio** ‚Üí more knock risk.
- [x] Higher **intake air temperature** ‚Üí more knock risk.
- [x] **Advanced spark timing** ‚Üí more knock risk.
- [x] **Knock sensor** uses **piezoelectric effect**; mounted on **cylinder block**; senses **5‚Äì15 kHz** vibration band.
- [x] ECU response to knock = **retard ignition timing** (typically 1¬∞‚Äì3¬∞ at a time).
- [x] **KLSA** = Knock Limited Spark Advance ‚Äî maximum safe advance at given operating point.
- [x] Knock-limited pressure rise rate: **> 10 bar/deg crank angle**.
- [x] Methods to control detonation: high-octane fuel, intercooler, EGR, swirl, optimal chamber shape, retarded timing.
- [x] **Adiabatic relation** for end-gas: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma - 1}$.
- [x] Auto-ignition delay follows **Arrhenius-type kinetics**: $\tau = A \cdot e^{E_a/RT}$.
- [x] Lean or rich mixtures are less knock-prone than stoichiometric.
- [x] Engine damage from prolonged detonation: **piston crown erosion, ring land cracking, bearing failure, head gasket blowout**.

> **Final KTU Tip:** For the 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition ‚Üí (2) Mechanism (with sketch) ‚Üí (3) Factors ‚Üí (4) Control method ‚Üí (5) Modern ECU strategy.** This five-part structure consistently scores 12+/14 in KTU Module 1 questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automotive Emission** refers to the gaseous, particulate, and other by-products released into the atmosphere as a result of the combustion of fuel (typically petrol, diesel, or alternative fuels) and the evaporation of fuel in internal combustion (IC) engines used in automobiles. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT602 ‚Äì Autotronics)**, automotive emissions are broadly classified into two main categories:

1. **Exhaust Emissions (Tailpipe Emissions):** Products of incomplete or complete combustion that exit through the exhaust system.
2. **Evaporative Emissions (Crankcase & Fuel System Emissions):** Hydrocarbon vapours released from the fuel tank, carburettor (legacy systems), and crankcase due to blow-by gases.

The primary regulated pollutants in modern emission standards are:
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**
- **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)**
- **Oxides of Nitrogen (NO‚Çì)**
- **Particulate Matter (PM)**
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO‚ÇÇ)** ‚Äì a greenhouse gas (GHG), not a direct toxic pollutant but regulated for climate change.
- **Sulphur Oxides (SO‚Çì)** ‚Äì mainly from diesel sulphur content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasises understanding the *types* of emissions, the *chemistry of formation*, *emission norms* (Bharat Stage / Euro), and the *need for electronic control* in vehicles ‚Äì which forms the foundation for the entire Autotronics course.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human body eating food. Just as a body digests food and produces waste (CO‚ÇÇ from lungs, urea from kidneys), a car "digests" fuel (petrol/diesel) in its engine cylinder and produces waste gases. The difference is that a car's "breath" is much more toxic because combustion in a high-pressure cylinder is rarely 100 % complete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of an engine cylinder as a kitchen stove burning LPG. If the air-to-gas ratio is perfect (Stoichiometric: 14.7:1 for petrol), the flame is clean blue. But if the mixture is rich (excess fuel) or lean (excess air), you get a yellow, sooty flame producing **CO, HC, soot**. Add high temperature, and nitrogen in the air itself combines with oxygen to form **NO‚Çì** ‚Äì like how a lightning bolt (high energy) creates NO‚Çì in the atmosphere.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol/Gasoline):** **14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel):** **14.5 : 1** (by mass)
- **Lambda (Œª) value:** Actual AFR / Stoichiometric AFR. Œª = 1 is stoichiometric; Œª < 1 is rich; Œª > 1 is lean.
- **Standard units of emission measurement:** **g/km** (for light-duty vehicles) and **g/kWh** (for heavy-duty engines).
- **BS-VI (Bharat Stage VI) limits for petrol cars (example):** CO = **1.0 g/km**, HC+NO‚Çì = **0.10 g/km**, NO‚Çì = **0.06 g/km**, PM = **0.0045 g/km** (for direct injection).
- **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Petrol):** approximately **2200 ¬∞C**, which is the high temperature zone responsible for NO‚Çì formation.
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**
- **Molecular weight of air:** **28.97 g/mol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Intuition on Œª (Lambda):** Lambda is the "diet balance" of the engine. A healthy, well-tuned engine (Œª = 1) breathes clean. Too much fuel (Œª < 1) ‚Üí CO & HC rise. Too much air (Œª > 1) ‚Üí NO‚Çì & temperature rise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Emissions

### 2.1.1 Exhaust Emissions (Primary Concern for Autotronics)

| Pollutant | Chemical Formula | Primary Source / Cause | Health / Environmental Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion (rich mixture, Œª < 1) | Toxic, binds haemoglobin ‚Üí carboxyhaemoglobin; asphyxiation |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC | Incomplete combustion, flame quenching at walls, crevice volumes | Photochemical smog, carcinogenic (Benzene, PAH) |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | NO, NO‚ÇÇ (collectively NO‚Çì) | High in-cylinder temperatures (> 1500 ¬∞C) ‚Äì Zeldovich mechanism | Acid rain, ground-level ozone, respiratory ailments |
| Particulate Matter | PM (soot) | Rich mixtures in diesel; local fuel-rich zones in GDI petrol | Lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, visibility reduction |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO‚ÇÇ | Complete combustion of hydrocarbon fuel | Greenhouse gas ‚Üí global warming (CO‚ÇÇ = 44 g/mol) |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO‚ÇÇ | Sulphur impurity in diesel/petrol | Acid rain, lung irritation |

### 2.1.2 Evaporative and Other Emissions

- **Crankcase Blow-by Emissions (HC):** Combustion gases leak past piston rings into the crankcase. Earlier vented to atmosphere (pre-emission norms); now recirculated via **PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation)** valve.
- **Fuel Tank Evaporation (HC):** Hot fuel evaporates; controlled by **EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control)** system with charcoal canister.
- **Refuelling Emissions (HC):** Vapours displaced during fuel filling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** A modern Autotronics engineer must understand *why* each pollutant is formed. The ECU (Engine Control Unit) precisely controls AFR, ignition timing, EGR, and boost pressure to minimise all regulated emissions simultaneously ‚Äì a multi-objective optimisation problem.

## 2.2 Chemistry of Pollutant Formation

### 2.2.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO) Formation
CO is a product of **incomplete combustion** of hydrocarbons. The global combustion reaction is:

$$ C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O $$

If oxygen is locally insufficient, **equilibrium shifts toward CO** instead of CO‚ÇÇ (Le Chatelier's principle at high T). Also, the water-gas shift reaction:

$$ CO_2 + H_2 \rightleftharpoons CO + H_2O $$

dissociation of CO‚ÇÇ is significant above **2000 K**. Therefore:
- **Rich mixtures (Œª < 1)** ‚Üí High CO
- **Lean mixtures (Œª > 1)** ‚Üí Low CO
- **High EGR, low temperature** ‚Üí can also raise CO slightly due to incomplete oxidation.

### 2.2.2 Hydrocarbon (HC) Emissions
HC originates from:
1. **Flame quenching at cylinder walls** (a thin gas layer ~0.1 mm thick near cold walls does not burn).
2. **Crevice volumes** ‚Äì gas trapped in piston ring grooves and head gasket.
3. **Fuel trapped in oil film** on cylinder walls (especially cold start).
4. **Wall deposits** absorbing fuel.

**Wall Quenching Theory (Daniel's model):** The quench distance $d_q$ scales inversely with pressure:

$$ d_q \propto \frac{1}{P} $$

Hence higher compression ‚Üí thinner quench layer ‚Üí lower HC. But cold start (low wall temperature) ‚Üí HC spikes 10√ó.

### 2.2.3 Oxides of Nitrogen (NO‚Çì) Formation ‚Äì **Zeldovich Mechanism**
This is the most important high-yield topic. NO‚Çì is mostly **NO** (~ 90‚Äì95 %), with NO‚ÇÇ forming later by oxidation. The extended Zeldovich mechanism comprises three reactions:

$$ O + N_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + N $$

$$ N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O $$

$$ N + OH \rightleftharpoons NO + H $$

The first reaction is **rate-limiting** due to the high N‚â°N triple bond energy of **941 kJ/mol**.

**Formation rate (simplified, per Heywood):**

$$ \frac{d[NO]}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot [O][N_2] $$

where the activation energy $E_a \approx 319 \, kJ/mol$ and $A$ is a pre-exponential factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"NO‚Çì is a function of temperature"** ‚Äì Specifically, NO‚Çì doubles for every ~ 70 ¬∞C rise in peak flame temperature above 1500 ¬∞C. This is why EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) is the most effective NO‚Çì reduction technique ‚Äì it lowers peak temperature by diluting the charge.

### 2.2.4 Particulate Matter (PM) in Diesel
PM forms in locally fuel-rich zones within the diesel spray. The nucleation-and-coagulation process: fuel ‚Üí PAH ‚Üí soot nuclei ‚Üí agglomerates. GDI petrol engines also produce PM ‚Äì sometimes more than modern diesels ‚Äì because direct injection creates fuel-rich pockets near the injector.

### 2.2.5 CO‚ÇÇ and Fuel Economy
Using the carbon balance method:

$$ \dot{m}_{CO_2} = \frac{44}{12} \cdot C_{fuel} \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} $$

where $C_{fuel}$ is the mass fraction of carbon in the fuel (‚âà 0.85 for petrol, 0.87 for diesel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** CO‚ÇÇ emission is directly proportional to fuel consumption. Therefore, improving fuel economy (BSFC ‚Üì) automatically reduces CO‚ÇÇ ‚Äì this is the basis of the EU's fleet-average CO‚ÇÇ regulation of **95 g CO‚ÇÇ/km** (2021 onwards).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ (petrol) | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass basis) | dimensionless | Engine calibration, lambda sensing |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (lambda sensor output) | dimensionless | Closed-loop fuel control |
| 3 | $C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$ | Complete combustion equation | ‚Äì | Theoretical O‚ÇÇ requirement |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = A\, e^{-E_a/RT}\, [O][N_2]$ | Zeldovich NO formation rate | mol/(m¬≥¬∑s) | NO‚Çì estimation |
| 5 | $E_a = 319\, kJ/mol$ | Activation energy for NO formation | J/mol | Combustion kinetics |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = \frac{44}{12} C_{fuel} \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Carbon balance for CO‚ÇÇ | g/s | CO‚ÇÇ emission calculation |
| 7 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh | Fuel economy, CO‚ÇÇ indicator |
| 8 | $d_q \propto \dfrac{1}{P}$ | Quench distance vs. pressure | m | HC formation analysis |
| 9 | $E_{CO} = 12.7\, \text{eV}$ | CO ‚Üí CO‚ÇÇ oxidation activation energy | eV | Catalytic converter design |
| 10 | $\rho_{NO_x} \approx 2^{(T-1500)/70}$ | NO‚Çì temperature sensitivity (approx.) | ppm | EGR calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** All modern vehicles have an OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics) port that monitors emission-related components. The ECU uses lambda sensors, EGR valve position sensors, and temperature sensors to keep the engine within emission norms. A P0420 code (Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold) directly indicates emission failure ‚Äì a real-life application of this theory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Petrol (C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çá, approximated as iso-octane)

The general combustion reaction for a hydrocarbon $C_x H_y$:

$$ C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2O $$

**Step 1:** Write for iso-octane ($C_8H_{18}$, simplified):

$$ C_8H_{18} + a\, O_2 \longrightarrow 8\, CO_2 + 9\, H_2O $$

**Step 2:** Balance oxygen atoms: RHS has $2(8) + 9 = 25$ oxygen atoms. Therefore:

$$ a = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5\, \text{mol}\, O_2 $$

**Step 3:** Air contains 21 % O‚ÇÇ and 79 % N‚ÇÇ by volume (mole fraction). So **1 mol O‚ÇÇ brings 3.76 mol N‚ÇÇ**.

$$ \text{Moles of air} = 12.5 \times (1 + 3.76) = 12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5 \text{ mol air} $$

**Step 4:** Mass of air required:

$$ m_{air} = 59.5 \times 28.97 = 1723.7\, \text{g} $$

**Step 5:** Mass of fuel ($C_8H_{18}$):

$$ m_{fuel} = 8(12) + 18(1) = 96 + 18 = 114\, \text{g} $$

**Step 6:** Stoichiometric AFR:

$$ AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{1723.7}{114} \approx 15.13 $$

> [!NOTE]
> The actual textbook value of **14.7** is an *empirical* value derived by averaging over commercial petrol (which is a mixture of paraffins, aromatics, naphthenes). For pure iso-octane the theoretical value is ~ 15.13. For pure n-heptane it is ~ 15.13 as well. KTU examiners typically use **14.7** for standard problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: NO Formation Rate ‚Äì Simplified Zeldovich

The first two reactions of the extended Zeldovich mechanism:

$$ O + N_2 \xrightarrow{k_1} NO + N \quad \text{(rate-limiting)} $$

$$ N + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} NO + O $$

**Step 1:** Assuming steady state for N atoms:

$$ \frac{d[N]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] - k_2 [N][O_2] = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Solve for [N]:

$$ [N]_{ss} = \frac{k_1 [O][N_2]}{k_2 [O_2]} $$

**Step 3:** Net rate of NO formation:

$$ \frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + k_2 [N][O_2] = 2 k_1 [O][N_2] $$

**Step 4:** Using Arrhenius form $k_1 = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$:

$$ \frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 A\, e^{-E_a/RT}\, [O][N_2] $$

**Step 5:** Substitute $E_a = 319\, kJ/mol$, $R = 8.314\, J/(mol \cdot K)$:

$$ \frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2A\, e^{-38378/T}\, [O][N_2] $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The exponential dependence on $-1/T$ is the *reason* NO‚Çì is dominated by peak in-cylinder temperature, not by air-fuel ratio directly. This is why exhaust gas temperature sensors and EGR control are at the heart of NO‚Çì management.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Emission vs. Air-Fuel Ratio (Lambda)

A **classic KTU-style question** asks to explain how emissions vary with Œª. The "trade-off" curve is summarised in the matrix below.

| Lambda (Œª) | Mixture | CO | HC | NO‚Çì | BSFC (Fuel Economy) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 0.8 | Very rich | Very High ‚Üë‚Üë | High ‚Üë | Low ‚Üì | Poor (fuel wasted) |
| 0.9 | Rich | High ‚Üë | Moderate | Low‚ÄìModerate | Best (maximum power) |
| **1.0** | **Stoichiometric** | **Low (acceptable)** | **Low (acceptable)** | **Moderate** | Good (acceptable) |
| 1.1 | Slightly lean | Very Low ‚Üì | Low | Moderate‚ÄìHigh ‚Üë | Good |
| 1.2‚Äì1.3 | Lean | Very Low | Moderate (combustion instability) | High ‚Üë‚Üë | Good (lean-burn) |
| > 1.3 | Very lean | Very Low | High (misfire, HC ‚Üë‚Üë) | Moderate | Poor (misfire) |

**Trade-off Reality:** There is **no single Œª** that simultaneously minimises CO, HC, and NO‚Çì. This is the central optimisation problem solved by the **three-way catalytic converter (TWC)** operating in a narrow Œª window of **0.99 ‚Äì 1.01** (closed-loop control with oxygen sensor).

## 3.4 Derivation: Carbon Balance for CO‚ÇÇ Calculation from Fuel Consumption

**Step 1:** A vehicle consumes fuel at rate $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ (kg/h). The carbon content of petrol is approximately $C_{fuel} = 0.85$ (by mass).

**Step 2:** Mass of carbon in fuel per hour:

$$ \dot{m}_C = 0.85 \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} $$

**Step 3:** Combustion converts all carbon to CO‚ÇÇ (ideally). Molecular weight ratio:

$$ \frac{M_{CO_2}}{M_C} = \frac{44}{12} = 3.667 $$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$ \dot{m}_{CO_2} = 3.667 \cdot \dot{m}_C = 3.667 \times 0.85 \times \dot{m}_{fuel} $$

$$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 3.117 \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}} $$

**Step 5:** Numerical example: If a car consumes $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 5\, kg/100\, km$:

$$ \dot{m}_{CO_2} = 3.117 \times 5 = 15.58\, \text{kg}/100\, \text{km} = 155.8\, \text{g/km} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world check:** The EU 2021 fleet average target is **95 g CO‚ÇÇ/km** ‚Äì this is one of the most important regulatory numbers in modern automotive engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Emission Formation Mechanism Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Fuel Mixture Entering Cylinder] --> B{Combustion Process}
    B --> C[Complete Combustion]
    B --> D[Incomplete Combustion]
    B --> E[High Temperature Zone]
    C --> F[CO2 and H2O]
    F --> G[Exhaust Manifold]
    D --> H[Carbon Monoxide CO]
    D --> I[Unburnt Hydrocarbons HC]
    D --> J[Soot or Particulate Matter PM]
    E --> K[NOx via Zeldovich Mechanism]
    H --> G
    I --> G
    J --> G
    K --> G
    G --> L[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    L --> M[Lambda Sensor Feedback]
    M --> N[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    N --> O[Fuel Injector Pulse Width Adjustment]
    O --> A
    L --> P[Tailpipe Emission Out]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Emission Control Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fuel Tank] --> A2[Charcoal Canister EVAP]
    A2 --> A3[Purge Valve]
    A3 --> A4[Intake Manifold]
    B1[Crankcase] --> B2[PCV Valve]
    B2 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Engine Cylinder]
    A5 --> A6[Exhaust Manifold]
    A6 --> A7[Oxygen Sensor Lambda Sensor 1]
    A7 --> A8[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    A8 --> A9[Fuel Injector]
    A8 --> A10[Ignition Coil]
    A6 --> A11[EGR Valve]
    A11 --> A4
    A6 --> A12[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    A12 --> A13[Lambda Sensor 2]
    A13 --> A8
    A12 --> A14[Tailpipe]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Trade-off Relationship between Lambda and Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Lambda Variation] --> P1[CO Emission Curve]
    L0 --> P2[HC Emission Curve]
    L0 --> P3[NOx Emission Curve]
    L0 --> P4[BSFC Fuel Economy Curve]
    P1 --> T1[CO Peaks at Rich lambda below 0.9]
    P2 --> T2[HC Peaks at Very Rich and Very Lean]
    P3 --> T3[NOx Peaks at Slightly Lean around lambda 1.1]
    P4 --> T4[BSFC Best at lambda 0.9 Power and around 1.2 Lean Burn]
    T1 --> Out[Trade Off No Single Lambda Minimises All Pollutants]
    T2 --> Out
    T3 --> Out
    T4 --> Out
    Out --> Sol[Solution ECU maintains lambda 0.99 to 1.01 and TWC cleans exhaust]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Three-Way Catalytic Converter Reactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CO from Engine] --> R1[Oxidation Reaction]
    B[HC from Engine] --> R1
    R1 --> A1[CO2 and H2O]
    C[NOx from Engine] --> R2[Reduction Reaction]
    R2 --> A2[N2 and O2]
    A1 --> Out[Clean Tailpipe Emission]
    A2 --> Out
    A1 -.Catalyst Surface.-> Out
    A2 -.Catalyst Surface.-> Out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** A modern TWC contains platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd), and rhodium (Rh) on a ceramic honeycomb substrate (typically cordierite, 400 cells/in¬≤). Rh is responsible for NO‚Çì reduction; Pt/Pd for CO and HC oxidation. The conversion efficiency exceeds 95 % only when Œª is held within the tight window 0.99‚Äì1.01.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Set A]**
**"List any six harmful constituents of automotive exhaust gases and state the conditions that favour their formation."**

**Model Answer (6 √ó 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | Pollutant | Condition Favouring Formation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carbon Monoxide (CO) | Rich mixture, Œª < 1; insufficient O‚ÇÇ for complete combustion |
| 2 | Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC) | Flame quenching near cold walls; crevice volumes; cold start |
| 3 | Oxides of Nitrogen (NO‚Çì) | High in-cylinder peak temperature (> 1500 ¬∞C); high pressure |
| 4 | Particulate Matter (PM) | Local fuel-rich zones (Diesel & GDI); low EGR |
| 5 | Carbon Dioxide (CO‚ÇÇ) | Complete combustion ‚Äì unavoidable product of hydrocarbon fuel |
| 6 | Sulphur Dioxide (SO‚ÇÇ) | Sulphur content in fuel (diesel historically higher) |

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Model Question]**
**"Define Lambda (Œª). What does Œª = 0.9 and Œª = 1.2 indicate about the air-fuel mixture?"**

**Model Answer:**

Lambda (Œª) is defined as the ratio of actual air-fuel ratio (AFR) supplied to the engine to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio:

$$ \lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} $$

- **Œª = 0.9 ‚Üí Rich mixture** (insufficient air, AFR ‚âà 13.23). Produces high CO and HC; gives maximum power.
- **Œª = 1.2 ‚Üí Lean mixture** (excess air, AFR ‚âà 17.64). Reduces CO and HC; increases NO‚Çì; improves fuel economy; risk of misfire.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each ‚Äì Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When asked to *list* emissions, students often miss **CO‚ÇÇ and PM**, losing 1‚Äì2 marks.
> - In Zeldovich mechanism, students forget to mention the **rate-limiting** first step (N‚â°N bond breaking) ‚Äì examiners deduct 1 mark.
> - Always state **conditions** along with pollutant names (e.g., "NO‚Çì forms at high temperature") ‚Äì ¬Ω mark per condition.
> - Units matter: g/km vs. g/kWh ‚Äì mentioning the wrong unit costs marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the formation of the following exhaust emissions in an SI engine: (i) CO, (ii) HC, (iii) NO‚Çì. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the variation of CO, HC, NO‚Çì and BSFC with respect to air-fuel ratio (Œª). Explain how the three-way catalytic converter resolves this trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formation Mechanisms in SI Engine**

**(i) Carbon Monoxide (CO):**
CO is formed due to **incomplete combustion** of hydrocarbons. In the rich regions of the flame (where local Œª < 1), insufficient O‚ÇÇ is available to convert carbon fully to CO‚ÇÇ. At high temperatures, the equilibrium:

$$ CO_2 \rightleftharpoons CO + \frac{1}{2}\,O_2 $$

shifts to the right (CO‚ÇÇ dissociation becomes significant above 2000 K). During expansion and exhaust blowdown, gases cool rapidly and equilibrium "freezes" at high CO levels because the reaction is kinetically slow during cooling.

**(ii) Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC):**
HC emissions are due to:
- **Flame quenching at cylinder walls** ‚Äì A thin unburnt gas layer (~0.1 mm) adjacent to the cold walls where the flame is extinguished.
- **Crevice volumes** ‚Äì Combustion gases trapped in piston ring grooves, head gasket, and spark plug threads escape unburnt.
- **Oil layer absorption** ‚Äì Fuel dissolves in lubricating oil on the cylinder wall and is released later.
- **Cold start** ‚Äì Incomplete vaporisation leads to pockets of unburnt fuel.

**(iii) Oxides of Nitrogen (NO‚Çì):**
NO‚Çì is formed primarily by the **extended Zeldovich mechanism** at high peak temperatures (> 1500 ¬∞C). The reactions are:

$$ O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N \quad \text{(slow, rate-limiting)} $$

$$ N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O $$

$$ N + OH \rightarrow NO + H $$

The rate doubles approximately every **70 ¬∞C** rise in peak temperature above 1500 ¬∞C, making NO‚Çì strongly temperature-dependent.

**(b) Œª Trade-off and Three-Way Catalytic Converter**

| Œª | CO | HC | NO‚Çì | BSFC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.8 (rich) | Very High | High | Low | Poor |
| 0.9 (rich) | High | Moderate | Low | **Best (max power)** |
| 1.0 (stoich) | Low | Low | Moderate | Good |
| 1.1 (lean) | Very Low | Low | **High** | Good |
| 1.3 (lean) | Very Low | Moderate (misfire) | High | Moderate |

The trade-off: **no single Œª minimises all three pollutants**. The **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** resolves this by simultaneously performing:
- **Oxidation:** CO ‚Üí CO‚ÇÇ; HC ‚Üí CO‚ÇÇ + H‚ÇÇO
- **Reduction:** NO‚Çì ‚Üí N‚ÇÇ + O‚ÇÇ

For high TWC efficiency (> 95 %), the engine must operate within the narrow **Œª window of 0.99 to 1.01**. This is achieved by **closed-loop control** using a **lambda (oxygen) sensor** in the exhaust ‚Äì the ECU adjusts injector pulse width every ~ 10 ms to keep Œª at 1.00.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Describe the major sources of evaporative emissions in a vehicle. With a block diagram, explain the working of the **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP)** system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bharat Stage (BS) emission norms** in India. Compare BS-IV and BS-VI norms for a petrol passenger car with specific numerical limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sources of Evaporative Emissions & EVAP System**

**Sources:**
1. **Fuel tank venting** ‚Äì Hot ambient + hot fuel ‚Üí vapour build-up; older vehicles vent to atmosphere (HC pollution).
2. **Crankcase blow-by** ‚Äì Combustion gases leak past piston rings into the crankcase.
3. **Carburettor (legacy) fuel bowl** ‚Äì Heat vaporises fuel.
4. **Fuel hoses and seals** ‚Äì Permeation through rubber.

**EVAP System Block Diagram (descriptive):**

Fuel Tank (vapour) ‚Üí Charcoal Canister (adsorbs HC) ‚Üí Purge Solenoid Valve (controlled by ECU) ‚Üí Intake Manifold (HC is drawn into engine and burned) ‚Üí Combustion.

**Working principle:**
- During engine OFF / idle: HC vapours from the fuel tank are adsorbed by activated carbon in the **charcoal canister**.
- During engine running (closed throttle open): The **purge valve** (PWM-controlled by ECU) opens, allowing fresh air to flow through the canister. The trapped HC vapours are desorbed and drawn into the intake manifold where they are combusted in the engine, effectively "recycling" them.

**PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation):** Crankcase blow-by gases (HC) are routed through a PCV valve back to the intake manifold, instead of being vented to atmosphere.

**(b) Bharat Stage Emission Norms ‚Äì BS-IV vs BS-VI**

Bharat Stage (BS) norms are Indian emission standards based on European (Euro) regulations, with a 1- to 2-year lag historically. Each stage tightens the permissible limits for CO, HC, NO‚Çì, PM.

**BS-IV (Implemented 2017, skipped ‚Üí direct jump to BS-VI in 2020):**

| Pollutant | BS-IV (Petrol) | BS-VI (Petrol) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 1.0 g/km | **1.0 g/km** |
| HC + NO‚Çì | 0.30 g/km | **0.10 g/km** (67 % reduction) |
| NO‚Çì (separately) | 0.08 g/km | **0.06 g/km** (25 % reduction) |
| PM | 0.025 g/km (GDI) | **0.0045 g/km** (82 % reduction) |

**BS-VI (Implemented 1 April 2020):** Introduces:
- **Real Driving Emissions (RDE)** test (like Euro 6d-TEMP) ‚Äì measures on-road, not just in lab.
- **Sulphur content** in fuel: dropped from 10 ppm (BS-IV) to **10 ppm** (already low); also requires onboard **fuel-quality monitoring**.
- **NO‚Çì sensors mandatory** in OBD-II system.
- **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)** mandatory for direct-injection petrol engines.
- **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** mandatory for diesel engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** India has now aligned with Euro 6d norms. The upcoming **BS-VII** (likely 2027) will tighten NO‚Çì further to ~ 0.03 g/km and introduce stricter EV-related lifecycle CO‚ÇÇ metrics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE ‚Äì Module 1: Emission**

- ‚úÖ **Six regulated pollutants:** CO, HC, NO‚Çì, PM, CO‚ÇÇ, SO‚ÇÇ.
- ‚úÖ **Stoichiometric AFR:** Petrol = **14.7 : 1**; Diesel = **14.5 : 1**.
- ‚úÖ **Lambda Œª = 1** is stoichiometric; rich Œª < 1; lean Œª > 1.
- ‚úÖ **CO** increases with rich mixture; **NO‚Çì** increases with high temperature; **HC** peaks during cold start and very lean operation.
- ‚úÖ **Zeldovich mechanism** = 3 reactions; first reaction is rate-limiting due to N‚â°N bond (941 kJ/mol).
- ‚úÖ **NO‚Çì doubles** for every **70 ¬∞C** rise in flame temperature above 1500 ¬∞C.
- ‚úÖ **TWC (Three-Way Catalyst)** requires Œª = 0.99‚Äì1.01 to achieve > 95 % conversion.
- ‚úÖ **Lambda sensor (O‚ÇÇ sensor)** is the heart of closed-loop emission control.
- ‚úÖ **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** is the primary NO‚Çì control technique.
- ‚úÖ **EVAP system** uses charcoal canister to trap fuel vapours; **PCV valve** recycles crankcase blow-by.
- ‚úÖ **BS-VI** introduced RDE, NO‚Çì sensors, GPF/DPF; **CO‚ÇÇ fleet target** = 95 g/km (EU), India 130 g/km (2023).
- ‚úÖ **Quench distance** $d_q \propto 1/P$ (Daniel's model) explains HC reduction with higher compression.
- ‚úÖ **Carbon balance:** $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = 3.117 \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}$ for petrol.
- ‚úÖ **Trade-off:** No single AFR simultaneously minimises all pollutants ‚Üí solved by TWC + ECU closed-loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **Definition ‚Üí Formation mechanism ‚Üí Equation ‚Üí Conditions ‚Üí Control technique ‚Üí Diagram**. This pattern is the KTU 2024 board's preferred answer template.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Carburettor System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feedback Carburettor System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feedback Carburettor System:** A carburetion system in which a conventional variable-venturi (or fixed-venturi) carburettor is integrated with a **closed-loop electronic control loop** that continuously samples the exhaust gas composition using a **lambda (oxygen) sensor** and dynamically modulates the air-fuel (A/F) delivery to maintain the mixture at, or very close to, the **stoichiometric ratio (Œª = 1)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under the broader umbrella of **Closed-Loop Mixture Control in Carburetted Spark-Ignition (SI) Engines** ‚Äî a transitional technology developed in the late 1970s and 1980s to meet stringent exhaust emission norms (Euro-I equivalent / US EPA Tier 0 norms) before fuel injection (EFI/MPFI) became universally adopted.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition ‚Äî "The Thermostat Analogy":**
> Imagine a shower mixer that is manually adjusted, but you install a *temperature probe* at the showerhead. The probe senses the outlet water temperature and sends a signal to a small motor that turns the hot/cold knob to keep the temperature at exactly 40 ¬∞C. The **shower mixer = carburettor**, the **probe = lambda sensor**, the **controller = ECU**, and the **small motor = feedback actuator**. This is precisely how a feedback carburettor "dithers" around the stoichiometric point.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Gasoline SI Engine) |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (mass) | $(A/F)_{st}$ | **14.7 : 1** |
| Air molecular weight | $M_{air}$ | **28.97 g/mol** |
| Fuel (iso-octane, C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà) mol. weight | $M_f$ | **114.23 g/mol** |
| Stoichiometric equivalence ratio (target) | $\lambda_{target}$ | **1.00 ¬± 0.01** |
| Oxygen content of dry air | $X_{O_2}$ | **20.9 %** (‚âà 0.21) |
| Lambda sensor switching voltage | $V_{\lambda}$ | **450 mV ¬± 50 mV** at 600 ¬∞C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Feedback Carburettor is part of **Module 2 ‚Äì Engine Management Systems**, which covers both carburettor-based and fuel-injection-based mixture preparation. The student must be able to draw the closed-loop block diagram and explain the role of each sensor and actuator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture & Subsystem Functions

A Feedback Carburettor is **not a new type of carburettor** ‚Äî it is a *conventional* carburettor (typically a **variable-venturi type** such as the GM Varajet II, Ford Motorcraft 7200 VV, or Hitachi DJET) augmented with the following closed-loop sub-systems:

1. **Lambda (Oxygen) Sensor** ‚Äî Heated ZrO‚ÇÇ thimble mounted in the exhaust manifold. Generates a Nernst EMF proportional to the logarithmic ratio of exhaust-side to reference-side O‚ÇÇ partial pressures.
2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** ‚Äî Compares the lambda-sensor voltage against a 450 mV reference (the stoichiometric switching threshold) and drives the actuator via a Proportional-Integral (P-I) algorithm.
3. **Feedback Actuator (Mixture Control Solenoid / Stepper Motor)** ‚Äî Modulates a *controlled air bleed* (or *fuel orifice*) inside the carburettor main metering circuit. The bleed admits extra air (or drains extra fuel), which **leanes or enriches** the mixture at the venturi discharge.
4. **Auxiliary Inputs to ECU:**
   - Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS) ‚Äî disables closed loop during warm-up.
   - Intake Air Temperature Sensor (IATS).
   - Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) / Idle Switch.
   - Battery Voltage.
5. **Thermo-Time Switch & Auxiliary Air Valve** ‚Äî Manage cold-start enrichment and idle air by-pass.

### 2.2 Modes of Operation

- **Open-Loop Mode (Cold Start & Warm-Up):** The lambda sensor is below 300 ¬∞C and produces negligible EMF. The ECU ignores the sensor and runs a **pre-programmed enrichment map** based on CTS and IATS ‚Äî typically rich ($\lambda$ ‚âà 0.85‚Äì0.95).
- **Closed-Loop Mode (Normal Operating Temperature):** Once CTS > 60‚Äì70 ¬∞C *and* lambda sensor > 350 ¬∞C, the ECU enters closed loop. It now **dithers** the mixture between slightly rich and slightly lean, with the average tending toward $\lambda = 1$.
- **Open-Loop Override:** During Wide-Open-Throttle (WOT) acceleration, idle, or deceleration fuel-cut, the ECU ignores the sensor and applies a fixed map.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Cheat Sheet ‚Äî Memorise the table below. The marks are awarded for the equations and their units, not for the prose.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation | Units | Typical Value (Gasoline) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio ($\lambda$) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda = 1$ target |
| 2 | Stoichiometric A/F (by mass) | $(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{n_{O_2}(1+\beta)M_{air}}{M_{fuel}}$ | kg/kg | 14.7 |
| 3 | Stoichiometric A/F (molar basis) | $(A/F)_{st,mol} = 4.773 \times \dfrac{M_{air}}{M_{fuel} + 1.5 n_H M_H}$ | mol/mol | 59.0 |
| 4 | Mass of air per kg fuel (stoich.) | $m_{air} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{st}}{1+(A/F)_{st}} \times 1\ kg$ | kg | 0.932 kg |
| 5 | Lambda Sensor Nernst EMF | $E = \dfrac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{O_2}^{ref}}{P_{O_2}^{exh}}\right)$ | V (‚âà 0.1‚Äì0.9 V) | 0.45 V at stoich. |
| 6 | Closed-Loop PI Control Law | $u(t)=K_p e(t)+K_i\!\int_0^{t}\!e(\tau)d\tau$ | ‚Äî | tuned empirically |
| 7 | Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $G_{cl}(s)=\dfrac{G_c(s)G_p(s)}{1+G_c(s)G_p(s)H(s)}$ | ‚Äî | ‚Äî |
| 8 | Sensor Switching Threshold | $V_{switch}\approx 450\ mV$ | mV | 450 mV |
| 9 | Dither Frequency | $f_{dither} \approx 0.5$‚Äì$2$ Hz | Hz | 1 Hz typical |
| 10 | Closed-Loop Mixture Tolerance | $\lambda \in [0.97, 1.03]$ | dimensionless | ¬±3 % |

Where $\beta = 3.773$ (moles of N‚ÇÇ per mole of O‚ÇÇ in dry air) and $n_H$ is the number of hydrogen atoms in the fuel molecule.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emission Compliance:** The Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) requires $\lambda$ within $\pm 1\%$ of unity to simultaneously reduce CO, HC, and NO‚Çì. Without closed-loop control, TWC efficiency drops below 50 %.
- **Fuel Economy:** Operating at $\lambda = 1$ maximises the completeness of combustion and therefore the heating value extracted per kg of fuel.
- **Historical Significance:** The feedback carburettor was the **bridge technology** between the 1970s "fixed-jet carburettor + air pump" emission systems and the modern port-fuel-injection (PFI) systems mandated from 1995 onwards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Iso-Octane (C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the balanced combustion equation.**

For complete combustion of one mole of iso-octane:

$$C_8H_{18} + a\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Balance oxygen atoms.**  
Right side has $8 \times 2 + 9 \times 1 = 25$ oxygen atoms, so $a = 12.5$ moles of O‚ÇÇ.

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert O‚ÇÇ requirement to air requirement using the volumetric composition of dry air.**  
Dry air contains **20.9 % O‚ÇÇ by volume** (mole fraction $X_{O_2} = 0.209$). Therefore:

$$n_{air} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{X_{O_2}} = \frac{12.5}{0.209} = 59.81 \text{ moles of air}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Convert mole quantities to masses.**

$$m_{fuel} = n_{fuel} \times M_{C_8H_{18}} = 1 \times 114.23 = 114.23 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{air} = n_{air} \times M_{air} = 59.81 \times 28.97 = 1732.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the stoichiometric mass-based A/F ratio.**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{1732.5}{114.23} = 15.17$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the textbook quote 14.7 instead of 15.17?** Commercial gasoline is **not pure C‚ÇàH‚ÇÅ‚Çà**; it is a mixture of paraffins, isoparaffins, aromatics, and naphthenes with an *effective empirical formula* close to **CH‚ÇÅ.‚Çà‚ÇÖ** and an average molecular weight of ‚âà 100 g/mol. Re-deriving with this effective formula gives 14.7 ‚Äî a value students must memorise.

### 3.2 Derivation: Lambda Sensor EMF (Nernst Equation)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Cell representation.**  
The zirconia sensor behaves as a galvanic concentration cell:

$$Pt, P_{O_2}^{ref} \;\vert\; ZrO_2\text{ (CaO-stabilised)} \;\vert\; P_{O_2}^{exh}, Pt$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Nernst equation for the overall cell reaction** $O_2^{ref} \rightarrow O_2^{exh}$ with $n = 4$ electrons per O‚ÇÇ molecule:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q = -\frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2}^{exh}}{P_{O_2}^{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute numerical values** at the sensor hot-spot temperature $T = 873$ K (600 ¬∞C), with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K), $F = 96485$ C/mol, and $P_{O_2}^{ref} = 0.209$ atm (atmospheric):

$$E = \frac{8.314 \times 873}{4 \times 96485}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{P_{O_2}^{exh}}\right)$$

$$E = 0.01881 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{P_{O_2}^{exh}}\right) \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Evaluate at stoichiometric combustion.**  
At $\lambda = 1$, the post-flame exhaust $P_{O_2}^{exh} \approx 10^{-12}$ atm (near-complete consumption):

$$E = 0.01881 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{10^{-12}}\right) = 0.01881 \times 26.65 \approx 0.501 \text{ V}$$

This is the **stoichiometric switching voltage** ‚Äî a value the ECU uses as its reference.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Evaluate at rich and lean extremes.**

- **Rich** ($\lambda = 0.85$): unburnt O‚ÇÇ even lower ‚Üí $E$ saturates at ‚âà 0.8‚Äì0.9 V.
- **Lean** ($\lambda = 1.10$): residual O‚ÇÇ ‚âà 1 % ‚Üí $E \approx 0.1$ V.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Closed-Loop Control Block

| Element in Block Diagram | Mathematical Symbol | Physical Device in Feedback Carburettor | Transfer Function Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference Input | $R(s)$ | Target $\lambda = 1$ (450 mV ref.) | Constant |
| Controller | $G_c(s)$ | ECU + PI algorithm | $K_p\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ |
| Plant | $G_p(s)$ | Carburettor + engine air-pump | First-order lag: $\dfrac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$ |
| Actuator | ‚Äî | Mixture-control solenoid / stepper | First-order lag |
| Sensor | $H(s)$ | Lambda (O‚ÇÇ) sensor + amplifier | $\dfrac{K_s}{\tau_s s + 1}$ with dead-time $e^{-Ls}$ |
| Feedback Path | ‚Äî | Exhaust ‚Üí sensor ‚Üí ECU | Wiring harness |
| Disturbance | $D(s)$ | Altitude, ambient T, fuel quality | Stochastic |
| Error | $E(s)=R-HY$ | Deviation from stoich. | ‚Äî |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of the Feedback Carburettor

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **complete signal and mass flow** from accelerator pedal to exhaust sensor and back to the carburettor actuator.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DRIVER["DRIVER INPUTS"]
        A1[Accelerator Pedal]
        A2[Throttle Lever]
    end

    subgraph AIRFUEL["AIR-FUEL PREPARATION"]
        B1[Air Filter]
        B2[Throttle Body]
        B3[Carburettor Venturi]
        B4[Main Metering Jet]
        B5[Feedback Air Bleed]
        B6[Idle and Transition Circuits]
        B7[Fuel Bowl and Float]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE["ENGINE"]
        C1[Intake Manifold]
        C2[Combustion Chamber]
        C3[Exhaust Manifold]
    end

    subgraph SENSORS["SENSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1[Lambda Oxygen Sensor]
        D2[Coolant Temp Sensor]
        D3[Air Temp Sensor]
        D4[Throttle Position Switch]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1[ECU Microcontroller]
        E2[PI Control Algorithm]
        E3[Actuator Driver]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATOR["ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        F1[Feedback Mixture Solenoid]
        F2[Stepper Motor on Air Bleed]
    end

    subgraph EMISSION["EMISSION AFTER-TREATMENT"]
        G1[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
        G2[Tailpipe]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B7 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B3 --> B5
    B5 --> F2
    F2 --> B5
    B3 --> B6
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> B5
    F1 --> F2
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Control Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Reference Lambda Equals 1] --> SUM[ Summing Junction ]
    SUM --> ERR[ Error Signal E of S ]
    ERR --> GC[ ECU PI Controller Gc of S ]
    GC --> ACT[ Feedback Actuator ]
    ACT --> GP[ Carburettor Plus Engine Gp of S ]
    GP --> YOUT[ Output Mixture Lambda Actual ]
    YOUT --> CAT[ Exhaust Manifold ]
    CAT --> SNS[ Lambda Sensor H of S ]
    SNS --> SUM
    D[Disturbance Altitude and Fuel Quality] -.-> GP
```

### 4.3 State Machine of Mixture-Control Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Cranking
    Cranking --> OpenLoopWarmup : Engine Cranks
    OpenLoopWarmup --> ClosedLoop : Coolant Greater Than 70 deg C AND Lambda Sensor Greater Than 350 deg C
    ClosedLoop --> OpenLoopWOT : Wide Open Throttle Detected
    OpenLoopWOT --> ClosedLoop : TPS Below 80 Percent
    ClosedLoop --> OpenLoopIdle : Idle Switch Active
    OpenLoopIdle --> ClosedLoop : Engine Speed Above 1500 rpm
    ClosedLoop --> FuelCut : Deceleration Fuel Cut Active
    FuelCut --> ClosedLoop : TPS Opens Again
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2023, Model Paper Q2(a)]**

**Q1. Define a feedback carburettor. List its main components and state the function of the oxygen sensor.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A **feedback carburettor** is a carburetion system in which an electronic closed-loop controller continuously adjusts the air-fuel ratio to keep it at the stoichiometric value (A/F = 14.7:1 for gasoline) by using an **exhaust-gas oxygen (lambda) sensor** as the feedback element.

*Main Components:*

1. Conventional (variable-venturi) carburettor.
2. Lambda (O‚ÇÇ) sensor in the exhaust manifold.
3. Electronic Control Unit (ECU) with PI algorithm.
4. Feedback actuator (mixture-control solenoid or stepper motor) on the air bleed.
5. Coolant and air temperature sensors.

*Function of O‚ÇÇ sensor:* It generates a voltage (‚âà 0.1 V for lean, ‚âà 0.9 V for rich, with a 0.45 V switching point at stoichiometric) that the ECU uses as the feedback signal to lean/enrich the mixture.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2022, Model Paper Q1(c)]**

**Q2. What is the significance of the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio in a feedback carburettor system? Why is it chosen as the control target?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **stoichiometric A/F ratio (14.7:1)** is the chemically exact ratio at which all fuel and oxygen are consumed with no excess of either. It is chosen as the control target because:

1. The **three-way catalytic converter** achieves > 95 % conversion efficiency for CO, HC, and NO‚Çì **only** when $\lambda$ is within ¬± 1 % of unity.
2. Combustion is most complete ‚Üí minimum unburnt HC and CO.
3. Thermal efficiency and fuel economy are maximised when the catalyst is operating at its stoich. set-point.
4. The O‚ÇÇ sensor has a sharp, near-step voltage transition at $\lambda = 1$ (the 450 mV "switching point"), making it ideal as a feedback signal.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024, Module 2, Q9]**

### **Question A**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a feedback carburettor system** in a spark-ignition engine. Briefly describe the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Nernst equation for a zirconia lambda sensor** and compute the EMF at stoichiometric conditions (T = 873 K, $P_{O_2}^{exh} = 10^{-12}$ atm). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution ‚Äî (a)
