> - **Injectors** ‚Üí the **precise valves** that release fuel into the combustion chambers (lungs of the engine) at the right moment.
> - **ECU + Sensors** ‚Üí the **autonomic nervous system**, constantly sensing and adjusting.

If any one of these "organs" fails, the entire engine either runs poorly (misfire, hesitation) or stops running completely.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Engineering Standards

- **Standard fuel system pressure (MPFI petrol engines)**: **2.5 bar to 4.0 bar** (‚âà **250 kPa to 400 kPa**) above manifold pressure.
- **Standard fuel system pressure (GDI petrol engines)**: **35 bar to 350 bar** depending on injection stage.
- **Standard fuel system pressure (Common-Rail Diesel)**: **200 bar to 2000 bar** (modern systems up to **2500 bar**).
- **Standard injection duration (typical idling)**: **2 ms to 4 ms** per injection event.
- **Injector coil resistance (saturated)**: **2 Œ© to 4 Œ©**; (peak-and-hold) **0.5 Œ© to 1 Œ©** primary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working of Major Fuel System Components

#### 2.1.1 Fuel Tank
- Stores the fuel safely; made of **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** for corrosion resistance.
- Contains the **fuel pump module** submerged inside, accessible through a service hatch on the top of the tank.
- Equipped with a **ventilation system** (charcoal canister + EVAP valve) to capture fuel vapors and prevent emission of hydrocarbons (HC) into the atmosphere ‚Äî mandated by **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms in India.
- Contains a **saddle tank design** in some cars to fit over the propeller shaft/rear axle.

#### 2.1.2 Electric Fuel Pump (In-Tank Type)
- A **DC brushless (or brushed) motor-driven** centrifugal or roller-vane pump.
- Operates at **12 V DC** in conventional vehicles; **48 V** in mild-hybrid systems.
- The pump is **controlled by the ECU via a fuel pump relay** that energizes the pump circuit within **1‚Äì2 seconds** of ignition ON (to build up rail pressure) and remains ON as long as the engine is running (sensed by the **CKP signal**).
- **Key operational feature**: If the engine stalls (no CKP pulses), the ECU cuts the relay to prevent fuel flooding, spillage, and fire hazard in case of a crash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Flow Control Strategies (KTU High Yield)**:
> 
> | Strategy | Mechanism | Typical Use |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Two-Speed Pump** | ECU switches between low and high resistance in series with pump | Older return-type systems |
> | **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** | ECU varies duty cycle of pump drive | Most modern return-less systems |
> | **DC Motor Speed Control** | ECU senses rail pressure via sensor and adjusts voltage | Demand-based systems |

#### 2.1.3 Fuel Filter
- Typically a **pleated paper element** housed in a sealed metal/plastic canister.
- Located **in-line between the pump outlet and the fuel rail**, often integrated with the fuel pump module for serviceability.
- Filtration rating: typically **10 ¬µm to 30 ¬µm** for gasoline injectors; **2 ¬µm to 5 ¬µm** for high-pressure common-rail diesel.
- **Working principle**: Fuel enters from the outside of the pleated element, dirt particles are trapped on the outer surface, and clean fuel passes through to the center and out the outlet.

#### 2.1.4 Fuel Pressure Regulator
- A **mechanical diaphragm-based valve** (older systems) or a **vacuum/electronic regulator** (modern return-less systems).
- Maintains a **constant pressure differential** between the fuel rail and the intake manifold.
- For a port-injected engine, manifold vacuum is routed to the regulator's spring chamber. The net force on the diaphragm determines whether fuel is recycled back to the tank (return) or held at pressure.

> **Governing Equation**:
> 
> $$P_{rail} = P_{manifold} + P_{spring}$$
> 
> where $P_{spring}$ is the preload set by the regulator spring (typically **2.5 bar to 3.5 bar** for MPFI).

#### 2.1.5 Fuel Rail
- A **rigid tubular manifold** (steel or aluminum) that distributes fuel uniformly to all injectors.
- Functions as a **small pressurized accumulator** that smooths out pressure pulsations caused by sequential injector openings.
- Equipped with a **Schrader valve** for diagnostic pressure testing and a **fuel pressure sensor** in modern return-less systems.

#### 2.1.6 Electronic Fuel Injector
The injector is the **most critical actuator** in the entire fuel system. Its working is described in detail below.

**Construction**:
- **Solenoid coil** (electromagnetic actuator)
- **Needle / pintle valve** (movable element)
- **Nozzle** (precision-machined orifice)
- **Spring** (closes the valve when coil is de-energized)
- **O-rings** (sealing against fuel rail and intake manifold)

**Working Sequence (Step-by-Step)**:

1. The **ECU** computes the required fuel mass (or volume) based on:
   - **MAF (Mass Air Flow)** or **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure)** sensor reading
   - **Engine speed** (CKP sensor)
   - **Coolant temperature**, **intake air temperature**
   - **Lambda sensor (O‚ÇÇ)** feedback
2. The ECU converts this mass into an **injection pulse width (t_i)** ‚Äî the time the injector remains open.
3. The ECU applies **12 V** to the injector solenoid via a **driver transistor** (low-side or high-side).
4. The magnetic field generated by the coil **overcomes the spring force** and lifts the needle.
5. Fuel sprays through the nozzle in a finely atomized cone pattern into the intake port (PFI) or directly into the cylinder (GDI).
6. When the ECU removes the voltage, the spring snaps the needle closed, ending the injection event.

**Advanced Driving Modes**:
- **Saturated (PWM off) drive**: Constant 12 V for entire pulse. Slow response, high heat. Used in low-impedance injectors via a current-limiting ballast resistor.
- **Peak-and-hold drive**: A high "peak" current (‚âà **4‚Äì6 A**) is applied for ~1 ms to open the injector quickly, then reduced to a "hold" current (‚âà **1‚Äì2 A**) to keep it open. Used in **low-impedance injectors** for high-speed response.

#### 2.1.7 Throttle Body
- Contains a **butterfly valve** controlled either by a **mechanical cable** (older cars) or an **electronic throttle body (ETC / drive-by-wire)**.
- The ECU uses a **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** ‚Äî typically a dual potentiometer ‚Äî to know the exact opening angle.
- In modern **ETC** systems, two **opposing DC motors** (with H-bridge drivers) move the valve based on the accelerator pedal position sensor (which is also redundant for safety, ASIL-C/D rated).

#### 2.1.8 Fuel Supply Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Major Fuel Supply Topologies (Frequently asked in KTU exams)**

| Configuration | Description | Pressure Control | Return Line? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Return-Type** | Fuel always returns to tank via regulator; excess fuel is cooled and recirculated | Mechanical vacuum-compensated regulator at rail end | **Yes** |
| **Return-Less (Mechanical)** | Regulator integrated into fuel pump module at tank; constant flow to rail is regulated by return flow within the module | Mechanical regulator in-tank | No external return |
| **Return-Less (Electronic)** | ECU controls a variable-speed pump (PWM) based on rail pressure sensor input | Electronic, fully closed-loop | No external return |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio target (stoichiometric) | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} \approx 14.7 \colon 1$ (petrol) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Lambda ($\lambda$) definition | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{actual AFR}}{\text{stoichiometric AFR}}$ | $\lambda = 1$ ideal; $<1$ rich; $>1$ lean |
| 3 | Fuel mass per cycle | $m_f = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\text{AFR}}$ | kg / cycle |
| 4 | Injection pulse width (basic) | $t_i = \dfrac{m_f}{Q_{inj}}$ | s or ms |
| 5 | Injector flow rate (typical) | $Q_{inj} \approx 200 \text{ to } 400$ | cc/min at 3 bar |
| 6 | Rail pressure (MPFI) | $P_{rail} - P_{manifold} = 2.5 \text{ to } 4.0$ | bar |
| 7 | Rail pressure (GDI) | $P_{rail} = 35 \text{ to } 350$ | bar |
| 8 | Rail pressure (Common Rail Diesel) | $P_{rail} = 200 \text{ to } 2500$ | bar |
| 9 | Volumetric efficiency correction | $m_{air,actual} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp}$ | kg / cycle |
| 10 | Fuel pump power | $P_{pump} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W |
| 11 | Bernoulli (venturi air flow) | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = \text{const}$ | Pa |
| 12 | Strain gauge MAP sensor output | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \left(0.1 + 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{max}}\right)$ | V |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fuel system components discussed here form the **backbone of engine management** in every road vehicle. Mastery of these systems is essential for:
- **Automotive service engineers** diagnosing no-start, hard-start, hesitation, stalling, and misfire issues using scan tools and oscilloscopes.
- **Calibration engineers** at OEMs (Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Mahindra) tuning ECUs for BS-VI and upcoming CAFE-2 norms.
- **EV / Hybrid transition engineers** ‚Äî residual skills in fuel metering transfer to hydrogen fuel-cell stack control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Injection Pulse Width from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **high-yield KTU 2024 question** and frequently tested. Memorize the logical flow.

**Objective**: Derive an expression for the **fuel injector pulse width** $t_i$ required to deliver a target fuel mass $m_f$ per cycle.

**Given / Known**:
- Intake air mass per cycle: $m_{air}$ (kg)
- Target air-fuel ratio: $\text{AFR}$
- Injector static flow rate: $Q_{inj}$ (kg/s) at reference rail pressure
- Manifold pressure: $P_{man}$
- Coolant temperature compensation factor: $f_{temp}$
- Battery voltage compensation factor: $f_{bat}$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the required fuel mass per cycle** from the air mass and AFR:

$$
m_f = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\text{AFR}}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Convert fuel mass to a volume flow** using fuel density $\rho_f$:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the base pulse width** by dividing volume by injector volumetric flow rate:

$$
t_{i,base} = \dfrac{V_f}{Q_{inj,ref}}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Apply pressure correction**. Injector flow is proportional to the square root of the pressure differential across the injector:

$$
t_{i,cor} = t_{i,base} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{rail} - P_{man}}}
$$

Here $P_{ref}$ is the reference rail pressure at which $Q_{inj,ref}$ was measured.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Apply battery voltage and temperature compensations** (these are empirical correction factors stored in the ECU's calibration maps):

$$
t_i = t_{i,cor} \cdot f_{bat} \cdot f_{temp}
$$

**Final compact expression**:

$$
t_i = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\text{AFR} \cdot \rho_f \cdot Q_{inj,ref}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{rail} - P_{man}}} \cdot f_{bat} \cdot f_{temp}
$$

**Why this matters in practice**:
- A drop in **battery voltage** (e.g., during cranking) reduces injector opening speed ‚Üí ECU must **lengthen** $t_i$ to compensate, otherwise the engine runs lean.
- A drop in **fuel rail pressure** (e.g., clogged filter) reduces mass flow ‚Üí ECU lengthens $t_i$ but if compensated fuel exceeds safe limits, the system triggers a **P0087 (Fuel Rail/System Pressure Too Low)** OBD-II code.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Mass Balance

For complete combustion of iso-octane ($C_8H_{18}$), the balanced chemical equation is:

$$
2\,C_8H_{18} + 25\,O_2 \rightarrow 16\,CO_2 + 18\,H_2O
$$

**Step 1** ‚Äî Molar mass of fuel: $2 \times 114 = 228$ g fuel per 25 mol $O_2$.

**Step 2** ‚Äî Mass of $O_2$ required: $25 \times 32 = 800$ g $O_2$.

**Step 3** ‚Äî Atmospheric air is 23.2% $O_2$ by mass (since oxygen is ~21% volumetric and air molar mass ‚âà 28.97):

$$
\text{mass of air} = \dfrac{800}{0.232} \approx 3448 \text{ g air}
$$

**Step 4** ‚Äî Air-Fuel ratio:

$$
\text{AFR} = \dfrac{3448}{228} \approx 14.7
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AFR}_{stoich} = 14.7 \colon 1}
$$

This is the **target AFR** that the ECU maintains using closed-loop control from the **Lambda (O‚ÇÇ) sensor**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Fuel Pump Power Requirement

**Step 1** ‚Äî Define required pressure rise: $\Delta P = P_{rail} - P_{tank} \approx 4 \text{ bar} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

**Step 2** ‚Äî Define required flow rate for a 2.0 L four-cylinder engine at 6000 RPM:

$$
Q = \dfrac{2.0 \times 6000}{2 \times 60} = 100 \text{ L/s of air} \approx 0.12 \text{ kg/s of air}
$$

**Step 3** ‚Äî Required fuel flow at stoichiometric ratio:

$$
Q_f = \dfrac{0.12}{14.7} \approx 0.00816 \text{ kg/s} \approx 8.16 \text{ g/s}
$$

**Step 4** ‚Äî Volumetric flow of liquid fuel (density $\rho_f \approx 750$ kg/m¬≥):

$$
Q_v = \dfrac{0.00816}{750} \approx 1.09 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5** ‚Äî Hydraulic power delivered to fluid (ideal):

$$
P_{ideal} = \Delta P \cdot Q_v = (4 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.09 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 4.35 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6** ‚Äî Assuming typical pump efficiency $\eta \approx 0.3$:

$$
P_{electrical} = \dfrac{P_{ideal}}{\eta} = \dfrac{4.35}{0.3} \approx 14.5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{electrical,pump} \approx 15 \text{ W (ideal case)}}
$$

In practice, automotive fuel pumps consume **40‚Äì80 W** to provide margin for transient demand (wide-open-throttle, hot restart).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Cause-and-Effect on Engine Symptoms

| Faulty Component | Likely Symptom | OBD-II Code | KTU Mark Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clogged fuel filter | Power loss at high RPM, hesitation | P0087 | High |
| Failed fuel pump relay | Engine cranks but does not start | P0230 / P0231 | High |
| Leaking injector (stuck open) | Rich mixture, black smoke, fouled spark plugs | P0172 | High |
| Stuck-closed injector | Misfire, lean code, rough idle | P0171 / P0300 | Very High |
| Failed pressure regulator (stuck open) | Hard start, fuel smell, low rail pressure | P0087 | Medium |
| Failed pressure regulator (stuck closed) | Excessive pressure, leaking injector seals | P0088 | Medium |
| Faulty Lambda sensor | Failed emissions test, poor fuel economy | P0130 ‚Äì P0135 | Very High |
| Faulty MAF sensor | Hesitation, stalling, poor idle | P0100 ‚Äì P0103 | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Complete Fuel System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ELECTRIC FUEL PUMP MODULE]
    B --> C[FUEL FILTER]
    C --> D[FUEL RAIL]
    D --> E1[INJECTOR 1]
    D --> E2[INJECTOR 2]
    D --> E3[INJECTOR 3]
    D --> E4[INJECTOR 4]
    E1 --> F1[CYLINDER 1 INTAKE PORT]
    E2 --> F2[CYLINDER 2 INTAKE PORT]
    E3 --> F3[CYLINDER 3 INTAKE PORT]
    E4 --> F4[CYLINDER 4 INTAKE PORT]
    D --> G[FUEL PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    G --> H[RETURN LINE TO TANK]
    F1 --> I[ENGINE COMBUSTION]
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    F4 --> I
    J[ECU / ECM] -->|PWM Drive| E1
    J -->|PWM Drive| E2
    J -->|PWM Drive| E3
    J -->|PWM Drive| E4
    J -->|Relay Control| B
    K[MAF / MAP SENSOR] -->|Air Mass| J
    L[CKP SENSOR] -->|Engine Speed| J
    M[LAMBDA O2 SENSOR] -->|Feedback| J
    N[COOLANT TEMP SENSOR] -->|Temp| J
    O[FUEL PRESSURE SENSOR] -->|Rail Pressure| J
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow ‚Äî ECU Control Loop for Fuel Metering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Ignition ON] --> stepB[ECU energizes fuel pump relay]
    stepB --> stepC[Pump primes fuel rail to target pressure]
    stepC --> stepD[Fuel Pressure Sensor confirms pressure OK]
    stepD --> stepE[Crank signal from CKP initiates engine start]
    stepE --> stepF[ECU computes base fuel mass from MAF and speed]
    stepF --> stepG[Apply temperature and voltage compensation]
    stepG --> stepH[Convert mass to injection pulse width]
    stepH --> stepI[Activate injector solenoids sequentially]
    stepI --> stepJ[Lambda sensor reads exhaust oxygen]
    stepJ --> stepK{Is lambda within +/- 2 percent of 1?}
    stepK -->|No| stepL[ECU adjusts pulse width trim short term fuel trim]
    stepL --> stepI
    stepK -->|Yes| stepM[Maintain current pulse width]
    stepM --> stepI
```

### 4.3 Internal Schematic ‚Äî Electronic Fuel Injector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    coilA[SOLENOID COIL TERMINALS]
    coilA --> coreA[FERROMAGNETIC CORE]
    coreA --> armA[ARMATURE / NEEDLE]
    armA --> springA[RETURN SPRING]
    springA --> sealA[SEALING SEAT]
    sealA --> nozzleA[NOZZLE ORIFICE]
    coilA -->|Energized by ECU| coreA
    coreA -->|Magnetic force lifts| armA
    armA -->|Needle moves up| springA
    springA -->|Compresses| armA
    armA -->|Fuel sprays out| nozzleA
    nozzleA -->|Atomized fuel| sprayA[FINE SPRAY CONE]
    coilA -->|De-energized| coreA
    coreA -->|Spring force returns| armA
    armA -->|Needle closes| sealA
    sealA -->|Injection ends| nozzleA
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram ‚Äî Return-Less vs Return-Type Fuel System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RET[RETURN-TYPE SYSTEM]
        T1[Fuel Tank] --> P1[Fuel Pump]
        P1 --> F1[Filter]
        F1 --> R1[Fuel Rail]
        R1 --> I1[Injectors]
        I1 --> Engine1[Engine]
        R1 --> REG1[Pressure Regulator]
        REG1 --> RT1[Return Line]
        RT1 --> T1
    end
    subgraph RE0[RETURN-LESS SYSTEM]
        T2[Fuel Tank] --> P2[Variable Speed Pump]
        P2 --> F2[Filter]
        F2 --> R2[Fuel Rail with Pressure Sensor]
        R2 --> I2[Injectors]
        I2 --> Engine2[Engine]
        R2 -.->|No Return Line| T2
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. List any three major components of an electronic fuel injection system and state the function of each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer**:
1. **Electric Fuel Pump** ‚Äì Delivers pressurized fuel from the tank to the fuel rail; typically submerged inside the tank and driven by a 12 V DC motor controlled by an ECU-driven relay.
2. **Fuel Pressure Regulator** ‚Äì Maintains a constant pressure differential (typically **2.5‚Äì4.0 bar** above manifold pressure) across the injector nozzle by mechanically or electronically regulating return flow.
3. **Electronic Fuel Injector** ‚Äì A solenoid-actuated valve that atomizes and sprays fuel into the intake port (MPFI) or directly into the cylinder (GDI) for a precise duration controlled by the ECU.

---

**Q2. What is the function of a fuel pressure regulator in a return-type MPFI system? Why is the vacuum hose from the intake manifold connected to it?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer**:
The fuel pressure regulator maintains a **constant pressure differential** between the fuel rail and the intake manifold so that the injector flow rate depends only on the **pulse width** (and not on fluctuating manifold vacuum). The vacuum hose from the manifold applies manifold vacuum to one side of the regulator diaphragm, which **cancels out** the manifold's pressure variations. As a result:

$$
P_{rail} - P_{manifold} = P_{spring} = \text{constant}
$$

This ensures consistent injector metering during idle (high vacuum) and wide-open throttle (low vacuum).

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]**

---

#### **Question A** ‚Äî *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Explain the working of an **electronic fuel injector** in a multi-point fuel injection (MPFI) system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the difference between **saturated** and **peak-and-hold** driving modes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a schematic, explain the working of an **electronic throttle body (drive-by-wire)**. How does the ECU maintain fail-safe operation? *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution ‚Äî Electronic Fuel Injector Working**

The electronic fuel injector is a **solenoid-actuated precision valve** that delivers atomized fuel into the intake port. Its working in an MPFI system is as follows:

1. **Fuel Supply**: Pressurized fuel (‚âà 3 bar) from the fuel rail is available at the injector's inlet.
2. **ECU Command**: The ECU computes the required fuel mass from the MAF, MAP, CKP, and coolant temperature sensors. It then translates this into an **injection pulse width** $t_i$ (typically 2‚Äì4 ms at idle).
3. **Solenoid Energization**: The ECU switches a driver transistor ON, applying battery voltage to the injector coil. A magnetic field builds up.
4. **Needle Lift**: When the magnetic force exceeds the spring preload, the armature/needle lifts, uncovering the nozzle orifice.
5. **Atomization**: Pressurized fuel sprays through the precision-machined nozzle as a finely atomized cone, mixing with the incoming air in the intake port.
6. **Cut-off**: When the ECU removes voltage, the spring force returns the needle to its seat, ending injection.

**Comparison of Driving Modes**:

| Parameter | Saturated Drive | Peak-and-Hold Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Coil resistance | High (12‚Äì16 Œ©) | Low (2‚Äì4 Œ©) |
| Drive waveform | Constant 12 V | Peak current then reduced hold |
| Opening speed | Slower (1‚Äì2 ms) | Faster (0.4‚Äì0.8 ms) |
| Heat dissipation | Lower | Higher, needs current limiting |
| Use case | Low-speed, low-pressure | High-speed sequential, GDI |

> **Key Conclusion**: Peak-and-hold is preferred in modern engines because it offers **faster response** (multiple injections per cycle possible) and **better linearity**.

---

**(b) Model Solution ‚Äî Electronic Throttle Body (Drive-by-Wire)**

The **electronic throttle body (ETB)** replaces the mechanical accelerator cable with an **electronic control loop**:

**Working**:
1. The driver presses the accelerator pedal. The **Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS)** ‚Äî typically a **dual redundant potentiometer or Hall-effect sensor** ‚Äî sends two independent voltage signals to the ECU.
2. The ECU processes the pedal input, applies **driver-intent algorithms** (including traction control, cruise control, idle speed control overrides), and computes a **target throttle angle**.
3. The ECU commands an H-bridge driver to actuate a **DC servo motor** (often with two motors in opposition for redundancy) that rotates the **throttle plate** via a gear train.
4. The actual throttle position is fed back to the ECU through **two Throttle Position Sensors (TPS1, TPS2)** mounted on the throttle shaft. TPS1 reads the angle in one direction; TPS2 is offset so that a short circuit or open circuit is detectable.
5. Closed-loop PID control ensures the actual angle tracks the target within ¬±0.5¬∞.

**Fail-Safe Operation**:
- **Default-Spring-Open or Spring-Closed** design: A return spring defaults the throttle to a specific limp-home position (typically slightly open at ~7¬∞) when power is lost.
- **Redundant sensors**: APPS1/APPS2 and TPS1/TPS2 disagreements trigger a **limp-home mode** with reduced power and a check-engine light.
- **Watchdog timer**: The ECU continuously runs a self-diagnostic; if a fault is detected, throttle is forced to default position.
- **Functional Safety**: Designed per **ISO 26262** with **ASIL-C or ASIL-D** rating.

---

#### **Question B** ‚Äî *(14 Marks)* *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the working of a **return-less electronic fuel supply system** with a block diagram. Compare it with the return-type system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **fuel injection pulse width** $t_i$ given the intake air mass, target AFR, and injector flow rate, including pressure, temperature, and battery voltage corrections. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution ‚Äî Return-Less Electronic Fuel Supply System**

In a **return-less system**, there is no external return line from the engine bay back to the tank. Instead:

1. A **variable-speed electric fuel pump** (PWM-controlled by ECU) is mounted inside the fuel tank as part of the **fuel pump module**.
2. The fuel pump module also houses a **mechanical bypass regulator** that returns excess fuel internally to the pump's inlet side, maintaining adequate pump inlet pressure.
3. A **fuel pressure sensor** is mounted on the fuel rail to provide closed-loop pressure feedback to the ECU.
4. The ECU continuously adjusts the **PWM duty cycle** of the pump (typically 20‚Äì80 kHz) to maintain the **target rail pressure** of around 3.5‚Äì4.0 bar.
5. Excess fuel at the rail is **not** returned to the tank; it is consumed in the injectors, eliminating hot fuel recirculation and improving hot-start performance.

**Comparison Table**:

| Aspect | Return-Type | Return-Less (Electronic) |
|---|---|---|
| Return line | Yes, to tank | No |
| Pressure regulation | Mechanical at rail | Electronic at pump |
| Hot fuel handling | Fuel is cooled in tank | Excess fuel stays in rail and heats up |
| Hot-start performance | Can be problematic | Excellent |
| Fuel economy | Slightly lower | Slightly higher |
| Emissions control | Harder to purge vapors | Easier with EVAP system |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (sensor + variable pump) |
| Examples | Maruti Alto older, Tata Nano | Honda City, Hyundai Creta BS-VI |

---

**(b) Model Solution ‚Äî Derivation of Injection Pulse Width**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Final compact expression** is:

$$
t_i = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\text{AFR} \cdot \rho_f \cdot Q_{inj,ref}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{rail} - P_{man}}} \cdot f_{bat} \cdot f_{temp}
$$

Where:
- $m_{air}$ = intake air mass per cycle (from MAF)
- $\text{AFR}$ = target air-fuel ratio
- $\rho_f$ = fuel density
- $Q_{inj,ref}$ = injector reference flow rate
- $P_{ref}$ = reference rail pressure
- $P_{rail}$ = current rail pressure
- $P_{man}$ = manifold pressure
- $f_{bat}$ = battery voltage correction factor (from calibration map)
- $f_{temp}$ = coolant temperature correction factor (from calibration map)

> **Examiner Note**: Most students forget the **square root of pressure ratio** term. This is a **3-mark penalty** in KTU valuation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing AFR with Lambda**: $\text{AFR} = 14.7$ is stoichiometric; $\lambda = \text{AFR}/14.7$. Students frequently write $\lambda = 14.7$ ‚Äî this is **wrong** and costs 1‚Äì2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Pressure Correction**: The injector flow depends on $\sqrt{\Delta P}$. A student writing $t_i = m_f / Q_{inj}$ without pressure correction **loses 2‚Äì3 marks** in any derivation question.
> 3. **Mixing MPFI and GDI pressures**: Don't write "fuel pressure is 200 bar" for an MPFI engine or "3 bar" for a common-rail diesel. Examiner will mark you down for factual errors.
> 4. **Missing fail-safe discussion in drive-by-wire questions**: Always include the spring-default position and dual-sensor redundancy. This is a **mandatory** discussion point.
> 5. **Confusing MAP and MAF sensor roles**: MAP is a **pressure** sensor; MAF is a **mass flow** sensor. Mixing these up is a 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ‚òê **Fuel system** = storage + delivery + metering + injection + electronic control.
- ‚òê **Stoichiometric AFR** for petrol = **14.7 : 1**; for diesel ‚âà **14.5 : 1**; for CNG ‚âà **17.2 : 1**; for LPG ‚âà **15.5 : 1**.
- ‚òê **MPFI rail pressure** = 2.5‚Äì4.0 bar; **GDI** = 35‚Äì350 bar; **Common Rail Diesel** = 200‚Äì2500 bar.
- ‚òê **Electric fuel pump** is in the tank, controlled by **fuel pump relay**, cut off if no CKP signal.
- ‚òê **Fuel filter** is typically **10‚Äì30 ¬µm** for petrol, **2‚Äì5 ¬µm** for common-rail diesel.
- ‚òê **Pressure regulator** maintains $P_{rail} - P_{man} = \text{constant}$ (‚âà 3 bar for MPFI).
- ‚òê **Injector** is a **solenoid-operated** valve; controlled by **pulse width modulation** (PWM) of the coil current.
- ‚òê **Peak-and-hold** drive = high peak current to open, then reduced hold current; used in modern high-speed injectors.
- ‚òê **Return-type** system has a return line; **return-less** system has PWM-controlled pump and rail-mounted pressure sensor.
- ‚òê **Drive-by-wire throttle** uses **redundant APPS** (pedal sensors) and **redundant TPS** (throttle sensors) with **spring-default** fail-safe.
- ‚òê **Lambda sensor (O‚ÇÇ sensor)** enables **closed-loop** control, maintaining $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.02$.
- ‚òê **Inj pulse width derivation** must include **pressure correction** ($\sqrt{P_{ref}/P_{rail} - P_{man}}$) and **empirical corrections** ($f_{bat}$, $f_{temp}$).
- ‚òê **Injector resistance**: Saturated = 2‚Äì4 Œ© (with ballast) or 12‚Äì16 Œ© (without); Peak-and-hold = 0.5‚Äì4 Œ©.
- ‚òê **Fuel pump power** = $\Delta P \times Q$ divided by efficiency (‚âà 30%).
- ‚òê **OBD-II codes** to remember: P0171 (system lean), P0172 (system rich), P0087 (rail pressure low), P0300 (random misfire), P0130‚ÄìP0135 (O‚ÇÇ sensor faults), P0230‚ÄìP0232 (fuel pump circuit).
- ‚òê **BS-VI compliance** mandates **EVAP canister**, **lambda control**, and **tight OBD-II diagnostics** on every fuel system component.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Electronic Ignition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Electronic Ignition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Ignition System (EIS)** is an ignition system in which the primary current in the ignition coil is switched (interrupted) by an electronic switching device ‚Äî typically a **power transistor (Darlington pair)** or a **thyristor (SCR/TRIAC)** ‚Äî rather than by the mechanical contact breaker (CB) points used in the conventional (Kettering) ignition system. The triggering pulse for the transistor is generated by a magnetic pickup, Hall-effect sensor, or optical pickup mounted at the distributor.

In KTU 2024 terminology, an Electronic Ignition System replaces the **mechanical contact-breaker** with an **electronic control unit (ECU)** or a **solid-state switching module**. The system falls under three broad sub-categories:

1. **Transistor-Assisted Contact (TAC) Ignition** ‚Äî CB points remain but only carry a tiny **control current (~1 mA)**, while a heavy primary current (~5 A) is handled by a transistor.
2. **Transistor-Coil Ignition (TCI)** ‚Äî No CB points at all; the trigger comes from a **magnetic pulse generator / reluctor** mounted in the distributor.
3. **Capacitive Discharge Ignition (CDI)** ‚Äî Energy is stored in a capacitor (~1‚Äì2 ¬µF at 400 V) and dumped into the coil primary via a **thyristor (SCR)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Light Switch Story**
>
> Imagine a classroom where the lights are controlled by a **40-year-old mechanical toggle switch** that physically rubs two metal contacts together. Every time you flick the switch, **tiny sparks** erode the contacts, the switch gets hot, and after a year it fails. Now replace it with a **solid-state electronic touch switch** ‚Äî a tiny micro-chip detects your finger and a power transistor silently carries the entire 5 A current with no moving parts, no sparks, no wear.
>
> That is *exactly* what an electronic ignition system does to your car: the **"light switch" is the contact-breaker points**, the **"touch switch" is the transistor**, and the **"bulb" is the spark plug**. No moving parts in the high-current path means **higher reliability, higher spark energy, and a longer-lived ignition system**.

### 1.3 Why Replace the Conventional Ignition?

The conventional Kettering system suffers from four fundamental limitations:

| Limitation | Mechanism of Failure |
|---|---|
| **Contact erosion** | Inductive kick-back of the coil (back-EMF ~300 V) welds, pits, and burns the CB points |
| **Limited primary current** | CB current must be kept low (~3‚Äì4 A) to prevent point welding, limiting spark energy |
| **Dwell angle variation** | Mechanical cam wear changes dwell time ‚Üí inconsistent spark energy at different RPM |
| **Speed limitation** | Points bounce at high RPM ‚Üí misfires above ~5,000 rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU Board Examiner Expectation):**
> A conventional ignition system typically delivers a peak secondary voltage of **15,000 ‚Äì 25,000 V** with a spark duration of **0.1 ‚Äì 0.3 ms**, whereas a well-designed electronic ignition system delivers **30,000 ‚Äì 40,000 V** with a spark duration of **0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 ms**.

### 1.4 Energy Equation ‚Äî The Foundation of "Why Electronic?"

The energy stored in the coil primary magnetic field is governed by:

$$E_{primary} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$$

where $L_p$ is the primary inductance and $I_p$ is the steady-state primary current. In a conventional system, $I_p$ is deliberately limited (to ~3‚Äì4 A) by a **ballast resistor** to prevent point welding, capping the available energy. The electronic switch has no such limitation ‚Äî it can comfortably switch **5 ‚Äì 7 A** continuously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Transistor-Coil Ignition (TCI)

The TCI architecture is the most commonly asked variant in KTU exams. We break it into 6 logical steps:

1. **Reluctor Tooth Approaches Pickup** ‚Äî A toothed wheel (reluctor) on the distributor shaft rotates with the engine. Each tooth represents an ignition event for one cylinder.
2. **Magnetic Flux Builds** ‚Äî A permanent magnet behind a pickup coil creates a magnetic circuit. As the tooth approaches, the air-gap shrinks, and the flux through the pickup coil **rises sharply**.
3. **EMF Induced in Pickup** ‚Äî According to Faraday's Law, the changing flux induces a small alternating EMF (typically **0.5 ‚Äì 3 V**) in the pickup coil.
4. **Signal Conditioning** ‚Äî The induced AC voltage is shaped by the **electronic control module (ECM)** into a clean square wave (0 V / 5 V logic).
5. **Transistor Switching** ‚Äî On the *rising* (or falling) edge of the pulse, the ECM drives the **base** of a Darlington power transistor, switching OFF the primary current of the ignition coil. The rapid $di/dt$ induces a **high-voltage spike** in the secondary winding.
6. **Spark at Plug** ‚Äî The high-voltage secondary pulse (30,000 ‚Äì 40,000 V) jumps the plug gap, igniting the air-fuel mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Darlington Pair?** The Darlington configuration provides a current gain $\beta_{total} = \beta_1 \times \beta_2$ of typically **1,000 ‚Äì 10,000**, allowing the tiny pickup signal (~5 mA) to switch a large primary current (~5 A). This is the heart of *electronic* ignition ‚Äî clean signal-to-power amplification with no moving parts.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet ‚Äî Memorise this table for the ESE.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary energy storage | $E_p = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | 60 ‚Äì 120 | mJ | Energy stored in coil magnetic field |
| 2 | Secondary voltage | $V_s = V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}$ | 30,000 ‚Äì 40,000 | V | Voltage step-up ratio of coil |
| 3 | Turns ratio (typical) | $N_s / N_p$ | 60 ‚Äì 100 | ‚Äî | Step-up transformer ratio |
| 4 | Dwell time | $t_d = \frac{\theta_d}{6N}$ (4-cyl) | 2 ‚Äì 8 | ms | Time primary current is ON |
| 5 | Spark duration | $t_{spark}$ | 0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 | ms | Time spark plug is conducting |
| 6 | Spark energy delivered | $E_{spark} = \eta \times E_p$ | 25 ‚Äì 80 | mJ | Energy actually in the arc |
| 7 | Coil primary inductance | $L_p$ | 3 ‚Äì 10 | mH | Energy storage inductance |
| 8 | Coil primary resistance | $R_p$ | 0.4 ‚Äì 2.0 | Œ© | Series resistance of primary |
| 9 | Time constant of primary | $\tau = L_p / R_p$ | 3 ‚Äì 12 | ms | Current build-up time constant |
| 10 | Required breakdown voltage | $V_{bd} = k \cdot p \cdot d$ | 5,000 ‚Äì 15,000 | V/cylinder | Paschen's Law for plug gap |
| 11 | CDI capacitor energy | $E_c = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | 60 ‚Äì 100 | mJ | Stored energy in CDI cap |
| 12 | Capacitor discharge current peak | $I_{peak} = V_c \sqrt{C/L_p}$ | 100 ‚Äì 300 | A | Peak current in CDI pulse |

> **Notation note:** $\eta$ (transfer efficiency) is typically **0.6 ‚Äì 0.85** for inductive (TCI) systems and **0.85 ‚Äì 0.95** for capacitive (CDI) systems.

### 2.3 Comparative Real-World Engineering Utility

In the automotive industry, the move from mechanical to electronic ignition was a **prerequisite** for the subsequent introduction of:
- **Electronic Spark Timing (EST)** ‚Äî precise crank-angle-based spark control.
- **Engine Management Systems (EMS)** ‚Äî closed-loop air-fuel ratio control.
- **OBD-II Diagnostics** ‚Äî on-board detection of misfires via ionization feedback.
- **Stratified-Charge & Lean-Burn Engines** ‚Äî which require **longer, more energetic sparks** to ignite ultra-lean mixtures.

Without electronic ignition, modern **3-way catalytic converters**, **Euro 6 / BS-VI emission norms**, and **45% thermal-efficiency engines** would simply not be feasible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Primary Current Build-up in an Inductive Coil

When the electronic switch (transistor) closes, the primary winding of the ignition coil behaves as a series **RL circuit** driven by the battery voltage $V_b$ through a ballast/primary resistance $R_p$.

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$V_b = i_p R_p + L_p \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

This is a first-order linear differential equation. Rearranging:

$$L_p \frac{di_p}{dt} + i_p R_p = V_b$$

$$\frac{di_p}{dt} + \frac{R_p}{L_p} i_p = \frac{V_b}{L_p}$$

**Solution via integrating factor** $e^{(R_p/L_p)t}$:

$$i_p(t) = \frac{V_b}{R_p} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{L_p}{R_p}$$

**Steady-state (saturation) current:**

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} i_p(t) = I_{sat} = \frac{V_b}{R_p}$$

**Energy stored at moment of spark:**

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love to ask ‚Äî *"Why does the electronic system allow higher spark energy than a contact-breaker system?"* The mathematical answer is: the ballast resistor $R_p$ must remain in the circuit to **limit the steady-state current** in both cases. However, in the conventional system, the *contacts themselves* add an extra ~0.3 Œ© contact resistance when new (rising to >1 Œ© when worn), **effectively reducing $I_{sat}$** and hence $E_p$. The electronic switch has an *on-state* resistance of only **~0.05 Œ©**, so $I_{sat}$ stays high throughout the coil's life.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Secondary Voltage Induced at Spark

At the moment the transistor switches OFF, the primary current $I_p$ collapses from $I_{sat}$ toward zero in a time $\Delta t$ of roughly **20 ‚Äì 200 ¬µs** (limited by the coil's self-capacitance and the flyback clamping circuit).

By Faraday's Law, the self-induced EMF in the primary is:

$$V_{p,kick} = -L_p \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

For a typical collapse from $I_p = 5$ A to $0$ in $\Delta t = 100\,\mu s$ with $L_p = 5$ mH:

$$V_{p,kick} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 250\,\text{V}$$

The secondary voltage is amplified by the turns ratio $N_s / N_p$:

$$V_s = V_{p,kick} \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

Assuming a turns ratio of 80:

$$V_s = 250 \times 80 = 20{,}000\,\text{V}$$

However, due to the *mutual coupling coefficient* $k$ (typically 0.85 ‚Äì 0.95) and the fact that some flux is "lost" to leakage:

$$V_s = k \cdot V_{p,kick} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

$$\boxed{V_s \approx (0.9)(250)(80) \approx 18{,}000\,\text{V base; with no contact degradation in an electronic system, this climbs to 30{,}000 - 40{,}000 V at the plug.}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Electronic vs. Conventional

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the "high-yield" matrix that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce.**

| # | Performance Parameter | Conventional Ignition | Electronic Ignition (TCI) | Advantage Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak secondary voltage | 15,000 ‚Äì 25,000 V | 30,000 ‚Äì 40,000 V | **~1.6 ‚Äì 2.0√ó** |
| 2 | Spark duration | 0.1 ‚Äì 0.3 ms | 0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 ms | **~3 ‚Äì 5√ó** |
| 3 | Primary current at saturation | 3.0 ‚Äì 4.0 A | 5.0 ‚Äì 7.0 A | **~1.6√ó** |
| 4 | Spark energy at plug | 15 ‚Äì 30 mJ | 40 ‚Äì 100 mJ | **~2.5 ‚Äì 3√ó** |
| 5 | Dwell-angle variation with RPM | High (¬±15¬∞) | Very low (<2¬∞) | **~7 ‚Äì 8√ó** |
| 6 | Points/contacts service interval | 10,000 km | None (no contacts) | **‚àû** |
| 7 | Maximum reliable engine RPM | ~5,000 | ~7,000 ‚Äì 10,000 | **~1.5 ‚Äì 2√ó** |
| 8 | Cold-start spark energy at ‚Äì10 ¬∞C | Reduced ~30% | Maintained | **Better** |
| 9 | Contact-point voltage drop | 0.2 ‚Äì 1.5 V | <0.05 V | **~30√ó** |
| 10 | Sensitivity to contact fouling/oil | Severe | Immune | **‚Äî** |
| 11 | Compatibility with electronic timing | Difficult (mechanical advance only) | Native | **‚Äî** |
| 12 | EMI generation | Moderate | Low (with zener clamp) | **Better** |

### 3.4 Mathematical Justification for "Longer Spark Duration"

In the conventional system, the **secondary LC oscillation** rings down very quickly because the plug-gap resistance is unstable and the parallel-arc resistance of the spark is high. The arc extinguishes when the secondary current falls below the **minimum arc-sustaining current** $I_{arc,min}$ (typically ~30 mA).

In an electronic system, the higher stored primary energy keeps secondary current above $I_{arc,min}$ for a longer time:

$$t_{spark} \propto \frac{E_{spark}}{V_s \cdot I_{arc,min}}$$

Plugging in the numbers:

$$t_{spark}^{conv} \approx \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{(20{,}000)(0.030)} \approx 42\,\mu s \approx 0.04\,\text{ms (minimum theoretical)}$$

In practice, the conventional system delivers ~0.1 ‚Äì 0.3 ms because coil energy exceeds the strict minimum; the electronic system delivers **0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 ms** because the available energy is 2.5 ‚Äì 3√ó higher.

> **Real-engine impact:** A 1.0 ms spark gives the air-fuel mixture **~25 times longer** to develop a propagating flame kernel compared to a 0.04 ms spark, drastically improving:
> - **Cold-start combustion stability** (lean mixture, low temperature)
> - **Lean-burn emission reduction** (lambda = 1.3 ‚Äì 1.5)
> - **Idle smoothness** at low RPM

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transistor-Coil Ignition (TCI) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT["12V BATTERY"] --> RES["BALLAST RESISTOR"]
    RES --> COILP["IGNITION COIL PRIMARY Lp"]
    COILP --> SW["DARLINGTON POWER TRANSISTOR"]
    COILSEC["IGNITION COIL SECONDARY Ns"] -.-> PLUG["SPARK PLUG"]
    COILP --- COILSEC
    COILSEC --> PLUG
    SW --> GND1["GROUND"]
    RELUCTOR["RELUCTOR / TRIGGER WHEEL"] --> PICKUP["MAGNETIC PICKUP COIL"]
    PICKUP --> SHAPER["SIGNAL SHAPER / ZENER CLAMP"]
    SHAPER --> ECM["IGNITION MODULE ECM"]
    ECM -->|Base drive| SW
    IGN_SW["IGNITION SWITCH"] --> RES
    BAT --> IGN_SW
    style BAT fill:#fde2e1
    style ECM fill:#cfe8fc
    style PICKUP fill:#fff3b0
    style PLUG fill:#d4edda
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow ‚Äî From Crank Rotation to Spark Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CRANKSHAFT ROTATES"] --> B["DISTRIBUTOR SHAFT TURNS"]
    B --> C["RELUCTOR TOOTH PASSES PICKUP"]
    C --> D["FLUX CHANGE INDUCES EMF IN PICKUP COIL"]
    D --> E["SIGNAL SHAPED INTO SQUARE WAVE"]
    E --> F["ECM SENDS BASE DRIVE TO TRANSISTOR"]
    F --> G["TRANSISTOR TURNS ON, PRIMARY CURRENT RISES"]
    G --> H["ENERGY STORED IN COIL MAGNETIC FIELD"]
    H --> I["TOOTH LEAVES PICKUP, ECM REMOVES BASE DRIVE"]
    I --> J["TRANSISTOR SWITCHES OFF ABRUPTLY"]
    J --> K["PRIMARY CURRENT COLLAPSES"]
    K --> L["HIGH-VOLTAGE SPIKE INDUCED IN SECONDARY"]
    L --> M["DISTRIBUTOR ROUTES VOLTAGE TO CORRECT CYLINDER"]
    M --> N["SPARK PLUG BREAKS DOWN, ARC FORMS"]
    N --> O["AIR-FUEL MIXTURE IGNITES"]
    style A fill:#e7f5ff
    style O fill:#d3f9d8
```

### 4.3 Architecture ‚Äî Comparison of Conventional vs. Electronic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONV["CONVENTIONAL KETTERING IGNITION"]
        C1["12V Battery"] --> C2["Ignition Switch"]
        C2 --> C3["Ballast Resistor"]
        C3 --> C4["Contact Breaker Points"]
        C4 --> C5["Coil Primary"]
        C4 -.Mechanical Cam.-> C6["Mechanical Advance Weights"]
        C5 --> C7["Coil Secondary"]
        C7 --> C8["Distributor Rotor"]
        C8 --> C9["Spark Plugs"]
        C5 -->|Mechanical switching| C4
    end
    subgraph ELEC["ELECTRONIC TCI IGNITION"]
        E1["12V Battery"] --> E2["Ignition Switch"]
        E2 --> E3["Ballast Resistor"]
        E3 --> E4["Coil Primary"]
        E5["Reluctor + Magnetic Pickup"] --> E6["Signal Conditioner"]
        E6 --> E7["Darlington Transistor"]
        E7 --> E4
        E4 --> E8["Coil Secondary"]
        E8 --> E9["Distributor Rotor"]
        E9 --> E10["Spark Plugs"]
        E7 --> E11["ECU Spark Advance Map"]
    end
    style CONV fill:#fff5f5
    style ELEC fill:#f0fff4
```

### 4.4 Signal Waveform ‚Äî Pickup vs. Coil Primary Current

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WAV["TIMING DIAGRAM"]
        W1["Trigger Tooth Profile: ‚ñÅ‚ñÇ‚ñÉ‚ñÖ‚ñÜ‚ñá‚ñà‚ñá‚ñÜ‚ñÖ‚ñÉ‚ñÇ‚ñÅ"] --> W2["Pickup EMF: Sine Pulse Positive then Negative"]
        W2 --> W3["Shaped Square Wave: 0V then 5V"]
        W3 --> W4["Transistor ON Primary Current RISING Exp Curve toward Isat"]
        W4 --> W5["Transistor OFF Current COLLAPSES Sharp Fall"]
        W5 --> W6["Secondary Voltage SPIKE 30000 to 40000 V"]
    end
    style W6 fill:#ffe3a3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the waveform:** Note how in the **electronic system**, the rising portion of the primary current is governed by the time-constant $\tau = L_p/R_p$ and the system reaches ~95% of $I_{sat}$ after $3\tau$. The ECM dynamically adjusts the *dwell* (ON time) so that the primary current is always high enough to deliver a full spark, regardless of engine RPM ‚Äî this is impossible with a fixed mechanical cam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**
*List any **four** advantages of an electronic ignition system over a conventional contact-breaker ignition system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî 1 mark per correct advantage with brief justification):**

1. **Higher secondary voltage (30,000 ‚Äì 40,000 V vs 15,000 ‚Äì 25,000 V):** The transistor switch has negligible voltage drop, allowing full primary current and hence higher secondary EMF.
2. **Longer spark duration (0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 ms):** Higher stored energy keeps the secondary current above the arc-sustaining threshold for a longer period, improving combustion stability.
3. **Virtually no maintenance:** The contact-breaker points are eliminated, so there is no contact wear, pitting, or need for periodic adjustment.
4. **Better high-speed performance:** There is no contact bounce or mechanical limitation, allowing reliable firing up to 7,000 ‚Äì 10,000 RPM.
5. *(Any other valid point: better cold-start, lower emissions, compatibility with ECU, etc.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
*State **two** reasons why a transistor (Darlington pair) is preferred over a single BJT in an electronic ignition module.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Very high current gain:** A Darlington pair gives $\beta_{total} = \beta_1 \times \beta_2 \approx 1{,}000 - 10{,}000$, allowing the tiny pickup signal (~5 mA) to switch the large primary current (~5 A) without an additional driver stage.
2. **High input impedance:** The Darlington's high $\beta$ means the input resistance is in the **MŒ© range**, drawing negligible current from the pickup coil, which protects the signal source.
3. **Better thermal stability & ruggedness:** Darlington modules are typically co-packaged with a zener clamp for flyback protection and a heatsink, giving a robust ignition switch.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Read Before You Write**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "spark duration" with "dwell time."** Dwell = time primary current is ON; spark duration = time the arc is sustained. Many students write them as the same and lose 1‚Äì2 marks.
> 2. **Always state units** (V, ms, mJ, RPM) when you quote a number. A bare number like "30,000" without a unit = **0 marks** for that statement.
> 3. **Draw a labelled block diagram** wherever the question says "explain with a diagram." A pure-text answer loses 2‚Äì3 marks even if your text is brilliant.
> 4. **Compare with a table.** KTU examiners award easy marks for a side-by-side comparison. Use the matrix in ¬ß3.3 as a template.
> 5. **Mention the actual numbers** (volts, milliseconds, millijoules). Generic statements like "higher voltage" fetch only 0.5 marks; quantified statements fetch 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Deep dive on TCI advantages*

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Transistor-Coil Ignition (TCI) system**. List **six advantages** of electronic ignition systems over conventional contact-breaker systems. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a coil primary inductance of $5\,\text{mH}$ and a primary resistance of $0.6\,\Omega$. The battery voltage is $12\,\text{V}$ and the transistor is ON for $4\,\text{ms}$ per cycle. Calculate:
> - (i) the steady-state primary current,
> - (ii) the primary current at the moment the transistor switches OFF,
> - (iii) the energy stored in the coil primary at the moment of spark,
> - (iv) the peak secondary voltage if the turns ratio is 80 and the coupling coefficient is 0.9. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram ‚Äî Working of TCI (7 marks):**

Draw the block diagram from ¬ß4.1. Mark the following on it:
- **Magnetic pickup** at the distributor.
- **Signal shaper / zener clamp** for clean pulse.
- **Darlington power transistor** as the switching device.
- **Ignition coil** with $N_p$ and $N_s$ windings.
- **Distributor rotor & cap** for spark routing.
- **Spark plugs** at the four cylinders.

**Working (narrative ‚Äî 4 marks):**
As the reluctor tooth approaches the magnetic pickup, a small EMF is induced. The ECM shapes this into a square wave and applies it to the Darlington base. The transistor switches ON, allowing the primary current to build up (limited by $L_p/R_p$ time constant) to a value close to $I_{sat} = V_b/R_p$. When the tooth leaves, the ECM removes the base drive, the transistor switches OFF abruptly, the collapsing primary flux induces a high-voltage spike in the secondary, and the rotor directs this to the correct cylinder.

**Six advantages (3 marks ‚Äî 0.5 each):**
1. Higher secondary voltage (30‚Äì40 kV).
2. Longer spark duration (up to 1.5 ms).
3. No contact-breaker maintenance.
4. Higher and more stable primary current.
5. Better high-RPM performance.
6. Better cold-start and lower emissions.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$L_p = 5\,\text{mH} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{H}$, $R_p = 0.6\,\Omega$, $V_b = 12\,\text{V}$, $t_{dwell} = 4\,\text{ms}$, $N_s/N_p = 80$, $k = 0.9$.

**(i) Steady-state primary current:**

$$I_{sat} = \frac{V_b}{R_p} = \frac{12}{0.6} = 20\,\text{A}$$

**(ii) Primary current at switch-OFF (after 4 ms of dwell):**

Time constant: $\tau = L_p/R_p = (5 \times 10^{-3})/0.6 = 8.33\,\text{ms}$

$$i_p(t) = I_{sat}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 20\left(1 - e^{-4/8.33}\right)$$

$$e^{-4/8.33} = e^{-0.48} = 0.6188$$

$$i_p(4\,\text{ms}) = 20\,(1 - 0.6188) = 20 \times 0.3812 = 7.62\,\text{A}$$

**(iii) Energy stored at moment of spark:**

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} (5 \times 10^{-3})(7.62)^2$$

$$E_p = 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 58.07 = 0.145\,\text{J} = 145\,\text{mJ}$$

**(iv) Peak secondary voltage:**

First, primary flyback voltage: $V_{p,kick} = L_p \times (I_p/\Delta t)$

Assuming $\Delta t = 100\,\mu s$:

$$V_{p,kick} = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{7.62}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 381\,\text{V}$$

Then, secondary voltage with coupling:

$$V_s = k \times V_{p,kick} \times \frac{N_s}{N_p} = 0.9 \times 381 \times 80 = 27{,}432\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_s \approx 27.4\,\text{kV}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Alternative choice on CDI*

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the working of a **Capacitive Discharge Ignition (CDI) system** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the **Transistor-Coil Ignition (TCI)** system in any **five** technical aspects. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a CDI system, the storage capacitor is $1.5\,\mu F$ and is charged to $400\,\text{V}$. The coil primary inductance is $1.0\,\text{mH}$. Calculate:
> - (i) the energy stored in the capacitor,
> - (ii) the peak discharge current through the coil primary,
> - (iii) the peak secondary voltage if the turns ratio is 100 and the coupling is perfect (k = 1),
> - (iv) comment on the **spark duration** of CDI vs TCI and explain why CDI is preferred for **two-wheeler and racing applications** while TCI is preferred for **passenger cars**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CDI block diagram & working (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["12V / Magneto AC"] --> B2["Oscillator / DC-DC Converter"]
    B2 --> B3["Capacitor 1.5uF charged to 400V"]
    B3 --> B4["SCR / Thyristor"]
    B5["Trigger Pulse Generator"] --> B4
    B4 --> B6["Ignition Coil Primary Lp"]
    B6 --> B7["Coil Secondary Ns"]
    B7 --> B8["Spark Plug"]
    style B3 fill:#fff3b0
    style B4 fill:#fde2e1
    style B8 fill:#d4edda
```

**Working:** A DC-DC converter (or magneto in 2-wheelers) charges a **storage capacitor** to ~400 V. When the trigger pulse arrives, the **thyristor (SCR) is fired**, dumping the capacitor's charge into the coil primary in a very fast (~10 ¬µs) pulse. The resulting $di/dt$ in the primary induces a high-voltage secondary spike.

**CDI vs TCI ‚Äî five technical aspects (3 marks ‚Äî 0.5 to 1 mark per row):**

| Parameter | TCI | CDI |
|---|---|---|
| Energy storage | Coil primary (inductor) | Capacitor |
| Switching device | Darlington transistor | SCR / Thyristor |
| Spark rise time | 50 ‚Äì 200 ¬µs | 5 ‚Äì 20 ¬µs (faster) |
| Spark duration | 0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 ms (longer) | 0.05 ‚Äì 0.3 ms (shorter) |
| Cold-start & idle | Excellent | Poor (short spark) |
| High-RPM & racing | Good | Excellent |
| Cost & complexity | Moderate | Low for 2-wheelers |

**(b) Numerical & comment (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C = 1.5\,\mu F = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F}$, $V_c = 400\,\text{V}$, $L_p = 1.0\,\text{mH} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{H}$, $N_s/N_p = 100$, $k = 1$.

**(i) Energy stored in capacitor:**

$$E_c = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \frac{1}{2} (1.5 \times 10^{-6})(400)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1.5 \times 10^{-6})(160{,}000)$$

$$E_c = 0.5 \times 0.24 = 0.12\,\text{J} = 120\,\text{mJ}$$

**(ii) Peak discharge current:**

$$I_{peak} = V_c \sqrt{\frac{C}{L_p}} = 400 \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$= 400 \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 400 \times 0.03873 = 15.49\,\text{A}$$

**(iii) Peak secondary voltage:**

In an ideal LC discharge, the peak primary current multiplied by $\sqrt{L_p/C}$ gives the peak voltage across the primary, which equals the capacitor voltage = 400 V. So:

$$V_{p,peak} = 400\,\text{V}$$

$$V_s = k \times V_{p,peak} \times \frac{N_s}{N_p} = 1.0 \times 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 40\,\text{kV}}$$

**(iv) Comment on spark duration and applications (3 marks):**

In CDI, the energy is delivered as a **very short, very high-peak-current pulse** (rise time ~10 ¬µs). The resulting spark is **short (~0.05 ‚Äì 0.3 ms)** but **very hot and intense**, which is ideal for igniting a well-mixed, stoichiometric air-fuel charge in a high-RPM engine. However, this short spark struggles to ignite **lean mixtures, cold starts, and idling conditions** where the flame kernel needs more time to propagate.

- **Why CDI for 2-wheelers & racing:** 2-wheelers typically use a *magneto* AC source, which is naturally suited to a CDI (the AC is rectified to DC, then charged into a capacitor via a simple flyback circuit). Racing engines run at **very high RPM** (>10,000) where a long-duration TCI spark is unnecessary ‚Äî a sharp, short, intense CDI spark is sufficient. CDI is also **cheaper, lighter, and more compact** ‚Äî perfect for a motorcycle.
- **Why TCI for passenger cars:** Cars operate over a much wider range of conditions (cold-start, idle, traffic, cold weather, lean cruise). TCI delivers a **longer, more sustained spark** that ensures reliable combustion under these varied conditions. TCI also integrates more easily with **ECU-based spark advance and OBD-II diagnostics**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Electronic Ignition Advantages**
>
> - **The two fundamental limitations of conventional ignition** are **(a) contact-breaker point wear** and **(b) limited primary current** due to contact resistance. Electronic ignition kills both.
> - **Peak secondary voltage** jumps from **~20 kV to ~30 ‚Äì 40 kV** when the mechanical contact-breaker is replaced by a transistor.
> - **Spark duration** lengthens from **0.1 ‚Äì 0.3 ms to 0.3 ‚Äì 1.5 ms** ‚Äî critical for lean-burn and cold-start combustion.
> - **Spark energy at the plug** rises from **15 ‚Äì 30 mJ to 40 ‚Äì 100 mJ**, improving flame-kernel formation.
> - **Primary current** can be increased from **3 ‚Äì 4 A to 5 ‚Äì 7 A** without point welding, because the transistor switch has no mechanical wear limit.
> - **Dwell angle** is now **electronically controlled** (closed-loop), so it stays nearly constant from idle to redline ‚Äî no RPM-related spark-energy drop.
> - **Maintenance** drops to near-zero: no points to file, no condenser to replace, no dwell-angle adjustment, no rubbing-block renewal.
> - **High-RPM performance** improves: no contact bounce above ~5,000 rpm, so reliable firing continues to 7,000 ‚Äì 10,000 rpm (and beyond with DIS).
> - **Cold-start and idle quality** improve due to the **longer, hotter spark** that successfully ignites the cold, slow-moving mixture.
> - **Emissions and fuel economy** improve as a *consequence* of better combustion ‚Äî the catalyst sees less unburnt hydrocarbon, lambda control is more precise, and the engine runs closer to stoichiometry.
> - **Compatibility with ECU** enables **electronic spark advance (ESA)**, **knock-sensor-based retard**, and **ionization-current feedback** for closed-loop combustion control.
> - **Two main electronic variants:** **TCI (Transistor-Coil Ignition)** ‚Äî long, sustained spark for cars; and **CDI (Capacitive Discharge Ignition)** ‚Äî short, intense spark for 2-wheelers and racing.
> - **The energy equation $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$** (for TCI) and **$E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$** (for CDI) are the two equations you **must** know cold for the ESE.
> - **Key names to drop in an answer:** **Darlington pair** (transistor switch), **reluctor** (trigger wheel), **Hall-effect sensor** (alternative trigger), **SCR/thyristor** (CDI switch), **ballast resistor** (current limiter in primary), **zener clamp** (flyback voltage limiter).
> - **Examiner's favourite one-liner:** *"By eliminating the mechanical contact-breaker, electronic ignition systems deliver higher, longer, and more reliable sparks across the entire RPM range, enabling the precise combustion control demanded by modern emission norms and engine-management systems."* ‚Äî memorise and adapt.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CI Engine Management: Parameters Affecting Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CI Engine Management: Parameters Affecting Combustion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine Combustion** is the auto-ignition process in a diesel engine where the air-fuel mixture ignites spontaneously due to the high temperatures generated by mechanical compression ‚Äî without an external spark. The combustion process is governed by a tightly coupled set of **fuel injection parameters**, **in-cylinder air motion parameters**, **thermodynamic state parameters**, and **fuel physico-chemical parameters** that collectively determine the rate of heat release (ROHR), pressure rise, noise (diesel knock), emissions, and brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Statement:** > *"In a CI engine, combustion is not initiated by a spark but by the auto-ignition of fuel injected into hot compressed air. The quality of combustion ‚Äî its phasing, duration, and completeness ‚Äî is a function of fuel injection timing, injection pressure, in-cylinder air motion (swirl/tumble), compression ratio, engine load, engine speed, ambient conditions, and fuel properties such as cetane number."* The parameter set is classified into the following KTU-designated groups: | Parameter Group | Examples | |---|---| | Injection Parameters | Injection timing, injection pressure, injection duration, number of injections | | Air Motion Parameters | Swirl ratio, squish velocity, tumble, EGR rate | | Thermodynamic Parameters | Compression ratio, in-cylinder T & P, load, speed | | Fuel Parameters | Cetane number, viscosity, density, volatility | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Pressure Cooker & Ink Dropper** > > Imagine a sealed **pressure cooker** (the cylinder) where air has been squeezed so hot that anything flammable inside bursts into flame on its own. Now, instead of using a match, you squirt a fine **mist of diesel from an ink dropper** (the injector) into this super-hot chamber. The moment the tiny fuel droplets touch the hot air, they **spontaneously ignite** ‚Äî this is the essence of CI combustion. > > The cooking result (combustion quality) depends on: > - **When** you drop the fuel (injection timing), > - **How hard** you squeeze the dropper (injection pressure), > - **How much swirling** the air inside has (air motion), > - **How hot** the cooker is (compression ratio / engine load). > > If you drop the fuel too early, it piles up and explodes violently (diesel knock). If you drop it too late, the cooker starts cooling down and the food (combustion) becomes incomplete (smoke, HC, CO). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Standard reference pressure:** $P_0 = 1.013 \ \text{bar}$ (intake manifold reference) - **Standard reference temperature:** $T_0 = 298 \ \text{K}$ (**25 ¬∞C**) - **Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (diesel):** $AFR_{st} = 14.5 : 1$ (by mass, theoretical for pure Cetene C‚ÇÅ‚ÇÜH‚ÇÉ‚ÇÇ) - **Practical diesel operating AFR:** $18 : 1$ to $70 : 1$ (always lean of stoichiometric, i.e., $\lambda > 1$) - **Typical compression ratio for CI:** $14 : 1$ to $22 : 1$ - **Ignition temperature of diesel:** approximately **250 ¬∞C** to **300 ¬∞C** in still air - **Auto-ignition temperature under compression:** approximately **700 K ‚Äì 900 K** in-cylinder # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Phases of CI Combustion (Foundation Concept) The combustion event in a CI engine is divided into **three sequential phases**, and almost every "parameter affecting combustion" can be mapped onto how it shifts or reshapes one of these phases. ### Phase 1 ‚Äî Ignition Delay (Ignition Lag) Period - The interval (in **crank angle degrees**, ¬∞CA, or milliseconds) between the **start of injection (SOI)** and the **start of combustion (SOC)**. - During this lag, the injected fuel is undergoing **atomization, vaporization, mixing with hot air, and pre-flame chemical reactions**. - A fraction of the injected mass accumulates as a **premixed air-fuel cloud**. ### Phase 2 ‚Äî Premixed Combustion (Uncontrolled / Rapid Phase) - The accumulated premixed charge burns **almost instantaneously**, producing the characteristic sharp rise in cylinder pressure. - This phase is responsible for **diesel knock** if the ignition delay is too long. - Governed primarily by: cetane number, ignition delay, EGR, in-cylinder T & P. ### Phase 3 ‚Äî Diffusion (Mixing-Controlled) Combustion - After the premixed charge is consumed, the **remaining fuel burns as it mixes** with surrounding air. - This phase is **slower and controlled by fuel-air mixing rate**, not chemical kinetics. - Governed by: spray penetration, swirl, injection pressure, load. ## 2.2 Detailed Parameter-by-Parameter Influence ### A. Injection Timing - Advanced timing (SOI earlier) ‚Üí **longer ignition delay** ‚Üí more fuel accumulation ‚Üí **harsher knock, higher peak pressure, higher NOx**. - Retarded timing ‚Üí lower peak pressure, **higher smoke, higher BSFC, more HC and CO**. $$P_{\max}(\theta) = f\left(\theta_{SOI}, \ \tau_{id}\right)$$ where $\tau_{id}$ is the ignition delay (s or ¬∞CA). ### B. Injection Pressure - Higher injection pressure ‚Üí finer atomization, smaller **Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD)** of droplets. - Reduced SMD ‚Üí faster evaporation, **shorter ignition delay, lower smoke, lower BSFC, more premixed burn**. $$\text{SMD} = 2.25 \left(\frac{\sigma \cdot \mu_l^2}{\rho_a \cdot \Delta P}\right)^{0.25} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_a}\right)^{0.25} \cdot \frac{1}{v_{\text{rel}}}$$ where $\Delta P$ is the injection pressure differential. ### C. Number of Injections (Modern Common-Rail Strategy) - **Pilot injection** ‚Üí small dose before main ‚Üí raises in-cylinder T slightly, shortens main ignition delay, **reduces knock and noise**. - **Main injection** ‚Üí dominant combustion event. - **Post injection** ‚Üí for DPF regeneration, not for tractive combustion. - **Rate Shaping** (e.g., "boot" or "ramp" shape) ‚Üí controls $\frac{dQ}{d\theta}$. ### D. Equivalence Ratio & Excess Air Factor - $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ ; $\lambda = 1/\phi$. - CI engines operate **always lean** ($\lambda > 1$). - Lean mixture ‚Üí **more excess air, lower peak T, lower NOx, but high smoke at very high loads** (insufficient air for all injected fuel to find O‚ÇÇ in time). ### E. Compression Ratio - Higher CR ‚Üí higher end-of-compression T and P ‚Üí **shorter ignition delay** ‚Üí less knock. - Modern CRDI diesels: $16:1$ to $22:1$ (bounded by $\text{NO}_x$ and mechanical limits). $$T_{comp} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \quad ; \quad P_{comp} = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$$ For a typical diesel ($r = 18$, $\gamma = 1.35$): end-of-compression **T ‚âà 850 K**, **P ‚âà 45 bar**. ### F. Engine Load & Speed - **Load ‚Üë** ‚Üí more fuel injected ‚Üí more diffusion burn, **higher smoke limit**, longer combustion duration. - **Speed ‚Üë** ‚Üí less time per cycle ‚Üí **shorter physical time** for mixing ‚Üí less complete combustion, more smoke, lower thermal efficiency at part load. ### G. In-Cylinder Air Motion ‚Äî Swirl - Swirl ratio $R_s = \dfrac{\omega_{swirl}}{\omega_{engine}}$. - Optimal swirl **2.0 to 2.5** for modern common-rail engines. - Excessive swirl ‚Üí heat loss to walls; insufficient swirl ‚Üí poor mixing, **smoke**. ### H. EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) - Inert exhaust gas dilutes the charge ‚Üí lowers flame temperature ‚Üí **lower NOx**. - Trade-off: increases smoke, raises BSFC, lengthens ignition delay. ### I. Fuel Properties - **Cetane Number (CN)** ‚Üí measures ignition quality. Higher CN ‚Üí shorter ignition delay. - **CN of normal diesel = 45 to 55**; **CN of biodiesel = 45 to 65**. $$\tau_{id} \propto \dfrac{1}{CN} \quad \text{(empirical, Arrhenius-like)}$$ ### J. Ambient Conditions - Cold ambient ‚Üí lower intake T ‚Üí longer ignition delay ‚Üí **white smoke (unburnt vapor) at start-up**. - High altitude ‚Üí lower intake P ‚Üí less air mass ‚Üí derating. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` (not `|`) in every table cell to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $x_b = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$ | **Wiebe function** ‚Äî mass fraction burned vs. crank angle | dimensionless | | 2 | $\dfrac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \dfrac{a(m+1)}{\Delta\theta}\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m}\!\exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$ | **Rate of mass fraction burned** | ¬∞CA‚Åª¬π | | 3 | $\dfrac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = m_f \cdot H_u \cdot \dfrac{dx_b}{d\theta}$ | **Rate of Heat Release (ROHR)** | J/¬∞CA | | 4 | $\tau_{id} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)$ | **Arrhenius-type ignition delay** (Hiroyasu model) | s or ms | | 5 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | **Equivalence ratio** | dimensionless | | 6 | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | **Excess air factor** | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{SMD} \propto \left(\dfrac{\sigma \cdot \mu_l^2}{\rho_a \cdot \Delta P}\right)^{0.25}$ | **Sauter Mean Diameter** (atomization) | m or ¬µm | | 8 | $T_{comp} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ | **End-of-compression temperature** | K | | 9 | $P_{comp} = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$ | **End-of-compression pressure** | bar or Pa | | 10 | $R_s = \dfrac{\omega_{swirl}}{\omega_{engine}}$ | **Swirl ratio** | dimensionless | | 11 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | **Otto/Air-standard efficiency** (for comparison) | dimensionless | | 12 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | **Brake specific fuel consumption** | g/kWh | | 13 | $\text{PM}_{\text{index}} \propto \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_a \cdot \text{SMD} \cdot R_s}$ | **Smoke / particulate index (qualitative)** | ‚Äî | | 14 | $\text{NO}_x \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{NO}}{T_{\max}}\right)$ | **Zeldovich mechanism proxy** for NO formation | ppm | **Wiebe function parameter meanings (KTU exam favourite):** - $\theta_0$ = start of combustion (crank angle) - $\Delta\theta$ = **combustion duration** (typically 30 ¬∞CA to 80 ¬∞CA for a diesel) - $a$ = efficiency parameter (commonly $a = 5$, ensures ~99.5% mass burned) - $m$ = **shape factor** (Wiebe form / Weibe exponent), typically $0.5$ to $3.0$ - $m+1$ is the **Wiebe exponent (n)** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Use of These Combustion Parameters | |---|---| | **Automotive ECU calibration** (Bosch, Continental, Denso) | ECU maps of SOI vs. RPM vs. load; pilot/main dose ratio; rail pressure | | **Aftertreatment design** (DPF, SCR, DOC) | Predicting PM and NO‚Çì to size the catalyst volume | | **Emissions legislation** (Euro 6, BS-VI, EPA Tier 4) | Dictates the parameter envelope an OEM must work within | | **HCCI / RCCI research** | Modifying ignition-delay parameters to enable low-temperature combustion | | **Marine / locomotive CI engines** | Long combustion durations, large $\Delta\theta$ | | **FAME (biodiesel) blending** | Re-mapping cetane sensitivity and density into the ECU | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Wiebe Function Rate of Heat Release The Wiebe function describes the **cumulative mass fraction burned** as a function of crank angle $\theta$: $$x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{n}\right]$$ where $n = m+1$ is the **Wiebe exponent** and $a$ is a **completeness parameter** (typically chosen as $a = 5$ so that at $\theta = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta$, $x_b = 1 - e^{-5} = 0.9933$, i.e., 99.33% of fuel mass burned). ### Step 1 ‚Äî Differentiate the Wiebe function Differentiate $x_b$ with respect to crank angle $\theta$ to obtain the **rate of mass burn**. Let $u = \dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}$, so $x_b = 1 - e^{-a u^n}$. Then $\dfrac{du}{d\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta\theta}$. ### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the chain rule $$\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = -\left(-a n u^{n-1} e^{-a u^n}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta\theta}$$ ### Step 3 ‚Äî Final rate of mass fraction burned $$\boxed{\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a n}{\Delta\theta} \left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{n-1} \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^n\right]}$$ ### Step 4 ‚Äî Multiply by fuel mass and heating value to get the **ROHR** $$\boxed{\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = m_f \cdot H_u \cdot \frac{a n}{\Delta\theta} \left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{n-1} \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^n\right]}$$ where $H_u$ is the **lower calorific value of diesel** ‚âà **42.5 MJ/kg**. **Engineering interpretation:** ROHR is the **primary diagnostic** used by ECU calibration engineers to evaluate a combustion strategy ‚Äî its peak value and the angle at which the peak occurs (CA50) are key combustion-phasing metrics. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî End-of-Compression State (CI Engine) Starting from the **isentropic compression** of the air charge (no fuel in cylinder at this stage): $$P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$$ Rearranging for $P_2$: $$P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma} = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$$ where $r = V_1/V_2$ is the **compression ratio**. Similarly, for temperature: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$ ### Worked Example (KTU-type numerical) **Given:** $T_1 = 320 \ \text{K}$ (intake temperature), $P_1 = 1.013 \ \text{bar}$, $r = 18$, $\gamma = 1.35$. **Step 1 ‚Äî End-of-compression pressure:** $$P_2 = 1.013 \times 18^{1.35} = 1.013 \times 47.5 \approx 48.1 \ \text{bar}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî End-of-compression temperature:** $$T_2 = 320 \times 18^{0.35} = 320 \times 2.886 \approx 923.5 \ \text{K} \approx 650 \ ^\circ\text{C}$$ This temperature comfortably exceeds the diesel auto-ignition temperature (~570 K), so injection will lead to spontaneous combustion. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Ignition Delay (Hiroyasu / Arrhenius Model) The **ignition delay** is modelled as a function of in-cylinder pressure and temperature, based on the Arrhenius chemical kinetics: $$\tau_{id} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)$$ where: - $A$ = pre-exponential constant (depends on fuel) - $p$ = in-cylinder pressure (Pa) - $n$ ‚âà 1.0 to 1.5 (Hiroyasu constant) - $E_a$ = **activation energy** (kJ/mol) - $R$ = **8.314 J/mol¬∑K** (universal gas constant) - $T$ = in-cylinder temperature (K) ### Worked Example **Given:** $A = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{ms¬∑Pa}^n$, $n = 1.2$, $E_a = 35 \ \text{kJ/mol}$, $p = 45 \ \text{bar} = 4.5 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa}$, $T = 900 \ \text{K}$. **Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $p^{-n}$:** $$p^{-1.2} = (4.5 \times 10^6)^{-1.2}$$ $$\log_{10}(4.5 \times 10^6) = 6.653$$ $$-1.2 \times 6.653 = -7.984$$ $$p^{-1.2} = 10^{-7.984} = 1.039 \times 10^{-8}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the exponential term:** $$\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T} = \frac{35\,000}{8.314 \times 900} = \frac{35\,000}{7482.6} = 4.677$$ $$e^{4.677} = 107.4$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Combine:** $$\tau_{id} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.039 \times 10^{-8} \times 107.4$$ $$\tau_{id} = 5.02 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{s} = 5.02 \ \text{ns}$$ > This is the **chemical delay** portion. The **physical delay** (atomization + vaporization + mixing) is added to get total $\tau_{id}$ (typically 0.5 ms to 2.0 ms in a real engine). **Physical delay model** (simplified, by cold-start physical mixing time): $$\tau_{phys} \approx \frac{d_{drop}^2}{k_{evap}} \quad ; \quad k_{evap} \propto \frac{\lambda_g \cdot \Delta T}{d_{drop}}$$ Hence $\tau_{phys} \propto d_{drop}$, so smaller droplets ‚Üí shorter delay ‚Üí less knock. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Parameter √ó Combustion Response | Parameter (‚Üë = increase) | Peak Pressure | Ignition Delay | Smoke / PM | NO‚Çì | BSFC | Noise / Knock | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Injection pressure** ‚Üë | ‚Üë slightly | ‚Üì | ‚Üì‚Üì | ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | | **Injection advance (SOI earlier)** ‚Üë | ‚Üë‚Üë | ‚Üë (longer) | ‚Üì | ‚Üë‚Üë | ‚Üë at light, ‚Üì at full | ‚Üë‚Üë (knock ‚Üë) | | **Compression ratio** ‚Üë | ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | | **EGR rate** ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üë‚Üë | ‚Üë | ‚Üì‚Üì | ‚Üë | ‚Üë | | **Cetane number** ‚Üë | similar | ‚Üì‚Üì | ‚Üì slightly | similar | similar | ‚Üì | | **Engine load** ‚Üë | ‚Üë‚Üë | ‚Üì slightly | ‚Üë‚Üë (at high) | ‚Üë‚Üë | similar | similar | | **Engine speed** ‚Üë | similar (timed) | similar | ‚Üë at high | ‚Üë | ‚Üì at part load | ‚Üë | | **Swirl ratio** ‚Üë | small ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üì (up to optimum) | ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | | **Intake temperature** ‚Üë | ‚Üë | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | ‚Üë‚Üë | ‚Üë | ‚Üì | | **Pilot injection dose** ‚Üë | small ‚Üë | ‚Üì (for main) | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | ‚Üì | ‚Üì‚Üì | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CI Engine Combustion Management ```mermaid flowchart TB node1[Driver Demand
Accelerator Pedal Position] --> node2[ECU Master Controller
Bosch EDC17 / Continental] node3[Crankshaft Position Sensor
CKP - 60-2 teeth] --> node2 node4[Camshaft Position Sensor
CMP] --> node2 node5[MAF / MAP Sensor
Intake Air Mass] --> node2 node6[Coolant Temp Sensor
CTS] --> node2 node7[Rail Pressure Sensor
in Common Rail] --> node2 node8[Boost Pressure Sensor
from VNT or WG turbo] --> node2 node9[Lambda / UEGO Sensor
Exhaust O2] --> node2 node10[EGT Sensor
Pre-turbine] --> node2 node2 --> node11[Combustion Phasing Calculator
computes SOI and CA50 target] node2 --> node12[Injection Quantity Calculator
computes m_f per cycle] node2 --> node13[Rail Pressure Controller
commands SCP HP pump] node2 --> node14[EGR Valve Actuator
Variable Geometry EGR] node2 --> node15[VNT Vane Actuator
Turbocharger control] node2 --> node16[INJECTOR DRIVER
Piezo / Solenoid stage] node16 --> node17[Fuel Injector
Common Rail 2000 bar] node17 --> node18[Combustion Chamber
Piston Bowl Swirl Geometry] node18 --> node19[Exhaust Manifold] node19 --> node20[EGT / Lambda Feedback] node20 --> node2 node15 --> node21[Variable Geometry Turbocharger
VNT] node21 --> node22[Intake Manifold Boost] node22 --> node18 node13 --> node23[High Pressure Pump] node23 --> node24[Common Rail Accumulator] node24 --> node17 style node2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node16 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node18 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Three-Phase Combustion Timeline (Wiebe-Style Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Start of Injection
SOI] --> nodeB[Ignition Delay Phase
tau_id ~ 0.5 to 2 ms] nodeB --> nodeC[Premixed Combustion
Sharp dQ/dtheta peak] nodeC --> nodeD[Diffusion Combustion
Mixing controlled] nodeD --> nodeE[End of Combustion
CA90 reached] nodeE --> nodeF[Expansion and Exhaust] nodeC --> nodeC1[Kp = Peak ROHR
~ 0.5 to 0.7 of dQ] nodeC --> nodeC2[CA50 = Crank angle at
50 percent mass burned] nodeD --> nodeD1[Combustion duration
