delta_theta ~ 30 to 80 degCA] style nodeA fill:#FFA07A style nodeB fill:#FFD700 style nodeC fill:#FF6347 style nodeD fill:#4682B4 style nodeE fill:#90EE90 style nodeC1 fill:#FFB6C1 style nodeC2 fill:#FFB6C1 style nodeD1 fill:#ADD8E6 ``` ## 4.3 Parameter-to-Combustion-Response Influence Map ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeP1[Injection Timing SOI] --> nodeR1[Ignition Delay] nodeP2[Injection Pressure] --> nodeR2[SMD and Spray Penetration] nodeP3[Number of Injections] --> nodeR3[Premixed Mass Fraction] nodeP4[Compression Ratio] --> nodeR1 nodeP5[Cetane Number] --> nodeR1 nodeP6[EGR Rate] --> nodeR4[In-Cylinder T and O2] nodeP7[Swirl Ratio] --> nodeR2 nodeP8[Engine Load] --> nodeR5[Fuel Mass per Cycle] nodeP9[Engine Speed] --> nodeR6[Time per Cycle] nodeP10[Intake T and Boost] --> nodeR4 nodeR1 --> nodeO1[Peak Pressure and Knock] nodeR2 --> nodeO2[Atomization and Mixing] nodeR3 --> nodeO3[Premixed Burn Intensity] nodeR4 --> nodeO4[NOx and Smoke] nodeR5 --> nodeO5[Diffusion Burn Duration] nodeR6 --> nodeO5 nodeO1 --> nodeOUT[CA50 and Combustion Phasing] nodeO2 --> nodeOUT nodeO3 --> nodeOUT nodeO4 --> nodeOUT nodeO5 --> nodeOUT style nodeOUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024, Model Paper]** **Define "ignition delay" in a CI engine and list the two sub-phases into which it is divided.** **Model Answer:** - **Definition (2 marks):** Ignition delay (or ignition lag) is the time interval, expressed in milliseconds or crank angle degrees, between the **start of injection (SOI)** and the **start of combustion (SOC)**. It is the period during which the injected fuel is being atomized, vaporized, mixed with the hot compressed air, and undergoing pre-flame chemical reactions. - **Two sub-phases (1 mark):** 1. **Physical delay** ‚Äî atomization + vaporization + mixing of fuel with air. 2. **Chemical delay** ‚Äî pre-flame chemical kinetics (cool-flame reactions) leading to auto-ignition. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]** **What is meant by "equivalence ratio" $\phi$ in a CI engine? Why are CI engines always operated with $\phi < 1$?** **Model Answer:** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Equivalence ratio is the ratio of the actual fuel-air ratio to the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio: $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$$ - **Why $\phi < 1$ (1.5 marks):** CI engines use a **homogeneous air charge first** and inject fuel into it (heterogeneous, in-cylinder mixing). To guarantee that **all the injected fuel can find sufficient oxygen** within the very short time available, the engine is always run **lean of stoichiometric** ($\phi < 1$, $\lambda > 1$). Typical $\lambda$ values range from **1.2 to 3.5** depending on load. Running rich ($\phi > 1$) would cause excessive smoke, unburnt hydrocarbons, and thermal overload of the injector tip. --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the three phases of combustion in a CI engine. How does injection timing influence peak pressure and NO‚Çì emissions? **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Wiebe function for mass fraction burned $x_b(\theta)$ in a CI engine and obtain an expression for the rate of heat release $\dfrac{dQ_B}{d\theta}$. --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî (a) [7 Marks] **1. Three phases of combustion (5 marks):** | Phase | Crank Angle Window | Description | Controlling Parameter | |---|---|---|---| | **1. Ignition Delay** | SOI ‚Üí SOC | Fuel atomizes, vaporizes, mixes, undergoes pre-flame reactions; a premixed air-fuel cloud forms. | Cetane number, in-cylinder T & P, EGR | | **2. Premixed Combustion** | SOC ‚Üí ~CA30 | The accumulated premixed charge burns almost instantaneously; sharp pressure rise. | Ignition delay duration | | **3. Diffusion Combustion** | ~CA30 ‚Üí CA90 | Remaining fuel burns as it mixes with air; controlled by fuel-air mixing rate. | Spray penetration, swirl, injection pressure | **2. Effect of injection timing on peak pressure and NO‚Çì (2 marks):** - **Advanced injection (earlier SOI):** Fuel is injected while the piston is still rising, giving a **longer ignition delay** ‚Üí more premixed accumulation ‚Üí **higher peak pressure** and **higher NO‚Çì** (because peak in-cylinder temperature is higher and persists for longer in the high-T window of the Zeldovich mechanism). - **Retarded injection (later SOI):** Injection begins later, ignition delay is shorter (T is higher), but combustion continues into the expansion stroke ‚Üí **lower peak pressure**, lower NO‚Çì, but **higher BSFC and smoke**. --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî (b) [7 Marks] **1. Wiebe function derivation (4 marks):** Define: - $\theta$ = instantaneous crank angle, - $\theta_0$ = crank angle at start of combustion, - $\Delta\theta$ = total combustion duration, - $a$ = efficiency parameter, - $n = m+1$ = Wiebe exponent (shape factor). The general Wiebe form is assumed as: $$x_b = 1 - \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^n\right]$$ **Justification:** The exponential form satisfies the two boundary conditions: - $x_b(\theta_0) = 0$ (no mass burned before $\theta_0$), - $x_b \to 1$ as $\theta \gg \theta_0 + \Delta\theta$ (asymptotically complete). **2. ROHR derivation (3 marks):** Differentiate using the chain rule with $u = (\theta - \theta_0)/\Delta\theta$: $$\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a n}{\Delta\theta}\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{n-1} \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^n\right]$$ The rate of heat release is: $$\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = m_f \cdot H_u \cdot \frac{dx_b}{d\theta}$$ Substituting: $$\boxed{\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = m_f \cdot H_u \cdot \frac{a n}{\Delta\theta}\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{n-1} \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^n\right]}$$ where $H_u \approx 42.5$ MJ/kg for diesel. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** List and explain any **six (6) parameters** that affect combustion in a CI engine. For each, state its qualitative influence on smoke and NO‚Çì emissions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A single-cylinder CI engine has a compression ratio of 18:1, intake conditions of 1.013 bar and 320 K. Assuming $\gamma = 1.35$, calculate (i) end-of-compression pressure, (ii) end-of-compression temperature, and (iii) state whether the conditions are sufficient for auto-ignition of diesel (auto-ignition temperature ‚âà 570 K). --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî (a) [7 Marks] **Six parameters with effect on smoke and NO‚Çì (‚âà 1.2 marks each):** | # | Parameter | Effect on Smoke (PM) | Effect on NO‚Çì | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Injection timing ‚Äî advanced** | ‚Üì (slight) | ‚Üë‚Üë (large increase) | | 2 | **Injection pressure ‚Äî high** | ‚Üì‚Üì (large decrease) | ‚Üë (slight) | | 3 | **EGR ‚Äî high** | ‚Üë (increase) | ‚Üì‚Üì (large decrease) | | 4 | **Cetane number ‚Äî high** | ‚Üì (slight) | ~ similar | | 5 | **Swirl ratio ‚Äî optimum** | ‚Üì (decrease) | ‚Üë (slight) | | 6 | **Engine load ‚Äî high** | ‚Üë‚Üë (sharp increase) | ‚Üë‚Üë (sharp increase) | --- #### Model Solution ‚Äî (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** $r = 18$, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = 320$ K, $\gamma = 1.35$, $T_{auto} \approx 570$ K. **Part (i) ‚Äî End-of-compression pressure (2.5 marks):** $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$$ $$r^{\gamma} = 18^{1.35}$$ Compute: $\log_{10}(18) = 1.2553$, multiply by $1.35$ ‚Üí $1.6946$, $10^{1.6946} = 49.49$. $$P_2 = 1.013 \times 49.49 \approx 50.13 \ \text{bar}$$ **Part (ii) ‚Äî End-of-compression temperature (2.5 marks):** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$ $$r^{\gamma-1} = 18^{0.35}$$ Compute: $1.2553 \times 0.35 = 0.4394$, $10^{0.4394} = 2.751$. $$T_2 = 320 \times 2.751 \approx 880.3 \ \text{K}$$ **Part (iii) ‚Äî Sufficiency for auto-ignition (2 marks):** Since $T_2 = 880.3 \ \text{K} \gg T_{auto} = 570 \ \text{K}$: $$\boxed{\text{The end-of-compression temperature is well above the diesel auto-ignition temperature. Auto-ignition is guaranteed.}}$$ Additionally, $P_2 = 50.13$ bar is well above the typical 30‚Äì40 bar ignition-pressure threshold, so combustion will be vigorous and the rate of pressure rise will be high (subject to the actual injection timing used in the calibration). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks)** > 1. **Confusing ignition delay with combustion duration.** Ignition delay = SOI ‚Üí SOC (typically 0.5 to 2 ms). Combustion duration = SOC ‚Üí CA90 (typically 30 ¬∞CA to 80 ¬∞CA). Examiners **deduct 1 to 2 marks** if you mix these up. > 2. **Forgetting units in the Wiebe function.** The numerator $\theta - \theta_0$ and denominator $\Delta\theta$ are both in **degrees of crank angle**, not seconds. State this explicitly to earn full marks. > 3. **Using $a = 1$ instead of $a = 5$.** The efficiency parameter $a$ should be cited as $a = 5$ (the standard KTU value) ‚Äî this ensures $x_b = 0.993$ at $\theta = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta$. Examiners expect this. > 4. **Equivalence ratio vs. excess air factor confusion.** $\phi = F/A_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ ; $\lambda = 1/\phi$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed **1-mark loss**. > 5. **Forgetting that CI engines are always lean** (with the *exception* of cold-start where over-fuelling occurs). State this explicitly. > 6. **In Wiebe/ROHR derivation, skipping the chain rule** ‚Äî show $\dfrac{du}{d\theta} = 1/\Delta\theta$ explicitly, or you will be marked down. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Read this list three times before your exam.** - ‚òê **CI combustion = auto-ignition**, no spark plug; relies on compression heating. - ‚òê **Three phases:** Ignition delay ‚Üí Premixed burn ‚Üí Diffusion burn. - ‚òê **Ignition delay** is shortened by: high cetane, high in-cylinder T, high in-cylinder P, **shortened** by high EGR? (No ‚Äî EGR *lengthens* it). **Lengthened** by low cetane, low T, high EGR. - ‚òê **Premixed burn** is the cause of **diesel knock**; **pilot injection** is the modern solution. - ‚òê **Diffusion burn** duration is controlled by **spray penetration, swirl, injection pressure**. - ‚òê **Injection pressure** ‚Üë ‚Üí SMD ‚Üì ‚Üí better atomization ‚Üí less smoke. - ‚òê **Injection timing advance** ‚Üí more NO‚Çì and knock, less smoke. - ‚òê **EGR** ‚Üë ‚Üí NO‚Çì ‚Üì, smoke ‚Üë, BSFC ‚Üë. - ‚òê **Equivalence ratio** $\phi$ in CI engines **always less than 1** (lean operation). - ‚òê **Compression ratio** 14:1 to 22:1 for modern CRDI diesels. - ‚òê **Wiebe function** $x_b = 1 - \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{n}\right]$ with $a = 5$, $n = m+1$. - ‚òê **ROHR** $\frac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = m_f H_u \frac{dx_b}{d\theta}$; **CA50** is the angle of 50% mass burned (key ECU target). - ‚òê **Swirl ratio** optimum ‚âà 2.0 to 2.5 for common-rail engines. - ‚òê **Cetane number** of standard diesel: 45 to 55. Higher CN ‚Üí shorter ignition delay. - ‚òê **Pilot + main + post** injection strategy = modern common-rail signature. - ‚òê **End-of-compression T** for $r = 18$, $T_1 = 320$ K, $\gamma = 1.35$ is **‚âà 880 K**, well above the 570 K auto-ignition threshold. - ‚òê **Modern KTU favourite for 14-mark question:** Wiebe derivation + parameter effect table + worked numerical on end-of-compression state.

topics.title: Noise and Emissions in CI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Noise and Emissions in CI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**CI (Compression Ignition) Engines** are internal combustion engines in which ignition of the fuel-air mixture is achieved purely by the high temperature generated during the compression of air ‚Äî there is no external spark plug. While they offer superior thermal efficiency (35‚Äì45%) and excellent torque characteristics, they are historically notorious for two major environmental and acoustic challenges: **Noise** and **Emissions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition ‚Äî Engine Noise:**
> Engine noise is the total acoustic energy radiated by a running engine, classified broadly into **Combustion Noise** (pressure-wave induced, dominant in CI engines) and **Mechanical Noise** (structural vibration of components). It is quantified in **dB(A)** (A-weighted decibels) referenced to **20 ¬µPa** sound pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition ‚Äî Engine Emissions:**
> Engine exhaust emissions are the gaseous and particulate by-products of incomplete or thermally-dissociated combustion. For CI engines, the **regulated pollutants** under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms are **CO**, **HC** (unburnt hydrocarbons), **NOx** (oxides of nitrogen), and **PM** (particulate matter / soot).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

**The Diesel Knock Analogy (Combustion Noise):**
Imagine popping a giant inflated paper bag with a sharp pin. The sudden explosion creates a loud "BANG" because pressure is released almost instantaneously. In a CI engine, when the diesel fuel injects and auto-ignites, the pressure rise inside the cylinder is so abrupt (rate of pressure rise, $\frac{dp}{d\theta}$, can exceed **10 bar/degree** of crank angle) that it sets the cylinder walls vibrating ‚Äî exactly like the paper bag. This mechanical vibration of the block radiates as the characteristic **"DIESEL KNOCK"** or combustion rattle. In petrol engines, combustion is smooth and gradual, so the noise is a soft "purr" rather than a knock.

**The Unwanted Kitchen Smoke Analogy (Emissions):**
Think of an open kitchen stove with a steel pan. When the fire is hot and the food (fuel) is overcooked, black smoke (soot/PM) rises, and the burner flame becomes yellow with unburnt carbon particles. Some fuel vapor escapes unburnt (HC), some carbon becomes carbon monoxide instead of CO‚ÇÇ because oxygen is insufficient locally, and the high heat "fixes" atmospheric nitrogen into NOx (the same brown haze you see over busy city intersections). CI engines, especially with their diffusion flame, behave exactly like that overcooked kitchen ‚Äî rich in soot, NOx, and noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU):**
> - Sound reference pressure: **$p_0 = 20 \,\mu Pa$**
> - Threshold of pain: **~120 dB(A)**
> - Bharat Stage VI NOx limit for CI cars: **80 mg/km**
> - Bharat Stage VI PM limit for CI cars: **4.5 mg/km**
> - Typical CI idle noise: **85‚Äì95 dB(A)**; petrol idle noise: **70‚Äì80 dB(A)**
> - SI engine NOx is **3‚Äì5 times** lower than CI engine NOx at similar loads.

### 1.3 Importance for KTU Autotronics

Noise and emission control in CI engines is not a stand-alone sub-topic ‚Äî it is the **driving force** behind the entire modern engine management system architecture. **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)**, **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)**, **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)**, **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)**, **DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst)**, and **LNT (Lean NOx Trap)** all exist *primarily* to address these two problems. A KTU examiner expects you to understand the *cause* before the *cure*.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of CI Engine Noise

CI engine noise can be decomposed into **four physical sources**:

**(A) Combustion Noise (60‚Äì70% contribution at idle/low load):**
- Caused by rapid cylinder pressure rise during premixed combustion phase.
- Quantified by the **rate of pressure rise** $\frac{dp}{d\theta}$ (bar/¬∞CA) and **peak pressure rise rate** $\left(\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$.
- Higher $\left(\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$ ‚Üí louder, harsher knock.
- Diesel knock is most severe at **cold start** and **low load** when ignition delay is long, accumulating a large premixed fuel charge that auto-ignites almost simultaneously.

**(B) Mechanical Noise (20‚Äì30% contribution):**
- Originates from piston slap, valve train impacts, bearing rattle, gear mesh, and timing chain noise.
- **Piston slap**: In CI engines, the high in-cylinder pressure during combustion forces the piston against the cylinder wall, hammering it tangentially ‚Üí audible high-frequency tick.

**(C) Aerodynamic / Intake & Exhaust Noise:**
- Gas pulsations in intake and exhaust manifolds, especially at high RPM.
- Turbocharger whistle (siren effect of compressor blades).

**(D) Structure-Borne Vibration:**
- Engine block mounted on chassis transmits vibration. Mitigated by **hydraulic engine mounts** and **dynamic dampers**.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of CI Engine Emissions

CI engines are dominated by **three pollutant families**:

**1. Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx):**
- Formed via the **Zeldovich Mechanism** at temperatures above **~1800 K**.
- Thermal (Zeldovich) NOx: NO + NO‚ÇÇ, with NO being 90‚Äì95% of total.
- The chemistry is "frozen" ‚Äî once formed, NOx persists downstream.

$$\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$$
$$\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$$
$$\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}$$

- **NOx formation is exponentially sensitive to peak flame temperature.**

**2. Particulate Matter (PM / Soot):**
- Formed in **fuel-rich zones** of the diffusion flame where local equivalence ratio $\phi > 2$.
- Sequence: Fuel ‚Üí Pyrolysis ‚Üí **Soot Precursors (PAHs)** ‚Üí Soot Nuclei ‚Üí Coagulation ‚Üí Soot Agglomerates ‚Üí Surface Growth.
- In CI engines, soot forms during combustion but a fraction is **oxidized** in the late expansion stroke. The leftover escapes as visible black smoke under high load.
- **PM‚ÇÇ.‚ÇÖ (particles < 2.5 ¬µm)** is the most health-hazardous fraction ‚Äî penetrates deep into alveoli.

**3. Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC) & Carbon Monoxide (CO):**
- Generally **lower in CI engines** than SI engines (because of excess air and complete combustion).
- Sources: crevice volumes (piston ring land), wall quenching, fuel injector dribble, over-mixing (over-lean pockets).
- CO is a marker of **local oxygen deficiency**.

### 2.3 The NOx‚ÄìPM Trade-Off (KTU Favourite Concept!)

This is the **single most important trade-off** in diesel engine calibration. A naive student might think: "Just lower combustion temperature ‚Äî that reduces NOx, problem solved." But:

- **Lower temperature** ‚Üí less thermal NOx ‚úÖ
- **But** ‚Üí less soot oxidation, more fuel impingement on cold walls, **higher PM** ‚ùå
- **And** ‚Üí more unburnt HC due to poor evaporation ‚ùå

Conversely, **raising temperature** reduces PM but spikes NOx. The "**NOx‚ÄìPM trade-off curve**" is a classic exam question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The NOx‚ÄìPM Trade-Off Principle:**
> No single in-cylinder strategy can simultaneously reduce NOx and PM. This is why modern BS-VI diesel vehicles use **multi-stage after-treatment**: DPF for PM + SCR (AdBlue/DEF injection) for NOx. The after-treatment chain essentially **decouples** the trade-off ‚Äî a key insight for KTU 2024.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | $p_0 = 20 \,\mu Pa$ reference; $L_p$ in dB |
| 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound Power Level | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W reference |
| 3 | $\left(\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$ | Peak Rate of Pressure Rise | bar/¬∞CA ‚Äî direct indicator of diesel knock intensity |
| 4 | $\tau_i = \tau_{phys} + \tau_{chem}$ | Ignition Delay | Sum of physical + chemical delay; longer $\tau_i$ ‚Üí harder knock |
| 5 | $\text{[NO]}_{eq} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T_{flame}}\right)$ | Extended Zeldovich Equilibrium | Doubling peak temperature roughly **doubles NOx** |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto Cycle Efficiency | For noise/emission reference; $r$ = compression ratio, $\gamma$ = 1.4 |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{NO_x} = f\!\left(T_{peak},\, t_{res},\, [O_2]\right)$ | NOx Formation Rate | Function of peak T, residence time, O‚ÇÇ conc. |
| 8 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi > 1$ rich, $\phi < 1$ lean; CI always lean overall ($\phi \approx 0.7$) |
| 9 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{P}_b}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh; emission indices often reported per kg fuel |
| 10 | $EI_{pollutant} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{pollutant}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | Emission Index | g pollutant / kg fuel burned |
| 11 | $\text{Opacity} \propto \int \text{PM}(t)\,dt$ | Smoke Opacity (Hartridge units) | Measured by smoke meter; BS-VI limits are in mg/km, not opacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not just academic ‚Äî they directly drive ECU calibration tables in production ECUs. For example, the **Smoke Limiter** in a mechanical inline injection pump and the modern **Electronic Smoke Limiter** in CRDi ECUs both use the inverse of EGT (exhaust gas temperature) and intake airflow to cap fuel quantity, suppressing PM formation. Similarly, the **EGR valve duty cycle** is mapped against the inverse of NOx sensor feedback to keep NOx below the legislative threshold.

### 2.5 The Why-How of Each Major Source

**Why is combustion noise worse in CI than SI?**
Because CI combustion is **heterogeneous** (diffusion flame + premixed pockets), leading to highly localized pressure spikes. SI combustion is **homogeneous** and propagates smoothly as a flame kernel ‚Äî smoother pressure curve.

**Why does EGR reduce NOx?**
EGR introduces inert exhaust gas (mostly CO‚ÇÇ and H‚ÇÇO) into the intake. This **lowers the specific heat capacity ratio**, reduces peak flame temperature, and **displaces oxygen** ‚Äî all three reduce the Zeldovich rate exponentially.

**Why does EGR increase PM?**
Lower flame temperature ‚Üí less soot oxidation, plus the added gas mass reduces in-cylinder temperature and pressure ‚Äî fuel droplets impinge on walls, and soot accumulates in crevices.

**Why is a DPF needed even with good combustion?**
Even under ideal BS-VI in-cylinder conditions, sub-23 nm nucleation-mode particles form during dilution cooling in the exhaust ‚Äî invisible to the eye but regulated. DPF captures these with >99% efficiency.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sound Pressure Level from a CI Engine

A KTU 14-mark question often asks you to derive or apply the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** formula from a measured RMS pressure. We work through it below.

**Given:**
- Measured RMS sound pressure at 1 m from a CI engine: $p_{rms} = 0.4$ Pa.
- Reference pressure: $p_0 = 20 \,\mu Pa = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Recall the definition of SPL in decibels.**
By international standard (IEC 61672), SPL is the logarithmic ratio of the measured RMS sound pressure to the reference sound pressure, multiplied by 20 (because sound intensity is proportional to $p^2$).

$$L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB re 20 ¬µPa]}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute the numerical values.**

$$L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.4}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Evaluate the ratio inside the logarithm.**

$$\frac{0.4}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.4}{0.00002} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-1}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 2 \times 10^{4} = 20{,}000$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the base-10 logarithm.**

$$\log_{10}(20{,}000) = \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{4}) = \log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10^4) = 0.3010 + 4 = 4.3010$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Multiply by 20.**

$$L_p = 20 \times 4.3010 = 86.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Apply A-weighting (for human ear response).**
The A-weighting correction at 1 kHz is 0 dB. At 500 Hz, it's $-3$ dB; at 100 Hz, it's $-19.1$ dB. For a broadband diesel engine dominated by low frequencies, the correction is typically **$-3$ to $-5$ dB**.

$$L_{A} \approx 86.02 - 3.0 = 83.02 \text{ dB(A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a typical idle-level noise for a small CI engine. A 10 dB increase corresponds to a **perceived doubling of loudness** for the human ear ‚Äî so even a 3 dB drop is a meaningful noise reduction target for OEMs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: NOx Mass Emission Index from a 4-Cylinder CI Engine

**Given:**
- 4-cylinder, 4-stroke CI engine, displacement $V_d = 2.0$ L, rated power $P_b = 110$ kW at 4000 rpm.
- Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) = 0.220 kg/kWh.
- Measured exhaust NOx concentration (dry, corrected) = 850 ppm.
- Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for diesel $\approx 14.6$; but CI uses **diffusion combustion** with overall $\lambda \approx 1.3$ to 1.7.
- Molar mass of dry exhaust $\bar{M}_{exh} \approx 28.97$ g/mol (close to air).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute fuel mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m}_f = BSFC \times P_b = 0.220 \times 110 = 24.2 \text{ kg/h} = \frac{24.2}{3600} \text{ kg/s} = 6.722 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the air mass flow rate (using $\lambda = 1.4$ as a typical value for CI).**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_f \times (AFR)_{stoich} \times \lambda = 6.722 \times 10^{-3} \times 14.6 \times 1.4$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 6.722 \times 10^{-3} \times 20.44 = 0.1374 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute total exhaust mass flow rate (fuel + air, accounting for combustion stoichiometry).**
For a hydrocarbon $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{23}$ (typical diesel surrogate), the burned gas mass ‚âà air mass + fuel mass (oxygen mass returned as CO‚ÇÇ/H‚ÇÇO).

$$\dot{m}_{exh} \approx \dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_f = 0.1374 + 0.006722 = 0.1441 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Convert ppm (molar) to mass fraction of NOx.**
NOx is dominated by NO (M = 30 g/mol). The conversion from volumetric ppm to mass fraction:

$$y_{NO_x} = \frac{\text{ppm} \times M_{NO_x}}{10^6 \times \bar{M}_{exh}} = \frac{850 \times 30}{10^6 \times 28.97}$$

$$y_{NO_x} = \frac{25{,}500}{28.97 \times 10^6} = 8.80 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{(kg NOx / kg exhaust)}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the mass flow rate of NOx.**

$$\dot{m}_{NO_x} = y_{NO_x} \times \dot{m}_{exh} = 8.80 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1441 = 1.268 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the Emission Index (EI) of NOx in g/kg fuel.**

$$EI_{NO_x} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NO_x}}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{1.268 \times 10^{-4}}{6.722 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01887 \text{ kg/kg-fuel}$$

$$EI_{NO_x} \approx 18.87 \text{ g/kg-fuel}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Convert to BSNOx (Brake Specific NOx) for comparison with regulations.**

$$BSNO_x = \frac{\dot{m}_{NO_x}}{P_b} = \frac{1.268 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{110} = 4.15 \text{ g/kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A BSNOx of ~4 g/kWh is typical for an un-controlled CI engine. Modern BS-VI with SCR targets < 0.5 g/kWh ‚Äî an **8√ó reduction**. The achievement comes from EGR + SCR + DPF combined, not from a single silver bullet.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Zeldovich NOx Formation Rate (Simplified)

For a KTU conceptual 7-mark question, you can be asked to derive the exponential temperature dependence of NOx.

**Starting point ‚Äî Arrhenius form of the first Zeldovich reaction:**

$$k_1 = A_1 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,1}}{R\,T}\right)$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Net rate of NO formation is rate-limited by the dissociation of N‚ÇÇ:**

$$\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [\text{O}_2][\text{N}_2] \quad \text{(steady-state, [N] assumed quasi-steady)}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Recognize that [O‚ÇÇ] itself is temperature-dependent through dissociation equilibrium:**

$$[\text{O}_2] \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,O_2}}{R\,T}\right)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Combine into a single Arrhenius expression:**

$$\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,1} + E_{a,O_2}}{R\,T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,eff}}{R\,T}\right)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Define the effective activation energy $E_{a,eff} \approx 320$ kJ/mol (experimentally validated).**

**Step 5 ‚Äî Apply a small temperature perturbation. If $T$ increases by $\Delta T$:**

$$\frac{\dot{n}_{NO}(T+\Delta T)}{\dot{n}_{NO}(T)} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_{a,eff}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T+\Delta T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî For $T = 2000$ K and $\Delta T = 100$ K:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T+\Delta T} = \frac{1}{2000} - \frac{1}{2100} = \frac{100}{2000 \times 2100} = 2.381 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_{a,eff}}{R} = \frac{320{,}000}{8.314} = 38{,}490 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{Exponent} = 38{,}490 \times 2.381 \times 10^{-5} = 0.9164$$

$$\text{Ratio} = e^{0.9164} = 2.50$$

**Conclusion:** A **100 K rise** in peak flame temperature **multiplies the NO formation rate by ~2.5√ó**. This is why every 10 K reduction in EGT via EGR translates into meaningful NOx reduction in calibration.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Emission Control Strategies vs. Pollutants

| Control Strategy | Targeted Pollutant | Mechanism | Trade-Off / Side Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) | **NOx** (‚Üì 30‚Äì60%) | Lowers peak T, displaces O‚ÇÇ | ‚Üë PM, ‚Üë HC, ‚Üë CO (at high EGR rates) |
| High-pressure Common Rail (HPCR) | **PM, HC, CO** (‚Üì 20‚Äì40%) | Finer atomization, better air-fuel mixing | Marginal ‚Üë NOx if not paired with EGR |
| Multiple Injection (Pilot + Main + After) | **PM and Noise** (‚Üì) | Pilot reduces ignition delay; after burns soot | Complexity, ‚Üë HC if over-piloted |
| Variable Geometry Turbocharger (VGT) | **PM** (‚Üì) | Maintains optimal Œª across RPM | Mechanical complexity, cost |
| DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) | **PM** (‚Üì >99%) | Wall-flow ceramic traps soot; periodically regenerates | Needs active management; fuel penalty for regen |
| DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst) | **HC, CO, soluble organics** | Catalytic oxidation | Cannot reduce NOx alone |
| SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) | **NOx** (‚Üì 80‚Äì95%) | AdBlue (urea) ‚Üí NH‚ÇÉ reduces NOx to N‚ÇÇ + H‚ÇÇO | Needs urea tank, dosing control |
| LNT / NSC (Lean NOx Trap) | **NOx** (‚Üì 70‚Äì90%) | NOx stored as nitrate during lean; reduced during rich pulse | Sulfur poisoning, complex control |
| Intake Air Throttling | **NOx** (mild) | Reduces pumping, but in CI throttling is rare | ‚Üì performance, ‚Üë PM |
| Combustion Chamber Optimization | **Noise + PM** | Bowl geometry promotes swirl, fuel-air mixing | Design-locked; cannot be tuned online |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture: CI Engine Noise and Emission Subsystems

The diagram below maps how the noise and emission phenomena are sensed, controlled, and mitigated in a modern Common Rail Diesel powertrain.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Air Intake] --> B[VGT Turbocharger]
    B --> C[Charge Air Cooler]
    C --> D[EGR Cooler]
    D --> E[Intake Manifold]
    E --> F[CI Combustion Chamber]
    G[CRDi High Pressure Pump] --> H[Rail Pressure Sensor]
    H --> I[Electronic Injector ECU Driver]
    I --> J[Solenoid Piezo Injector]
    J --> F
    F --> K[Exhaust Manifold]
    K --> L[Turbine of VGT]
    L --> B
    K --> M[EGT Sensor]
    M --> N[ECU]
    K --> O[DOC Diesel Oxidation Catalyst]
    O --> P[DPF Diesel Particulate Filter]
    P --> Q[NOx Sensor Upstream]
    Q --> R[SCR Module with AdBlue Injector]
    R --> S[NOx Sensor Downstream]
    S --> T[Tailpipe]
    U[Engine Mount Accelerometer] --> V[Noise Vibration Harness NVH Controller]
    V --> N
    W[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> N
    X[Coolant Temperature Sensor] --> N
    Y[MAF MAP Sensors] --> N
    N --> I
    N --> D
    N --> R
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **air path** (A ‚Üí F) delivers charge through VGT ‚Üí CAC ‚Üí EGR ‚Üí manifold, all of which influence combustion temperature and thus NOx.
- The **fuel path** (G ‚Üí J) is fully electronically controlled; injection timing and pattern are the primary noise/PM levers.
- The **exhaust after-treatment chain** (O ‚Üí P ‚Üí R) is the "second line of defense" ‚Äî it cleans what combustion could not.
- The **ECU** (N) is the central brain, fusing all sensor inputs (EGT, NOx, rail pressure, MAF, MAP, crank position, coolant T) and commanding injector events, EGR valve position, VGT vanes, and AdBlue dosing.
- The **NVH controller** (V) uses accelerometer feedback to characterize combustion irregularity and trigger re-calibration of pilot injection quantity.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow: Zeldovich NOx Formation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[In-cylinder Air N2 and O2] --> B[High Temperature Greater than 1800 K]
    B --> C[O Radical Attacks N2]
    C --> D[Reaction 1: N2 + O rightarrow NO + N]
    D --> E[Reaction 2: N + O2 rightarrow NO + O]
    E --> F[Reaction 3: N + OH rightarrow NO + H]
    F --> G[NO Accumulated in Combustion Products]
    G --> H[Frozen by Rapid Quench in Expansion Stroke]
    H --> I[NOx Emitted via Tailpipe]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart is the **exact chemical pathway** the ECU's NOx model emulates. Real-time NOx estimation (called **virtual sensor** or **NOx model-based control**) is a major research area in production ECUs ‚Äî Bosch, Continental, and Denso all ship in-cylinder NOx estimators that avoid the cost of a physical NOx sensor on the upstream side.

---

### 4.3 Subsystem Mapping: Sources of CI Engine Noise

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CI Engine Noise] --> B[Combustion Noise 60 to 70 percent]
    A --> C[Mechanical Noise 20 to 30 percent]
    A --> D[Aerodynamic Noise 5 to 10 percent]
    A --> E[Structure Borne Noise]
    B --> B1[Rapid Premixed Burn]
    B --> B2[Diesel Knock]
    B --> B3[High Rate of Pressure Rise dp by dtheta]
    C --> C1[Piston Slap]
    C --> C2[Valve Train Impact]
    C --> C3[Bearing Rattle]
    C --> C4[Timing Chain]
    D --> D1[Intake Manifold Pulsation]
    D --> D2[Exhaust Pulse]
    D --> D3[Turbocharger Whistle]
    E --> E1[Engine Block Vibration]
    E --> E2[Chassis Radiation]
    E --> E3[Hydraulic Mount Damping]
```
