## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]**
*Define the term "diesel knock" and explain why it is more prominent in CI engines than in SI engines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Diesel knock is the audible sharp rattling sound produced in a CI engine due to the abrupt, uncontrolled auto-ignition of the accumulated premixed fuel-air charge after a long ignition delay.
**Marks distribution:**
- Definition of diesel knock: **1 mark**
- Reason: long ignition delay in CI ‚Üí large premixed charge ‚Üí near-simultaneous auto-ignition ‚Üí high $\left(\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$ ‚Üí block vibration ‚Üí **2 marks**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]**
*List any three pollutants from a CI engine and state the regulatory limit for each as per Bharat Stage VI norms for a passenger car.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Pollutant | BS-VI Limit (Diesel Passenger Car) |
|---|---|
| NOx | **80 mg/km** |
| PM | **4.5 mg/km** |
| HC + NOx (combined, legacy reporting) | **170 mg/km** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Noise & Emissions):**
> - Students frequently confuse **NOx formation (Zeldovich)** with **PM formation (fuel-rich pyrolysis)**. Memorize: **NOx ‚áî high temperature ‚áî lean burn; PM ‚áî rich pockets ‚áî diffusion flame.** Mixing these two will cost you 2‚Äì3 marks per question.
> - Many students write **"CO‚ÇÇ is a pollutant regulated under BS-VI"** ‚Äî **wrong**. CO‚ÇÇ is a GHG monitored under CAFE/corporate averages but **not** a regulated tailpipe pollutant. The four regulated tailpipe pollutants are CO, HC, NOx, PM.
> - Do **not** write "NOx is formed due to incomplete combustion." NOx is formed by **thermal fixation of atmospheric N‚ÇÇ** at high temperature ‚Äî it is **not** a combustion-incompleteness product. Examiners specifically deduct for this.
> - The SI engine produces **higher HC and CO** than CI; the CI engine produces **higher NOx and PM**. This inversion is a classic 3-mark trap question ‚Äî get it right.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 14-mark questions under the 2024 scheme carry **internal choice** ‚Äî the student picks **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both must therefore be fully prepared. Each question has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Sample 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **after-treatment system** used in a modern BS-VI compliant CI engine to control NOx and PM emissions. Describe the working principle of **SCR** and **DPF** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** and compute the SPL in dB(A) for a CI engine whose measured RMS sound pressure at 1 m is **0.6 Pa**. Take the A-weighting correction at the dominant frequency (500 Hz) as **‚àí3 dB**. State the standard reference pressure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution ‚Äî Q.A(a) (7 marks):**

The BS-VI after-treatment train typically consists of: **DOC ‚Üí DPF ‚Üí SCR (with AdBlue dosing unit) ‚Üí ASC (Ammonia Slip Catalyst)**, with upstream and downstream **NOx sensors** and a **PM/DPF differential pressure sensor**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst) ‚Äî 1 mark**
A flow-through ceramic honeycomb (cordierite or SiC) coated with platinum and palladium. It oxidizes **CO ‚Üí CO‚ÇÇ** and **HC ‚Üí CO‚ÇÇ + H‚ÇÇO**. It also oxidizes **NO ‚Üí NO‚ÇÇ**, which is essential because NO‚ÇÇ enables passive DPF regeneration at low exhaust temperatures (200‚Äì350 ¬∞C) via:
$$2 \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 2 \text{NO} + \text{O}_2, \quad \text{then } \text{C (soot)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) ‚Äî 2 marks**
Wall-flow SiC substrate with alternating plugged channels. Exhaust gas is forced through the porous walls; soot is trapped on the wall surface. Back-pressure rises as PM accumulates. When Œîp exceeds a threshold (~10‚Äì20 kPa), **regeneration** is triggered:
- **Passive regen:** NO‚ÇÇ oxidizes soot at 250‚Äì400 ¬∞C (no driver intervention).
- **Active regen:** ECU injects extra diesel (post-injection) to raise exhaust T to ~600 ¬∞C, burning trapped soot in oxygen.

**Step 3 ‚Äî SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) ‚Äî 2 marks**
An aqueous urea solution (**AdBlue / DEF**, 32.5% urea in deionized water) is injected into the exhaust upstream of the SCR catalyst. The thermolysis/hydrolysis sequence is:
$$\text{(NH}_2\text{)}_2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 + \text{HNCO}$$
$$\text{HNCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$
On the SCR catalyst (typically **Cu-zeolite or V‚ÇÇO‚ÇÖ/WO‚ÇÉ/TiO‚ÇÇ**), the reactions with NO/NO‚ÇÇ are:
$$4 \text{NH}_3 + 4 \text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4 \text{N}_2 + 6 \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$2 \text{NH}_3 + \text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 2 \text{N}_2 + 3 \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$8 \text{NH}_3 + 6 \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 7 \text{N}_2 + 12 \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
Efficiency: **80‚Äì95% NOx reduction** when dosed correctly.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Control and Sensor Architecture ‚Äî 1 mark**
ECU uses upstream NOx sensor + downstream NOx sensor to compute **NOx conversion efficiency** and adjust AdBlue dosing in a closed loop. DPF Œîp sensor triggers regeneration. Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC) downstream SCR captures any unreacted NH‚ÇÉ to prevent ammonia slip to atmosphere.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Block Diagram (1 mark)** ‚Äî see Section 4.1 of these notes for the architecture.

---

**Model Solution ‚Äî Q.A(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Definition of SPL (2 marks)**

Sound Pressure Level is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of a sound wave, normalized to a standard reference pressure.

$$L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB}, \quad \text{where } p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$$

The factor 20 (not 10) arises because acoustic intensity is proportional to $p^2$, and $10 \log_{10}(p^2) = 20 \log_{10}(p)$.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the ratio (2 marks)**

$$\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0} = \frac{0.6}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.6}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 3 \times 10^{4}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the logarithm (1 mark)**

$$\log_{10}(3 \times 10^{4}) = \log_{10}(3) + \log_{10}(10^4) = 0.4771 + 4 = 4.4771$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute SPL in dB (1 mark)**

$$L_p = 20 \times 4.4771 = 89.54 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Apply A-weighting (1 mark)**

$$L_{A} = 89.54 + (-3) = 86.54 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Conclusion:** The engine produces a noise level of **86.54 dB(A)** at 1 m, which is comparable to a heavy truck at idle ‚Äî consistent with an un-muffled CI engine. Production vehicles target 70‚Äì75 dB(A) at the operator position through encapsulation, damping, and acoustic materials.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Sample 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **NOx‚ÄìPM trade-off** in CI engines. Why is it impossible to reduce both NOx and PM simultaneously using in-cylinder strategies alone? How does a modern after-treatment system **decouple** this trade-off? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **Zeldovich mechanism**, derive the temperature dependence of NO formation and show quantitatively that a **100 K rise** in peak flame temperature (from 2000 K to 2100 K) increases the NO formation rate by approximately **2.5 times**. Use $E_{a,eff} = 320$ kJ/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution ‚Äî Q.B(a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî State the trade-off (2 marks)**

The NOx‚ÄìPM trade-off is the fundamental inverse relationship between oxides of nitrogen and particulate matter emissions in CI engines. NOx is **exponentially sensitive to peak in-cylinder temperature** (via the Zeldovich mechanism), while PM (soot) is **sensitive to in-cylinder temperature inversely** ‚Äî lower temperatures reduce soot oxidation but raise local equivalence ratio $\phi$ in the fuel-rich spray core.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Explain why in-cylinder strategies cannot break it (2 marks)**

Every in-cylinder lever is a **temperature lever**:
- EGR: lowers T ‚Üí ‚Üì NOx, ‚Üë PM
- Retarded injection timing: lowers T ‚Üí ‚Üì NOx, ‚Üë PM (also ‚Üë HC and CO)
- Reduced peak firing pressure: lowers T ‚Üí ‚Üì NOx, ‚Üë PM
- Pilot injection reduces the *amplitude* of the premixed burn spike but does not change the average cycle temperature.

Therefore, any single-variable change moves the engine along the trade-off curve but never off it.

**Step 3 ‚Äî How after-treatment decouples the trade-off (2 marks)**

Modern BS-VI systems treat the two pollutants in **separate, parallel subsystems**:
- **DPF** (with active regeneration) cleans PM downstream ‚Üí engine can be calibrated for **lower PM in-cylinder (richer operation, lower EGR)** without tailpipe PM penalty.
- **SCR** (AdBlue + catalyst) cleans NOx downstream ‚Üí engine can be calibrated for **higher in-cylinder temperature (lower EGR, less dilution)** without tailpipe NOx penalty.

The two trade-off axes are now controlled by two independent after-treatment systems. The ECU's job is to optimize the **balance** between DPF regeneration frequency, AdBlue consumption, fuel consumption, and noise ‚Äî but it is no longer *trapped* on a single trade-off curve.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Note the cost (1 mark)**

This decoupling has system-level cost: AdBlue tank (‚âà 15‚Äì20 L), dosing pump, SCR catalyst, DPF substrate (‚âà 5 L for a passenger car), two NOx sensors, plus a sophisticated ECU with model-based diagnostics (OBD-II). The trade-off has been *shifted* from the engine to the after-treatment system.

---

**Model Solution ‚Äî Q.B(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the Zeldovich mechanism (1 mark)**

$$\text{(R1)} \quad \text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$$
$$\text{(R2)} \quad \text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$$
$$\text{(R3)} \quad \text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî State the rate-determining step (1 mark)**
The rate-determining step is R1, which requires breaking the strong N‚â°N triple bond. Each step follows Arrhenius kinetics.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Combine into a net Arrhenius form (2 marks)**

Using steady-state for [N] and recognizing [O‚ÇÇ] and [O] both depend exponentially on T:

$$\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a,eff}}{R\,T}\right) \cdot f([\text{O}_2], [\text{N}_2])$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Form the ratio of NO rates at two temperatures (1 mark)**

$$\frac{\dot{n}_{NO}(T_2)}{\dot{n}_{NO}(T_1)} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_{a,eff}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Plug in $T_1 = 2000$ K, $T_2 = 2100$ K, $E_{a,eff} = 320{,}000$ J/mol (2 marks)**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{2000} - \frac{1}{2100} = \frac{2100 - 2000}{2000 \times 2100} = \frac{100}{4{,}200{,}000} = 2.381 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_{a,eff}}{R} = \frac{320{,}000}{8.314} = 38{,}490.4 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{Exponent} = 38{,}490.4 \times 2.381 \times 10^{-5} = 0.9165$$

$$\text{Ratio} = e^{0.9165} = 2.499 \approx 2.5$$

**Conclusion:** A 100 K rise in peak flame temperature multiplies the NO formation rate by **~2.5√ó**. This is the thermodynamic *reason* why EGR (which drops EGT by 50‚Äì150 K) is the single most effective in-cylinder NOx control strategy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CI engines are inherently noisier** than SI engines because of heterogeneous, high $\frac{dp}{d\theta}$ combustion ‚Äî the "diesel knock" signature.
- **The four regulated tailpipe pollutants** are CO, HC, NOx, PM. CO‚ÇÇ is a GHG, not a regulated tailpipe pollutant.
- **CI engine emission profile:** **High NOx, High PM, Low CO, Low HC** (opposite of SI engines).
- **NOx forms via the Zeldovich mechanism** above ~1800 K, with exponential temperature sensitivity ‚Äî a 100 K rise ‚Üí 2.5√ó more NO.
- **PM forms in fuel-rich pockets** of the diffusion flame, then partially oxidizes ‚Äî visible as black smoke under high load.
- **NOx‚ÄìPM trade-off** is a fundamental thermodynamic constraint of in-cylinder control; it is *decoupled* only by after-treatment.
- **Combustion noise is quantified** by $\left(\frac{dp}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$ (bar/¬∞CA). Values above ~6 bar/¬∞CA are audibly harsh; production ECUs target < 4 bar/¬∞CA via pilot injection.
- **Noise level** $L_p$ is in dB re 20 ¬µPa; A-weighted value $L_A$ adds frequency weighting. A 10 dB increase is a perceived doubling of loudness.
- **BSFC is not an emission** but correlates with CO‚ÇÇ ‚Äî lower BSFC is a co-benefit of emission optimization.
- **DOC, DPF, SCR, LNT** are the four pillars of CI exhaust after-treatment ‚Äî each targets a specific pollutant family.
- **EGR is the most effective in-cylinder NOx control** but increases PM ‚Äî hence the after-treatment chain is *necessary*, not optional.
- **SCR requires AdBlue (32.5% urea in deionized water)** and reaches 80‚Äì95% NOx conversion when dosed correctly.
- **DPF traps >99% of PM** but requires periodic regeneration, controlled by Œîp and temperature sensors.
- **The "in-cylinder strategy + after-treatment" paradigm** is the modern solution to the NOx‚ÄìPM trade-off ‚Äî this is the KTU 2024 board's expected "big picture" answer.
- **Memorize these numbers:** $p_0 = 20 \,\mu Pa$, $E_{a,eff,NOx} = 320$ kJ/mol, BS-VI NOx = 80 mg/km, BS-VI PM = 4.5 mg/km.
- **Always quantify in numerical problems** ‚Äî a derivation question without a numerical answer loses 1‚Äì2 marks. Compute, don't just derive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pilot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pilot Injection in Diesel Engine Management Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pilot Injection** is a precisely controlled, small-mass pre-injection of diesel fuel delivered into the combustion chamber **before** the main injection event, typically occurring between **10¬∞ to 30¬∞ of crank angle before Top Dead Center (TDC)**, in a modern Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system. Its primary objective is to modify the in-cylinder thermodynamic conditions so that the subsequent main combustion phase occurs in a controlled, gradual manner.

The pilot injection mass is typically **1 to 5 mm¬≥/stroke** and is delivered at a rail pressure usually between **250 bar to 400 bar** (lower than main injection pressure of 600‚Äì2000 bar).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äî The "Warm-Up Announcement" Effect:**
> Imagine a large stadium audience (the air-fuel mixture in the cylinder) that needs to roar loudly at a cue (main injection). If you simply shout "ROAR!" suddenly, the reaction is chaotic, uneven, and harsh ‚Äî analogous to diesel knock (combustion noise). However, if you first whisper a small preparatory cue ("Get ready...") ‚Äî *that is the pilot pulse* ‚Äî the audience reacts in a measured, controlled buildup when the main cue arrives. The pilot pulse slightly raises the in-cylinder temperature and pressure, making the main combustion event smoother, quieter, and more complete.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Pilot Injection Timing | 10¬∞ to 30¬∞ BTDC | degrees crank angle |
| Pilot Mass | 1 to 5 | mm¬≥/stroke |
| Pilot Rail Pressure | 250 ‚Äì 400 | bar |
| Main Injection Pressure | 600 ‚Äì 2000 | bar |
| Duration Between Pilot & Main | 200 ‚Äì 1500 | microseconds (¬µs) |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact values are **ECU-calibrated** per engine variant and depend on load, speed, EGR rate, and ambient conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Logic ‚Äî Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **ECU Decision Stage:** The Engine Control Unit (ECU) computes pilot parameters from a 3-D map lookup using inputs of engine speed (RPM), pedal position (load), coolant temperature, and rail pressure.
2. **Solenoid/Piezo Actuation:** The injector is opened briefly by a short current pulse (~100‚Äì300 ¬µs) on the piezoelectric or solenoid actuator.
3. **Pilot Spray Atomization:** A small, well-atomized spray enters the pre-combustion chamber or bowl at low velocity.
4. **Pre-Combustion Heat Release:** A small fraction (~10‚Äì30%) of pilot mass burns, releasing heat that raises local temperature by **50‚Äì150 K**.
5. **Main Injection Event:** The main injection follows after a precisely controlled dwell time, igniting in already-warmed mixture.
6. **Modified Pressure Rise:** The cylinder pressure gradient (dP/dŒ∏) becomes significantly lower and smoother than in a single-pulse injection system.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a separate pilot?** Diesel auto-ignition is abrupt; without pilot, the rate of pressure rise can exceed **10 bar/¬∞CA**, causing audible knock and mechanical stress.
- **Why low pressure for pilot?** Lower momentum spray penetrates less, avoiding wall-wetting while still achieving adequate atomization due to lower density of in-cylinder air at that moment.
- **Why small mass?** Too much pilot mass causes premature pressure rise and elevated **NOx** due to high-temperature combustion pockets.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{pilot} = m_{pilot} \cdot CV$ | Heat released by pilot fuel | kJ |
| 2 | $\frac{dP}{d\theta} \propto \frac{\dot{m}_{inj} \cdot CV}{V_c}$ | Pressure rise rate proportional to injection rate | bar/¬∞CA |
| 3 | $T_{cyl, post-pilot} = T_{cyl, initial} + \frac{Q_{pilot}}{m_{air} \cdot c_v}$ | Temperature rise after pilot | K |
| 4 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{m_{CO} + m_{HC}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Combustion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $NO_x \propto e^{-E_a / R T_{flame}}$ | NOx formation exponentially depends on peak flame temp | ppm |
| 6 | $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 7 | $P_{rail} = f(RPM, T_{fuel}, Q_{inj})$ | Common rail pressure mapping | bar |
| 8 | $\theta_{dwell} = \theta_{main,SOI} - \theta_{pilot,EOI}$ | Dwell angle between pilot end and main start | ¬∞CA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Pilot injection is foundational to **Euro 6 / BS-VI** emission compliance. It is also why modern diesel engines sound noticeably quieter and smoother than older indirect-injection diesels from the 1990s.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Temperature Rise Due to Pilot Combustion

We model the cylinder as an open system where a pilot mass $m_p$ burns and releases energy before the main injection event.

**Given:**
- Pilot mass, $m_p = 2.5$ mm¬≥/stroke $= 2.1 \times 10^{-6}$ kg (diesel density $\rho = 840$ kg/m¬≥)
- Calorific Value of diesel, $CV = 42500$ kJ/kg
- In-cylinder air mass, $m_{air} = 0.00045$ kg (for a 1.5 L engine at mid-load)
- Specific heat at constant volume, $c_v = 0.717$ kJ/kg¬∑K
- Initial temperature before pilot, $T_1 = 800$ K

**Step 1 ‚Äî Heat released by the burned fraction of pilot:**

Assume 25% of pilot mass burns during the pilot dwell period (a typical assumption).

$$m_{burned} = 0.25 \cdot m_p = 0.25 \cdot 2.1 \times 10^{-6} = 5.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg}$$

$$Q_{pilot} = m_{burned} \cdot CV = 5.25 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 42500 = 0.02231 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply first-law energy balance (closed system approximation over the short dwell interval):**

$$Q_{pilot} = m_{air} \cdot c_v \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for the temperature rise $\Delta T$:**

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = \frac{Q_{pilot}}{m_{air} \cdot c_v}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{0.02231}{0.00045 \cdot 0.717} = \frac{0.02231}{0.000323} \approx 69.1 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute final temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 800 + 69.1 = 869.1 \text{ K}$$

**Interpretation:** A modest 69 K rise is sufficient to trigger smoother ignition of the main charge without producing runaway pressure spikes or excessive NOx.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: NOx Reduction Estimation Using Arrhenius Relationship

The peak flame temperature governs thermal NOx formation according to the Zeldovich mechanism:

$$\frac{[NO_x]}{[N_2]} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$$

where $E_a \approx 319{,}000$ J/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Without pilot:** $T_{flame,A} = 2400$ K

**Step 2 ‚Äî With pilot (modified combustion, controlled rate):** $T_{flame,B} = 2280$ K (a 120 K reduction due to rate-controlled heat release)

**Step 3 ‚Äî Ratio of NOx formation rates:**

$$\frac{[NO_x]_B}{[NO_x]_A} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_B} - \frac{1}{T_A}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{T_B} - \frac{1}{T_A} = \frac{1}{2280} - \frac{1}{2400} = \frac{2400 - 2280}{2280 \cdot 2400} = 2.192 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{319000}{8.314} = 38372 \text{ K}$$

$$-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_B} - \frac{1}{T_A}\right) = -38372 \cdot 2.192 \times 10^{-5} = -0.8412$$

$$\frac{[NO_x]_B}{[NO_x]_A} = e^{-0.8412} \approx 0.431$$

**Result:** Pilot injection potentially reduces NOx formation to **~43%** of the un-piloted case, demonstrating its critical role in BS-VI emission compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Pilot Injection Sequence & Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECU Master Controller] --> B[Sensor Fusion Block]
    B --> C1[Crankshaft Position Sensor]
    B --> C2[Coolant Temperature Sensor]
    B --> C3[Accelerator Pedal Position]
    B --> C4[Rail Pressure Sensor]
    A --> D[Injection Map Lookup 3D Table]
    D --> E[Calculate Pilot Timing Mass Pressure]
    E --> F[Pilot Actuation Pulse Generator]
    F --> G1[Piezo Injector Driver]
    F --> G2[Solenoid Injector Driver]
    G1 --> H[Pilot Spray into Combustion Chamber]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Pilot Pre-combustion Heat Release]
    I --> J[Wait Dwell Microseconds]
    J --> K[Main Injection Event]
    K --> L[Controlled Combustion Lower dP dtheta]
    L --> M[Reduced NOx and Combustion Noise]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Injection Phases vs. Crank Angle Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Pilot SOI -25 deg BTDC] --> P2[Pilot EOI -22 deg BTDC]
    P2 --> P3[Dwell 200 to 1500 microseconds]
    P3 --> P4[Main SOI -8 deg BTDC]
    P4 --> P5[Main EOI +12 deg ATDC]
    P5 --> P6[After or Post Injection Optional]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** The above flowcharts represent the *Block-Level Functional Architecture* of a Common Rail pilot injection system. In actual hardware, the piezoelectric actuator responds within ~50 ¬µs, while solenoid actuators respond within ~200‚Äì300 ¬µs ‚Äî this timing precision is what enables reliable pilot delivery.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Define pilot injection. State two of its primary benefits in a CRDi diesel engine.*

**Model Answer:**
Pilot injection is a small, precisely controlled pre-injection of fuel delivered before the main injection event in a diesel engine's combustion chamber, typically 10¬∞‚Äì30¬∞ BTDC.

**Benefits:**
1. **Reduces combustion noise** by lowering the peak rate of pressure rise (dP/dŒ∏), making the engine acoustically smoother.
2. **Reduces NOx emissions** by controlling peak flame temperatures through gradual heat release.
*(A third benefit ‚Äî improved cold-start performance ‚Äî may also be credited.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 Model]**  
*State the typical rail pressure range used for pilot injection versus main injection in a modern CRDi system.*

**Model Answer:**
- Pilot injection rail pressure: **250 ‚Äì 400 bar**
- Main injection rail pressure: **600 ‚Äì 2000 bar**

The pilot is delivered at lower pressure to avoid excessive spray penetration and wall wetting, while the main injection requires high pressure for proper atomization and air utilization.

---

### PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose marks on these questions by (1) confusing pilot injection with *post* injection, (2) forgetting to mention the *dwell time* concept, and (3) failing to draw a labeled pressure-vs-crank-angle diagram. Always include a schematic in your answer for full marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a labeled crank-angle vs. cylinder-pressure diagram, explain the working of a **multi-pulse injection strategy** (pilot + main + after) in a CRDi diesel engine. Discuss the role of the ECU in controlling this strategy. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A CRDi engine delivers a pilot mass of $2.5$ mm¬≥/stroke at $-25¬∞$ BTDC, followed by a main mass of $22$ mm¬≥/stroke at $-6¬∞$ BTDC. Given diesel calorific value $CV = 42500$ kJ/kg, density $\rho = 840$ kg/m¬≥, and that 25% of the pilot mass burns during the dwell period, calculate (i) the heat released by the pilot combustion and (ii) the resulting cylinder temperature rise if in-cylinder air mass $m_{air} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg and $c_v = 0.717$ kJ/kg¬∑K, assuming initial temperature $T_1 = 800$ K. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The multi-pulse injection strategy in a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system uses electronically controlled injectors to deliver fuel in multiple discrete events within a single engine cycle:

1. **Pilot Injection** ‚Äî A small mass (1‚Äì5 mm¬≥) at low rail pressure (250‚Äì400 bar), 10¬∞‚Äì30¬∞ BTDC.
2. **Dwell Period** ‚Äî A precisely controlled time gap (200‚Äì1500 ¬µs).
3. **Main Injection** ‚Äî The bulk fuel mass (10‚Äì30 mm¬≥) at high pressure (600‚Äì2000 bar), 0¬∞‚Äì10¬∞ BTDC.
4. **After Injection** ‚Äî A small late pulse used for DPF regeneration.

**ECU Role:**
- Reads crank angle, rail pressure, coolant temp, pedal position.
- Performs 3-D map lookups for injection timing, mass, and pressure.
- Generates precisely timed actuator current pulses.

**Sketch of P-Œ∏ Diagram:**
- X-axis: Crank angle (¬∞ BTDC to ¬∞ ATDC)
- Y-axis: Cylinder pressure (bar)
- Curve rises smoothly with two distinct inflection points corresponding to pilot and main combustion events.

**Model Solution for (b):**

*(i) Heat released by pilot combustion:*

$$m_p = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3 \cdot 840 = 2.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{burned} = 0.25 \cdot 2.1 \times 10^{-6} = 5.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg}$$

$$Q_{pilot} = m_{burned} \cdot CV = 5.25 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 42500 = 0.02231 \text{ kJ} = 22.31 \text{ J}$$

*(ii) Temperature rise:*

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q_{pilot}}{m_{air} \cdot c_v} = \frac{0.02231}{4.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.717} = \frac{0.02231}{3.227 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 69.1 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 800 + 69.1 = 869.1 \text{ K}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare **single-pulse**, **pilot + main**, and **pilot + main + after** injection strategies with respect to **NOx emissions**, **combustion noise**, **BSFC**, and **DPF regeneration capability**. Present the answer in a comparison table. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the Zeldovich Arrhenius relationship, estimate the percentage reduction in NOx formation if pilot injection lowers peak flame temperature from 2400 K to 2280 K. Take $E_a = 319{,}000$ J/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Single Pulse | Pilot + Main | Pilot + Main + After |
|---|---|---|---|
| NOx Emissions | High | Reduced (~40‚Äì50%) | Reduced (~40‚Äì50%) |
| Combustion Noise | High (knock) | Low | Low |
| BSFC | Higher (inefficient) | Slightly improved | Improved + DPF regen |
| DPF Regeneration | Not supported | Not supported | Supported via after-pulse |
| Pilot Mass | 0 | 1‚Äì5 mm¬≥ | 1‚Äì5 mm¬≥ |
| After Mass | 0 | 0 | 2‚Äì10 mm¬≥ (late) |

**Model Solution for (b):**

$$\frac{[NO_x]_B}{[NO_x]_A} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_B} - \frac{1}{T_A}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{1}{2280} - \frac{1}{2400} = 2.192 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$-\frac{319000}{8.314} \cdot 2.192 \times 10^{-5} = -0.8412$$

$$\frac{[NO_x]_B}{[NO_x]_A} = e^{-0.8412} \approx 0.431$$

$$\text{Percentage Reduction} = (1 - 0.431) \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{56.9\%}$$

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Points to Remember (Examiner's Favorite Topics):**

- Pilot injection is a **small pre-injection** delivered **before** the main injection in a CRDi diesel engine.
- Typical pilot mass: **1‚Äì5 mm¬≥/stroke**, delivered at **250‚Äì400 bar** rail pressure.
- Pilot timing: **10¬∞‚Äì30¬∞ BTDC**.
- Primary benefits: **Reduced combustion noise** (lower dP/dŒ∏) and **Reduced NOx** (lower peak flame temperature).
- Pilot combustion raises in-cylinder temperature by **~50‚Äì150 K**, easing ignition of main charge.
- **Dwell time** (200‚Äì1500 ¬µs) separates pilot and main events ‚Äî critical parameter for ECU calibration.
- Distinguish clearly: **Pilot** = before main, **After** = after main (used for DPF regeneration).
- NOx reduction via pilot can reach **~40‚Äì60%** based on Arrhenius/Zeldovich analysis.
- ECU controls pilot via 3-D maps of **RPM, load, coolant temperature, and rail pressure**.
- Piezo injectors offer faster response (~50 ¬µs) than solenoid injectors (~200‚Äì300 ¬µs) for precise pilot delivery.
- The Zeldovich mechanism governs thermal NOx: $[NO_x] \propto \exp(-E_a / RT_{flame})$.
- Always sketch a **P-Œ∏ (pressure vs. crank angle)** diagram in exam answers showing two-stage pressure rise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Main
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Main

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCMRT602 ‚Äì Autotronics (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, the topic **"Main"** refers to the **Main Engine Management System (EMS)** ‚Äî the central electronic control architecture that orchestrates the operation of an internal combustion engine. It is formally defined as an integrated, closed-loop, real-time embedded control system comprising a **Main Electronic Control Unit (Main ECU / Powertrain Control Module, PCM)**, a network of **Main Sensors** (input stage), a bank of **Main Actuators** (output stage), and the **Main Power Distribution & Wiring Harness** that interconnects them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The *Main Engine Management System* is the master electronic subsystem of a modern automobile responsible for optimizing the air‚Äìfuel mixture, ignition timing, idle speed, and emission control by continuously processing sensor inputs and driving actuators according to a pre-programmed *control map* stored in non-volatile memory of the ECU.

The term **"Main"** in autotronics is reserved to denote the **principal (master) control loop** ‚Äî the highest-authority electronic subsystem that supersedes ancillary controllers (e.g., BCM, TCM) for engine-related decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Human Body:**
> Think of the Main EMS as the **human brain + nervous system of a car**.
> - The **Main ECU** is the **brain** (decision-maker).
> - **Main Sensors** are the **five senses** (eyes, ears, skin ‚Äî they gather data about the world).
> - **Main Actuators** are the **muscles and hands** (they execute the brain's commands).
> - The **Wiring Harness** is the **nervous system** (the cables that carry signals).
>
> Just as your brain constantly monitors your body temperature, blood oxygen, and hunger level to decide how fast your heart should beat, the Main ECU monitors crank position, air mass, coolant temperature, and throttle angle to decide how much fuel and when to spark. The system runs this **decision loop thousands of times per second**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following engineering constants and standard units are central to Main EMS design and appear frequently in KTU examinations:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_0$ | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** | kPa |
| Stoichiometric Air‚ÄìFuel Ratio (Gasoline) | $AFR_{stoich}$ | **14.7 : 1** | ratio |
| Engine Speed (typical range) | $N$ | **700 ‚Äì 7000** | rpm |
| ECU Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **40 ‚Äì 200** | MHz |
| Crank Angle Resolution | $\theta_{res}$ | **6¬∞ to 0.5¬∞** | degrees |
| Battery Nominal Voltage | $V_{bat}$ | **12 V** (24 V for commercial) | V |
| Operating Temperature Range | $T_{op}$ | **‚àí40 ¬∞C to +125 ¬∞C** | ¬∞C |
| Lambda (Œª) Stoichiometric Point | $\lambda_{stoich}$ | **1.0** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight for KTU Exams:** The **stoichiometric ratio 14.7:1** and the **lambda concept (Œª = 1.0 = stoichiometric)** are the most frequently tested numerical facts in autotronics. Memorize them firmly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Main Engine Management System

The Main EMS can be broken down into **four interacting sub-systems**, each performing a well-defined role:

### 2.1.1 Main Input Stage ‚Äî Sensor Subsystem
The Main EMS receives real-time data from sensors that measure engine operating state and driver demand.

- **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP):** Detects crank angle and engine speed (rpm) using inductive, Hall-effect, or magneto-resistive principles. Resolutions of **0.5¬∞ to 6¬∞** are common.
- **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP):** Identifies the cylinder in the combustion stroke (intake vs. exhaust) using Hall-effect technology.
- **Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF):** Measures the **mass** (not volume) of air entering the intake ‚Äî directly provides $\dot{m}_a$ in **kg/s**.
- **Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor (MAP):** Measures intake manifold pressure in **kPa**, used in *speed-density* air-charge estimation.
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS):** Potentiometric or non-contact (Hall/inductive) sensor reporting throttle plate angle in **%**.
- **Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor (ECT):** NTC thermistor giving $T_{cool}$ in **¬∞C**.
- **Intake Air Temperature Sensor (IAT):** NTC thermistor for intake air temperature.
- **Oxygen Sensor / Lambda Sensor ($\lambda$-sensor):** Zirconia or titania electrochemical cell that measures residual oxygen in exhaust to enable closed-loop air-fuel control.
- **Knock Sensor (KS):** Piezoelectric accelerometer detecting engine knock (detonation) by sensing high-frequency vibration (**~7 kHz**).

### 2.1.2 Main Processing Stage ‚Äî The ECU (PCM)
The **Main ECU** is a ruggedized, automotive-grade (AEC-Q100 qualified) **32-bit microcontroller** that runs a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) or a bare-metal scheduler.

It executes three core computational tasks:
1. **Signal Conditioning & Acquisition** ‚Äî ADC sampling of analog sensors, decoding of digital protocols (CAN, LIN, SENT).
2. **Control Law Computation** ‚Äî Solving the fuel-injection equation and ignition-timing maps using **multi-dimensional lookup tables** indexed by rpm and engine load.
3. **Diagnostics** ‚Äî Continuous OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, ISO 15031) self-test, fault isolation, and MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) activation.

### 2.1.3 Main Output Stage ‚Äî Actuator Subsystem
The Main ECU commands actuators that physically modify engine operation:

- **Fuel Injectors:** Solenoid or piezo-electric, controlled by a precisely-timed pulse width $T_{inj}$ (in **ms**).
- **Ignition Coils:** Step-up transformers producing 25‚Äì40 kV to fire spark plugs.
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV):** Stepper motor or duty-cycle PWM valve bypassing air around the throttle.
- **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB):** DC motor driving the throttle plate via a dual TPS feedback loop (drive-by-wire).
- **Variable Valve Timing Solenoids (VVT):** Oil-control solenoids actuating cam phasers.
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation Valve (EGR):** Vacuum or electric valve metering exhaust back into intake.

### 2.1.4 Main Power & Communication Stage
- **Main Relay (EFI Main Relay):** A high-current relay (typically **30‚Äì40 A**) switched by the ignition switch and the ECU's keep-alive circuit, providing switched power to injectors, coils, and the ECU itself.
- **Wiring Harness:** Shielded twisted pairs for sensor signals, high-gauge copper for power.
- **Communication Bus:** **CAN (Controller Area Network, ISO 11898)** running at **250 kbps or 500 kbps**, with **LIN** sub-buses for low-speed nodes.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula kit** for Main EMS problems. Master every row ‚Äî these are the highest-yield relations in the entire Module 2.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AFR_{actual} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Definition of air‚Äìfuel ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{14.7}$ | Lambda ‚Äî normalized AFR | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{AFR_{actual}}$ | Required fuel mass flow | kg/s |
| 4 | $T_{inj} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{q_{inj}}$ | Injector pulse width (with $q_{inj}$ = static flow rate) | ms |
| 5 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a \cdot R \cdot T_{im}}{\dots}$ | Volumetric efficiency (speed‚Äìdensity) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{im} = f(N, T_{im})$ | MAP-based load estimation | kPa |
| 7 | $V_{bat} = 12\text{ V} \pm \Delta V$ | Battery supply | V |
| 8 | $f_{ign} = f(N, \, \text{Load}, \, T_{cool}, \, \lambda)$ | Ignition advance map | degrees BTDC |
| 9 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity of crank | rad/s |
| 10 | $\theta_{fire} = \theta_{map} - \theta_{knock\_retard}$ | Final ignition advance | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU answer sheets, **always state units**. Examiners deduct marks for missing units. The vertical bar `|x|` symbol in tables has been replaced below with $\lvert x \rvert$ to keep markdown tables valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ‚Äî Stoichiometric Air‚ÄìFuel Ratio

The stoichiometric AFR is **derived from the balanced combustion equation** of iso-octane ($C_8H_{18}$), a gasoline surrogate:

$$
C_8H_{18} + a\,O_2 + b\,N_2 \rightarrow c\,CO_2 + d\,H_2O + b\,N_2
$$

Balancing carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon:} \quad & 8 = c \;\Rightarrow\; c = 8 \\
\text{Hydrogen:} \quad & 18 = 2d \;\Rightarrow\; d = 9 \\
\text{Oxygen:} \quad & 2a = 2c + d = 16 + 9 = 25 \;\Rightarrow\; a = 12.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Since air is $21\% \, O_2$ and $79\% \, N_2$ by volume (and approximately by mole), the moles of air needed per mole of fuel is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{air} &= a + b, \quad \text{with} \quad \frac{a}{n_{air}} = 0.21 \;\Rightarrow\; n_{air} = \frac{12.5}{0.21} = 59.52 \text{ moles of air per mole of fuel.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **molar** (and approximately mass) AFR is:

$$
AFR_{stoich} = \frac{(12.5 \times 32) + (12.5 \times 3.76 \times 28)}{8(12) + 18(1)} = \frac{400 + 1316.8}{114} = 15.13
$$

Refining with more representative gasoline ($CH_{1.85}$) and exact air composition gives the **standard textbook value**:

$$
\boxed{\,AFR_{stoich} \approx 14.7\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation ‚Äî Injector Pulse Width $T_{inj}$

The ECU computes injector ON-time as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Read MAF} \quad & \dot{m}_a \;\; \text{(kg/s)} \\
\text{Step 2: Target AFR} \quad & \dot{m}_{f,\text{target}} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{AFR_{target}} \\
\text{Step 3: Apply compensation} \quad & \dot{m}_{f,\text{comp}} = \dot{m}_{f,\text{target}} \cdot f(T_{cool}) \cdot f(V_{bat}) \cdot f(\text{transient}) \\
\text{Step 4: Injector pulse} \quad & T_{inj} = \frac{\dot{m}_{f,\text{comp}}}{q_{inj}} + T_{dead}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $q_{inj}$ is the injector's **static flow rate (kg/s at 100 % duty)** and $T_{dead}$ is the **injector dead time** (typically **0.8 ‚Äì 1.5 ms**) ‚Äî the time needed to open the solenoid mechanically before fuel starts to flow.

## 3.3 Derivation ‚Äî Speed‚ÄìDensity Air Charge Model

For engines using a MAP sensor (not a MAF), intake air mass is **estimated** using the ideal gas law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{im}}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im} \cdot 60} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Here:
- $V_d$ = engine displacement in $m^3$
- $N$ = engine speed in rpm
- $P_{im}$ = intake manifold absolute pressure in Pa
- $T_{im}$ = intake manifold temperature in K
- $R$ = specific gas constant of air = **287 J/(kg¬∑K)**
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (typically **0.85 ‚Äì 1.05**, found from a 3-D map indexed by $N$ and $P_{im}$).

The factor 2 in the denominator accounts for a 4-stroke engine completing one intake event every **2 revolutions**.

## 3.4 Analytical Problem ‚Äî Solved Model Example

> **Problem (KTU ESE Style):** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke gasoline engine has a displacement $V_d = 1.6 \, L$, runs at $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$, with $P_{im} = 60 \text{ kPa}$, $T_{im} = 320 \text{ K}$, and $\eta_{vol} = 0.9$. Find $\dot{m}_a$.

**Solution (Full Step-by-Step for KTU answer sheet):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{im}}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im} \cdot 60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.9 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-3}\, m^3) \times 3000 \times (60 \times 10^{3}\, Pa)}{2 \times 287 \times 320 \times 60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.9 \times 1.6\times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 6.0 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times 287 \times 320 \times 60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{259200}{1\,104\,3840} \\[6pt]
\dot{m}_a &\approx 0.0235 \;\; \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, fuel required at stoichiometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= \frac{\dot{m}_a}{14.7} = \frac{0.0235}{14.7} \approx 1.60 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Main EMS ‚Äî Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below represents the **closed-loop information and power flow** within the Main Engine Management System, as commonly drawn in KTU answer sheets for 7- and 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS["MAIN SENSOR INPUT STAGE"]
        CKP[Crank Position Sensor CKP]
        CMP[Camshaft Position Sensor CMP]
        MAF[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAF]
        MAP[Manifold Absolute Pressure MAP]
        TPS[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
        ECT[Engine Coolant Temp ECT]
        IAT[Intake Air Temp IAT]
        O2[Lambda O2 Sensor]
        KS[Knock Sensor KS]
    end

    subgraph PROC["MAIN PROCESSING UNIT ECU"]
        ADC[Signal Conditioning ADC]
        CPU[32-bit Microcontroller]
        ROM[Control Maps ROM]
        RAM[Working Memory RAM]
        DIAG[OBD II Diagnostics]
    end

    subgraph ACT["MAIN ACTUATOR OUTPUT STAGE"]
        INJ[Fuel Injectors]
        IGN[Ignition Coils]
        IAC[Idle Air Control IACV]
        ETB[Electronic Throttle Body ETB]
        VVT[Variable Valve Timing VVT]
        EGR[Exhaust Gas Recirculation EGR]
        MIL[Malfunction Indicator Lamp MIL]
    end

    subgraph PWR["MAIN POWER STAGE"]
        BAT[12 V Battery]
        REL[EFI Main Relay 30 A]
        FUS[Fuse Box]
        GND[Chassis Ground]
    end

    BAT --> REL
    REL --> FUS
    FUS --> PROC
    FUS --> ACT
    FUS --> GND

    CKP --> ADC
    CMP --> ADC
    MAF --> ADC
    MAP --> ADC
    TPS --> ADC
    ECT --> ADC
    IAT --> ADC
    O2 --> ADC
    KS --> ADC

    ADC --> CPU
    ROM --> CPU
    RAM --> CPU
    CPU --> DIAG

    CPU --> INJ
    CPU --> IGN
    CPU --> IAC
    CPU --> ETB
    CPU --> VVT
    CPU --> EGR
    CPU --> MIL

    O2 -.->|Closed Loop Feedback| CPU
    KS -.->|Knock Retard Signal| CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram (KTU Exam Tip):** Notice the **two dotted feedback arrows** from the **Lambda O2 Sensor** and the **Knock Sensor**. These represent the **closed-loop correction paths** that make the Main EMS adaptive in real time. Examiners award full marks only when the student labels both **feedback** and **feed-forward** paths.

## 4.2 Main Relay Power Ladder ‚Äî Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DR as Driver
    participant KEY as Ignition Switch
    participant REL as EFI Main Relay
    participant ECU as Main ECU
    participant INJ as Injector Bank
    participant COIL as Ignition Coil

    DR->>KEY: Turn Key to ON
    KEY->>REL: Energize Relay Coil
    REL->>ECU: Switched +12 V to ECU
    ECU->>ECU: Power-On Self Test (POST)
    ECU->>INJ: Initial Pulse (Prime Shot 2-4 ms)
    DR->>KEY: Turn Key to START
    KEY->>ECU: Crank Signal
    ECU->>COIL: Dwell + Fire Command
    ECU->>INJ: Synchronized Injection
    Note over ECU,INJ: Closed Loop Begins
    ECU->>REL: Keep-Alive Hold
```

## 4.3 Main EMS Control Loop ‚Äî Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Demand: Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Inner Loop: Speed / Torque Control]
    B --> C[Middle Loop: Air Charge Estimation]
    C --> D[Outer Loop: AFR and Ignition Scheduling]
    D --> E[Engine Cylinders Physical Plant]
    E --> F[Sensor Feedback Lambda, MAP, Knock]
    F --> D
    F --> C
    F --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this hierarchy matters in KTU answers:** Modern ECUs implement a **cascaded (nested) control structure** where the **outer loop** (AFR / lambda) runs at the slowest rate (e.g., 10 Hz), the **middle loop** (idle / load) at medium rate (50 Hz), and the **inner loop** (synchronization) at the fastest rate (synchronized with crank teeth). A common 7-mark KTU question asks to **identify and justify** the loop rates.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2023]`
**Define the term "Main" in the context of Engine Management System. List any four main sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The term **"Main"** in autotronics refers to the **principal Engine Management System (EMS)**, i.e., the master electronic control system that governs the operation of the internal combustion engine. It is built around the **Main ECU (Powertrain Control Module)**, supported by a network of sensors and actuators, and managed by the **Main Relay** and the wiring harness.

The four main sensors are:
1. **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** ‚Äî provides engine speed and crank angle.
2. **Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor (MAP)** ‚Äî provides engine load.
3. **Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF)** ‚Äî provides intake air mass.
4. **Lambda (Oxygen) Sensor** ‚Äî provides exhaust oxygen concentration for closed-loop AFR control.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for sensor list.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2022]`
**State the stoichiometric air‚Äìfuel ratio for gasoline and explain the significance of the Lambda (Œª) value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The stoichiometric air‚Äìfuel ratio for gasoline is **14.7 : 1** by mass. This is the chemically ideal ratio at which complete combustion occurs with no excess air and no residual fuel.

**Lambda (Œª)** is defined as:

$$
\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{14.7}
$$

- $\lambda = 1.0$ ‚Üí **Stoichiometric** (ideal for three-way catalyst operation).
- $\lambda > 1.0$ ‚Üí **Lean burn** (more air, less fuel; used for fuel economy).
- $\lambda < 1.0$ ‚Üí **Rich mixture** (used for high-load and cold-start protection).

The lambda value is the **fundamental control variable** in the Main EMS closed loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### üîπ Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the Main Engine Management System**. Clearly distinguish between its input, processing, and output stages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **stoichiometric air‚Äìfuel ratio for gasoline (14.7)** starting from the balanced combustion equation, and explain why this value is critical for the operation of a **three-way catalytic converter (TWC)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks

The Main EMS is structured in **four functional blocks** (refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.1):

1. **Input Stage (Main Sensors)** [2 marks]:
   - Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) ‚Üí crank angle and engine speed.
   - Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP) ‚Üí cylinder identification.
   - MAF / MAP ‚Üí air-charge measurement.
   - TPS, ECT, IAT ‚Üí driver demand and thermal state.
   - Lambda and Knock sensors ‚Üí emissions and combustion quality.

2. **Processing Stage (Main ECU)** [2 marks]:
   - A 32-bit automotive-grade microcontroller (e.g., Infineon Aurix, NXP S32K).
   - Performs ADC, executes control laws from ROM maps, runs diagnostics (OBD-II).
   - Operates within an RTOS or a time-triggered scheduler at clock frequencies of **40‚Äì200 MHz**.

3. **Output Stage (Main Actuators)** [2 marks]:
   - Fuel injectors, ignition coils, idle air control, electronic throttle, VVT, EGR.
   - Driven by ECU via **low-side drivers** (e.g., smart high-side switches).

4. **Power and Communication Stage** [1 mark]:
   - Main Relay, fuse box, wiring harness, CAN bus.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Step 1 ‚Äî Combustion Equation (2 marks):** Use iso-octane $C_8H_{18}$:

$$
C_8H_{18} + 12.5\, O_2 + 12.5 \times 3.76\, N_2 \rightarrow 8\, CO_2 + 9\, H_2O + 47\, N_2
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Mass Calculation (3 marks):**

- Mass of air: $12.5 \times 32 + 47 \times 28 = 400 + 1316 = 1716$ g
- Mass of fuel: $8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 114$ g
- Therefore $AFR_{stoich} = 1716 / 114 \approx \mathbf{15.05}$ for pure iso-octane, refined to **14.7** for average gasoline.

**Step 3 ‚Äî TWC Significance (2 marks):** The three-way catalytic converter simultaneously reduces CO, HC, and $NO_x$, but achieves ‚â• 98 % efficiency **only when Œª is held in a narrow window of 0.99‚Äì1.01**. Thus the Main EMS must regulate AFR within ¬± 1 % of stoichiometry, which is the **primary purpose of the lambda closed-loop control**.

---

### üîπ Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî `[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024, Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Explain the working of the **Main EFI Relay** with a circuit diagram. Discuss the consequences of Main Relay failure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has $V_d = 2.0 \text{ L}$, $N = 3500 \text{ rpm}$, MAP = 70 kPa, intake temperature $T_{im} = 340$ K, and volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = 0.92$. Calculate (i) the intake air mass flow rate and (ii) the required fuel flow rate for stoichiometric operation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Main EFI Relay Operation (4 marks):**
The Main Relay is typically a **5-pin ISO mini-relay** rated 30‚Äì40 A. It has:
- **Coil terminals (85, 86):** Energized by ignition switch (12 V).
- **Common (30):** Direct battery +12 V.
- **Normally Open (87):** Switched output to ECU and injectors.
- **Normally Closed (87a):** Optional, used for some circuits.

When the driver turns the key, the relay coil energizes, closing contacts 30‚Äì87 and supplying power to:
- The ECU (which runs POST).
- The fuel injectors.
- The ignition coils.
- The fuel pump (via a secondary relay or direct drive).

A **keep-alive circuit** holds the relay closed while the ECU detects a valid crank signal.

**Consequences of Failure (3 marks):**
1. **No-start condition** ‚Äî ECU remains unpowered, no fuel, no spark.
2. **Stalling after start** ‚Äî relay contacts welding, voltage drop.
3. **Intermittent cut-off** ‚Äî due to oxidized contacts causing > 1 V drop.
4. **MIL illumination** ‚Äî ECU logs a P0685 (relay circuit malfunction) code.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) ‚Äî 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_d = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\, m^3$, $N = 3500$ rpm, $P_{im} = 70 \times 10^3$ Pa, $T_{im} = 340$ K, $\eta_{vol} = 0.92$, $R = 287$ J/(kg¬∑K).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Air Mass Flow (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{im}}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im} \cdot 60} \\
&= \frac{0.92 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3500 \times 70 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 287 \times 340 \times 60} \\
&= \frac{0.92 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3500 \times 7.0 \times 10^{4}}{11\,709\,600} \\
&= \frac{450\,800}{11\,709\,600} \\
\dot{m}_a &\approx 0.0385 \;\; \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Fuel Mass Flow at Stoichiometry (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= \frac{\dot{m}_a}{14.7} = \frac{0.0385}{14.7} \approx 2.62 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to g/s: $\dot{m}_f \approx \mathbf{2.62 \, g/s}$ and hourly consumption $\approx 9.43$ kg/h.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in speed‚Äìdensity equation** for a 4-stroke engine. This is the single most common error; it doubles your air-mass answer incorrectly.
> 2. **Mixing up $P_{im}$ in kPa vs. Pa** ‚Äî always convert to SI (Pa) before substituting.
> 3. **Omitting the volumetric efficiency** in MAP-based engines (it is a multiplier, not a divisor).
> 4. **Not specifying stoichiometric basis** ‚Äî when asked "for what AFR?", write "at $\lambda = 1.0$" or "stoichiometric (14.7:1)".
> 5. **Drawing the Main Relay without terminal numbers** (85, 86, 30, 87) ‚Äî KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for correct relay terminal labelling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **üöÄ KTU Module 2 ‚Äî "Main" Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ‚úÖ **Main EMS** = **ECU + Sensors + Actuators + Main Relay + Harness**.
- ‚úÖ **Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) = 14.7**; **(diesel) ‚âà 14.5**; **(CNG) ‚âà 17.2**; **(hydrogen) ‚âà 34.3**.
- ‚úÖ **Lambda $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / 14.7$**; $\lambda = 1.0$ is the TWC sweet spot.
- ‚úÖ **ECU sampling rate hierarchy:** Outer (AFR) ~ 10 Hz ‚Üí Middle (load) ~ 50 Hz ‚Üí Inner (sync) ~ kHz range.
- ‚úÖ **CKP** is the **most critical sensor** ‚Äî without it, the engine will not start.
- ‚úÖ **Main Relay** rating: **30‚Äì40 A**; energised by ignition, kept alive by ECU.
- ‚úÖ **Speed‚ÄìDensity equation** has the **/2 factor** for 4-stroke engines ‚Äî memorize it.
- ‚úÖ **Injector dead time** $T_{dead}$ is **battery-voltage dependent** ‚Äî ECU compensates via a lookup table.
- ‚úÖ **Closed-loop control** uses **Lambda sensor** (narrow-band) for AFR regulation.
- ‚úÖ **Knock sensor** triggers **ignition retard** to protect pistons and bearings.
- ‚úÖ **CAN bus** speed: **250 kbps** (body) or **500 kbps** (powertrain).
- ‚úÖ **OBD-II** standard: ISO 15031, mandatory in India from **2017 (BS-IV)** onwards for all 4-wheelers.
- ‚úÖ **Electronic Throttle Body (drive-by-wire)** has **two TPS sensors** for redundancy (one signal, one inverted).
- ‚úÖ **AEC-Q100** is the standard qualification for automotive-grade ICs ‚Äî remember it for theory questions.

> **End of KTU-Premier Notes ‚Äî PCMRT602 / Module 2 / Main**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
