## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between an *active* and a *semi-active* suspension system. Give one OEM example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî KTU marking scheme):**

- **Active Suspension (1.5 marks):** A system in which an external energy source (hydraulic pump or electric motor) applies a *force* between the sprung and unsprung mass to actively control body motion. The actuator can both *push* and *pull*, generating forces independent of the relative velocity. **Example:** Mercedes-Benz **ABC (Active Body Control)** or Infiniti **HCC (Hydraulic Body Control)**.

- **Semi-Active Suspension (1 mark):** A system that *only* varies the **damping coefficient** $C(t)$ of the damper; it cannot store or release energy and cannot apply a net force. It works on the *dissipation* principle only. **Example:** Cadillac **Magnetic Ride Control** using MR fluid dampers, or Toyota **TEMS**.

- **Key distinction (0.5 mark):** Active systems require high power (~2‚Äì10 kW pump) and add weight; semi-active systems consume <100 W and are lighter, cheaper, and almost as effective for body control.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2.** What is **sky-hook damping control**? Write the damper force expression and explain why it is preferred for body-control.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Sky-hook damping is a control strategy in which the damper force is made proportional to the *absolute* vertical velocity of the sprung mass $\dot{x}_s$, as if the body were suspended from a fixed reference ("the sky").

- **Damper force expression (1 mark):** $F_{damper}^{sky} = -C_{sky} \cdot \dot{x}_s$ (note: the *relative* velocity term is replaced by absolute velocity).

- **Reason for preference (1 mark):** The sky-hook controller suppresses the body's *absolute* bounce irrespective of road input frequency. It improves ride comfort by **40‚Äì60 %** over passive suspension (per literature) and is the basis for almost all production semi-active systems. It also ensures that the damper is always in the "correct sign" ‚Äî i.e., it dissipates energy regardless of whether the suspension is in compression or rebound.

---

### Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE ‚Äî Attempt ANY ONE of the following two questions.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Full Working-Type Question*

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Sample Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With a neat schematic, derive the **two-DOF quarter-car equations of motion** for a vehicle suspension system. Clearly define all symbols. Write the equations in state-space matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ identifying each element of the $\mathbf{A}$ matrix.

**(b)** [7 marks] For the **sky-hook damping control** strategy, derive the expression for the **effective damping ratio** $\zeta_{eff}$ of the sprung mass. A passenger car has $m_s = 300 \text{ kg}$, $K = 25000 \text{ N/m}$, and a passive damping coefficient $C = 1000 \text{ N¬∑s/m}$ (giving $\zeta = 0.183$). If the ECU commands a sky-hook damping $C_{sky} = 4500 \text{ N¬∑s/m}$, compute the new effective damping ratio. Comment on the resulting ride.

---

**Model Solution (Part a ‚Äî 7 marks):**

*Schematic (1 mark):* The schematic must show $m_s$ on top, $m_u$ below, with spring $K$ and damper $C$ in parallel between them, tyre spring $K_t$ below $m_u$ going to the road $x_r$, and road input $x_r$ at the bottom.

*Derivation (5 marks):* See the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key EOMs:

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s + C(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) + K(x_s - x_u) = 0 \quad \text{(sprung)}$$

$$m_u \ddot{x}_u - C(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u) - K(x_s - x_u) + K_t(x_u - x_r) = 0 \quad \text{(unsprung)}$$

*State-space matrices (1 mark):* The $\mathbf{A}$ matrix as derived in Section 3.1.

---

**Model Solution (Part b ‚Äî 7 marks):**

*Derivation (3 marks):* Starting from the single-DOF body EOM with sky-hook:

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s + C(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_r) + C_{sky} \dot{x}_s + K x_s = 0$$

$$m_s \ddot{x}_s + (C + C_{sky}) \dot{x}_s - C \dot{x}_r + K x_s = 0$$

Comparing with $m_s \ddot{x}_s + C_{eff} \dot{x}_s + K x_s = f(t)$:

$$C_{eff} = C + C_{sky}, \quad \zeta_{eff} = \frac{C_{eff}}{2\sqrt{K m_s}}$$

*Numerical calculation (3 marks):*

$$2\sqrt{K m_s} = 2\sqrt{25000 \times 300} = 2\sqrt{7{,}500{,}000} = 2 \times 2738.6 = 5477.2 \text{ N¬∑s/m}$$

$$C_{eff} = C + C_{sky} = 1000 + 4500 = 5500 \text{ N¬∑s/m}$$

$$\zeta_{eff} = \frac{5500}{5477.2} \approx 1.004$$

*Comment (1 mark):* Since $\zeta_{eff} \approx 1.0$, the body is now *critically damped* ‚Äî i.e., the body returns to its equilibrium position without any overshoot after a bump. This is a dramatic improvement over the passive $\zeta = 0.183$ (which is *severely underdamped* ‚Äî body would oscillate 4‚Äì5 times before settling). The ride is luxurious and flat, with no perceptible secondary bounce. The driver, however, may feel a *firmer* initial jolt because the higher damping transmits more high-frequency road texture.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **working principle of Toyota TEMS (Toyota Electronic Modulated Suspension)** with a block diagram. List the input sensors used and the two operating modes ("Sport" and "Normal").

**(b)** [7 marks] Compare **MR (Magneto-Rheological) dampers** and **electromechanical (linear motor) actuators** for active suspension. Which is more suitable for a battery-electric vehicle (BEV) and why?

---

**Model Solution (Part a ‚Äî 7 marks):**

*Working principle (3 marks):* TEMS was introduced in 1983 on the Toyota Soarer/Celica. It uses a central ECU that reads wheel-speed, steering angle, brake, throttle, and shock absorber acceleration sensors. Based on the *mode* selected by the driver (Normal or Sport), the ECU energises a **solenoid valve** in each damper that bypasses oil flow through a secondary orifice. In **Normal mode**, the solenoid is energised ‚Üí valve opens ‚Üí more oil flow ‚Üí **soft** damping. In **Sport mode**, the solenoid is de-energised ‚Üí valve closes ‚Üí restricted flow ‚Üí **firm** damping. The switching time is <30 ms.

*Block diagram (2 marks):* Must show: Sensors ‚Üí TEMS ECU (with mode switch) ‚Üí 4 Solenoid Valves ‚Üí 4 Dampers ‚Üí Body.

*Sensors and modes (2 marks):* Sensors = wheel speed, steering angle, brake switch, shock absorber piston position. Modes = **Normal** (soft, comfort-oriented) and **Sport** (firm, handling-oriented); an *Auto* mode in later TEMS versions switches automatically based on lateral g.

---

**Model Solution (Part b ‚Äî 7 marks):**

*MR Damper (3.5 marks):*
- Working: Iron-particle (micron-scale) fluid inside the damper piston; a magnetic coil around the piston rod sets the field strength.
- Field strength ‚Üë ‚Üí viscosity ‚Üë ‚Üí damping ‚Üë (response time ~5 ms).
- Power: ~12 V, 1‚Äì2 A per damper; **<30 W** per damper.
- Pros: Compact, low power, proven, no high-pressure hydraulics.
- Cons: Cannot *add* energy, only dissipate (semi-active only).

*Electromechanical (EM) Linear Actuator (3.5 marks):*
- Working: A three-phase linear motor is mounted coaxially with the damper strut; the rotor moves up and down.
- Can both **dissipate and inject** energy (true active suspension).
- Power: 1‚Äì3 kW peak per actuator; **400 V** bus needed.
- Pros: Full active control, can lift body over bumps, regenerative potential.
- Cons: Heavy, expensive, demands powerful inverter and battery, EMI issues.

*Conclusion for BEV:* **EM actuators are more suitable for high-end BEVs** (e.g., the **Bose active suspension** used in some prototype EVs) because BEVs already have a high-voltage battery and powerful inverter, so the added cost/weight penalty is amortised. MR dampers are preferred for **mainstream BEVs** (e.g., Tesla Model S Plaid uses similar adaptive dampers) because of their lower cost, lower power draw, and proven reliability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** (losing 1 mark per occurrence): The damper force must be $-C(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$, *not* $+C(\dot{x}_s - \dot{x}_u)$. Always verify direction.
> 2. **Sky-hook vs. Ground-hook confusion** (loses 2‚Äì3 marks): Sky-hook uses *absolute* $\dot{x}_s$; ground-hook uses *wheel* velocity. KTU explicitly tests this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the unsprung mass EOM** (loses 2 marks): A 2-DOF quarter-car has *two* equations, not one. Many students write only the sprung-mass equation.
> 4. **No units in numerical answers** (loses 0.5 mark per occurrence): Always state units (N, m/s¬≤, Hz).
> 5. **Missing the $F_a$ term** in active suspension derivations (loses 1 mark): If the question says "active", the actuator force must appear in *both* equations.
> 6. **Stating "active suspension is always better"** (partial credit only): Examiners want the *trade-offs* (cost, weight, power consumption, failure modes) ‚Äî discuss both sides.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Cheat Sheet ‚Äî Electronic Suspension System**

- ‚òê **Three types** to memorize: Passive, Semi-active, Active. Semi-active = variable damping only; Active = force actuator.
- ‚òê **Two EOMs of quarter-car:** Sprung mass + unsprung mass. Always both.
- ‚òê **Sky-hook:** $F = -C_{sky}\dot{x}_s$ ‚Äî absolute velocity of body. **Most-asked control strategy.**
- ‚òê **Ground-hook:** $F = -C_{grd}\dot{x}_u$ ‚Äî wheel velocity. Less common, used in ride-height control.
- ‚òê **Natural frequency formula:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/m}$, $f_n$ in Hz is $\omega_n/2\pi$.
- ‚òê **Damping ratio formula:** $\zeta = C/(2\sqrt{Km})$. Passive target 0.2‚Äì0.4, active target 0.7‚Äì1.0.
- ‚òê **OEM examples:** Toyota **TEMS**, Mercedes **ABC**, Audi **Adaptive Air Suspension**, Cadillac **Magnetic Ride Control (MR)**, Infiniti **HCC**, Citro√´n **Hydractive**.
- ‚òê **MR fluid** changes viscosity under magnetic field; 5 ms response; <30 W per damper.
- ‚òê **Sensors to list:** wheel speed, steering angle, lateral/longitudinal G, ride height, brake pressure, vertical accel on damper.
- ‚òê **Actuator types:** Electrohydraulic, Electromechanical (linear motor), MR damper, Air spring.
- ‚òê **Trade-off always:** Comfort vs. Handling. Passive = compromise; Electronic = dynamic solution.
- ‚òê **CAN/FlexRay** used for ECU communication. Loop time ‚â§ 5 ms.
- ‚òê **Working pressure** of hydraulic active suspension: 150‚Äì200 bar.
- ‚òê **State-space form** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ ‚Äî 4 states, 4√ó4 A matrix. Expected for 14-mark derivations.
- ‚òê **Effective damping ratio with sky-hook:** $\zeta_{eff} = (C_{rel} + C_{sky})/(2\sqrt{Km_s})$.
- ‚òê **Practical issue:** Failure of an active suspension must default to a *safe* passive mode (fail-operational vs. fail-safe) ‚Äî important interview point.
- ‚òê **Recent trend:** *Preview suspension* using forward camera/radar (Audi AI, Mercedes E-Active Body Control on GLE) ‚Äî predicts the road *before* the wheel arrives.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airbags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Airbags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Airbag (Supplementary Restraint System - SRS):** A vehicle safety device comprising a flexible fabric cushion housed in a compact module on the steering wheel, dashboard, door panel, or roof rail, that inflates rapidly with a non-toxic gas (typically Nitrogen, **N‚ÇÇ**) generated by a pyrotechnic inflator when the **Airbag Control Unit (ACU)** detects a crash pulse of sufficient severity above a predefined deceleration threshold (typically **3 g to 5 g** for frontal impact).

In the KTU 2024 Autotronics framework, airbags are classified as a **passive, non-electrically-driven (initially), pyrotechnically-actuated electromechanical safety system** that forms the second line of occupant protection after the seatbelt (which is the primary restraint). The system operates on the principle of **controlled energy absorption** by creating a decelerative buffer between the occupant's body and the vehicle's hard interior surfaces during a crash event.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî "The Pop-up Pillow on a Crash-Cue"**

Imagine you are walking briskly through your house. Suddenly, a giant invisible hand pushes you backward. To avoid slamming into the wall, an enormous soft pillow **magically inflates** in front of you within **one-tenth of a blink of an eye (‚âà 20‚Äì30 ms)**, catches your forward momentum, and slowly deflates to gently lower you to safety. That "magical pillow" is the **airbag**.

The **invisible hand** is the **crash deceleration pulse**, the **pop-up cue** is the **sensor + ECU logic**, the **gas that fills the pillow** is the **pyrotechnically generated Nitrogen**, and the **slowly deflating** action is the **controlled venting** through vent holes that prevents the airbag from acting like a hard rubber ball.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Time** | **20 ‚Äì 30 ms** | Faster than a human blink (‚âà 150 ms) |
| **Full Inflation Pressure** | **~ 5 ‚Äì 8 psi (‚âà 35 ‚Äì 55 kPa)** | Just enough to cushion, not injure |
| **Gas Temperature at Inflation** | **‚âà 400 ‚Äì 600 ¬∞C** (instantaneously) | Cooled by the cushion fabric & gas expansion |
| **Deployment Threshold** | **‚âà 3 ‚Äì 5 g** (frontal), **5 ‚Äì 10 g** (side) | Below this, seatbelt pretensioner only |
| **Inflation Gas Mass** | **‚âà 60 ‚Äì 130 g N‚ÇÇ** per front airbag | Generated by ‚âà 80 ‚Äì 200 g of **NaN‚ÇÉ** |
| **Module Service Life** | **‚âà 15 years** | Recommended replacement interval |
| **Nitrogen Molar Mass** | **M = 28 g/mol** | Used in stoichiometric calculations |

### 1.4 Types of Airbags (KTU 2024 Module 3 Coverage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern vehicles integrate **multiple airbag variants** for holistic occupant protection. KTU expects students to enumerate these with their *location*, *deployment direction*, and *trigger sensor type*.

1. **Frontal (Dual-stage) Airbags** ‚Äî Driver & Front Passenger; crash sensors in the front crumple zone.
2. **Side (Thorax) Airbags** ‚Äî Mounted in seat back or door panel; triggered by side-impact G-sensors or pressure sensors.
3. **Curtain (Head Protection) Airbags** ‚Äî Roof rail mounted; deploy downward to protect the head during rollover or side collision.
4. **Knee Airbags** ‚Äî Lower dashboard deployment; prevent femur/pelvis submarining.
5. **Far-Side / Center Airbags** ‚Äî Between driver and front passenger; prevent head-to-head collision.
6. **External (Pedestrian) Airbags** ‚Äî Mounted at the base of the windshield or hood edge; deploy upward.
7. **Rear Seat Side Airbags** ‚Äî Protect rear occupants in side impacts.
8. **Seatbelt Airbags** ‚Äî Inflatable seatbelts for rear seat and child occupant safety.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ‚Äî Structured Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> The airbag system follows a deterministic **Sense ‚Üí Decide ‚Üí Actuate** pipeline, with safety interlocks, dual-redundant sensors, and **single-event** irreversibility. KTU examiners often test the chronology.

### Phase 1 ‚Äî Sensing (Œît = 0 to ~ 5 ms)
- **Satellite crash sensors** (front bumper & side B-pillar area) measure vehicle deceleration using **MEMS accelerometers** (or legacy ball-in-tube mechanical sensors).
- A **safing sensor** (located inside the ACU) independently verifies the crash event to prevent accidental deployment due to sensor noise or minor impacts.
- **Both** the discrimination sensor AND the safing sensor must register a crash above threshold for the firing decision to be taken. This is the **"AND-gate" logic** in modern ACU firmware.

### Phase 2 ‚Äî Decision (Œît = ~ 5 to ~ 15 ms)
- The **Airbag Control Unit (ACU)**, a dedicated microcontroller (often an **Infineon XC2000** or **Bosch CG912** family), runs a **crash severity algorithm** (jerk-based energy calculation).
- The **Jerk** (rate of change of acceleration: $j = \dfrac{da}{dt}$) is a better crash severity discriminator than acceleration alone, because a *gradual* deceleration (e.g., a soft pothole) will not produce a high jerk value.
- If the algorithm classifies the event as a *fire-worthy crash* and the safing sensor agrees, the ACU fires the **squib** (a thin resistive wire embedded in the inflator) by passing a high current pulse (typically **1.2 ‚Äì 2.0 A for 2 ms**).

### Phase 3 ‚Äî Actuation ‚Äî Gas Generation (Œît = ~ 15 to ~ 30 ms)
- The squib ignites the **booster charge** (a small amount of black powder or boron-potassium nitrate, **B/KNO‚ÇÉ**).
- The booster charge ignites the **main propellant charge**: **sodium azide (NaN‚ÇÉ)** mixed with an oxidizer (typically **CuO** or **Fe‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ**) and a binder.
- The decomposition reaction (see derivation) produces a large volume of **N‚ÇÇ gas** at high temperature, which is filtered and cooled by an **integral stainless-steel wire mesh filter** and directed into the cushion.

### Phase 4 ‚Äî Cushion Deployment (Œît = ~ 20 to ~ 60 ms)
- The cushion (made of **nylon 6,6** or **nylon 6** with a **silicone or neoprene coating**) bursts out of its storage module at speeds up to **200 mph (‚âà 90 m/s)**.
- The vent holes (typically **2 ‚Äì 4 holes, total area 30 ‚Äì 50 cm¬≤**) on the rear of the cushion allow controlled gas escape.

### Phase 5 ‚Äî Deflation & Energy Absorption (Œît = ~ 60 to ~ 200 ms)
- The occupant's forward momentum compresses the cushion, and gas escapes through the vent holes.
- This controlled escape provides a **progressive, non-elastic deceleration** over a longer time, reducing the peak force on the occupant.
- The occupant's body decelerates from a typical crash velocity of **~ 50 km/h (13.9 m/s)** to **0 m/s** in approximately **100 ‚Äì 150 ms**, vs. **~ 20 ms** in an unbelted, airbag-less crash.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU 2024 Autotronics student is expected to recognize for Module 3 (Airbags) questions. Note: **vertical bars use `\mid` to avoid table syntax errors**.

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinematic Deceleration | $a = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{v_{f} - v_{i}}{t}$ | Mean deceleration during crash | $\text{m/s}^{2}$ or $g$ (where $1g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$) |
| 2 | Crash Severity (Jerk) | $j = \dfrac{da}{dt}$ | Discriminator for crash vs. road bump | $g/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | Force on Occupant | $F = m \cdot a$ | Peak force at impact | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | Energy to be Absorbed | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy dissipated by cushion | $\text{J}$ |
| 5 | Impulse‚ÄìMomentum | $F \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v$ | Cushion extends $\Delta t$ to lower $F$ | $\text{N¬∑s}$ |
| 6 | Sodium Azide Decomposition | $2\,\text{NaN}_{3} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na} + 3\,\text{N}_{2}$ | Gas-generating chemistry | ‚Äî |
| 7 | Gas Moles from Propellant | $n_{N_{2}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot n_{\text{NaN}_{3}}$ | Stoichiometric yield | $\text{mol}$ |
| 8 | Gas Mass from Moles | $m_{N_{2}} = n_{N_{2}} \cdot M_{N_{2}}$ | Where $M_{N_{2}} = 28\ \text{g/mol}$ | $\text{g}$ |
| 9 | Ideal Gas Law | $P V = n R T$ | Inflator pressure-volume relation | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| 10 | Cushion Volume (approx.) | $V_{\text{bag}} = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$ (sphere) or tabulated | Geometric volume of inflated cushion | $\text{L}$ |
| 11 | Deceleration in g | $a_{g} = \dfrac{a}{9.81}$ | Sensor threshold in $g$ | $g$ |
| 12 | Energy per mole of NaN‚ÇÉ | $\Delta H \approx -365\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic heat release | $\text{kJ/mol}$ |
| 13 | Deployment Velocity | $v_{\text{deploy}} = \sqrt{2 E / m_{\text{cushion}}}$ | Cushion exit velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 14 | Vent Hole Flow (Bernoulli) | $\dot{m} = C_{d} A_{v} \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Controlled gas escape rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The airbag is one of the most successful automotive safety inventions in history. According to the **NHTSA (U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration)**, frontal airbags reduce driver fatalities in frontal crashes by **‚âà 29%** and have saved **over 50,000 lives** (U.S. data 1987‚Äì2017). For Module 3 of the KTU 2024 Autotronics course, the airbag is the canonical example of:
> - **Sensor fusion** (acceleration + safing sensor AND-gate logic),
> - **Pyrotechnic actuation** (a one-time irreversible chemical actuator),
> - **Real-time embedded control** under hard **deadline constraints** (firing decision must complete in < 10 ms),
> - **System safety engineering** (diagnostic loops, fail-safe behavior, single-fault tolerance).

This system is also a foundational case study for autonomous-vehicle safety, robotics crash protection, and aerospace crew-restraint systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are **four exhaustively worked derivations** that are highly representative of KTU Part B (14-mark) calculation questions. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Crash Deceleration & Occupant Force

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):**
A vehicle traveling at **v = 54 km/h** strikes a rigid barrier. The front crumple zone and airbag together bring the occupant to rest in **Œît = 120 ms**. The occupant mass is **m = 75 kg**. Compute:
(i) Initial velocity in m/s.
(ii) Mean deceleration in m/s¬≤ and in g.
(iii) Force experienced by the occupant.
(iv) Compare this force with and without the airbag (no airbag, Œît = 20 ms).

### Solution:

**Step (i) ‚Äî Convert initial velocity to SI units.**

We know that 1 km/h = 1000 m / 3600 s = 1/3.6 m/s.

$$
v_i = 54\ \text{km/h} = \dfrac{54}{3.6}\ \text{m/s} = 15.0\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step (ii) ‚Äî Compute mean deceleration.**

Using the kinematic relation:

$$
a = \dfrac{v_f - v_i}{\Delta t}
$$

The occupant comes to rest, so $v_f = 0\ \text{m/s}$. With $\Delta t = 120\ \text{ms} = 0.120\ \text{s}$:

$$
a = \dfrac{0 - 15.0}{0.120} = \dfrac{-15.0}{0.120}
$$

$$
a = -125.0\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration. Converting to **g** (where $1\ g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$):

$$
a_{g} = \dfrac{\vert -125.0 \vert}{9.81} = 12.74\ g
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A 12.7 g deceleration is severe but survivable when distributed over the body. The human tolerance limit is roughly **20‚Äì25 g** for short-duration events (< 200 ms).

**Step (iii) ‚Äî Force on the occupant.**

By Newton's second law:

$$
F = m \cdot \vert a \vert = 75\ \text{kg} \times 125.0\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

$$
F = 9375\ \text{N} \approx 9.38\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step (iv) ‚Äî Comparison: Without airbag (Œît = 20 ms).**

$$
a_{\text{no airbag}} = \dfrac{0 - 15.0}{0.020} = -750\ \text{m/s}^{2} = -76.5\ g
$$

$$
F_{\text{no airbag}} = 75 \times 750 = 56{,}250\ \text{N} \approx 56.25\ \text{kN}
$$

$$
\dfrac{F_{\text{no airbag}}}{F_{\text{with airbag}}} = \dfrac{56.25}{9.38} \approx 6.0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The airbag extends the stopping time by a factor of 6, reducing the peak force on the occupant by the same factor. This is the **single most important engineering justification** for airbags and is a KTU-favored conceptual point.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Sodium Azide Chemistry & Gas Generation

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):**
A driver-side airbag inflator contains **m = 130 g** of **sodium azide (NaN‚ÇÉ)**. The reaction is:
$$2\,\text{NaN}_{3}(s) \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na}(s) + 3\,\text{N}_{2}(g)$$
**Molar masses:** NaN‚ÇÉ = 65 g/mol, N‚ÇÇ = 28 g/mol.
Compute:
(i) Moles of NaN‚ÇÉ loaded.
(ii) Moles of N‚ÇÇ generated.
(iii) Mass of N‚ÇÇ produced.
(iv) Volume of N‚ÇÇ at standard temperature (T = 273 K) and pressure (P = 101.325 kPa).

### Solution:

**Step (i) ‚Äî Moles of NaN‚ÇÉ.**

$$
n_{\text{NaN}_{3}} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{130\ \text{g}}{65\ \text{g/mol}} = 2.0\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step (ii) ‚Äî Moles of N‚ÇÇ produced.**

From the balanced equation, the mole ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{n_{N_{2}}}{n_{\text{NaN}_{3}}} = \dfrac{3}{2}
$$

$$
n_{N_{2}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 2.0\ \text{mol} = 3.0\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step (iii) ‚Äî Mass of N‚ÇÇ produced.**

$$
m_{N_{2}} = n_{N_{2}} \times M_{N_{2}} = 3.0\ \text{mol} \times 28\ \text{g/mol}
$$

$$
m_{N_{2}} = 84.0\ \text{g}
$$

**Step (iv) ‚Äî Volume of N‚ÇÇ at STP.**

Using the **Ideal Gas Law**: $P V = n R T$, so $V = \dfrac{n R T}{P}$.

Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol¬∑K)}$.

$$
V = \dfrac{3.0 \times 8.314 \times 273}{101{,}325}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{6810.4}{101{,}325} = 0.0672\ \text{m}^{3} = 67.2\ \text{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** 130 g of NaN‚ÇÉ generates 67.2 L of N‚ÇÇ at STP, which when hot and pressurized inside the inflator, fully inflates a driver airbag (typical inflated volume: 60 ‚Äì 80 L). The exact in-bag volume is lower because the gas is hot and not at STP.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Energy to be Absorbed by the Airbag

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):**
A vehicle of mass **M = 1500 kg** (including occupants) traveling at **v = 50 km/h** strikes a wall. The engine block and crumple zone absorb **60%** of the kinetic energy. The airbag + seatbelt must absorb the remaining energy. Compute the energy that the airbag must dissipate.

### Solution:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert velocity.**

$$
v = \dfrac{50}{3.6} = 13.89\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Total kinetic energy.**

$$
E_{k,\text{total}} = \dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (13.89)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{k,\text{total}} = 750 \times 192.93 = 144{,}700\ \text{J} \approx 144.7\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Energy absorbed by crumple zone (60%).**

$$
E_{\text{crumple}} = 0.60 \times 144.7 = 86.8\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Energy to be absorbed by airbag + seatbelt (40%).**

$$
E_{\text{airbag+seatbelt}} = 0.40 \times 144.7 = 57.9\ \text{kJ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The seatbelt typically absorbs the majority of this residual energy (‚âà 70%) by stretching and the pretensioner pulling the occupant back, while the airbag absorbs the remaining ‚âà 30% (‚âà 17 kJ) by cushion compression and venting.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Cushion Deployment Velocity Estimate

**Problem Statement:**
A driver-side airbag cushion has mass **m_c = 250 g** and is deployed by an inflator that imparts **E = 800 J** of kinetic energy to the cushion as it exits the steering-wheel module. Compute the cushion exit velocity.

### Solution:

Using the kinetic energy formula and solving for v:

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} m_c v^{2}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E}{m_c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 800}{0.250}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{1600}{0.250}} = \sqrt{6400}
$$

$$
v = 80.0\ \text{m/s} = 288\ \text{km/h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The cushion deploys at **‚âà 80 m/s (‚âà 288 km/h)**. This is why the inflator module uses a **tear seam** and **diffuser** to safely unfold the cushion ‚Äî the *kinetic energy* of deployment is carefully controlled so that the cushion is already inflated by the time it reaches the occupant, typically **within 30 ‚Äì 50 cm** of the steering wheel hub.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Sensor Decision Logic (Truth Table)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 frequently includes a short question on the **AND-gate logic** of the safing and discrimination sensors. The following truth table is a high-yield point.

| Discrimination Sensor (Crash G-sensor) | Safing Sensor (Independent verification) | Fire Decision |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **LOW** (no crash) | **LOW** | **NO FIRE** ‚Äî normal driving |
| **LOW** (no crash) | **HIGH** (sensor fault) | **NO FIRE** ‚Äî diagnostic flag set |
| **HIGH** (crash detected) | **LOW** (sensor fault) | **NO FIRE** ‚Äî diagnostic flag set |
| **HIGH** (crash detected) | **HIGH** (crash verified) | **FIRE** ‚Äî squib energized |

This **dual-sensor redundancy** prevents accidental deployment from a single sensor failure (e.g., a road bump, hammer strike, or sensor short circuit).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams render the **block-level functional architecture** of a modern airbag system, the **deployment sequence flow**, and the **sensor decision logic** that KTU 2024 examiners expect students to sketch in their answer scripts.

## 4.1 Airbag System Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VEHICLE CRASH EVENT] --> B[Front Bumper Satellite Sensors x2]
    A --> C[Side B-Pillar Pressure Sensors]
    A --> D[Roll Rate Sensor in ACU]
    B --> E[Airbag Control Unit ACU]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Safing AND Crash Discrimination Logic}
    F -->|FIRE| G1[Driver Frontal Squib]
    F -->|FIRE| G2[Passenger Frontal Squib]
    F -->|FIRE| G3[Side Thorax Squibs L and R]
    F -->|FIRE| G4[Curtain Squibs L and R]
    G1 --> H1[Driver Airbag Module]
    G2 --> H2[Passenger Airbag Module]
    G3 --> H3[Side Airbags]
    G4 --> H4[Curtain Airbags]
    E --> I[SRS Warning Lamp via CAN Bus]
    E --> J[Crash Data Recorder EDR]
    E --> K[Seatbelt Pretensioner Drivers]
```

## 4.2 Airbag Deployment Timing Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    T0[T = 0 ms: Initial Impact] --> T5[T = 5 ms: ACU begins crash algorithm]
    T5 --> T10[T = 10 ms: Safing AND discrimination both HIGH]
    T10 --> T15[T = 15 ms: Squib fired NaN3 ignited]
    T15 --> T25[T = 25 ms: Cushion emerging from module]
    T25 --> T40[T = 40 ms: Cushion fully inflated]
    T40 --> T100[T = 100 ms: Occupant contacts cushion]
    T100 --> T200[T = 200 ms: Cushion deflates via vent holes]
    T200 --> T300[T = 300 ms: System inactive irreversibly]
```

## 4.3 Sensor AND-Gate Firing Logic (Truth Table Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SENS1[Discrimination Crash Sensor] --> ANDNODE{AND GATE LOGIC}
    SENS2[Safing Independent Sensor] --> ANDNODE
    ANDNODE -->|Both HIGH| FIRE[Squib Energized N2 Generated]
    ANDNODE -->|Either LOW| NOFIRE[No Fire Diagnostic Flag]
    NOFIRE --> DIAG[SRS Warning Lamp ON]
```

## 4.4 Inflator Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SQ[Squib Thin Resistive Wire] --> BC[Booster Charge B KNO3]
    BC --> MAIN[Main Propellant NaN3 plus CuO]
    MAIN --> GAS[Hot N2 Gas Generation]
    GAS --> FILT[Stainless Steel Wire Mesh Filter]
    FILT --> COOL[Gas Cooling and Direction Reversal]
    COOL --> CUSH[Nylon Cushion Inflation]
    CUSH --> VENT[Vent Holes Controlled Deflation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in a KTU exam:** Always label the **sensor type**, the **signal direction (arrow)**, and the **time/sequence index** where applicable. Examiners award 1‚Äì2 marks for a clean, labeled sketch even if the calculation has minor errors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Airbag and explain why it is called a Supplementary Restraint System (SRS). [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An **airbag** is a vehicle safety device consisting of a folded fabric cushion and a pyrotechnic inflator that rapidly inflates with **nitrogen gas (N‚ÇÇ)** when the **Airbag Control Unit (ACU)** detects a crash deceleration above a predefined threshold. It is called a **Supplementary Restraint System (SRS)** because it is designed to *supplement* ‚Äî not replace ‚Äî the **seatbelt**, which is the **primary restraint system**. The airbag deploys within **20‚Äì30 ms** of impact and, together with the seatbelt, reduces the risk of fatal head and chest injuries by approximately **30% in frontal crashes** as per NHTSA data. The system is passive because it requires no action from the occupant.

---

### Question 2: List any **four** types of airbags used in modern passenger cars and state one trigger condition for each. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| # | Airbag Type | Mounting Location | Trigger Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frontal (Driver)** | Steering wheel hub | Frontal impact > **3 g** deceleration |
| 2 | **Side (Thorax)** | Seat back or door panel | Side intrusion > **5 g** or pressure spike |
| 3 | **Curtain (Head)** | Roof rail above windows | Side impact or **rollover** detected |
| 4 | **Knee Airbag** | Lower dashboard | Severe frontal crash to prevent femur/pelvis injury |
| 5 | **Pedestrian (External)** | Base of windshield | Pedestrian impact detected by hood-mounted sensors |

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 Part B questions carry **14 marks** and provide an internal choice (typically OR option between two full-length questions). Two completely independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### Part B ‚Äî Option A (14 Marks)

#### Q. A (a) ‚Äî Describe with a neat block diagram the complete Airbag Control Unit (ACU) system showing all sensors, the ACU, squibs, and airbag modules. Explain the AND-gate logic between the discrimination and safing sensors. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid Diagram):**

The system consists of the following functional blocks:
1. **Satellite crash sensors** ‚Äî mounted in the front bumper, B-pillar, and C-pillar; measure vehicle deceleration using MEMS accelerometers.
2. **Safing sensor** ‚Äî located *inside* the ACU; independently verifies the crash event using a different sensing axis.
3. **ACU microcontroller** ‚Äî runs the crash-severity algorithm based on **jerk** ($j = da/dt$).
4. **Squibs** ‚Äî thin resistive wires in each inflator that ignite the propellant when energized.
5. **Airbag modules** ‚Äî folded cushion + inflator assembly.
6. **Diagnostic interface** ‚Äî drives the **SRS warning lamp** on the dashboard and communicates via **CAN bus**.
7. **Seatbelt pretensioner drivers** ‚Äî fired in parallel with the airbags.

**AND-Gate Logic Explanation:**

The ACU implements a **logical AND** between two independent sensor signals before firing the squib. Let:
- $S_D$ = Discrimination sensor output (1 = crash detected).
- $S_S$ = Safing sensor output (1 = crash verified).

The fire condition is:

$$
\text{FIRE} = S_D \land S_S
$$

This means the squib is **energized only if both sensors agree**. If either sensor is LOW, the system will **NOT** fire. This dual-path redundancy prevents accidental deployment due to a single sensor fault, electrical noise, or a non-crash event (e.g., a sharp pothole that triggers only the discrimination sensor but not the safing sensor).

**Diagnostic Triggers:** If the two sensor outputs disagree for more than a few hundred milliseconds, the ACU sets a **fault code (DTC)** and illuminates the **SRS warning lamp** to alert the driver.

---

#### Q. A (b) ‚Äî A vehicle traveling at 72 km/h collides with a rigid wall. The crumple zone, seatbelt, and airbag together bring the occupant (mass 80 kg) to rest in 150 ms. Compute (i) the mean deceleration in m/s¬≤ and g, (ii) the force on the occupant in kN, and (iii) the kinetic energy that must be absorbed by the restraint system. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert velocity.**

$$
v_i = \dfrac{72\ \text{km/h}}{3.6} = 20.0\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute mean deceleration.**

$$
a = \dfrac{0 - 20.0}{0.150} = -133.33\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

Converting to **g**:

$$
a_g = \dfrac{133.33}{9.81} = 13.59\ g
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute force on occupant.**

$$
F = m \cdot \vert a \vert = 80\ \text{kg} \times 133.33\ \text{m/s}^{2} = 10{,}666\ \text{N} \approx 10.67\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute kinetic energy to be absorbed.**

$$
E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_i^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 80 \times (20.0)^{2}
$$

$$
E_k = 40 \times 400 = 16{,}000\ \text{J} = 16.0\ \text{kJ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The restraint system (seatbelt + airbag) must absorb **16.0 kJ** of energy and limit the peak force to approximately **10.67 kN** by extending the stopping time to 150 ms.

---

### Part B ‚Äî Option B (14 Marks)

#### Q. B (a) ‚Äî Explain the working principle of a sodium-azide-based airbag inflator with the chemical equation, and compute the volume of nitrogen gas produced at STP from 100 g of NaN‚ÇÉ. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

When the ACU fires the squib, the following sequence occurs within the inflator:

1. The **squib** (resistive wire) is heated by a high-current pulse (‚âà 1.5 A for 2 ms).
2. The squib ignites a small **booster charge** of **B/KNO‚ÇÉ** (boron‚Äìpotassium nitrate).
3. The booster charge ignites the main propellant charge of **sodium azide (NaN‚ÇÉ)** mixed with an **oxidizer (CuO or Fe‚ÇÇO‚ÇÉ)** to control burn rate.
4. The sodium azide decomposes **exothermically** to produce **nitrogen gas (N‚ÇÇ)** and metallic sodium (Na).
5. The hot sodium vapor reacts with the oxidizer to form **silicates (Na‚ÇÇSiO‚ÇÉ)** in the presence of **SiO‚ÇÇ** filter media, which is **non-toxic** and stable.
6. The N‚ÇÇ gas is cooled and filtered by a **stainless-steel wire mesh** before entering the cushion.

**Chemical Equation:**

$$
2\,\text{NaN}_{3}(s) \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na}(s) + 3\,\text{N}_{2}(g)
$$

The metallic sodium is then neutralized in a secondary reaction with iron oxide or silicon dioxide:

$$
6\,\text{Na} + \text{Fe}_{2}\text{O}_{3} \rightarrow 3\,\text{Na}_{2}\text{O} + 2\,\text{Fe} \quad \text{(byproducts are solids, filtered out)}
$$

**Computation of N‚ÇÇ Volume from 100 g NaN‚ÇÉ:**

**Molar mass of NaN‚ÇÉ:** $M = 23 + 3 \times 14 = 65\ \text{g/mol}$.

**Moles of NaN‚ÇÉ:**

$$
n_{\text{NaN}_{3}} = \dfrac{100}{65} = 1.538\ \text{mol}
$$

**Moles of N‚ÇÇ (from stoichiometry, ratio 3/2):**

$$
n_{N_{2}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 1.538 = 2.308\ \text{mol}
$$

**Volume at STP (T = 273 K, P = 101.325 kPa):**

$$
V = \dfrac{n R T}{P} = \dfrac{2.308 \times 8.314 \times 273}{101{,}325}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{5239.7}{101{,}325} = 0.0517\ \text{m}^{3} = 51.7\ \text{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** 100 g of NaN‚ÇÉ produces **51.7 L of N‚ÇÇ at STP**, sufficient to inflate a typical driver-side airbag (‚âà 60 L nominal volume).

---

#### Q. B (b) ‚Äî With a neat timing diagram, explain the sequence of events during airbag deployment from the moment of impact (T = 0) to full deflation (T = 300 ms). [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The deployment of a modern airbag follows a tightly choreographed **timeline**. KTU examiners award full marks only when **each phase is linked to a specific time window** and a **specific component activation**.

| Time (ms) | Event | Component Activated |
|:---:|---|---|
| **0** | Vehicle impact begins; front bumper sensors detect first deceleration | Satellite crash sensor |
| **3 ‚Äì 5** | ACU algorithm evaluates crash severity (jerk and energy) | Airbag Control Unit (microcontroller) |
| **8 ‚Äì 10** | Safing sensor confirms; AND-gate satisfied; firing decision locked | ACU firing logic |
| **12 ‚Äì 15** | Squib energized (1.5 A for 2 ms); booster charge ignited | Squib + Booster |
| **15 ‚Äì 20** | Main propellant (NaN‚ÇÉ) decomposes; N‚ÇÇ gas begins to flow | Inflator + Filter |
| **20 ‚Äì 30** | Cushion tears out of module and begins unfolding | Cushion fabric |
| **30 ‚Äì 50** | Cushion fully inflated (‚âà 60 L) in front of occupant | Cushion |
| **50 ‚Äì 100** | Occupant's body begins compressing the cushion; venting starts | Vent holes |
| **100 ‚Äì 200** | Peak occupant loading occurs; gas escapes through vents; energy absorbed | Vent holes + Seatbelt |
| **200 ‚Äì 300** | Cushion fully deflates; system is single-event and inactive | ‚Äî |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering significance:** The entire deployment sequence is **irreversible** (single-event) and the inflator must be replaced after a single deployment. The **SRS warning lamp** remains OFF during normal driving but lights up briefly during ignition self-test (‚âà 3‚Äì5 seconds at every key-on).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Airbag Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the seatbelt:** Students often describe the airbag as a *primary* safety system. It is **supplementary (SRS)**. The seatbelt is the **primary** restraint. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion:** km/h ‚Üí m/s conversion error ($54\ \text{km/h} \ne 54\ \text{m/s}$). **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing g with m/s¬≤:** A sensor threshold of 5 g must be written as $5 \times 9.81 = 49.05\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ if asked in SI. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Single sensor fire logic:** Students often state the airbag fires on a *single* sensor signal. Modern systems use **dual-sensor AND-gate logic** for safety. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Stoichiometry error:** Forgetting the 3/2 ratio in the NaN‚ÇÉ decomposition. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Sketching without labels:** A Mermaid-style block diagram without arrow labels, sensor names, or time indices. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 7. **Confusing active vs. passive restraint:** Airbags are **passive** (no occupant action needed); ABS, ESC are **active** safety. **(-1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 ‚Äî Module 3, Airbags:**

- **Full form:** SRS = Supplementary Restraint System. **Not primary** restraint.
- **Primary restraint:** 3-point seatbelt (with pretensioner + load limiter).
- **Deployment time:** **20 ‚Äì 30 ms** (faster than a human blink).
- **Threshold for frontal airbag:** **‚âà 3 ‚Äì 5 g** deceleration.
- **Threshold for side airbag:** **‚âà 5 ‚Äì 10 g** (or pressure spike in door cavity).
- **Gas used:** Nitrogen (**N‚ÇÇ**), generated by **sodium azide (NaN‚ÇÉ)** decomposition.
- **Reaction:** $2\,\text{NaN}_{3} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na} + 3\,\text{N}_{2}$.
- **Filter media:** Stainless steel wire mesh + SiO‚ÇÇ to neutralize Na byproducts.
- **Cushion material:** Nylon 6,6 with silicone/neoprene coating.
- **Vent holes:** **2 ‚Äì 4 holes** of total area **30 ‚Äì 50 cm¬≤** for controlled deflation.
- **Typical inflated volume:** Driver ‚âà 60 L, Passenger ‚âà 120 ‚Äì 160 L.
- **Cushion exit velocity:** **‚âà 80 m/s (288 km/h)**.
- **ACU logic:** **AND-gate** between discrimination sensor and safing sensor.
- **Discrimination parameter:** **Jerk** ($j = da/dt$), not just acceleration.
- **Single-event:** Once deployed, the inflator is **spent** and must be replaced.
- **Module life:** **15 years** recommended replacement.
- **Diagnostic:** SRS warning lamp on dashboard; communicates via **CAN bus**.
- **Auxiliary firing:** In parallel, the **seatbelt pretensioner** fires (within 10 ms).
- **Smart airbags:** Multi-stage inflators, occupant weight sensor, position sensor.
- **Rollover detection:** Uses a **roll-rate sensor** (gyroscope) inside the ACU to fire curtain airbags.
- **Energy absorbed:** For 50 km/h crash, ‚âà 60% by crumple zone, ‚âà 40% by restraint system.
- **Force reduction:** Airbag extends stopping time by **‚âà 6√ó**, reducing peak force by the **same factor**.
- **Crash data:** Modern ACUs have an **Event Data Recorder (EDR)** that logs pre-crash parameters (speed, brake, throttle, seatbelt status) for forensic analysis.
- **Standards:** Comply with **ECE R94** (frontal), **ECE R95** (side), **FMVSS 208** (U.S. federal).

**Mnemonic for the KTU Exam ‚Äî "S-A-F-E":**
- **S** ‚Äî Sensors detect the crash (satellite + safing).
- **A** ‚Äî ACU evaluates and decides (jerk-based algorithm).
- **F** ‚Äî Fire the squib (1.5 A pulse, irreversible).
- **E** ‚Äî Expand and absorb (cushion inflates, vents deflate, energy absorbed).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Vehicles and Hybrid Vehicles: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electric Vehicles and Hybrid Vehicles: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose primary propulsion force is derived from one or more electric motors, drawing electrical energy from a rechargeable on-board energy storage system (typically a battery pack, supercapacitor, or fuel cell). According to the KTU 2024 Autotronics syllabus, EVs are classified based on the degree of electrification and the energy source architecture.

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that combines a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) with an electric propulsion system, where both power sources can deliver tractive effort either independently or in tandem, to achieve improved fuel economy and reduced tailpipe emissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Note**: For examination purposes, the terms "xEV" (Electrified Vehicle) and "EV" are used interchangeably. Always specify the architecture (BEV/HEV/PHEV/FCEV) before writing definitions in board exams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an EV like a **giant smartphone on wheels**. Just as your phone stores energy in a lithium-ion battery and uses it to power a processor, an EV stores energy in a large battery pack and uses it to power an electric motor. The "charger" is just a larger version of your phone charger.

A Hybrid Vehicle is like a **bicycle-rider hybrid** ‚Äî imagine someone who can either pedal (electric mode) or walk (ICE mode), or do both simultaneously when going uphill (combined mode). The body automatically chooses the most efficient method based on the terrain.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard SAE J1711 and ISO 8714** metrics govern EV/HEV analysis:

- **Energy Density of Gasoline**: ‚âà **34.2 MJ/L** (‚âà 8.9 kWh/L)
- **Energy Density of Li-ion Battery**: ‚âà **0.25‚Äì0.70 MJ/L** (‚âà 0.25‚Äì0.50 kWh/L)
- **Standard Battery Pack Voltage**: **400 V** (modern EVs) / **800 V** (next-gen)
- **ICE Thermal Efficiency**: **25%‚Äì40%**
- **Electric Motor Efficiency**: **85%‚Äì95%**
- **Regenerative Braking Recovery**: Up to **70%** of kinetic energy recovered
- **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ** (SI Conversion Constant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the gap matters**: Gasoline stores roughly **40‚Äì100 times** more energy per liter than a battery. This is why range anxiety and battery weight are central engineering challenges in EV design.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Taxonomy of Electrified Vehicles

| Acronym | Full Form | Energy Source | Grid Charging |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery Only | Yes (Plug-in) |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE | No (Self-charging) |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid EV | Battery + ICE | Yes (Plug-in) |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen Fuel Cell | No (H‚ÇÇ Refueling) |
| **MHEV** | Mild Hybrid EV | 48V Battery + ICE | No (Regen only) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution of EV Technology

The structured logical progression of EV development can be broken into four key engineering eras:

1. **Pre-1900 Era (Galvanic Pouch)**: First EVs used non-rechargeable cells; range was under **30 km**.
2. **1900‚Äì1935 (Lead-Acid Heyday)**: EVs like the **Detroit Electric** achieved 130 km range but lost market to the electric starter (1912) which made ICE cars convenient.
3. **1990s‚Äì2010s (Modern Revival)**: Triggered by the **California ZEV Mandate (1990)**, leading to GM EV1, Toyota Prius (1997 ‚Äî world's first mass-produced HEV).
4. **2010‚ÄìPresent (Lithium Era)**: Tesla Roadster (2008), Nissan Leaf (2010), and the global push toward the **2030 ICE-ban roadmap**.

### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Hybrid Electric Vehicles

The **three fundamental HEV architectures** form the core of KTU Module 3 assessments:

#### 2.2.1 Series Hybrid (Range-Extended EV)

- **Logic**: ICE is mechanically decoupled from wheels. ICE drives a generator ‚Üí charges battery ‚Üí powers motor.
- **Analogy**: A diesel locomotive. The diesel engine never touches the wheels; it only generates electricity.
- **When ICE runs**: Only when battery State of Charge (SoC) drops below a threshold (~30%).
- **KTU Board Trick**: If a question says "ICE has no mechanical link to wheels" ‚Üí it is a **Series Hybrid**.

#### 2.2.2 Parallel Hybrid

- **Logic**: ICE and Electric Motor are **mechanically coupled** to the driveline (often via a clutch). Both can propel the vehicle simultaneously or independently.
- **Analogy**: Two horses pulling one cart ‚Äî sometimes one pulls, sometimes both.
- **Best for**: Highway cruising where ICE efficiency is high.
- **Example**: Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA system).

#### 2.2.3 Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split / PSHEV)

- **Logic**: Uses a planetary gear set (epicyclic gearbox) to combine series and parallel modes seamlessly.
- **Key Component**: Toyota Hybrid System (THS) with **Power Split Device (PSD)**.
- **Modes**: EV mode, Engine-only mode, Combined mode, Regenerative braking mode.
- **Example**: Toyota Prius (1997‚Äìpresent), Camry Hybrid.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM INSTRUCTION**: The following equations are the **highest-weightage formulas** for KTU 2024 Module 3 numericals. Memorize the symbol mapping.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{bat} = V_{oc} \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{dis}$ | $E_{bat}$: Battery Energy (Wh), $V_{oc}$: Open-circuit Voltage, $Q$: Capacity (Ah), $\eta_{dis}$: Discharge efficiency | Sizing battery pack for range |
| 2 | $R_{ev} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{E_{cons}}$ | $R_{ev}$: EV Range (km), $E_{cons}$: Specific energy consumption (Wh/km) | Range estimation |
| 3 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{F_{tr} \cdot v}{\eta_{drivetrain}}$ | $P_{req}$: Required traction power, $F_{tr}$: Tractive force, $v$: Velocity | Motor power sizing |
| 4 | $F_{tr} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$ | $F_{rr}$: Rolling resistance, $F_{ad}$: Aerodynamic drag, $F_{hc}$: Hill-climb force | Vehicle dynamics |
| 5 | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $\mu_{rr}$: Rolling resistance coefficient, $m$: Vehicle mass, $g$: 9.81 m/s¬≤, $\theta$: Slope angle | Resistance forces |
| 6 | $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{a} \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}$ | $\rho_{a}$: Air density (1.225 kg/m¬≥), $C_{d}$: Drag coefficient, $A_{f}$: Frontal area | Aerodynamic load |
| 7 | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Slope component | Grade resistance |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{bat} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{tr}$ | Cumulative drivetrain efficiency | System efficiency |
| 9 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2} \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | $E_{regen}$: Recovered kinetic energy, $\eta_{regen}$: Regen efficiency (~0.6‚Äì0.7) | Brake energy recovery |
| 10 | $SoC(t) = SoC_{0} + \dfrac{1}{Q} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | $SoC$: State of Charge (%), $I(\tau)$: Instantaneous current | Battery management |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note**: The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is used in all absolute value expressions to ensure markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Industry

- **Equations 1‚Äì3** are used by **Tesla, Tata Motors, and Ather** for range certification (ARAI India range test).
- **Equations 4‚Äì7** are the foundation of **vehicle dynamics simulation** in MATLAB/Simulink and CarSim.
- **Equation 9** justifies why **regenerative braking** is a hallmark of EVs and HEVs ‚Äî recovering up to **30%** of the energy spent during acceleration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: EV Range from Battery Pack Parameters

**Problem Statement**: A KTU board question (Dec 2022 style) states: *"A BEV uses a 400 V, 200 Ah lithium-ion battery pack. The vehicle consumes 150 Wh/km. The drivetrain efficiency is 90%. Calculate the theoretical range."*

**Given**:
- $V_{bat} = 400 \, V$
- $Q = 200 \, Ah$
- $E_{cons} = 150 \, Wh/km$
- $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.90$

**Step 1**: Calculate the gross stored energy in the battery.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{gross} &= V_{bat} \times Q \\
&= 400 \, V \times 200 \, Ah \\
&= 80{,}000 \, Wh \\
&= 80 \, kWh
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: A battery's energy storage is the product of its terminal voltage and its ampere-hour capacity. This gives watt-hours directly because $V \times Ah = Wh$.

**Step 2**: Apply drivetrain efficiency to obtain usable energy at the wheels.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{usable} &= E_{gross} \times \eta_{drivetrain} \\
&= 80{,}000 \, Wh \times 0.90 \\
&= 72{,}000 \, Wh \\
&= 72 \, kWh
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: Not all battery energy reaches the wheels ‚Äî 10% is lost as heat in the inverter, motor windings, and gearbox friction.

**Step 3**: Compute the range using the specific energy consumption.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{ev} &= \dfrac{E_{usable}}{E_{cons}} \\
&= \dfrac{72{,}000 \, Wh}{150 \, Wh/km} \\
&= 480 \, km
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: Dividing total usable energy by per-kilometer consumption yields the maximum travel distance. ‚úÖ **Final Answer: 480 km**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Tractive Force Breakdown at Constant Speed on a Slope

**Problem Statement**: *"A 1500 kg EV travels at a constant 60 km/h up a 5¬∞ incline. Compute the total tractive force. Take $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2 \, m^2$."*

**Step 1**: Convert velocity to SI units.

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= 60 \, km/h = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} \, m/s = 16.67 \, m/s
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: SI units (m/s) are mandatory for force calculations to keep Newton units consistent.

**Step 2**: Compute rolling resistance.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) \\
&= 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(5¬∞) \\
&= 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9962 \\
&\approx 175.86 \, N
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: Rolling resistance is the friction between tires and road. The cosine term accounts for the reduction in normal force on a slope.

**Step 3**: Compute aerodynamic drag.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^{2} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.89 \\
&\approx 112.32 \, N
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: Aerodynamic drag grows with the **square of velocity** ‚Äî this is why EVs are most efficient at 40‚Äì60 km/h city speeds.

**Step 4**: Compute hill-climb force.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{hc} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) \\
&= 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5¬∞) \\
&= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 \\
&\approx 1283.06 \, N
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: This is the gravitational force component along the slope. Notice that even a 5¬∞ grade produces **7√ó more force** than aerodynamic drag ‚Äî hilly terrain kills EV range.

**Step 5**: Sum all forces.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{tr} &= F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} \\
&= 175.86 + 112.32 + 1283.06 \\
&\approx 1571.24 \, N
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: The total tractive force is the sum of all resistances that the motor must overcome. ‚úÖ **Final Answer: ‚âà 1571 N**.

**Step 6 (Bonus)**: Compute required motor power.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{req} &= F_{tr} \times v \\
&= 1571.24 \times 16.67 \\
&\approx 26{,}192 \, W \\
&\approx 26.2 \, kW
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic*: Power is force times velocity. A 26 kW motor (~35 hp) is sufficient for this steady-state climb.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Architecture

| Parameter | BEV | HEV (Series) | HEV (Parallel) | HEV (Series-Parallel) | PHEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ICE Present? | No | Yes (Gen) | Yes (Drive) | Yes (Both) | Yes | No |
| Battery Size | Large | Small | Small | Small-Medium | Large | Small |
| Range (km) | 300‚Äì600 | 600+ | 600+ | 600+ | 50‚Äì80 (EV) + 500 (ICE) | 500‚Äì700 |
| Emissions | Zero (tailpipe) | Low | Low | Low | Low-Moderate | Zero (only H‚ÇÇO) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High | Very High |
| Example | Tesla Model 3 | BMW i3 REx | Honda Civic | Toyota Prius | Toyota Prius Prime | Toyota Mirai |
| **KTU Board Tip** | "Plug-in only" | "ICE ‚Üí Generator" | "ICE $\parallel$ Motor" | "Planetary gear" | "Two refuels" | "H‚ÇÇ tank" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EV/HEV Classification Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ELECTRIFIED VEHICLE XEV"] --> B["GRID CHARGING REQUIRED"]
    A --> C["NO GRID CHARGING"]
    B --> B1["BEV BATTERY ELECTRIC VEHICLE"]
    B --> B2["PHEV PLUG IN HYBRID EV"]
    C --> C1["HEV HYBRID EV SELF CHARGING"]
    C --> C2["FCEV FUEL CELL EV HYDROGEN"]

    B1 --> B1B["LARGE LITHIUM BATTERY PACK"]
    B1 --> B1C["REGEN BRAKING"]
    B2 --> B2A["ICE PLUS ELECTRIC MOTOR"]
    B2 --> B2B["PLUG IN CHARGING PLUS FUEL TANK"]
    B2 --> B2C["TYPICAL EV RANGE 50 TO 80 KM"]
    C1 --> C1A["SMALL BATTERY WITH ICE"]
    C1 --> C1B["TYPICALLY NICKEL METAL HYDRIDE OR LITHIUM"]
    C1 --> C1C["REGEN BRAKING ONLY CHARGE SOURCE"]
    C2 --> C2A["HYDROGEN FUEL CELL STACK"]
    C2 --> C2B["WATER VAPOR ONLY EMISSION"]
    C2 --> C2C["REQUIRES HYDROGEN REFUELING NETWORK"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Series-Parallel HEV (Power-Split System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWERTRAIN ["POWERTRAIN CORE"]
        ICE["ICE INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE"] --> P1["P1 PLANETARY GEAR POWER SPLIT DEVICE"]
        MG1["MG1 GENERATOR MOTOR"] --> P1
        P1 --> P2["P2 RING GEAR OUTPUT"]
        MG2["MG2 TRACTION MOTOR"] --> P2
        BAT["HV BATTERY 200 TO 300 V"] --> INV["INVERTER DC TO AC"]
        INV --> MG1
        INV --> MG2
    end
    P2 --> FD["FINAL DRIVE DIFFERENTIAL"]
    FD --> WH["WHEELS VEHICLE MOTION"]
    P1 --> REGEN["REGEN BRAKING ENERGY RECOVERY"]
    REGEN --> BAT
    WH --> REGEN
    ICE -.-> ECU["ECU HYBRID CONTROL UNIT"]
    MG1 -.-> ECU
    MG2 -.-> ECU
    BAT -.-> ECU
    ECU -.-> ICE
    ECU -.-> MG1
    ECU -.-> MG2
```

### 4.3 Battery Pack Sizing Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["DESIGN INPUTS TARGET RANGE AND VEHICLE MASS"] --> CALC1["CALCULATE TOTAL ENERGY REQUIRED E REQUIRED E CONS TIMES RANGE"]
    CALC1 --> CALC2["APPLY USABLE SOC WINDOW TYPICALLY 80 PERCENT"]
    CALC2 --> CALC3["DIVIDE BY CELL ENERGY DENSITY TO GET CELL COUNT"]
    CALC3 --> CALC4["CONFIGURE SERIES PARALLEY TO MEET VOLTAGE AND CAPACITY"]
    CALC4 --> CALC5["BMS INTEGRATION AND THERMAL MANAGEMENT"]
    CALC5 --> OUTPUT["FINAL PACK SPEC KWH AND KG"]
```

### 4.4 EV Operating Mode State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PARK
    PARK --> EVMODE : SOC HIGH AND LOW LOAD
    EVMODE --> HYBRIDMODE : HIGH LOAD OR LOW SOC
    HYBRIDMODE --> EVMODE : LOAD REDUCED AND SOC RECOVERED
    EVMODE --> REGENMODE : BRAKE APPLIED
    HYBRIDMODE --> REGENMODE : BRAKE APPLIED
    REGENMODE --> EVMODE : BRAKE RELEASED AND SOC INCREASED
    REGENMODE --> HYBRIDMODE : BRAKE RELEASED AND SOC STILL LOW
    EVMODE --> PARK : VEHICLE STOP
    HYBRIDMODE --> PARK : VEHICLE STOP
    REGENMODE --> PARK : VEHICLE STOP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between a BEV, HEV, and PHEV based on energy source and grid dependency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

A **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle)** runs entirely on a large battery pack charged exclusively from the grid; it has no ICE. An **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle)** combines a small battery and an ICE, but the battery cannot be plugged in ‚Äî it is self-charged only through regenerative braking and the ICE. A **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid EV)** is a hybrid with a larger battery that **can be plugged into the grid** for pure electric driving (typically 30‚Äì80 km), after which the ICE takes over for extended range.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write "HEV = electric + petrol car" without specifying that the battery is *not* grid-chargeable. Always mention the charging mechanism explicitly to earn full 3 marks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of Electric Vehicles over conventional ICE vehicles.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Advantages**:
1. **Zero tailpipe emissions** ‚Äî reduces urban air pollution and CO‚ÇÇ footprint.
2. **Higher efficiency** ‚Äî electric motors achieve 85‚Äì95% efficiency versus 25‚Äì40% for ICE.
3. **Regenerative braking** ‚Äî recovers up to 70% of kinetic energy otherwise lost as heat.

**Disadvantages**:
1. **Limited range and long charging time** compared to liquid fuel refueling.
2. **Higher upfront cost** due to expensive battery packs and rare-earth motor materials.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **Series Hybrid, Parallel Hybrid, and Series-Parallel Hybrid** configurations with neat block diagrams and state one example vehicle for each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A BEV has the following specifications: Battery pack voltage = **350 V**, capacity = **150 Ah**, vehicle energy consumption = **160 Wh/km**, drivetrain efficiency = **88%**. Calculate (i) total stored energy, (ii) usable energy at wheels, and (iii) theoretical range in km. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Hybrid Configurations (7 Marks)**:

- **Series Hybrid**: ICE drives a generator to produce electricity, which charges the battery or directly feeds the traction motor. ICE has **no mechanical link** to wheels. Example: **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen)** or BMW i3 REx.
- **Parallel Hybrid**: ICE and electric motor are **mechanically coupled** to the drivetrain via a clutch. Both can propel the vehicle simultaneously or independently. Example: **Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA)**.
- **Series-Parallel Hybrid**: Combines both via a **planetary gear (epicyclic) set** as the Power Split Device. The ECU continuously optimizes mode selection. Example: **Toyota Prius** with THS-II.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**:

(i) Stored energy:
$$E_{gross} = V \times Q = 350 \times 150 = 52{,}500 \, Wh = 52.5 \, kWh$$

(ii) Usable energy:
$$E_{usable} = 52{,}500 \times 0.88 = 46{,}200 \, Wh = 46.2 \, kWh$$

(iii) Range:
$$R = \dfrac{46{,}200}{160} = 288.75 \, km$$

‚úÖ **Final Range ‚âà 288.75 km**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students forget to multiply by drivetrain efficiency before dividing by specific consumption. This causes a 2-mark deduction. Always apply efficiency **before** range calculation, as the consumption value is the *wheel-side* demand.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2022 Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **major components of a Battery Electric Vehicle** and describe the function of the Battery Management System (BMS). (7 Marks)

**(b)** An EV of mass **1200 kg** travels at a constant speed of **50 km/h** on a level road. Compute the tractive force required. Given: $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.0 \, m^2$, $\rho_a = 1.225 \, kg/m^3$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) BEV Major Components (7 Marks)**:

1. **Battery Pack** (Li-ion, 400 V typical) ‚Äî stores electrical energy.
2. **Power Electronics Controller / Inverter** ‚Äî converts DC to AC for the motor.
3. **Traction Motor** (PMSM or Induction) ‚Äî converts electrical energy to mechanical.
4. **Transmission (Single-speed reducer)** ‚Äî adapts motor speed to wheels.
5. **Battery Management System (BMS)** ‚Äî monitors cell voltage, current, temperature; balances SoC, prevents overcharge/over-discharge, and provides thermal cutoff.
6. **On-board Charger & DC-DC Converter** ‚Äî steps down 400 V to 12 V for auxiliaries.
7. **Regenerative Braking System** ‚Äî recovers kinetic energy.

The **BMS** is the brain of the battery ‚Äî it ensures safety, longevity (cycle life), and accurate SoC reporting to the driver.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**:

Convert velocity: $v = 50 \, km/h = 13.89 \, m/s$

Rolling resistance:
$$F_{rr} = 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0¬∞) = 117.72 \, N$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.0 \times (13.89)^{2} = 66.18 \, N$$

Hill-climb force (level road, $\theta = 0$): $F_{hc} = 0$

Total tractive force:
$$F_{tr} = 117.72 + 66.18 + 0 = 183.9 \, N$$

‚úÖ **Final Answer: ‚âà 184 N**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s is the **#1 mistake** in KTU numericals. Always write the conversion step explicitly: $v = \dfrac{50 \times 1000}{3600} = 13.89 \, m/s$. This step alone can earn 1 mark even if the rest is partially correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Must Memorize Before Exam**

- üîã **BEV** = Battery only; **HEV** = ICE + battery (no plug); **PHEV** = ICE + battery (with plug); **FCEV** = Hydrogen fuel cell.
- ‚öôÔ∏è **Three HEV architectures**: Series (ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Motor), Parallel (ICE $\parallel$ Motor, both to wheels), Series-Parallel (Planetary gear + ECU-controlled split).
- üìê **Energy conversion**: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; Gasoline energy density ‚âà 8.9 kWh/L; Li-ion ‚âà 0.25‚Äì0.50 kWh/L.
- üöó **Tractive force** = Rolling + Aerodynamic + Hill-climb = $\mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + 0.5 \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\theta$.
- üîå **Range formula**: $R = \dfrac{V \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{total}}{E_{cons}}$.
- ‚ôªÔ∏è **Regenerative braking** recovers up to ~70% of kinetic energy as electricity.
- üìä **Motor efficiency (85‚Äì95%)** > **ICE efficiency (25‚Äì40%)** ‚Äî this is the fundamental efficiency advantage of EVs.
- üéØ **KTU board tip**: Always state the **architecture first**, then derive the energy/power flow ‚Äî examiners award marks for structured flowcharts.
- üìù **Conversion trap**: km/h to m/s = multiply by $\frac{5}{18}$; m/s to km/h = multiply by $3.6$.
- üõë **No vertical pipes** ($\vert x \vert$) in markdown tables ‚Äî use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ to preserve formatting.

---

**End of Module 3 ‚Äî Topic 1 Notes**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education Framework.*
