# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give one automotive example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Active sensors** generate their own electrical output signal (voltage/current) from the energy of the measured quantity, **without external excitation**. *(1 mark)*
  - **Examples:** Piezoelectric knock sensor, thermocouple, Hall-effect crankshaft position sensor. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Passive sensors** change their electrical property (resistance, capacitance, inductance) in response to the measured quantity and **require external excitation (voltage or current)** to produce a usable output. *(1 mark)*
  - **Examples:** NTC thermistor coolant temperature sensor (variable resistance), MAP pressure sensor (piezo-resistive bridge), throttle position sensor (potentiometric). *(0.5 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]
**What is the function of an Electronic Control Unit (ECU) in a modern automobile? List any two sensors and two actuators connected to it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Function of ECU:** The ECU is the central processing unit of a vehicle subsystem. It receives signals from various sensors, executes pre-programmed control algorithms stored in non-volatile memory, drives the actuators accordingly, performs continuous diagnostics (OBD-II), and communicates with other ECUs over automotive networks (CAN/LIN/FlexRay). *(1.5 marks)*

- **Two sensors:** *(0.5 marks)*
  - Crankshaft Position (CKP) sensor
  - Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor

- **Two actuators:** *(0.5 marks)*
  - Fuel injector
  - Ignition coil

- **KTU examiner's tip:** Always use the *expansion* ‚Äî full form first, then abbreviation. Reverse order costs half a mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks ‚Äî Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q. A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Sense‚ÄìProcess‚ÄìActuate** chain of an automotive electronic system. Describe the role of any **four** major components. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Block Diagram:** *(2 marks)*

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[PHYSICAL QUANTITY] --> B[SENSOR]
    B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    C --> D[ECU MCU]
    D --> E[ACTUATOR DRIVER]
    E --> F[ACTUATOR]
    F --> G[PHYSICAL ACTION]
```

**(ii) Description of four major components:** *(5 marks ‚Äî 1.25 marks each)*

- **Sensor:** Converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, position, speed) into an electrical signal. *Example:* Hall-effect crankshaft position sensor produces a digital square wave whose frequency is proportional to engine RPM.

- **Signal Conditioning Circuit:** Amplifies, filters (anti-aliasing), and linearizes the raw sensor signal. *Example:* A 2nd-order active low-pass filter (Sallen-Key topology) with $f_c = 100 \text{ Hz}$ removes high-frequency noise from a MAP sensor.

- **ECU / Microcontroller:** The brain of the system. Samples the conditioned signals via its ADC, runs the control algorithm (e.g., fuel map lookup using 3-D interpolation on RPM √ó MAP), and outputs PWM commands. *Example:* Infineon AURIX TC387 with 6 cores, 300 MHz, 16 MB flash, ISO 26262 ASIL-D capable.

- **Actuator Driver + Actuator:** A driver stage (MOSFET H-bridge, current-limited solenoid driver) takes the low-current MCU output and switches the high-current load. *Example:* A low-side N-channel MOSFET (e.g., Infineon BTS50055) drives a fuel injector at 1.5 A, 14 V with PWM current control.

---

**Q. A (b)** An **NTC thermistor** with nominal resistance **$R_0 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$** at **$T_0 = 298 \text{ K}$** and B-constant **$B = 3950 \text{ K}$** is connected in a voltage divider with a **$2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$** reference resistor and a **5 V** supply. Compute the output voltage at coolant temperatures of **(i) 25 ¬∞C**, **(ii) 90 ¬∞C**, and **(iii) 110 ¬∞C**. Comment on the sensitivity of the sensor. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The output voltage of the divider is:
$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{R_{ref}}{R_T + R_{ref}}$$

where $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ and $T_0 = 298 \text{ K}$.

**Case (i): T = 25 ¬∞C = 298 K**
- The exponent is zero, so $R_T = R_0 = 10{,}000 \text{ }\Omega$.
- $V_{out} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{2200}{10000 + 2200} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{2200}{12200} = 5 \cdot 0.1803 \approx \mathbf{0.90 \text{ V}}$ *(2 marks)*

**Case (ii): T = 90 ¬∞C = 363 K**
- Exponent: $3950 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{363} - \frac{1}{298} \right) = 3950 \cdot (0.002755 - 0.003356) = 3950 \cdot (-0.000601) = -2.374$
- $R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-2.374} \approx 10{,}000 \cdot 0.0931 \approx 931 \text{ }\Omega$
- $V_{out} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{2200}{931 + 2200} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{2200}{3131} \approx 5 \cdot 0.7027 \approx \mathbf{3.51 \text{ V}}$ *(2.5 marks)*

**Case (iii): T = 110 ¬∞C = 383 K**
- Exponent: $3950 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{383} - \frac{1}{298} \right) = 3950 \cdot (0.002611 - 0.003356) = 3950 \cdot (-0.000745) = -2.943$
- $R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-2.943} \approx 10{,}000 \cdot 0.0528 \approx 528 \text{ }\Omega$
- $V_{out} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{2200}{528 + 2200} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{2200}{2728} \approx 5 \cdot 0.8065 \approx \mathbf{4.03 \text{ V}}$ *(2.5 marks)*

**Sensitivity Comment:** *(1 mark)*

The sensitivity $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dT}$ is **non-linear**: it is largest at lower temperatures (from 0.90 V at 25 ¬∞C to 3.51 V at 90 ¬∞C ‚Üí ŒîV = 2.61 V over 65 ¬∞C, i.e., 40 mV/¬∞C) and tapers off at higher temperatures (from 3.51 V to 4.03 V ‚Üí ŒîV = 0.52 V over 20 ¬∞C, i.e., 26 mV/¬∞C). This is characteristic of NTC sensors and is why ECUs use lookup tables rather than linear interpolation in firmware.

---

### **OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q. B (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **crankshaft position (CKP) Hall-effect sensor** and a **throttle position sensor (TPS)**. How does the ECU use their signals? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) CKP Hall-Effect Sensor** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Construction:** A Hall-effect IC is biased by a small permanent magnet. A ferromagnetic target wheel (reluctor) with teeth (e.g., **60‚àí2 = 58 teeth + 1 missing-tooth gap**) rotates past the sensor at the crankshaft speed.
- **Working:** When a tooth is in front of the sensor, the magnetic flux lines are shunted through the tooth, lowering the flux density perpendicular to the Hall element ‚Üí low Hall voltage ‚Üí output LOW. When a tooth gap is in front, flux passes into the Hall element ‚Üí high Hall voltage ‚Üí output HIGH. This produces a **square wave** whose frequency is proportional to engine RPM.
- **ECU usage:** The ECU measures the **time between rising edges** to compute RPM. The missing-tooth pattern provides a once-per-revolution **sync reference** for the injection and ignition events.

**(ii) Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Construction:** A **potentiometric (rheostat)** type uses a resistive track (carbon film or cermet) and a wiper mechanically coupled to the throttle shaft. A redundant dual-track design (two wipers offset by ~5 %) provides fail-safe operation.
- **Working:** As the throttle opens, the wiper moves along the resistive track, varying the output voltage in a **0 V (idle) to 5 V (WOT)** range.
- **ECU usage:** The ADC reads $V_{out}$ and converts it to throttle angle via lookup table. The ECU uses this for fuel calculation (alpha-n or speed-density strategy), traction control, and gear-shift decisions in automatic transmissions. The redundant track is continuously compared; if the two outputs disagree by >5%, a **P0120 / P0123** fault is logged.

---

**Q. B (b)** A **Hall-effect wheel speed sensor** for ABS produces **80 pulses per revolution**. The wheel has a rolling diameter of **600 mm**. The vehicle moves at **120 km/h**. Calculate **(i) wheel RPM**, **(ii) sensor output frequency**, and **(iii) the time between two consecutive pulses**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Wheel RPM:** *(2.5 marks)*

Vehicle speed $v = 120 \text{ km/h} = 120 \cdot \dfrac{1000}{3600} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$

Wheel circumference $C = \pi \cdot d = \pi \cdot 0.6 = 1.885 \text{ m}$

Wheel RPM $n = \dfrac{v}{C} \cdot 60 = \dfrac{33.33}{1.885} \cdot 60 = 17.69 \cdot 60 \approx \mathbf{1061 \text{ RPM}}$

**(ii) Sensor Output Frequency:** *(2.5 marks)*

$$f = \frac{n \cdot N}{60} = \frac{1061 \cdot 80}{60} = \frac{84880}{60} \approx \mathbf{1415 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(iii) Time Between Two Consecutive Pulses:** *(2 marks)*

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1415} \approx \mathbf{707 \text{ }\mu s}$$

**Verification:** The ABS ECU must sample this signal at least 10√ó faster than $f$ per the **Nyquist criterion** plus automotive margin, i.e., ‚â• 15 kHz sample rate. Most modern ABS MCUs sample at 100 kHz to ensure sub-millisecond slip detection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up "active" and "passive" sensors** ‚Äî An *active* sensor generates its own signal (e.g., piezoelectric); a *passive* sensor modulates external excitation (e.g., thermistor). Confusing these costs a full 1-mark definition deduction. **(Q1 of Part A)**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ¬∞C to Kelvin** in the Steinhart-Hart equation. The thermistor formula uses **absolute temperature in Kelvin**, not Celsius. Inserting 90 ¬∞C directly into $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0})}$ yields a wildly wrong answer. **(Q.A(b) of Part B)**
> 3. **Not showing the full B-parameter exponent calculation** ‚Äî Examiners award partial credit for the exponent step, not just the final voltage. Always write: *‚ÄúExponent = B √ó (1/T ‚àí 1/T‚ÇÄ) = ‚Ä¶‚Äù* explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring units in RPM‚Äìfrequency conversion** ‚Äî The formula $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$ assumes *N* is teeth/rev and *n* is in RPM. Writing $n$ in rev/s without dividing by 60 is a frequent 0.5-mark error.
> 5. **Omitting the "missing tooth" or "reference gap"** when describing the CKP sensor ‚Äî this is the **key** element that synchronizes the ECU; leaving it out costs 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Writing `|x|` inside a table cell** breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead. The examiner sees a corrupted table and deducts 0.5 marks for presentation.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** when computing wheel RPM. Always state the conversion explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Four Functional Blocks:** Sensor ‚Üí Signal Conditioning ‚Üí ECU/Processor ‚Üí Actuator (+ Power Supply).
- **Sense‚ÄìProcess‚ÄìActuate** is the universal architecture; memorize this flow.
- **Active vs Passive Sensors** ‚Äî Active generates its own signal (piezo, Hall, thermocouple); passive needs excitation (thermistor, potentiometric).
- **Thermistor Equation:** $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ ‚Äî *always use Kelvin*.
- **Voltage Divider for Resistive Sensors:** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{ref}}{R_T + R_{ref}}$.
- **Hall Sensor Output:** Digital square wave; frequency = $\dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$.
- **CKP Reference Pattern:** 60-2 (or 36-1) missing-tooth pattern = once-per-revolution sync.
- **TPS Range:** 0 V (idle) to 5 V (WOT), with dual-track redundancy.
- **MAP Sensor:** Piezo-resistive Wheatstone bridge, ratiometric output 0‚Äì5 V.
- **Lambda Sensor:** Outputs 0.1 V (lean) to 0.9 V (rich) for narrow-band; 0‚Äì5 V for wide-band.
- **Wheel Speed Sensor:** 80‚Äì120 pulses/rev; ABS sample rate ‚â• 15 kHz.
- **ADC Resolution:** 10-bit (1024), 12-bit (4096); $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$.
- **PWM:** $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{total}}$; for throttle motors, $f = 25 \text{ kHz}$.
- **CAN Bus Speed:** 1 Mbit/s (high-speed CAN) and 125‚Äì500 kbit/s (low-speed CAN); LIN: 19.2 kbit/s.
- **Battery:** 12 V lead-acid, 45‚Äì80 Ah; alternator regulates to 14 ¬± 0.2 V.
- **Wiring Drop:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{I \cdot \rho \cdot L}{A}$; copper $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \text{ }\Omega\cdot m$.
- **Automotive Operating Range:** ‚àí40 ¬∞C to +125 ¬∞C (AEC-Q100 Grade 1).
- **Functional Safety:** ISO 26262 ASIL-D for steering, braking, airbag; ASIL-B for body.
- **OBD-II:** SAE J2012 fault codes; SAE J1962 diagnostic connector pinout.
- **High-Voltage EV:** 400 V or 800 V traction bus; insulation monitoring required.
- **Famous Sensors to Always Remember:** CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT, O2 (Lambda), Knock, Wheel Speed, Yaw Rate, Steering Angle, Radar, Camera, LIDAR.
- **Famous Actuators to Always Remember:** Fuel Injector, Ignition Coil, EGR Valve, Throttle BLDC, Turbo Wastegate, Glow Plug, Variable Valve Timing Solenoid, ABS Modulator, EPAS Motor, Radiator Fan.
- **Exam Mantra:** Always draw the block diagram, always state the formula in symbols *before* substituting, always convert units, always add the final sentence of **engineering interpretation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Cell** is an electrochemical energy conversion device that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (typically oxygen from air) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as the primary by-products. Unlike a conventional battery, a fuel cell does not require recharging ‚Äî it generates electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are continuously supplied, making it a true *primary energy transducer* rather than an energy storage device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Compliant):** A fuel cell is a galvanic cell in which the chemical energy of a continuously supplied fuel and oxidant is converted directly into electrical energy by means of an electrochemical reaction, with no intermediate thermal or mechanical stage. It thus bypasses the **Carnot efficiency limit** that constrains conventional heat engines.

Mathematically, the open-circuit terminal voltage of a single fuel cell is governed by the **Gibbs free energy change** of the overall cell reaction, expressed through the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

where $E^{\circ}$ is the standard cell potential, $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J¬∑mol‚Åª¬π¬∑K‚Åª¬π**), $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96,485 C¬∑mol‚Åª¬π**), and $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **conventional battery** as a *sealed lunchbox*: the food (chemical reactants) is packed inside, and once you eat it all, the box is useless until refilled (recharged).

A **fuel cell**, in contrast, is like a **kitchen stove connected to a gas pipeline**. As long as you keep feeding gas (fuel) and air (oxidant), the stove keeps producing flame (electricity). You never "empty" the stove ‚Äî it operates continuously, day and night, provided the supply lines are open.

In an **automobile context**, this means:
- A fuel-cell vehicle (FCV) can be refueled in **3‚Äì5 minutes** (like petrol cars), unlike battery EVs which take 30 minutes to several hours.
- Range is comparable to internal combustion engines (~500‚Äì700 km per tank).
- The only tailpipe emission is **water vapor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Battery** ‚Üí Energy **storage** device (finite reactants).
> - **Fuel Cell** ‚Üí Energy **conversion** device (continuous reactants).

## 1.3 Historical Context & Engineering Relevance

The fuel cell principle was first demonstrated by **Sir William Grove** in **1839** (the "Gas Battery"), but practical automotive application took nearly 170 years. In the autotronics domain, fuel cells are significant as:

1. **Auxiliary Power Units (APUs)** for heavy-duty trucks.
2. **Primary traction power** in hydrogen fuel-cell vehicles (FCVs) such as Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.
3. **Range-extenders** for battery-electric vehicles in commercial fleets.

The **standard reference potential** for the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell at **25 ¬∞C (298.15 K)** and **1 atm** pressure is **E¬∞ = 1.229 V**, which is the maximum theoretical voltage of a single cell.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ‚Äî Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

A fuel cell consists of three primary sections:

1. **Anode (Fuel Electrode / Negative Terminal):** Hydrogen fuel is supplied and oxidized.
2. **Cathode (Oxidant Electrode / Positive Terminal):** Oxygen (from air) is supplied and reduced.
3. **Electrolyte:** An ion-conducting medium separating the two electrodes (specific to fuel-cell type).

### The Half-Cell Reactions (Hydrogen-Oxygen Cell)

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**
$$
\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-
$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction):**
$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Net Cell Reaction:**
$$
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}
$$

### Why It Works ‚Äî The "How" Behind Each Step

- At the **anode**, a platinum (or similar) catalyst splits the hydrogen molecule into protons and electrons.
- The **electrolyte** allows only protons ($H^+$) to migrate to the cathode side.
- The **electrons**, being blocked by the electrolyte, are forced through the external circuit ‚Äî this flow is the **useful electric current**.
- At the **cathode**, protons, electrons, and oxygen recombine to form water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Autotronics:** The student must remember that in a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** ‚Äî the type used in automobiles ‚Äî the electrolyte is a hydrated polymer (Nafion‚Ñ¢) that conducts only $H^+$ ions.

## 2.2 Classification of Fuel Cells (High-Yield for KTU)

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | Charge Carrier | Mobile Ion | Automotive Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane) | Polymer Membrane (Nafion) | 60‚Äì100 ¬∞C | $H^+$ | Proton | **Most suitable for vehicles** |
| **AFC** (Alkaline Fuel Cell) | KOH solution | 60‚Äì250 ¬∞C | $OH^-$ | Hydroxyl | Used in space (Apollo) |
| **PAFC** (Phosphoric Acid FC) | Liquid $H_3PO_4$ | 150‚Äì220 ¬∞C | $H^+$ | Proton | Stationary power |
| **MCFC** (Molten Carbonate FC) | Molten $(Li/K)_2CO_3$ | 600‚Äì700 ¬∞C | $CO_3^{2-}$ | Carbonate | Large stationary |
| **SOFC** (Solid Oxide FC) | Yttria-stabilized zirconia | 600‚Äì1000 ¬∞C | $O^{2-}$ | Oxide ion | Heavy vehicles/APU |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ‚Äî Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Cell EMF | $E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ | J¬∑mol‚Åª¬π |
| 3 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | V (non-isothermal correction) |
| 4 | Nernst at 25 ¬∞C | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$ | V (decimal form) |
| 5 | Nernst for $H_2/O_2$ cell | $E = 1.229 - 0.000845(T - 298.15) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$ | V |
| 6 | Theoretical Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \dfrac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Practical Efficiency | $\eta_{practical} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.48} \times \eta_{th}$ | Used for $H_2/O_2$ cells |
| 8 | Current Density | $j = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A¬∑m‚Åª¬≤ or A¬∑cm‚Åª¬≤ |
| 9 | Power Density | $P_d = j \times V_{cell}$ | W¬∑cm‚Åª¬≤ |
| 10 | Stack Voltage | $V_{stack} = N \times V_{cell}$ | V (for N cells in series) |
| 11 | Faraday's First Law | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$ | Mass of substance deposited |
| 12 | Fuel Consumption Rate | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{nF} M_{H_2}$ | kg¬∑s‚Åª¬π |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constant for KTU Numerical Problems:** $1\text{ atm} = 101.325\text{ kPa}$. The standard state for the Nernst equation is **298.15 K** and **1 atm (or 101.325 kPa)** partial pressure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Traction:** PEMFC stacks of 300‚Äì400 V DC deliver 100‚Äì120 kW for passenger FCVs.
- **Efficiency Advantage:** A fuel cell vehicle achieves ~50‚Äì60% well-to-wheel efficiency versus ~20‚Äì25% for a gasoline ICE vehicle.
- **Cold-Start Limitation:** PEMFCs lose conductivity below 0 ¬∞C; thermal management is a key autotronics challenge.
- **Hydrogen Storage:** Typically 350 bar or 700 bar compressed gas tanks, monitored by sophisticated sensors.

## 2.5 Comparison Table ‚Äî Fuel Cell vs Battery vs ICE (Autotronics Perspective)

| Parameter | Fuel Cell (PEMFC) | Li-Ion Battery | Internal Combustion Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | External $H_2$ | Internal (chemical) | Petrol/Diesel |
| Refuel/Recharge Time | 3‚Äì5 min | 30 min ‚Äì 8 h | 3‚Äì5 min |
| Efficiency | 50‚Äì60% | 80‚Äì90% (tank-to-wheel) | 20‚Äì30% |
| Range | 500‚Äì700 km | 200‚Äì500 km | 600‚Äì1000 km |
| Tailpipe Emissions | $H_2O$ only | Zero | $CO_2$, $NO_x$ |
| Operating Temp | 60‚Äì100 ¬∞C | 25‚Äì45 ¬∞C | 400‚Äì800 ¬∞C (exhaust) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Relationship Between Gibbs Free Energy and Cell EMF

### Statement
The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from an electrochemical cell equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy of the cell reaction.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a generic cell reaction:

$$
aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD
$$

The Gibbs free energy change at constant temperature and pressure is given by:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

For a reversible electrochemical process, the maximum non-expansion work is the electrical work:

$$
W_{elec} = -nFE \quad \text{(per mole of reaction)}
$$

The negative sign indicates work done *by* the system. Equating this to the Gibbs free energy decrease:

$$
-nFE = \Delta G
$$

Therefore:

$$
E = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}
$$

At standard conditions ($Q = 1$, $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ}$):

$$
E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}
$$

**For the Hydrogen-Oxygen Reaction:**

$$
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)
$$

At standard conditions:
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $n = 2$ electrons
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$

$$
E^{\circ} = -\frac{(-237.13 \times 10^3)}{(2)(96{,}485)}
$$

$$
E^{\circ} = \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970} \approx 1.229 \text{ V}
$$

This matches the experimentally observed open-circuit voltage of a single $H_2/O_2$ cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** A common mistake is using $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83$ kJ/mol (enthalpy of combustion) instead of $\Delta G^{\circ}$. If $\Delta H$ is used, the resulting voltage is **1.48 V** ‚Äî this is the **Higher Heating Value (HHV) voltage**, which assumes all product water is condensed. For practical calculations with water as vapor, $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and **1.23 V** are used (Lower Heating Value basis).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic Principles

### Starting Point

We have already shown:
$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

Substituting $-\Delta G = nFE$ and $-\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**This is the Nernst Equation.**

### Application to the $H_2/O_2$ Fuel Cell

For the reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$:

$$
Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}
$$

If water is produced as pure liquid, $a_{H_2O} = 1$:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)
$$

$$
E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)
$$

### Numerical Example ‚Äî Non-Standard Pressure

**Given:** $T = 353 \text{ K}$ (80 ¬∞C), $P_{H_2} = 3 \text{ atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 1 \text{ atm}$.

$$
E = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln(3 \times 1^{1/2})
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + (0.01521) \ln(3)
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + (0.01521)(1.0986)
$$

$$
E \approx 1.229 + 0.0167 = 1.2457 \text{ V}
$$

This demonstrates how elevated pressure and temperature modify the open-circuit voltage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Theoretical Efficiency of a Fuel Cell

### Efficiency Definition

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Electrical Energy Output}}{\text{Chemical Energy Input}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}
$$

### Step-by-Step

Since $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta H - T\Delta S}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}
$$

For the $H_2/O_2$ reaction at 298 K:

- $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\Delta S^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{-48{,}700}{298} \approx -163.4 \text{ J/(mol¬∑K)}$

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{-237.13}{-285.83} = 0.8295 \approx 83\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 83 % and not 100 %?**
> Even though fuel cells avoid the Carnot limit, they are *not* 100 % efficient at room temperature because of entropy losses. The "missing" 17 % manifests as **irreversible heat** at the cell terminals. This is still vastly superior to a Carnot engine operating between the same temperatures ($\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 298/2000 \approx 85\%$ at 2000 K combustion, but real engines achieve only 25‚Äì35 %).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Voltage Loss Mechanisms (Polarization Curve)

In real operation, a fuel cell's terminal voltage drops below $E^{\circ}$ due to three irreversibilities:

| Loss Type | Symbol | Cause | Dominant Region | Approximate Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Activation Loss** | $\eta_{act}$ | Slow electrode kinetics | Low current | ~0.05‚Äì0.20 V at start |
| **Ohmic Loss** | $\eta_{ohm} = IR_{int}$ | Ionic + electronic resistance | Mid current | Linear with current |
| **Concentration Loss** | $\eta_{conc}$ | Mass-transport limit | High current | Sharp drop at limit |

The full polarization equation is:

$$
V_{cell} = E^{\circ} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}
$$

This is the **Butler-Volmer + Ohmic + Mass Transport** framework, central to fuel-cell modeling in autotronics.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem ‚Äî KTU Style

### Problem
A PEM fuel cell stack has 250 cells connected in series. Each cell operates at 0.65 V under load, with hydrogen consumption of 1.5 kg/hour. Calculate:
(a) The stack power output.
(b) The practical efficiency (assume HHV of hydrogen = 141.86 MJ/kg).
(c) The current drawn at this operating point.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 250$ cells
- $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V
- $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.5$ kg/h
- HHV = $141.86 \times 10^6$ J/kg

**(a) Stack Power:**

$$
V_{stack} = N \times V_{cell} = 250 \times 0.65 = 162.5 \text{ V}
$$

To find current, use Faraday's law. Moles of $H_2$ per second:

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{1.5}{3600 \times 0.002} = 0.2083 \text{ mol/s}
$$

Each mole of $H_2$ produces 2 moles of electrons:

$$
I = n F \dot{n}_{H_2} = 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.2083 = 40{,}196 \text{ A}
$$

$$
P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I = 162.5 \times 40{,}196 \approx 6.53 \text{ MW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Realistic KTU Note:** In practice, fuel-cell stacks produce 50‚Äì120 kW, not MW. The above is an ideal example to test numerical skill; in actual FCVs, current densities are kept at ~0.5‚Äì1.5 A/cm¬≤ and active areas are ~200‚Äì400 cm¬≤ per cell.

**(b) Practical Efficiency:**

Energy input rate:

$$
P_{in} = \dot{m} \times \text{HHV} = \frac{1.5}{3600} \times 141.86 \times 10^6 = 59.1 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{65{,}318}{59{,}100} \approx 1.10
$$

This gives > 100 %, indicating our assumed numbers are unphysical ‚Äî confirms the warning above. For real numbers, use:

$$
\eta_{practical} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.48} = \frac{0.65}{1.48} \approx 43.9\%
$$

**(c) Current (Recomputed for realistic stack):**

For a 100 kW FCV stack at 250 V:

$$
I = \frac{100{,}000}{162.5} \approx 615 \text{ A}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen Fuel Cell ‚Äî Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2Tank[Hydrogen Supply Tank] -->|Pressurized H2| AnodeInlet[Anode Flow Plate]
    AirComp[Air Compressor and Filter] -->|Filtered Air| CathodeInlet[Cathode Flow Plate]
    AnodeInlet --> AnodeElectrode[Anode Electrode with Pt Catalyst]
    CathodeInlet --> CathodeElectrode[Cathode Electrode with Pt Catalyst]
    PEM[Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA Nafion]:::core
    AnodeElectrode --> PEM
    CathodeElectrode --> PEM
    PEM --> ProtonFlow[H plus ions migrate across membrane]
    AnodeElectrode -->|Electrons forced| ExternalCircuit[External Circuit DC Load]
    ExternalCircuit --> CathodeElectrode
    CathodeElectrode --> WaterOut[Water and Heat Output]
    ThermalMgmt[Thermal Management System]:::support
    ThermalMgmt --> PEM
    ThermalMgmt --> ExternalCircuit
    ControlUnit[ECU and BMS Interface]:::support
    ControlUnit --> AirComp
    ControlUnit --> H2Tank
    ControlUnit --> ThermalMgmt

    classDef core fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Fuel Cell Stack ‚Äî Series Connection Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cell1[Cell 01]
        A1[Anode] -->|H2 in| M1[Membrane]
        M1 --> C1[Cathode]
        C1 -->|H2O out| A1
    end
    subgraph Cell2[Cell 02]
        A2[Anode] --> M2[Membrane]
        M2 --> C2[Cathode]
    end
    subgraph Cell3[Cell 03]
        A3[Anode] --> M3[Membrane]
        M3 --> C3[Cathode]
    end
    C1 -->|Bipolar plate| A2
    C2 -->|Bipolar plate| A3
    C3 -->|Stack terminal positive| BusPos[Positive Bus]
    A1 -->|Stack terminal negative| BusNeg[Negative Bus]
    BusPos --> Load[Vehicle Traction Motor Inverter Battery Buffer]
    BusNeg --> Load
```

## 4.3 Polarization Curve ‚Äî Voltage vs Current Density

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low current density region]:::reg1 -->|Activation loss dominant| B[Mid range linear region]:::reg2
    B -->|Ohmic loss dominant| C[High current density region]:::reg3
    C -->|Concentration loss kicks in| D[Limiting current collapse]:::reg4

    classDef reg1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#060,stroke-width:1px
    classDef reg2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
    classDef reg3 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef reg4 fill:#cc0000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** As current density rises, cell voltage drops in three distinct regions ‚Äî activation, ohmic, and concentration ‚Äî culminating in a limiting current where mass transport fails.

## 4.4 Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCV) ‚Äî System-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H2Storage[H2 Storage Tank 350 or 700 bar] --> Reg[Pressure Regulator]
    Reg --> FCStack[PEMFC Stack 100 kW class]
    AirIntake[Air Intake and Humidifier] --> FCStack
    FCStack -->|DC high voltage 300 to 400 V| DCBus[DC Bus Capacitor]
    DCBus --> Inverter[DC to AC Inverter]
    Inverter --> Motor[Three Phase Traction Motor]
    Motor --> Wheels[Drive Wheels]
    DCBus --> AuxBat[Auxiliary Li Ion Battery Buffer]
    AuxBat -->|Bidirectional DC DC| DCBus
    FCStack --> Thermal[Thermal Management Radiator Coolant Pump]
    Thermal --> FCStack
    ECU[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]:::ctrl
    ECU --> FCStack
    ECU --> Inverter
    ECU --> AuxBat
    ECU --> Thermal
    ECU --> Reg

    classDef ctrl fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q: Define a fuel cell. How does it differ fundamentally from a primary battery?**

**Model Answer:**

A fuel cell is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of continuously supplied fuel (typically hydrogen) and oxidant (typically oxygen) directly into electricity through an electrochemical reaction, producing water and heat as by-products.

**Fundamental Differences:**

| Feature | Fuel Cell | Primary Battery |
|---|---|---|
| Reactant supply | Continuous (external) | Built-in (finite) |
| Energy type | Conversion device | Storage device |
| Refueling | Replace fuel supply | Replace entire cell |
| Operating life | Indefinite (with maintenance) | Limited to stored charge |
| Recharging needed | No | No (primary); Yes (secondary) |

A fuel cell does not run down or require recharging ‚Äî it produces energy as long as fuel is fed, whereas a primary battery exhausts its internal reactants and must be discarded.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q: State the Nernst equation for the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
E = 1.229 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)
$$

**Significance of Each Term:**
- **E** = Actual cell voltage under operating conditions (V).
- **E¬∞** = Standard EMF = 1.229 V at 25 ¬∞C, 1 atm.
- **R** = Universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K).
- **T** = Absolute temperature (K).
- **F** = Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol.
- **2** = Number of electrons transferred per mole of $H_2$.
- **$P_{H_2}, P_{O_2}$** = Partial pressures of hydrogen and oxygen (atm).

**Engineering significance:** Increasing reactant pressure raises cell voltage logarithmically ‚Äî this justifies the use of high-pressure $H_2$ tanks (350/700 bar) in fuel-cell vehicles.

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). State the half-cell and overall reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell operates at 80 ¬∞C and 5 atm pressure for both reactants. Calculate the open-circuit voltage using the Nernst equation.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of PEMFC [7 Marks]**

**Construction (Diagram Description):**

```
[ H2 In ] ‚Üí [ Anode Flow Plate ] ‚Üí [ Anode Catalyst (Pt) ] ‚Üí [ PEM (Nafion) ] ‚Üí [ Cathode Catalyst (Pt) ] ‚Üí [ Cathode Flow Plate ] ‚Üí [ Air In ] ‚Üí [ H2O Out ]
              ‚Üë_______________________|                                                                              |
                              Electrons forced through external circuit (load) ‚Üê‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî‚Äî|
```

Key Components:
1. **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA):** Nafion‚Ñ¢ polymer membrane (50‚Äì175 ¬µm thick) sandwiched between two Pt-loaded carbon electrodes.
2. **Bipolar Plates:** Graphite or metal plates with machined flow channels for $H_2$ and air distribution; also serve as current collectors.
3. **Gaskets:** Seal the cell against gas leakage.
4. **End Plates:** Provide mechanical compression and house inlet/outlet manifolds.

**Working:**
- Pressurized $H_2$ enters the anode flow plate and diffuses through the gas diffusion layer (GDL) to the Pt catalyst.
- At the catalyst, $H_2$ molecules split into protons and electrons (oxidation).
- Protons migrate through the hydrated Nafion membrane toward the cathode.
- Electrons are blocked by the membrane and travel through the external circuit, producing useful current.
- At the cathode, $O_2$ from air combines with arriving protons and electrons to form $H_2O$ (reduction).
- Water is removed from the cathode side; heat is managed by a cooling loop.

**Reactions:**

Anode (Oxidation):
$$
H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-
$$

Cathode (Reduction):
$$
\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O
$$

Overall:
$$
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $T = 80¬∞C = 353 K$
- $P_{H_2} = P_{O_2} = 5 \text{ atm}$
- $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}$ at 298 K

**Nernst Equation:**

$$
E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)
$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Substitute values:**

$$
E = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln(5 \times 5^{1/2})
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Simplify argument:**

$$
5 \times 5^{1/2} = 5^{3/2} = \sqrt{125} \approx 11.180
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute natural log:**

$$
\ln(11.180) \approx 2.4149
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute prefactor:**

$$
\frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} \approx 0.01521
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Final calculation:**

$$
E = 1.229 + (0.01521)(2.4149)
$$

$$
E = 1.229 + 0.03673
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 1.266 \text{ V}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing reactant pressure from 1 atm to 5 atm raises the open-circuit voltage from 1.229 V to approximately 1.266 V.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the five major types of fuel cells (PEMFC, AFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC) in terms of electrolyte, operating temperature, charge carrier, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define fuel cell efficiency. The standard enthalpy of combustion of hydrogen is ‚àí285.83 kJ/mol and Gibbs free energy change is ‚àí237.13 kJ/mol at 25 ¬∞C. Calculate (i) the theoretical efficiency, and (ii) the standard cell EMF.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Fuel Cell Types [7 Marks]**

*(Refer to Section 2.2 table for full data)*

**Key Points to Write:**

1. **PEMFC** ‚Äî Polymer electrolyte (Nafion), 60‚Äì100 ¬∞C, $H^+$ ion, **best for automotive traction**.
2. **AFC** ‚Äî KOH (alkaline), 60‚Äì250 ¬∞C, $OH^-$ ion, **Apollo spacecraft** (high purity $H_2$ and $O_2$ required).
3. **PAFC** ‚Äî Phosphoric acid, 150‚Äì220 ¬∞C, $H^+$ ion, **stationary power plants**.
4. **MCFC** ‚Äî Molten carbonate, 600‚Äì700 ¬∞C, $CO_3^{2-}$ ion, **large-scale stationary power**.
5. **SOFC** ‚Äî Yttria-stabilized zirconia (solid), 600‚Äì1000 ¬∞C, $O^{2-}$ ion, **heavy vehicles, APUs**.

**Distinguishing features for autotronics:**
- Only **PEMFC** has the low-temperature fast-start capability required for cars.
- MCFC and SOFC are unsuitable due to slow startup and thermal-shock fragility.

**(b) Efficiency and EMF Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Efficiency Definition:**

The theoretical efficiency of a fuel cell is the ratio of useful electrical energy output (Gibbs free energy change) to the total chemical energy input (enthalpy change) of the fuel:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}}
$$

**(i) Theoretical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{-237.13}{-285.83} = 0.8295
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 82.95\%}
$$

**(ii) Standard Cell EMF:**

Using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ with $n = 2$:

$$
E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = -\frac{(-237.13 \times 10^3)}{(2)(96{,}485)}
$$

$$
E^{\circ} = \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970}
$$

$$
\boxed{E^{\circ} \approx 1.229 \text{ V}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Despite being an electrochemical device, the fuel cell cannot exceed 83 % efficiency at room temperature because of entropy generation ($\Delta S < 0$). This 17 % "loss" appears as waste heat, but is still vastly superior to internal combustion engines operating on the same fuel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\Delta G$ with $\Delta H$:** Many students compute $E^{\circ}$ using $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83$ kJ/mol, getting 1.48 V. For the standard hydrogen-oxygen cell at 25 ¬∞C producing **liquid water**, the correct value is **1.229 V** using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13$ kJ/mol. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Using $n = 1$ instead of $n = 2$:** Hydrogen oxidation releases 2 electrons per molecule. Using $n = 1$ halves the EMF and inflates the current by 2√ó ‚Äî losing **1‚Äì2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting temperature units:** Plugging 80 instead of 353 K in the Nernst equation produces a wildly incorrect voltage. Convert to Kelvin **before** substituting.
> 4. **Reversing the Nernst sign:** Some students write $E = E^{\circ} + (RT/nF)\ln Q$ instead of minus. This gives the wrong voltage sign convention; examiners will deduct **1 mark**.
> 5. **Omitting partial pressure exponents:** The reaction quotient for $O_2$ must include $P_{O_2}^{1/2}$ (not $P_{O_2}$). Mark loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap ‚Äî Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **fuel cell** is an *energy conversion* device, not a storage device ‚Äî fuel and oxidant are fed continuously.
- [x] The standard hydrogen-oxygen cell EMF at 25 ¬∞C is **E¬∞ = 1.229 V** (using $\Delta G^{\circ}$, liquid water product).
- [x] Higher Heating Value (HHV) voltage is **1.48 V** (using $\Delta H^{\circ}$); never confuse the two.
- [x] The **Nernst equation** is $E = E^{\circ} - (RT/nF)\ln Q$ ‚Äî fundamental for non-standard conditions.
- [x] For the $H_2/O_2$ cell, **$n = 2$** electrons per mole of $H_2$.
- [x] **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; **R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**.
- [x] The **theoretical efficiency** at 25 ¬∞C is **$\eta_{th} = \Delta G/\Delta H \approx 83\%$**.
- [x] For PEMFC (used in cars): electrolyte is **Nafion**, charge carrier is **$H^+$**, temperature 60‚Äì100 ¬∞C.
- [x] A typical FCV stack voltage is **300‚Äì400 V** (250‚Äì350 cells in series); each cell operates near **0.6‚Äì0.7 V** under load.
- [x] Three voltage losses in a real cell: **activation**, **ohmic**, **concentration** ‚Äî collectively called *polarization*.
- [x] Only **water and heat** are the tailpipe products of a hydrogen FCV (zero $CO_2$ emissions at point of use).
- [x] Fuel cells **bypass the Carnot limit** but are not 100 % efficient ‚Äî entropy loss at 25 ¬∞C is the unavoidable 17 %.
- [x] **High-pressure $H_2$ storage** (350 or 700 bar) is standard in modern FCVs to improve cell voltage and energy density.
- [x] Cold-start below 0 ¬∞C is a key challenge for PEMFC ‚Äî requires thermal management and membrane humidification strategies.
- [x] Fuel-cell vehicles: **Toyota Mirai** (1st generation 2014, 2nd generation 2020), **Hyundai Nexo** are commercial benchmarks.
- [x] KTU exam trap: writing "fuel cells produce pollution" ‚Äî they produce only **water vapor**; pollution, if any, is upstream in $H_2$ production (grey/blue/green hydrogen).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electric Vehicle # ‚ö° ELECTRIC VEHICLE (EV) ‚Äî CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose **propulsion system** is entirely or partially driven by one or more **electric motors**, using electrical energy stored in an onboard energy storage system (typically a rechargeable battery, supercapacitor, or fuel cell) and managed by a dedicated **power electronic controller** and **Battery Management System (BMS)**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PCMRT602 syllabus, an EV is defined as an integration of the following subsystems: 1. **Energy Storage System (ESS)** ‚Äî Traction battery pack. 2. **Electric Traction Motor** ‚Äî BLDC, PMSM, or Induction motor. 3. **Power Electronic Converter/Inverter** ‚Äî DC‚ÄìDC converter and DC‚ÄìAC inverter. 4. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** ‚Äî Master controller. 5. **Onboard Charger (OBC)** ‚Äî AC-to-DC conversion. 6. **Regenerative Braking System** ‚Äî Energy recovery mechanism. The industry standard SAE J1772 defines EV charging levels into **AC Level 1 (1.4 kW)**, **AC Level 2 (19.2 kW)**, and **DC Fast Charging (50‚Äì350 kW)**. ## 1.2 Classification of Electric Vehicles (KTU Board Essential Table) | Category | Abbreviation | Energy Source | Grid Plug-in | Range Extender | |----------|-------------|---------------|--------------|----------------| | Battery Electric Vehicle | **BEV** | Battery Only | Yes | No | | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | **HEV** | Petrol + Battery | No | ICE | | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | **PHEV** | Petrol + Battery | Yes | ICE + Battery | | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | **FCEV** | Hydrogen Fuel Cell | No (H‚ÇÇ Refuel) | Fuel Cell | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Giant Smartphone on Wheels" üçé Imagine your **smartphone**. It has: - A **battery** (energy storage) üì±üîã - A **power management chip** (controls current flow) - A **charging circuit** (converts wall power to battery-friendly DC) Now imagine a smartphone the size of a **Tata Nexon EV** with 100√ó bigger battery, 1000√ó bigger motor, and intelligent software to decide when to accelerate, brake, or recharge. **That is an Electric Vehicle!** > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an EV as a *closed-loop energy ecosystem*. Unlike a petrol car that throws away energy as heat (via exhaust), an EV **recycles** braking energy back into its battery (regenerative braking). This is why EVs can achieve 90%+ drivetrain efficiency versus 30‚Äì40% for petrol cars. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics You Must Memorize - **Energy Density of Petrol:** ‚âà **12,000 Wh/kg** (the reason EVs struggle with range). - **Energy Density of Li-ion Battery:** ‚âà **150‚Äì250 Wh/kg**. - **EV Drivetrain Efficiency:** **85%‚Äì95%** (vs ICE at 25‚Äì35%). - **Regenerative Braking Efficiency:** **30%‚Äì70%** energy recovery rate. # üî¨ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Architecture of a Pure Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) A BEV consists of **five interconnected subsystems** forming a tightly-coupled electro-mechanical chain: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Flow Equation (KTU Board Favorite):** > > $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{battery} \cdot \eta_{converter} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$$ ### Step-by-Step Energy Flow Logic: 1. **Battery Pack (400V‚Äì800V DC)** stores electrochemical energy. 2. **DC-DC Converter** steps voltage down to **12V/48V** for auxiliary loads (lights, ECU). 3. **DC-AC Inverter** converts DC into **3-phase AC** for the traction motor. 4. **Traction Motor (BLDC/PMSM)** converts electrical energy into **mechanical rotational energy**. 5. **Transmission (Single-speed reducer)** transfers torque to wheels. 6. **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** ‚Äî acts as the **"brain"** controlling all subsystems via **CAN bus**. ## 2.2 Electric Motor Selection for EVs (Comparative Analysis) | Motor Type | Power Density | Efficiency | Cost | Used In | |------------|--------------|-----------|------|---------| | **BLDC Motor** | High | 85‚Äì90% | Moderate | Low-speed EVs, 2-wheelers | | **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** | Very High | 92‚Äì97% | High (rare-earth magnets) | Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon | | **Induction Motor (ACIM)** | Moderate | 90‚Äì93% | Low (no magnets) | Tesla Model S (older), Audi e-tron | | **SRM (Switched Reluctance Motor)** | Moderate | 85‚Äì90% | Very Low | Renault, Research EVs | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** PMSM is the **gold standard** for modern EVs because of its superior **torque density** and **field-weakening capability** at high speeds. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Used For | |---|---------|---------|-------|----------| | 1 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical Output Power | W | Motor shaft power | | 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$ | Torque from RPM | Nm | Vehicle dynamics | | 3 | $E_{battery} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{Ah}$ | Battery Stored Energy | Wh | Range estimation | | 4 | $V_{range} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{\text{Wh/km}}$ | Vehicle Range | km | Trip planning | | 5 | $F_{tractive} = F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{acceleration}$ | Total Tractive Force | N | Acceleration calc | | 6 | $F_{rolling} = \mu_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | Rolling Resistance | N | Force balance | | 7 | $F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | Aerodynamic Drag | N | High-speed resistance | | 8 | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Grade Climbing Force | N | Hill climbing | | 9 | $F_{acceleration} = m \cdot a$ | Linear Acceleration Force | N | 0‚Äì100 km/h analysis | | 10 | $P_{regen} = \tau_{brake} \cdot \omega \cdot \eta_{gen}$ | Regenerative Power | W | Brake energy recovery | | 11 | $\eta_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{DC,stored}}{E_{AC,consumed}}$ | Charging Efficiency | % | OBC performance | | 12 | $SOC(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \cdot 100$ | State of Charge | % | BMS estimation | | 13 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}}$ | Discharge Rate | h‚Åª¬π | Battery stress analysis | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Lockdown:** In the table above, all conditions such as **vertical bars**, **sets**, and **boundary conditions** are written using `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage. NEVER use raw `|` inside table cells. ## 2.4 The "Why & How" Behind EV Components - **Why Inverter?** Because batteries give **DC**, but motors need **AC** (PMSM/BLDC are 3-phase AC machines). The inverter uses **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** with MOSFETs/IGBTs. - **Why BMS?** To prevent **thermal runaway**, balance cell voltages, and estimate **State of Charge (SOC)** using **Coulomb Counting**. - **Why Regenerative Braking?** To convert kinetic energy (lost as heat in conventional brakes) back into electrical energy, extending range by **10%‚Äì30%**. # üìê STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vehicle Range from Battery Energy ### Problem Statement Given a **60 kWh battery**, an EV consuming **150 Wh/km**, compute the **maximum theoretical range**. ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation The fundamental relationship governing EV range is: $$E_{stored} = E_{consumed}$$ Let: - $E_{batt}$ = Battery energy capacity (Wh) - $E_{cons}$ = Energy consumption rate (Wh/km) - $R$ = Range (km) $$\begin{aligned} E_{batt} &= E_{cons} \cdot R \\ R &= \dfrac{E_{batt}}{E_{cons}} \end{aligned}$$ Substituting the given numerical values: $$\begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{60{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{150 \text{ Wh/km}} \\ &= 400 \text{ km} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer:** Maximum theoretical range = **400 km**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Tractive Force on a Climbing EV ### Problem Statement A **1500 kg EV** climbs a **10¬∞ incline** at **60 km/h** on a flat road with **rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.015$**, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$, frontal area $A = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, and air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Compute the **total tractive force required**. ### Step-by-Step Solution The total tractive force equation (KTU board standard) is: $$F_{tractive} = F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}$$ Assuming **constant speed**, $F_{accel} = 0$. ### Step 1: Rolling Resistance $$\begin{aligned} F_{rolling} &= \mu_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) \\ &= 0.015 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(10^{\circ}) \\ &= 0.015 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9848 \\ &= 217.39 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2: Aerodynamic Drag Convert 60 km/h to m/s: $$\begin{aligned} v &= 60 \cdot \dfrac{5}{18} = 16.67 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ Now compute drag: $$\begin{aligned} F_{aero} &= 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2} \\ &= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (16.67)^{2} \\ &= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.3 \cdot 277.89 \\ &= 109.65 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Grade Climbing Force $$\begin{aligned} F_{grade} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) \\ &= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(10^{\circ}) \\ &= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1736 \\ &= 2555.04 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Total Tractive Force $$\begin{aligned} F_{tractive} &= 217.39 + 109.65 + 2555.04 \\ &= 2882.08 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer:** $F_{tractive} \approx \mathbf{2882 \text{ N}}$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Motor Power ### Step-by-Step $$\begin{aligned} P_{motor} &= F_{tractive} \cdot v \\ &= 2882.08 \text{ N} \cdot 16.67 \text{ m/s} \\ &= 48{,}043 \text{ W} \approx 48 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice that the grade component contributes **88.6%** of the total force. This proves why **hill climbing dominates** EV energy consumption. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery ### Problem Statement A 1500 kg EV decelerates from **60 km/h to 30 km/h**. Compute the **recovered energy** assuming 60% regen efficiency. ### Step-by-Step Initial KE: $$\begin{aligned} KE_i &= 0.5 \cdot m \cdot v_i^2 \\ &= 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot (16.67)^2 \\ &= 208{,}418 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ Final KE: $$\begin{aligned} KE_f &= 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot (8.33)^2 \\ &= 52{,}049 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ Kinetic energy available for recovery: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta KE &= KE_i - KE_f \\ &= 208{,}418 - 52{,}049 \\ &= 156{,}369 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$ Energy actually recovered: $$\begin{aligned} E_{regen} &= \Delta KE \cdot \eta_{regen} \\ &= 156{,}369 \cdot 0.60 \\ &= 93{,}821 \text{ J} \\ &= 26.06 \text{ Wh} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer:** Regenerative braking recovers **‚âà 26 Wh** per braking event. # üèóÔ∏è STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 BEV Powertrain Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery Pack
400V 800V DC] -->|High Voltage DC| B(DC DC Converter) A -->|High Voltage DC| C(Inverter DC to AC 3 phase) B -->|Low Voltage DC| D[Auxiliary Loads
12V 48V] C -->|3 phase AC PWM| E[Traction Motor
PMSM BLDC] E -->|Mechanical Torque| F[Single Speed
Reducer Transmission] F -->|Drive Shaft| G[Wheels] H[VCU Vehicle
Control Unit] -->|CAN Bus| A H -->|PWM Control| C H -->|Torque Command| E I[Brake Pedal
Accelerator] -->|Sensor Signal| H G -.->|Regen Current| C C -.->|Charging Battery| A styleA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 EV Classification Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Vehicle Type] --> Q1{External Charging
Required?} Q1 -->|Yes| A1[BEV or PHEV] Q1 -->|No| A2{ICE Present?} A2 -->|Yes| B1[HEV] A2 -->|No| B2[FCEV] A1 --> Q2{ICE Backup?} Q2 -->|Yes| C1[PHEV] Q2 -->|No| C2[BEV] styleA0 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleC2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleC1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleB1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleB2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Battery Management System (BMS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB CellA[Cell 1] --> SM1[Slave Monitor] CellB[Cell 2] --> SM1 CellC[Cell n] --> SM1 SM1 -->|Cell Voltage Temperature| MA1[Master BMS] MA1 -->|SOC SOH Estimate| VCU2[VCU] MA1 -->|Thermal Control| TMS2[Thermal Management] MA1 -->|Cell Balancing| Bal[Active Passive
Balancer] MA1 -->|Protection| Relay[Contactor Relay] Relay -->|Open on Fault| BattLink[Pack Isolation] styleMA1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff styleVCU2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px styleBattLink fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery Loop ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Driver participant Brake as Brake Pedal participant VCU3 as VCU Controller participant Motor as Traction Motor participant Inverter3 as Inverter participant Batt as Battery Pack Driver->>Brake: Presses Brake Pedal Brake->>VCU3: Brake Pressure Signal VCU3->>Motor: Switch to Generator Mode Motor->>Inverter3: 3 phase AC Generated Inverter3->>Batt: Rectified DC Charging Batt-->>VCU3: SOC Updated via CAN VCU3-->>Driver: Brake Force Blended with Regen ``` # üìù KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## ‚úÖ PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]** Define Electric Vehicle. List any **four major components** of a BEV. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** An Electric Vehicle is a vehicle propelled by one or more electric motors using electrical energy stored in an onboard rechargeable battery, controlled by a power electronic converter and managed by a Vehicle Control Unit (VCU). > > **Four Major Components (2 marks ‚Äî 0.5 each):** > 1. **Traction Battery Pack** ‚Äì Energy storage (Li-ion). > 2. **Power Inverter (DC‚ÄìAC)** ‚Äì Converts DC battery supply to 3-phase AC. > 3. **Traction Motor (PMSM/BLDC)** ‚Äì Converts electrical to mechanical energy. > 4. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** ‚Äì Supervisory controller using CAN communication. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **BEV, HEV, and PHEV**. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | BEV | HEV | PHEV | |---------|-----|-----|------| | Energy Source | Battery only | Petrol + Battery | Petrol + Battery | | External Charging | Yes | No | Yes | | Range | Battery limited | Long (ICE backup) | Very long | | Example | Tata Nexon EV | Toyota Camry Hybrid | Toyota Prius Prime | > **Conclusion (1 mark):** BEV is **zero-emission**, HEV is **non-plug-in hybrid**, and PHEV offers **dual flexibility**. --- ## ‚úÖ PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)**. List the functions of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **expression for tractive force** required to propel an EV. List all resistive forces. **(7 Marks)** --- #### üîπ Model Solution for (a) ‚Äî BEV Architecture (7 Marks) **Step 1 ‚Äî Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Refer to the BEV powertrain architecture shown in **SECTION 4.1** above. **Step 2 ‚Äî Function of Each Block (4 Marks ‚Äî 1 each):** 1. **Battery Pack (400‚Äì800V DC):** Stores electrochemical energy; delivers high voltage DC to inverter. 2. **DC‚ÄìDC Converter:** Steps down high-voltage DC to 12V/48V for auxiliaries. 3. **Inverter (DC‚ÄìAC):** Converts DC into variable-frequency 3-phase AC for the motor using IGBTs/MOSFETs. 4. **Traction Motor:** Converts electrical energy into mechanical torque; acts as generator during regen braking. 5. **VCU + CAN Bus:** Acts as the master brain; receives accelerator/brake signals and commands inverter & motor. --- #### üîπ Model Solution for (b) ‚Äî Tractive Force Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 ‚Äî Statement (1 Mark):** The total tractive force $F_{tractive}$ must overcome all opposing forces to maintain vehicle motion. **Step 2 ‚Äî Equation (3 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} F_{tractive} &= F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Individual Forces (3 Marks ‚Äî 0.75 each):** $$\begin{aligned} F_{rolling} &= \mu_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) \\ F_{aero} &= 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot v^{2} \\ F_{grade} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) \\ F_{accel} &= m \cdot a \end{aligned}$$ > **Final Conclusion:** The motor must deliver torque ‚â• $F_{tractive} \cdot r_{wheel}$ to maintain motion. --- ### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **working principle of regenerative braking** in EVs. Derive the expression for **recovered energy**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare **BLDC, PMSM, and Induction Motors** for EV applications on the basis of efficiency, cost, and maintenance. **(7 Marks)** --- #### üîπ Model Solution for (a) ‚Äî Regenerative Braking (7 Marks) **Step 1 ‚Äî Concept (2 Marks):** During deceleration, the traction motor is electronically switched to operate as a **generator**. The kinetic energy of the moving vehicle is converted into electrical energy, which is fed back to the battery via the inverter (now acting as rectifier). **Step 2 ‚Äî Derivation (3 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} E_{regen} &= 0.5 \cdot m \cdot (v_i^2 - v_f^2) \cdot \eta_{regen} \end{aligned}$$ Where $\eta_{regen}$ = overall regen efficiency (motor + inverter + charging). **Step 3 ‚Äî Typical Values (2 Marks):** - $\eta_{regen} = 50\%‚Äì70\%$ in modern EVs. - Range extension = **10%‚Äì30%**. - Example: Tesla recovers up to **30 kW** during aggressive braking. --- #### üîπ Model Solution for (b) ‚Äî Motor Comparison (7 Marks) | Parameter | BLDC | PMSM | Induction Motor | |-----------|------|------|-----------------| | Efficiency | 85‚Äì90% | **92‚Äì97%** | 90‚Äì93% | | Cost | Moderate | High (rare-earth) | **Low** | | Maintenance | Low | Low | Moderate (bearings) | | Power Density | High | **Very High** | Moderate | | Cooling | Easy | Complex | Complex | | Example Use | 2-wheelers | Tesla Model 3 | Tesla Model S (old) | > **Conclusion (1 Mark):** **PMSM is preferred** for premium EVs due to highest efficiency; **BLDC is preferred** for low-cost 2-wheelers; **Induction motors** offer a low-cost alternative. --- ## ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers ‚Äî Always write N, Wh, km, m/s explicitly. > 2. **Mixing up** battery **Energy (Wh)** with **Charge (Ah)** ‚Äî multiply by voltage. > 3. **Sign error in grade force** ‚Äî Use $+F_{grade}$ for uphill, $-F_{grade}$ for downhill (regen). > 4. **Not converting km/h to m/s** ‚Äî KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this. > 5. **Skipping the VCU** in BEV diagrams ‚Äî 1 mark is reserved for supervisory controller. > 6. **Conflating BMS with Battery** ‚Äî BMS is a *controller*, battery is a *storage device*. --- ## üßÝ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - ‚úÖ **EV = Battery + Motor + Inverter + VCU + OBC** ‚Äî remember all 5 blocks for diagrams. - ‚úÖ **PMSM is the gold standard** motor for modern EVs. - ‚úÖ **Li-ion battery** is the dominant ESS technology (150‚Äì250 Wh/kg). - ‚úÖ **Tractive Force Equation** is the most-tested formula in KTU exams. - ‚úÖ **Regenerative braking** can extend range by **10%‚Äì30%**. - ‚úÖ **Always convert km/h ‚Üí m/s** by multiplying $\dfrac{5}{18}$. - ‚úÖ **CAN bus** is the communication backbone between VCU and subsystems. - ‚úÖ **BMS** prevents thermal runaway, balances cells, and estimates SOC. - ‚úÖ **EV efficiency (85‚Äì95%)** vs **ICE efficiency (25‚Äì35%)** ‚Äî explain this difference in Part A. - ‚úÖ **Standard EV charging levels:** AC L1 (1.4 kW), AC L2 (19.2 kW), DC Fast (50‚Äì350 kW). - ‚úÖ **Difference between BEV and PHEV:** BEV has NO ICE; PHEV has BOTH ICE + battery. - ‚úÖ **Formula $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{conv} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}$** ‚Äî multiply individual efficiencies for total.

topics.title: Hybrid Vehicle: Series Hybrid Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Vehicle: Series Hybrid Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**: A hybrid electric vehicle powertrain architecture in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **mechanically decoupled** from the driving wheels. The engine drives a **generator** exclusively, producing electrical energy that is conditioned by a power electronics inverter, stored in a **traction battery pack**, and ultimately delivered to an **electric traction motor** that propels the vehicle through a (typically single-speed) transmission.

In this configuration, there is **no mechanical pathway** between the prime mover (engine) and the road wheels. The ICE, the generator, and the traction motor are connected in a **strictly series electrical/driveline chain**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Diesel-Electric Locomotive

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Series Hybrid exactly like a diesel-electric locomotive used by Indian Railways.**

In a train engine, the massive diesel engine does not turn the wheels directly. Instead, the diesel engine spins a generator, producing electricity. That electricity is then routed to huge electric traction motors mounted on each axle, which finally turn the wheels. The diesel engine simply acts as an **on-board power plant** (a "range extender"). A Series Hybrid Vehicle works on the *identical* principle ‚Äî just scaled down for road use. Even when the engine is running, you can lift the bonnet and you'll find **no driveshaft** going to the wheels.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Distinction

| Parameter | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid |
|---|---|---|
| Engine-to-Wheel Path | **Electrical only** | Mechanical (direct) + Electrical |
| Transmission Type | Single-speed reduction gear | Multi-speed or CVT possible |
| Engine Size (typical) | Smaller (driven at optimal point) | Larger (must match road loads) |
| Complexity of Control | High (energy management) | Moderate (torque blending) |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in SHEV Analysis

- **Battery State of Charge (SOC)**: typically maintained between **30% ‚Äì 80%** for longevity.
- **Engine Operating Point**: locked to the **Best Operating Line (BOL)** on the BSFC map.
- **System DC Bus Voltage**: typically **200 V ‚Äì 450 V** in modern SHEVs.
- **Specific Energy of Traction Battery**: **100 ‚Äì 250 Wh/kg** (Li-ion).
- **Net Powertrain Efficiency (Œ∑_total)**: typically **0.55 ‚Äì 0.70**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-wise Functional Logic

A Series Hybrid powertrain contains **five mandatory subsystems** plus one optional:

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)**: Runs at its **Best Operating Line (BOL)** ‚Äî a narrow band on the Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) map where thermal efficiency peaks. Decoupled from the wheels, it can run at *constant speed* (often governed by an optimal power/rotational speed curve).
2. **Electrical Generator (G)**: A three-phase AC synchronous or induction machine. Converts mechanical energy from the ICE into electrical energy. Rated at approximately **1.0√ó to 1.2√ó** the engine's peak power.
3. **Power Electronics Inverter (DC/AC + AC/DC)**: Bidirectional. Acts as a rectifier when the generator charges the battery; acts as an inverter when the battery discharges to feed the traction motor. Uses IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs switching at **5 ‚Äì 20 kHz**.
4. **Traction Battery Pack (B)**: High-voltage Li-ion or NiMH. Acts as a **buffer (energy buffer)** ‚Äî smooths transient power demands that the slow-responding engine cannot meet instantly.
5. **Traction Motor (M)**: A high-power AC induction or PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor). Connected to wheels via a **single-speed reduction gearbox + differential**.
6. *(Optional)* **On-board Charger (OBC)**: For grid charging (PHEV variant).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Two Energy Stores" Principle**: In a Series Hybrid, there are **two energy reservoirs** ‚Äî the fuel tank (chemical) and the battery (electrochemical). The engine charges the battery, and the battery *plus* the engine both feed the motor. This dual-source topology is the hallmark of series architecture.

### 2.2 Operational Logic ‚Äî Step by Step

- **Step 1: Driver demand** ‚Üí Accelerator pedal position is interpreted as a *power request* $P_{req}$ by the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU).
- **Step 2: VCU decision** ‚Üí Determines how to split $P_{req}$ between engine power $P_e$ and battery power $P_b$.
- **Step 3: ICE output** ‚Üí Engine produces mechanical power $P_e$, generator converts to electrical $P_g = \eta_g \cdot P_e$.
- **Step 4: Battery buffer** ‚Üí Either absorbs excess ($P_g - P_{req} > 0$) or supplies deficit ($P_{req} - P_g > 0$).
- **Step 5: Motor drive** ‚Üí Traction motor draws $P_m = \eta_m^{-1} \cdot (P_g + P_b)$ and delivers tractive force.
- **Step 6: SOC management** ‚Üí When SOC < threshold (e.g., 30%), engine starts and charges battery. When SOC > threshold (e.g., 80%), engine shuts off (pure EV mode).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power balance at DC bus | $P_g + P_b = P_m / \eta_{inv}$ | kW | Steady-state, ignoring losses |
| 2 | Generator output | $P_g = \eta_g \cdot P_e$ | kW | $\eta_g \in [0.85, 0.95]$ |
| 3 | Traction power to wheels | $P_w = \eta_m \cdot \eta_t \cdot P_m$ | kW | $\eta_t$ = transmission efficiency |
| 4 | Net powertrain efficiency | $\eta_{net} = \eta_e \cdot \eta_g \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_t$ | ‚Äî | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Vehicle tractive force | $F_t = M \cdot a + \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + M g (C_r \cos\theta + \sin\theta)$ | N | Standard road-load equation |
| 6 | Power required at wheels | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | kW | $v$ in m/s |
| 7 | Battery energy balance | $E_b(t) = E_b(0) + \int_0^t (P_g - P_m/\eta_{inv}) \, d\tau$ | kWh | SOC tracking equation |
| 8 | Engine BSFC optimum | $\dot{m}_f = \frac{P_e \cdot b_{e,opt}}{3600}$ | kg/s | $b_{e,opt} \approx 220$ g/kWh (gasoline) |
| 9 | Engine turn-on SOC threshold | $SOC_{low} = 0.30$ | ‚Äî | Lower bound; do NOT discharge below |
| 10 | Engine turn-off SOC threshold | $SOC_{high} = 0.80$ | ‚Äî | Upper bound; do NOT over-charge |
| 11 | Fuel energy to electric energy | $E_{elec} = \eta_e \cdot \eta_g \cdot E_{fuel}$ | kWh | Energy conversion chain |
| 12 | Equivalent electric range extension | $\Delta R = \frac{E_{fuel} \cdot \eta_{net}}{\rho_f \cdot E_d}$ | km | $E_d$ = energy per km |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Series Hybrids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where are Series Hybrids Used?**
> - **Diesel-electric locomotives** (Indian Railways WAP, WAG series)
> - **Heavy mining dump trucks** (e.g., Caterpillar 793F, Liebherr T 284)
> - **Submarines** (diesel-electric, surface ships)
> - **Range-Extended Passenger Cars**: Chevrolet Volt Gen 1, BMW i3 (REx), Fisker Karma
> - **Series Hybrid Buses**: Volvo 7900 Electric Hybrid, Scania Citywide

The defining use-case is **any application where the engine runs at a near-constant load** for long durations, and the *duty cycle is highly variable*. The engine's freedom to operate at its sweet spot makes series hybrids **excellent for city buses** (constant stop-go) and **heavy haul trucks** (constant high torque demand).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Power Flow Equation at the DC Bus

**Starting Premise**: In a series hybrid, all electrical energy produced by the generator and discharged by the battery must, after inverter losses, equal the electrical power demanded by the traction motor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input side of DC bus:} \quad & P_{DC,in} = P_g + P_b \\[4pt]
\text{Output side of DC bus:} \quad & P_{DC,out} = \frac{P_m}{\eta_{inv}} \\[4pt]
\text{Energy conservation:} \quad & P_g + P_b = \frac{P_m}{\eta_{inv}} \\[4pt]
\text{Substituting } P_g = \eta_g \cdot P_e: \quad & \eta_g \cdot P_e + P_b = \frac{P_m}{\eta_{inv}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation**: $P_g$ is the generator's electrical output, $P_b$ is the battery's discharge power (positive) or charge power (negative), and $P_m$ is the mechanical power demanded at the motor shaft. The inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv}$ accounts for switching and conduction losses in the IGBTs.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Net Powertrain Efficiency from Fuel to Wheels

We trace energy from the fuel tank all the way to the road surface, multiplying each stage's efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{net} &= \eta_{engine} \cdot \eta_{generator} \cdot \eta_{rectifier} \cdot \eta_{battery(discharge)} \cdot \eta_{inverter} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example** (typical SHEV at optimal operating point):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{net} &= (0.38)(0.92)(0.97)(0.95)(0.96)(0.93)(0.95) \\[4pt]
&= 0.38 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.96 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \\[4pt]
&= 0.38 \times 0.7291 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.277 \;\;(27.7\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is why SHEVs use a smaller engine than a conventional car!** The conventional car engine has a peak thermal efficiency of ~38%, but only at one point; its *average* efficiency over a real drive cycle is ~20%. An SHEV runs the engine at that 38% point continuously, so even after the 73% cascading losses, you land close to the conventional car's *average* efficiency ‚Äî but with **dramatically lower emissions** at idle and under part load.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Required Engine Power for Steady Cruise

**Given**: Vehicle of mass $M = 1500$ kg, cruise speed $v = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s, on flat road ($\theta = 0$), drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m¬≤, rolling resistance coefficient $C_r = 0.012$.

**Step 1**: Compute resistive forces (since $a = 0$ and $\theta = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + M g C_r \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.30)(2.2)(27.78)^2 + (1500)(9.81)(0.012) \\[4pt]
&= 0.396 \times 771.6 + 176.6 \\[4pt]
&= 305.6 + 176.6 \\[4pt]
&= 482.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Power required at wheels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= F_t \cdot v = 482.2 \times 27.78 \approx 13.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Power required at motor shaft (back-calculating through transmission + motor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_m &= \frac{P_w}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{13.4}{0.93 \times 0.95} \approx 15.2 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Power required at generator terminals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_g &= \frac{P_m}{\eta_{inv}} = \frac{15.2}{0.96} \approx 15.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Power required at engine output shaft:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_e &= \frac{P_g}{\eta_g} = \frac{15.8}{0.92} \approx 17.2 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: For a 1500 kg vehicle cruising at 100 km/h on a flat road, a Series Hybrid only needs a **~17 kW engine** ‚Äî about **23 horsepower**! A conventional car would need ~30+ kW because its engine must cover transient peaks. This is why SHEV engines can be sized for *cruise* power, not peak power.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Fuel Consumption per 100 km

Using the result above: for steady 100 km/h cruise, $P_e \approx 17.2$ kW.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time for 100 km at 100 km/h} &= 1 \text{ h} = 3600 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
\text{Energy from fuel} &= P_e \times t = 17.2 \text{ kWh} \\[4pt]
\text{Using BSFC optimum } b_{e,opt} &= 220 \text{ g/kWh} \\[4pt]
\text{Fuel mass} &= \frac{17.2 \times 220}{1000} = 3.78 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Using gasoline density } \rho_f &= 0.745 \text{ kg/L} \\[4pt]
\text{Fuel volume} &= \frac{3.78}{0.745} \approx 5.08 \text{ L per 100 km}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 ‚Äî Battery SOC Dynamics (Discrete Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SOC(t+1) &= SOC(t) + \frac{\Delta t}{E_{b,max}} \left( P_g(t) - \frac{P_m(t)}{\eta_{inv}} \right) \\[4pt]
\text{Charge mode (engine dominant):} \quad & SOC(t+1) = SOC(t) + \frac{\Delta t \cdot \eta_{ch} \cdot P_{ch}}{E_{b,max}} \\[4pt]
\text{Discharge mode (EV dominant):} \quad & SOC(t+1) = SOC(t) - \frac{\Delta t \cdot P_{dis}}{E_{b,max} \cdot \eta_{dis}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Constraint for SOC**: $0.30 \leq SOC(t) \leq 0.80$ (operational). Exceeding these limits degrades the battery. The VCU enforces these hard limits in real time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Series Hybrid Vehicle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE / ENGINE]
    B --> C[GENERATOR]
    C --> D[RECTIFIER DC-DC]
    D --> E[DC BUS 350V]
    F[BATTERY PACK Li-ion] --> E
    E --> G[INVERTER VFD]
    G --> H[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM]
    H --> I[SINGLE SPEED REDUCTION GEAR]
    I --> J[DIFFERENTIAL]
    J --> K[ROAD WHEELS]
    L[VCU VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT] -.-> B
    L -.-> D
    L -.-> F
    L -.-> G
    M[ACCELERATOR PEDAL] --> L
    N[BRAKE PEDAL] --> L
    L -.-> H
```

### 4.2 Power Flow State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PureEV : SOC above 80 percent
    PureEV --> HybridDrive : SOC drops to 30 percent
    HybridDrive --> EngineOnly : High sustained load request
    HybridDrive --> PureEV : SOC reaches 80 percent
    EngineOnly --> HybridDrive : Load reduces
    PureEV --> RegenBraking : Brake pedal pressed
    HybridDrive --> RegenBraking : Brake pedal pressed
    RegenBraking --> PureEV : SOC less than 80 percent
    RegenBraking --> HybridDrive : SOC above 80 percent
```

### 4.3 Energy Conversion Chain (Block Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CHEMICAL Energy in Fuel] --> B[THERMAL Energy in ICE]
    B --> C[MECHANICAL Energy at Crank]
    C --> D[ELECTRICAL AC at Generator Terminals]
    D --> E[DC at Battery Bus]
    E --> F[AC at Motor Terminals]
    F --> G[MECHANICAL Energy at Motor Shaft]
    G --> H[KINETIC Energy at Wheels]
    A1[eta1 = 0.38] -.-> B
    B1[eta2 = 0.92] -.-> C
    C1[eta3 = 0.97] -.-> D
    D1[eta4 = 0.95] -.-> E
    E1[eta5 = 0.96] -.-> F
    F1[eta6 = 0.93] -.-> G
    G1[eta7 = 0.95] -.-> H
```

### 4.4 Component Coupling Matrix (How each block interacts)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Powertrain [SHEV POWERTRAIN SUBSYSTEMS]
        ICE1[ICE Controller ECU]
        GEN1[Generator Inverter GCU]
        BMS1[Battery Management System BMS]
        MCU1[Motor Control Unit MCU]
        VCU1[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    end
    subgraph Inputs [DRIVER INPUTS]
        AP1[Accelerator Pedal]
        BP1[Brake Pedal]
        GS1[Gear Selector P R N D]
    end
    AP1 --> VCU1
    BP1 --> VCU1
    GS1 --> VCU1
    VCU1 -->|Torque Request| MCU1
    VCU1 -->|Engine ON OFF| ICE1

    VCU1 -->|SOC Limits| BMS1
    BMS1 -->|SOC SOCmin SOCmax| VCU1
```
