## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]`** Define a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV). State TWO distinguishing features that separate it from a Parallel Hybrid configuration.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV) is a hybrid powertrain architecture in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) is **mechanically decoupled** from the driving wheels. The engine drives a generator only; the generated electrical energy charges a battery and powers an electric traction motor which finally drives the wheels through a single-speed transmission.

**Distinguishing features (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **No mechanical driveline** exists between engine and wheels. In a Parallel Hybrid, both the engine *and* the motor are mechanically coupled to the wheels through a torque blender or clutch.
2. The engine in an SHEV is **free to operate at its Best Operating Line (BOL)** independent of vehicle speed, since it is not connected to the wheels. In a Parallel Hybrid, the engine speed is locked to the wheel speed (modulated by gear ratios).

**Marks distribution:** Definition = 1 mark; Two features = 2 marks (1 each).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`** List the major components of a Series Hybrid powertrain and state the function of each. Why is the battery called a "buffer"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Components and functions (2 marks):**

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** ‚Üí Prime mover; converts chemical energy in fuel to mechanical energy. Operates at constant optimal point.
2. **Electrical Generator** ‚Üí Converts the mechanical output of the engine into electrical (AC) energy.
3. **Power Electronics Inverter/Rectifier** ‚Üí Conditions electrical energy between AC generator output and DC battery, and between DC bus and AC traction motor.
4. **Traction Battery Pack** ‚Üí Stores electrical energy; smooths power transients.
5. **Traction Motor** ‚Üí Converts electrical energy back into mechanical energy to propel the vehicle.

**Why battery is called a "buffer" (1 mark):**

The battery is termed a "buffer" because it absorbs the difference between the slowly-responding constant engine power output and the rapidly-varying driver power demand. When the driver demands more power than the engine can instantly supply, the battery discharges the deficit; when the engine produces excess power, the battery absorbs and stores it. It thus *buffers* (smooths) the transient mismatch.

---

### 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024 ‚Äì Module 3]`**

**Question A (14 marks):**

> (a) **With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and power flow of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle.** List the major components and describe the function of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) **A Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle has the following parameters:** Vehicle mass $M = 1400$ kg, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.32$, frontal area $A = 2.0$ m¬≤, rolling resistance coefficient $C_r = 0.013$. The vehicle is required to cruise at a constant speed of **90 km/h on a flat road**. The component efficiencies are: $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.93$, $\eta_t = 0.95$. **Determine:**
>   (i) The tractive force required at the wheels. **(2 marks)**
>   (ii) The power required at the wheels, motor, generator, and engine. **(4 marks)**
>   (iii) The net powertrain efficiency and comment on the result. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Answer ‚Äî Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

In a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle, the **power flow path is strictly series**:

$$\text{ICE} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier} \rightarrow \text{DC Bus} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

The battery pack is connected in parallel at the DC bus and acts as an energy buffer.

**Major Components (3 marks ‚Äî list and function):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Fuel tank | Stores chemical energy (gasoline/diesel) |
| ICE | Converts chemical ‚Üí mechanical energy; runs at constant optimal point |
| Generator (3-phase AC) | Converts mechanical ‚Üí electrical energy |
| Power electronics inverter (rectifier + inverter) | Conditions voltage/frequency; bidirectional AC-DC |
| Traction battery pack (Li-ion) | Stores electrical energy; smooths transients |
| Traction motor (PMSM/IM) | Converts electrical ‚Üí mechanical energy |
| Single-speed reduction gearbox + differential | Reduces speed and transmits torque to wheels |
| VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) | Supervises energy management strategy |

**Power Flow Description (2 marks):**

The driver inputs accelerator pedal position to the VCU, which interprets it as a power demand $P_{req}$. The VCU determines how much of this demand is met by the engine-generator pair ($P_g$) and how much by the battery ($P_b$). The traction motor draws $P_m = P_g + P_b$ (after inverter efficiency) and produces torque at the wheels. During braking, the motor acts as a generator and feeds $P_b$ back to the battery (regenerative braking).

---

**Model Answer ‚Äî Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $M = 1400$ kg, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.0$ m¬≤, $C_r = 0.013$
- $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s, $\theta = 0$
- $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.93$, $\eta_t = 0.95$
- Air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m¬≥

**(i) Tractive Force at wheels (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + M g C_r \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.32)(2.0)(25)^2 + (1400)(9.81)(0.013) \\[4pt]
&= 0.384 \times 625 + 178.5 \\[4pt]
&= 240 + 178.5 \\[4pt]
&= 418.5 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Power at various stages (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= F_t \cdot v = 418.5 \times 25 = 10{,}462.5 \text{ W} \approx 10.46 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
P_m &= \frac{P_w}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{10.46}{0.93 \times 0.95} = \frac{10.46}{0.8835} \approx 11.84 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
P_g &= \frac{P_m}{\eta_{inv}} = \frac{11.84}{0.96} \approx 12.33 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
P_e &= \frac{P_g}{\eta_g} = \frac{12.33}{0.92} \approx 13.40 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Net powertrain efficiency and comment (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{net} &= \eta_g \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_t = (0.92)(0.96)(0.93)(0.95) \\[4pt]
&= 0.780 \text{ (or } 78.0\%\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment**: The electrical driveline from engine shaft to wheel shaft delivers approximately **78% efficiency** ‚Äî this is excellent. The remaining 22% loss is dominated by motor/generator copper and iron losses plus inverter switching losses. The engine still wastes ~62% of its fuel energy as heat (since $\eta_e \approx 0.38$), so fuel-to-wheel net efficiency $\approx 0.38 \times 0.78 \approx 29.6\%$.

---

**Question B (14 marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice:**

> (a) Explain the concept of "engine operating at the Best Operating Line (BOL)" in a Series Hybrid. Why is this concept NOT achievable in a conventional vehicle? Discuss with the help of BSFC curves. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) **Derive** the net powertrain efficiency equation for a Series Hybrid Vehicle, identifying each loss stage. If $\eta_e = 0.36$, $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\eta_{ch} = 0.95$, $\eta_{dis} = 0.95$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.93$, $\eta_t = 0.95$, calculate the net efficiency. Compare this with the efficiency of a conventional petrol car (15‚Äì20% average over a drive cycle) and comment. **(7 marks)**

**(Key model solution points):**

- **Part (a):** BOL is the locus on the BSFC map where minimum specific fuel consumption occurs. SHEV's decoupled engine can hold this point via electronic throttle control regardless of vehicle speed. Conventional car's engine speed is tied to wheel speed via the gearbox, so it frequently operates off the BOL. Include sketch of BSFC map with isoefficiency curves (iso-BSFC contours closing around the BOL).
- **Part (b):** Show derivation as in Section 3.2. Calculate: $\eta_{net} = 0.36 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.96 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \approx 0.249$ (24.9%). Compare: SHEV delivers 25‚Äì30% net efficiency; conventional car averages 15‚Äì20%. SHEV is **25‚Äì50% more fuel-efficient** even with cascading electrical losses.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** before substituting into $F_t$ and $P_w$ equations. (‚Äì1 to ‚Äì2 marks)
> 2. **Inverting the efficiency chain**: Students often divide instead of multiply by efficiency when going *backwards* from wheels to engine. Always remember: power *decreases* as you move backward through each lossy stage.
> 3. **Ignoring rolling resistance**: Many students compute only the aerodynamic component. Both $F_{aero}$ and $F_{roll}$ must be included on a flat road.
> 4. **Confusing SOC with battery capacity units**: SOC is dimensionless (0‚Äì1 or 0‚Äì100%). Energy is in kWh.
> 5. **Drawing a mechanical shaft from the engine to the wheels in the block diagram**: This is the most critical *visual* error. In a SHEV, **NO mechanical shaft connects engine to wheels** ‚Äî only electrical wires. Examiners deduct **2‚Äì3 marks** for this error.
> 6. **Stating "Parallel Hybrid" instead of "Series Hybrid" features**: Read the question twice; misreading costs full marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ‚úÖ **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Series Hybrid Vehicle**
>
> - **Definition**: Engine is mechanically decoupled from wheels; power flows ICE ‚Üí Generator ‚Üí Battery/Motor ‚Üí Wheels.
> - **No mechanical driveline** between engine and wheels (single most distinguishing feature).
> - **Engine operates at BOL** ‚Äî constant optimal point on BSFC map; independent of vehicle speed.
> - **Battery is a "buffer"** ‚Äî absorbs transient mismatch between constant engine output and variable driver demand.
> - **Key components (7)**: ICE, Generator, Rectifier, DC Bus + Battery, Inverter, Traction Motor, Transmission.
> - **Efficiencies** (typical): $\eta_e = 0.36$‚Äì$0.40$, $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$, $\eta_m = 0.93$, $\eta_t = 0.95$.
> - **Net powertrain efficiency** (fuel ‚Üí wheel): ~25‚Äì30%, vs. ~15‚Äì20% for conventional car ‚Üí **25‚Äì50% fuel savings**.
> - **Operating modes**: Pure EV, Hybrid Drive, Engine-Only (charging), Regenerative Braking.
> - **SOC limits**: $0.30 \leq SOC \leq 0.80$ (operational envelope).
> - **Road-load equation**: $F_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + Mg(C_r\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$.
> - **Power flow**: $P_e \xrightarrow{\eta_g} P_g$, $P_g + P_b \xrightarrow{1/\eta_{inv}} P_m$, $P_m \xrightarrow{\eta_m \cdot \eta_t} P_w$.
> - **Real-world examples**: Chevrolet Volt Gen 1, BMW i3 REx, diesel-electric locomotives, Volvo 7900 hybrid bus.
> - **Best use-case**: City buses, heavy trucks, locomotives ‚Äî anywhere engine can run at near-constant load.
> - **Limitation**: Two energy conversions (mech‚Üíelec‚Üímech) introduce cumulative losses; only justified if engine *operating-point gain* exceeds those losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Hybrid Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parallel Hybrid Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Hybrid Vehicle (PHV):** A *Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)* is a class of Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) in which the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** and the **Electric Motor (EM)** are mechanically coupled in **parallel** to the driveline, such that **both power sources can simultaneously deliver tractive power to the wheels** through a common mechanical transmission.

In this architecture, the ICE and EM are connected through a **mechanical coupler** (typically a simple gear pair, chain, belt, or a torque-coupling device) so that their rotational speeds are either equal or proportionally related, and their **torques add at the transmission input**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 3 ‚Äì Hybrid & Electric Vehicles):** Parallel Hybrid Vehicles are classified under the broader category of *Combined Hybrid Vehicles*, where the energy paths of two or more energy converters are mechanically merged in parallel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **two people pushing a heavy cart** together from behind. One person is strong and steady (the **ICE** ‚Äî robust for highways), while the other is quick and agile (the **EM** ‚Äî excellent for city starts). They are connected *side-by-side*, both pushing the same cart at the same speed, but each contributing a share of the force.

- During **highway cruising** ‚Üí The strong pusher (ICE) does most of the work.
- During **traffic lights & slow acceleration** ‚Üí The agile pusher (EM) takes over.
- During **hard acceleration (passing)** ‚Üí **Both** push together ‚Üí maximum combined thrust.
- During **braking** ‚Üí The agile pusher acts as a *generator*, converting the cart's kinetic energy back into stored energy (like a treadmill in reverse).

This side-by-side mechanical linkage ‚Äî where both sources contribute simultaneously or interchangeably ‚Äî is the **essence of a Parallel Hybrid**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $P_{ICE}$ | Power delivered by ICE | kW |
| $P_{EM}$ | Power delivered by Electric Motor | kW |
| $T_{ICE}$ | Torque from ICE | N¬∑m |
| $T_{EM}$ | Torque from Electric Motor | N¬∑m |
| $\omega$ | Angular speed of driveline | rad/s |
| $\eta_{trans}$ | Transmission efficiency | dimensionless |
| $SOC$ | State of Charge of battery | % (0‚Äì100) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Efficiency Benchmark:** A modern automotive-grade traction motor in a parallel hybrid typically operates at **90%‚Äì95% peak efficiency**, while a gasoline ICE operates at **35%‚Äì40% peak thermal efficiency**.

## 1.4 Hybrid Classification Recap (KTU Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Topologies under HEV:**
> 1. **Series Hybrid** ‚Äî Only EM drives wheels; ICE acts purely as a *range extender*.
> 2. **Parallel Hybrid** ‚Äî Both ICE and EM can drive wheels in parallel.
> 3. **Series‚ÄìParallel (Power-Split) Hybrid** ‚Äî A combination of both, e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (HSD).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture & Components of a Parallel Hybrid Vehicle

A parallel hybrid drivetrain typically contains the following components:

1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** ‚Äî Primary propulsion source (petrol/diesel).
2. **Electric Motor / Generator (EMG)** ‚Äî Acts as both motor (traction) and generator (regen).
3. **Battery Pack** ‚Äî High-voltage storage (e.g., Li-ion, NiMH).
4. **Power Electronics Converter (Inverter)** ‚Äî DC ‚Üî AC conversion between battery and motor.
5. **Transmission (Manual / Automatic / CVT)** ‚Äî Provides gear ratio between coupler and wheels.
6. **Mechanical Coupler** ‚Äî Torque-summing device (gear train or chain).
7. **Final Drive (Differential)** ‚Äî Distributes torque to wheels.
8. **Battery Management System (BMS)** ‚Äî Monitors SOC, temperature, voltage.
9. **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** ‚Äî Brain of the system; decides operating mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Parallel"?** The ICE and EM are mechanically arranged on **parallel branches** that converge at the transmission input ‚Äî hence the name.

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Parallel Hybrid Vehicle

The HCU intelligently switches between the following modes:

| Mode | ICE | EM | Description | Typical Condition |
|------|:---:|:--:|-------------|-------------------|
| **1. ICE Only** | ON | OFF | Conventional driving mode | Highway cruising, high SOC |
| **2. Motor Only (EV Mode)** | OFF | ON | Pure electric drive | Low-speed city driving, low SOC not required |
| **3. Combined (Power Assist)** | ON | ON | Both propel the vehicle | Hard acceleration, hill climbing |
| **4. Regenerative Braking** | OFF (or idle) | Generator | EM acts as generator | Braking / deceleration |
| **5. ICE Charging (Stationary Gen.)** | ON | Generator | EM charges battery while vehicle is idle | Battery low, vehicle stopped |
| **6. Power Split / Mixed** | Variable | Variable | Continuous blending | Cruise with battery management |

## 2.3 The Core 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why combine ICE and EM?** Because neither is optimal across the *entire* driving cycle. ICE is efficient at steady high loads; EM is efficient at variable/low loads. Combining them gives the **best of both worlds**.
- **Why parallel specifically?** Because it preserves the *mechanical link* to the ICE ‚Äî meaning if the battery dies, the vehicle can still drive on the ICE alone (a major advantage over series hybrids).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$ | Total propulsive power in parallel mode | kW |
| 2 | $T_{total} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$ | Torque summation at coupler | N¬∑m |
| 3 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Fundamental rotational power equation | W |
| 4 | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ | Power delivered to wheels | W |
| 5 | $F_{trac} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r_{wheel}}$ | Tractive force at tire contact patch | N |
| 6 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel}}$ | Well-to-wheel efficiency of the hybrid system | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$ | Energy recovered during regenerative braking | J |
| 8 | $P_{req} = F_{trac} \cdot v = (mg\sin\theta + mgC_r\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + ma) \cdot v$ | Tractive power demand (road load equation) | W |
| 9 | $\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM} = \omega_{coupler}$ (in simple parallel) | Speed equality in rigid mechanical coupling | rad/s |
| 10 | $SOC_{new} = SOC_{old} + \dfrac{E_{gen} - E_{motor}}{E_{batt}} \times 100\%$ | Battery state-of-charge update | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The road-load equation (Formula 8) and the regenerative braking energy (Formula 7) are the two **most frequently tested** equations in any HEV question paper.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parallel hybrid architecture is widely used in **production vehicles** such as:
- **Honda Civic Hybrid** (IMA ‚Äî Integrated Motor Assist)
- **Honda Insight (2nd Gen)**
- **Hyundai Sonata Hybrid (early models)**
- **Chevrolet Silverado Hybrid**
- **BMW ActiveHybrid (early variants)**

The parallel topology is favored when **cost** and **simplicity** are priorities ‚Äî there is no need for a large generator or complex planetary gearset as in Toyota's power-split (series‚Äìparallel) system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Parallel hybrids typically use **smaller battery packs (1‚Äì2 kWh)** compared to series hybrids, since the ICE handles sustained power demands directly.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Propulsive Power in a Parallel Hybrid

We begin from the fundamental power-torque-speed relationship:

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the sources at the coupler:**
At the mechanical coupler, both the ICE and the EM contribute torque. Let $T_{ICE}$ be the torque from the internal combustion engine and $T_{EM}$ be the torque from the electric motor.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply superposition (torque addition):**
In a parallel architecture, the torques from both sources sum at the coupler input:

$$T_{coupler} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the speed equality:**
For a simple rigid mechanical coupler (no planetary gearset), the rotational speed at the coupler is the same for both machines:

$$\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM} = \omega_{coupler}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute total power at the coupler:**

$$P_{coupler} = T_{coupler} \cdot \omega_{coupler}$$

Substituting Steps 2 and 3:

$$P_{coupler} = (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{coupler}$$

Expanding the product:

$$P_{coupler} = T_{ICE} \cdot \omega_{ICE} + T_{EM} \cdot \omega_{EM}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Recognize the individual power contributions:**

$$P_{coupler} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$$

This is the **fundamental parallel hybrid power equation**.

$$\boxed{P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Flow from Coupler to Wheels

**Step 1 ‚Äî Account for transmission efficiency:**
Power passes through a multi-speed transmission with overall efficiency $\eta_{trans}$ (typically 0.85‚Äì0.95).

$$P_{after\_trans} = \eta_{trans} \cdot P_{coupler}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Account for final drive (differential) efficiency:**
The differential introduces additional losses captured by $\eta_{fd}$ (typically 0.95‚Äì0.97).

$$P_{wheel} = \eta_{fd} \cdot P_{after\_trans}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Combine both efficiencies:**

$$P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (P_{ICE} + P_{EM})$$

$$\boxed{P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (P_{ICE} + P_{EM})}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Express in terms of torque and angular speed:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{coupler} \\
&= T_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\omega_{wheel} = \omega_{coupler} / G_{total}$ and $G_{total}$ is the total gear ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation: Tractive Force at the Tire Contact Patch

**Step 1 ‚Äî Wheel torque from wheel power:**
The torque delivered to the wheel is:

$$T_{wheel} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\omega_{wheel}}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Convert wheel torque to tractive force:**
The tractive force $F_{trac}$ acting at the tire contact patch is related to wheel torque by the effective wheel radius $r_{wheel}$:

$$F_{trac} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{r_{wheel}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute and simplify:**

$$\boxed{F_{trac} = \dfrac{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot G_{total}}{r_{wheel}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Road-Load Power Demand Equation

The vehicle's instantaneous power demand is dictated by four resistive forces:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Grade resistance force:**
Force needed to overcome gravity on a slope of angle $\theta$:

$$F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Rolling resistance force:**
Empirical rolling resistance coefficient $C_r$:

$$F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Aerodynamic drag force:**

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

where $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m¬≥** at sea level, ISA), $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area, $v$ = vehicle speed.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Inertial (acceleration) force:**

$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a$$

where $m$ = vehicle mass, $a$ = acceleration.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Total tractive force:**

$$F_{trac,req} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 + m \cdot a$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Total power demand:**

$$P_{req} = F_{trac,req} \cdot v$$

$$\boxed{P_{req} = v \cdot \left[ mg\sin\theta + mgC_r\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + ma \right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This single equation is the **starting point** for nearly every hybrid drivetrain design problem on the board exam.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mode-wise Power Flow

| Operating Mode | ICE State | EM State | Power Flow Equation |
|----------------|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **ICE Only** | Motoring | Off | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans}\eta_{fd}\,P_{ICE}$ |
| **EM Only (EV)** | Off / Idle | Motoring | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans}\eta_{fd}\,\eta_{inv}\,P_{batt}$ |
| **Combined** | Motoring | Motoring | $P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans}\eta_{fd}\,(P_{ICE} + P_{EM})$ |
| **Regen Braking** | Off | Generating | $P_{batt} = \eta_{gen}\,\eta_{trans}\,\eta_{fd}\,P_{kinetic}$ |
| **Battery Charging (Stationary)** | Motoring | Generating | $P_{batt} = \eta_{gen}\,P_{ICE,excess}$ |

## 3.6 Sample Numerical ‚Äî Tractive Power Computation

> **Problem:** A parallel hybrid vehicle of mass **1500 kg** travels at a constant **60 km/h** on a flat road. Given: $C_r = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2\,\text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.90$, $\eta_{fd} = 0.95$, $r_{wheel} = 0.32\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert speed:**
$v = 60\,\text{km/h} = 60/3.6 = 16.667\,\text{m/s}$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute rolling resistance:**

$$F_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times \cos(0¬∞) = 176.58\,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.78$$

$$F_{aero} = 112.32\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute total tractive force (flat road, constant speed ‚Üí no grade or inertia term):**

$$F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = 176.58 + 112.32 = 288.90\,\text{N}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute tractive power:**

$$P_{req} = F_{trac} \times v = 288.90 \times 16.667 = 4814.96\,\text{W} \approx 4.815\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute required power at the ICE output (before transmission & final drive):**

$$P_{ICE} = \dfrac{P_{req}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd}} = \dfrac{4814.96}{0.90 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{4814.96}{0.855}$$

$$\boxed{P_{ICE} \approx 5631.5\,\text{W} \approx 5.63\,\text{kW}}$$
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ‚Äî Parallel Hybrid Drivetrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Internal Combustion Engine]
    C[Battery Pack] --> D[Power Electronics Inverter]
    D --> E[Electric Motor Generator]
    F[Hybrid Control Unit HCU] -. controls .-> B
    F -. controls .-> D
    F -. monitors .-> C
    B --> G[Mechanical Coupler]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Transmission]
    H --> I[Final Drive Differential]
    I --> J[Drive Wheels]
    J --> K[Tractive Force to Road]
    J -. kinetic energy .-> E
    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ffd1a4,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top branch (orange/green):** ICE ‚Üí Coupler.
- **Bottom branch (blue/green):** Battery ‚Üí Inverter ‚Üí EM ‚Üí Coupler.
- Both branches converge at the **Mechanical Coupler (purple)** ‚Äî this is the *parallel* connection.
- The **HCU (red)** sits at the top, sending control signals to ICE, Inverter, and Battery.
- The **dotted line** from Wheels back to EM indicates the **regenerative braking** feedback path.

## 4.2 Power Flow State Diagram ‚Äî Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> MotorOnly : Light load AND SOC high
    Idle --> ICEOnly : Steady cruise AND SOC acceptable
    Idle --> Combined : High torque demand
    MotorOnly --> Combined : Driver demands more torque
    ICEOnly --> Combined : Driver demands more torque
    Combined --> ICEOnly : Demand reduces
    Combined --> MotorOnly : Demand reduces AND SOC ok
    ICEOnly --> RegenBraking : Braking detected
    MotorOnly --> RegenBraking : Braking detected
    Combined --> RegenBraking : Braking detected
    RegenBraking --> Idle : Vehicle stops
    ICEOnly --> BatteryCharging : SOC low AND vehicle idle
    BatteryCharging --> Idle : SOC target reached
```

## 4.3 Torque vs. Speed Operating Envelope (Conceptual)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "ICE and EM Operating Envelopes in a Parallel Hybrid"
    x-axis "Speed (RPM)" [0, 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5000, 6000, 7000]
    y-axis "Torque (N.m)" 0 --> 350
    line [320, 310, 280, 240, 210, 190, 170, 150]
    line [200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200]
```

**Reading the chart:**
- The **first line (declining)** represents the ICE torque envelope ‚Äî peak torque at low RPM, tapering as speed increases.
- The **second line (flat)** represents the Electric Motor ‚Äî *constant torque* region (typical of AC traction motors) up to base speed.
- The **combined envelope** (parallel mode) is the *sum* of both ‚Äî explaining why parallel hybrids deliver strong launch performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The constant-torque region of an EM typically extends up to its **base speed** (around 1500‚Äì2500 RPM for automotive applications), after which it enters a **constant-power** region where torque drops inversely with speed.

## 4.4 Schematic: Mechanical Coupling Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph powertrain[Parallel Hybrid Powertrain]
        iceShaft[ICE Output Shaft] --> couplerGear1[Coupler Gear A]
        emShaft[EM Output Shaft] --> couplerGear2[Coupler Gear B]
        couplerGear1 --> commonShaft[Common Output Shaft]
        couplerGear2 --> commonShaft
        commonShaft --> transInput[Transmission Input]
    end
    transInput --> clutch[Clutch or Torque Converter]
    clutch --> gearbox[Gearbox]
    gearbox --> propShaft[Propeller Shaft]
    propShaft --> differential[Final Drive Differential]
    differential --> wheelL[Left Drive Wheel]
    differential --> wheelR[Right Drive Wheel]

    style emShaft fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style couplerGear1 fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style couplerGear2 fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style commonShaft fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style transInput fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle. Mention any two distinguishing features compared to a Series Hybrid.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) is a hybrid configuration in which the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and the Electric Motor (EM) are **mechanically coupled in parallel** to the driveline, allowing both power sources to simultaneously deliver tractive power to the wheels.

**Two distinguishing features (vs. Series Hybrid):**
1. **Mechanical Linkage:** In a parallel hybrid, the ICE is *mechanically* connected to the wheels ‚Äî there is a direct mechanical path. In a series hybrid, the ICE only drives a generator; it has *no* mechanical link to the wheels.
2. **Battery Size:** Parallel hybrids use **smaller battery packs (1‚Äì2 kWh)** because the EM only assists the ICE; series hybrids require **larger batteries (10‚Äì20 kWh)** because the EM alone must propel the vehicle.
3. *(Bonus distinguishing feature)* **Fail-safe Operation:** A parallel hybrid can operate *even if the battery is fully depleted*, since the ICE alone can drive the wheels ‚Äî this is **not** possible in a pure series hybrid.

---

### Question 2: Operating Mode
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]**

**Q:** List any three operating modes of a Parallel Hybrid Vehicle and state the role of the Electric Motor in each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Mode | Role of Electric Motor |
|---|------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Motor Only (EV Mode)** | EM **propels** the vehicle alone; ICE is OFF. Used in low-speed city driving. |
| 2 | **Combined / Power Assist** | EM **assists** the ICE during high torque demand (e.g., overtaking, hill climbing). |
| 3 | **Regenerative Braking** | EM acts as a **generator**, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî With Model Solution

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Discuss its **operating modes** in detail and derive the **total tractive power** delivered to the wheels, accounting for transmission and final drive efficiencies.

*(Part (a): 7 Marks ‚Äî Architecture & Block Diagram; Part (b): 7 Marks ‚Äî Operating Modes & Tractive Power Derivation)*

---

### Part (a) ‚Äî Architecture & Block Diagram [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) consists of two parallel-arranged propulsion sources mechanically coupled at a common transmission input.

**Major Components (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE):** Primary propulsion; runs on gasoline/diesel.
2. **Electric Motor/Generator (EM):** Provides traction assist and regenerative braking.
3. **Battery Pack:** Stores electrical energy (Li-ion / NiMH, 144‚Äì300 V).
4. **Power Electronics Inverter:** Converts DC (battery) ‚Üî AC (motor).
5. **Mechanical Coupler:** Sums torques from ICE and EM (gear pair / chain / belt).
6. **Transmission:** Multi-speed gearbox or CVT for speed reduction.
7. **Final Drive (Differential):** Distributes torque to drive wheels.
8. **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU):** Supervisory controller; decides operating mode.

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Fuel] --> a2[ICE]
    a3[Battery] --> a4[Inverter]
    a4 --> a5[Electric Motor]
    a2 --> a6[Mechanical Coupler]
    a5 --> a6
    a6 --> a7[Transmission]
    a7 --> a8[Final Drive]
    a8 --> a9[Wheels]
    a10[HCU] -.-> a2
    a10 -.-> a4
    style a2 fill:#ffd1a4,stroke:#333
    style a5 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333
    style a6 fill:#e2d6f9,stroke:#333
    style a10 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333
```

**Working Principle (2.5 Marks):**
- The ICE and EM are mounted on **parallel branches** that converge at the coupler.
- The **HCU** continuously monitors vehicle speed, accelerator pedal position, brake pedal, and battery SOC.
- Based on these inputs, the HCU selects the optimal mode to minimize fuel consumption and emissions.

---

### Part (b) ‚Äî Operating Modes & Tractive Power Derivation [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Operating Modes (3.5 Marks):**

1. **ICE Only Mode:** Used during highway cruising. EM is OFF; ICE alone delivers power.
2. **Motor Only (EV) Mode:** Used during low-speed urban driving. ICE OFF; EM alone propels vehicle (zero tailpipe emissions).
3. **Combined (Power Assist) Mode:** During hard acceleration or hill climbing, both ICE and EM propel the vehicle simultaneously ‚Äî delivering peak combined torque.
4. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** During deceleration, the EM acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy.
5. **Battery Charging Mode:** While stationary or cruising, ICE drives the EM (acting as generator) to charge the battery.

**Tractive Power Derivation (3.5 Marks):**

At the coupler, torques add:

$$T_{coupler} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$$

Total power at the coupler:

$$P_{coupler} = T_{coupler} \cdot \omega_{coupler} = (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{coupler}$$

Accounting for transmission efficiency $\eta_{trans}$ and final drive efficiency $\eta_{fd}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= \eta_{trans} \cdot P_{after\_trans} \\
&= \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot P_{coupler}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{coupler}}$$

The corresponding tractive force is:

$$F_{trac} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{v} = \dfrac{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (T_{ICE} + T_{EM}) \cdot \omega_{coupler}}{v}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** Explain the **operating modes** of a Parallel Hybrid Vehicle. Derive the **road-load power demand equation** and compute the tractive power required for a vehicle of mass **1200 kg** cruising at **50 km/h** on a level road. Given: $C_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.0\,\text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.92$, $\eta_{fd} = 0.96$.

*(Part (a): 7 Marks ‚Äî Operating Modes; Part (b): 7 Marks ‚Äî Derivation & Numerical)*

---

### Part (a) ‚Äî Operating Modes [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A Parallel Hybrid Vehicle operates in **five primary modes**, controlled by the HCU:

**Mode 1 ‚Äî ICE Only Mode (1.5 Marks):**
The ICE alone propels the vehicle. The EM is OFF (or simply idling). Used during steady highway cruising when battery SOC is acceptable. Efficiency advantage: ICE operates near its optimal BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) region.

$$P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot P_{ICE}$$

**Mode 2 ‚Äî Motor Only (EV) Mode (1.5 Marks):**
The ICE is OFF and decoupled. The EM draws power from the battery and propels the vehicle alone. Used during low-speed city driving and parking maneuvers. Zero tailpipe emissions, silent operation.

$$P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot P_{batt}$$

**Mode 3 ‚Äî Combined (Power Assist) Mode (1.5 Marks):**
Both ICE and EM operate together to deliver maximum torque. Used during hard acceleration, overtaking, and hill climbing.

$$P_{wheel} = \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd} \cdot (P_{ICE} + P_{EM})$$

**Mode 4 ‚Äî Regenerative Braking Mode (1.5 Marks):**
The ICE is OFF. The EM acts as a generator, converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery.

$$E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Mode 5 ‚Äî Battery Charging Mode (1 Mark):**
When SOC is low, the ICE drives the EM as a generator to charge the battery. The vehicle may be stationary or the EM may also assist propulsion simultaneously.

---

### Part (b) ‚Äî Derivation & Numerical [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Road-Load Power Demand ‚Äî Derivation (3 Marks):**

The vehicle must overcome four resistive forces:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{grade} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \\
F_{roll} &= m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta \\
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \\
F_{acc} &= m \cdot a
\end{aligned}$$

Total tractive force:

$$F_{trac} = mg\sin\theta + mgC_r\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + ma$$

Total power demand:

$$\boxed{P_{req} = v \cdot \left[ mg\sin\theta + mgC_r\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + ma \right]}$$

**Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

Given:
- $m = 1200\,\text{kg}$, $v = 50\,\text{km/h} = 13.889\,\text{m/s}$
- $\theta = 0¬∞$ (level road), $a = 0$ (cruising)
- $C_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.0\,\text{m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_{roll} = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times \cos(0¬∞) = 176.58\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{aero} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times (13.889)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times 192.91 \\
&= 75.63\,\text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Total Tractive Force:**

$$F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = 176.58 + 75.63 = 252.21\,\text{N}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Power at the wheels:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \times v = 252.21 \times 13.889 = 3502.7\,\text{W} \approx 3.50\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Power required at ICE output (before transmission losses):**

$$P_{ICE} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd}} = \dfrac{3502.7}{0.92 \times 0.96} = \dfrac{3502.7}{0.8832}$$

$$\boxed{P_{ICE} \approx 3965.6\,\text{W} \approx 3.97\,\text{kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h ‚Üí m/s. Always divide speed in km/h by **3.6** before substituting into power equations.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** Rolling resistance and drag *oppose motion*; they are positive forces that the propulsion system must overcome. Do not subtract them.
> 3. **Missing Efficiencies:** Many students compute $P_{wheel}$ correctly but forget to divide by $\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{fd}$ when asked for power at the engine/motor output. This is a **2-mark penalty** in KTU valuations.
> 4. **SOC Constraint:** In Mode 1, the HCU will not allow *pure* ICE operation if SOC falls below a threshold (typically **30‚Äì40%**). Always mention this in descriptive answers.
> 5. **Regenerative Braking Limit:** Not all braking energy can be recovered. Maximum regen occurs when battery SOC < **80%** and brake demand is moderate. Full hard braking still requires **friction brakes**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ‚Äî Parallel Hybrid Vehicle:**

- ‚òê **Definition:** ICE and EM are mechanically coupled *in parallel*; both can deliver tractive power.
- ‚òê **Core Power Equation:** $P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{EM}$
- ‚òê **Core Torque Equation:** $T_{coupler} = T_{ICE} + T_{EM}$
- ‚òê **Speed Equality:** $\omega_{ICE} = \omega_{EM}$ (simple coupler)
- ‚òê **Road-Load Equation:** $P_{req} = v \cdot [mg\sin\theta + mgC_r\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + ma]$
- ‚òê **Regen Energy:** $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta_{gen}\,m\,(v_1^2 - v_2^2)$
- ‚òê **Five Operating Modes:** ICE Only, Motor Only, Combined, Regen Braking, Battery Charging.
- ‚òê **Advantages over Series:** Smaller battery, lower cost, ICE-only operation possible, higher mechanical efficiency at highway speeds.
- ‚òê **Disadvantages vs. Series-Parallel:** No continuous variable power split; less efficient in city driving compared to power-split hybrids (Toyota HSD).
- ‚òê **Standard Efficiencies:** $\eta_{trans} \approx 0.85\text{‚Äì}0.95$; $\eta_{fd} \approx 0.95\text{‚Äì}0.97$; $\eta_{EM} \approx 0.90\text{‚Äì}0.95$.
- ‚òê **Air Density Standard:** $\rho = \mathbf{1.225\,\text{kg/m}^3}$ at sea level, ISA conditions.
- ‚òê **Battery SOC Window:** Typical operating window = **30%‚Äì80%**; outside this, mode-switching occurs.
- ‚òê **Real-World Examples:** Honda Insight, Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA), Hyundai Sonata Hybrid, Chevrolet Silverado Hybrid.
- ‚òê **Key Exam Mantra:** *"In parallel, **torques add**, speeds are equal, and **both can drive** the wheels."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle** is a sophisticated automotive powertrain architecture that integrates two distinct energy sources and conversion systems: a **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)** fueled internal combustion engine (typically a spark-ignition SI engine) and one or more **electric machines** (traction motors and generators) coupled with an **energy storage system** (battery pack and/or supercapacitors). This dual-source configuration is classified as a **Bi-Fuel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** under the SAE J1715 standard, and falls under the broader KTU 2024 syllabus classification of *Alternative Fuel Hybrid Powertrains*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** A CNG-Electric Hybrid is a vehicle in which the propulsion energy is supplied *partly by an IC engine operating on Compressed Natural Gas* and *partly by an electric machine drawing current from a rechargeable energy storage device*. The supervisory control unit (VCU - Vehicle Control Unit) intelligently apportions power demand between the two sources to optimize fuel economy, emissions, and performance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Two-Muscle Athlete"

Imagine an athlete who has **two types of muscles**: a *high-power, stamina-limited muscle* (the CNG engine ‚Äî great for steady, long-distance cruising on highways) and a *quick-burst, instantly-rechargeable muscle* (the electric motor ‚Äî perfect for city acceleration, hill starts, and stop-and-go traffic). The athlete's **brain** acts as the *Vehicle Control Unit*, deciding in real-time which muscle to use, when to use both together, and when to recharge the quick-burst muscle from the high-power one. The CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle works exactly like this athlete ‚Äî the CNG engine provides sustained base power, the electric motor provides peak/assist power, and the VCU is the orchestrator.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics are essential for KTU numerical and design problems:

- **CNG Storage Pressure:** Standard service pressure is **20 MPa (200 bar)** for Type 3 and Type 4 cylinders, and up to **25 MPa (250 bar)** for higher-capacity Type 4 tanks.
- **Methane Number (MN) of Natural Gas:** Typically **MN ‚â• 80** (often cited as **MN = 85 to 95** for pipeline-quality CNG).
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of CNG:** Approximately **49 MJ/kg** (compared to gasoline's **44 MJ/kg**).
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) for CNG:** **17.2:1** by mass (much leaner than gasoline's 14.7:1).
- **Octane Rating (RON) of CNG:** **120+** (excellent knock resistance).
- **Standard Battery Pack Voltage for Mild Hybrids:** **48 V** DC (per ISO 21780).
- **Standard Battery Pack Voltage for Full Hybrids:** **200 V to 400 V** DC (per ISO 6469-3).
- **Energy Density of CNG:** **9 MJ/L** (at 200 bar), compared to gasoline's **32 MJ/L**.
- **Volumetric Energy Storage of CNG Tank:** Approximately **210 kWh/m¬≥** of CNG at 200 bar.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** The student should remember the "**Rule of 20**" ‚Äî CNG is stored at roughly **20 MPa**, has a methane number of roughly **80+**, an octane rating of **120+**, and an LHV roughly **10% higher** than gasoline. These four numbers are frequently tested.

### 1.4 Classification of CNG-Electric Hybrids

CNG-Electric Hybrids can be sub-classified based on powertrain topology and degree of hybridization:

| Hybridization Level | Electric Machine Power | Battery Voltage | CNG Engine Role | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Micro Hybrid** | < 5 kW | 12 V | Unchanged; Start-Stop only | Entry-level CNG cars |
| **Mild Hybrid** | 5‚Äì20 kW | 48 V | Primary propulsion | Maruti Suzuki CNG mild hybrid concepts |
| **Full Hybrid** | 20‚Äì100 kW | 200‚Äì400 V | Equal partner with motor | Toyota Prius CNG variant |
| **Plug-In Hybrid (PHEV)** | > 100 kW | 300‚Äì400 V | Range-extender | Tata/Hyundai concept prototypes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why CNG-Electric Hybridization? ‚Äî The Engineering Motivation

CNG as a standalone fuel reduces CO‚ÇÇ by approximately **20‚Äì25%** versus gasoline and eliminates particulate matter (PM) almost entirely. However, a pure CNG vehicle has three problems:

1. **Low energy density** ‚Üí large heavy cylinders reduce vehicle range and payload.
2. **Refueling infrastructure** ‚Üí CNG stations are sparse compared to gasoline.
3. **Cold-start emissions** ‚Üí CNG vehicles often run richer at cold start to protect the catalyst.

The **electric hybridization** elegantly addresses all three:

- The **electric motor** provides instant torque at cold start, allowing the engine to skip rich cold-start operation.
- **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy that would otherwise be lost, offsetting the lower energy density of CNG.
- The **electric range** (in PHEVs) reduces dependency on CNG refueling frequency for daily commutes.
- **Engine downsizing** is possible ‚Äî the electric motor fills the torque gap during transient demands.

## 2.2 Operational Architecture of a CNG-Electric Hybrid

The powertrain consists of the following major subsystems:

### A. CNG Storage and Delivery Subsystem
- **CNG cylinders** (Type 1 steel; Type 2 hoop-wrapped; Type 3 metal-lined composite; Type 4 all-composite).
- **High-pressure regulator** (reduces 200 bar to ~7‚Äì10 bar).
- **Low-pressure regulator** (reduces to 1‚Äì2 bar for injector rail).
- **CNG injectors** (solenoid or piezo, port-fuel or direct-injection).
- **Gas Control Unit (GCU)** ‚Äî ECU for the CNG side.
- **Fill valve** with integrated check valve (ANS / NGV1 / NGV2 standard).

### B. Electric Propulsion Subsystem
- **Traction motor** ‚Äî typically a 3-phase AC induction or permanent-magnet synchronous motor (PMSM).
- **Inverter** ‚Äî DC link capacitors, IGBT/MOSFET bridge, gate drivers.
- **DC-DC converter** ‚Äî steps high-voltage battery down to 12 V / 48 V auxiliaries.
- **On-board charger (OBC)** ‚Äî for PHEV variants (Level 1: 1.4 kW; Level 2: 6.6‚Äì22 kW).

### C. Energy Storage Subsystem
- **High-voltage battery pack** ‚Äî Li-ion (NMC or LFP chemistry), typically 1.5‚Äì24 kWh.
- **BMS (Battery Management System)** ‚Äî cell monitoring, balancing, thermal control.
- **Supercapacitor bank** (optional) ‚Äî for high-power transient assist.

### D. Control & Supervisory Subsystem
- **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** ‚Äî Master controller; receives torque demand from accelerator pedal and brake pedal sensors.
- **HCU (Hybrid Control Unit)** ‚Äî Applies the energy management strategy (EMS).
- **Engine ECU** ‚Äî Manages CNG injection, ignition timing, lambda control.
- **CAN bus** ‚Äî Communication backbone (ISO 11898), typically 500 kbps.

## 2.3 Energy Management Strategies (EMS)

The Energy Management Strategy is the "brain policy" that decides power split between the CNG engine and the electric motor. The major strategies are:

### (i) Thermostatic / Rule-Based Strategy (CD-CS)
- **Logic:** If State of Charge (SOC) < SOC_min, run engine. If SOC > SOC_max, run electric.
- **Advantages:** Simple, real-time implementable, low computational load.
- **Disadvantages:** Sub-optimal fuel economy; does not anticipate road conditions.

### (ii) Power-Follower Strategy
- **Logic:** Engine follows average power demand; motor handles transients above average.
- **Use case:** CNG-electric buses and heavy-duty applications.

### (iii) Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)
- **Logic:** Equate electrical energy cost to equivalent fuel cost using a weighting factor *s*.
- **Equation:** 
$$J = \dot{m}_{fuel,eng}(t) + s(t) \cdot \frac{P_{bat}(t)}{Q_{LHV}}$$
where *s* is the equivalence factor, *P_bat* is battery power (positive = discharge), and *Q_LHV* is CNG's lower heating value.
- **Use case:** Real-time sub-optimal control; embedded in production vehicles.

### (iv) Dynamic Programming (DP) / Global Optimization
- **Logic:** Solves Bellman's equation backward in time over a known drive cycle.
- **Disadvantage:** Non-causal; used only as benchmark or for offline training.

### (v) Machine Learning Based (Neural Networks, Fuzzy Logic, RL)
- **Logic:** Learns optimal power split from historical data or simulation.
- **Trend:** Increasingly used in 2024+ production vehicles.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of CNG-Electric Hybrids

- **Public Transit:** CNG-electric buses in Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru achieve 30‚Äì40% fuel savings over pure-CNG buses.
- **Last-Mile Delivery:** E-commerce fleets (e.g., Amazon, Flipkart) use CNG-electric LCVs to access low-emission zones (LEZ) in cities.
- **Three-Wheelers:** India and Bangladesh operate millions of CNG auto-rickshaws; mild-hybrid retrofitting is an active R&D area.
- **Mining & Off-Road:** Underground mines use CNG-electric LHD (Load-Haul-Dump) vehicles to avoid diesel particulate exposure.
- **Maritime Auxiliary Power:** Hybrid CNG-electric APUs reduce ship idling emissions at port.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in a CNG Tank

**Problem Statement:** A CNG-electric hybrid car has a Type 4 composite cylinder of volume 60 L, filled to a pressure of 20 MPa at a temperature of 25¬∞C. The cylinder contains 95% methane (CH‚ÇÑ) by mole and 5% ethane (C‚ÇÇH‚ÇÜ). Calculate the mass of CNG stored and the chemical energy content.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the Equation of State

We use the **real gas form of the ideal gas law** (compressibility factor correction):

$$P \cdot V = Z \cdot n \cdot R \cdot T$$

For methane at 20 MPa and 298 K, the compressibility factor is approximately $Z \approx 1.3$ (from generalized compressibility charts). For simplicity in KTU problems, $Z$ is often given in the question.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Solve for Number of Moles

Rearranging for $n$:

$$n = \frac{P \cdot V}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$n = \frac{(20 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}) \times (60 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3)}{(1.3) \times (8.314 \, \text{J/mol¬∑K}) \times (298 \, \text{K})}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Compute Numerator and Denominator

Numerator:

$$P \cdot V = 20 \times 10^6 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \, \text{J}$$

Denominator:

$$Z \cdot R \cdot T = 1.3 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 3221.7 \, \text{J/mol}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Final Number of Moles

$$n = \frac{1.2 \times 10^6}{3221.7} = 372.5 \, \text{mol}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Calculate Mass of Methane (95%) and Ethane (5%)

Moles of methane: $n_{CH_4} = 0.95 \times 372.5 = 353.9 \, \text{mol}$

Mass of methane: $m_{CH_4} = n_{CH_4} \times M_{CH_4} = 353.9 \times 0.016 = 5.66 \, \text{kg}$

Moles of ethane: $n_{C_2H_6} = 0.05 \times 372.5 = 18.6 \, \text{mol}$

Mass of ethane: $m_{C_2H_6} = 18.6 \times 0.030 = 0.56 \, \text{kg}$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Total Mass of CNG

$$m_{CNG} = 5.66 + 0.56 = 6.22 \, \text{kg}$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Total Chemical Energy

Using LHV: $Q_{LHV,CNG} \approx 49 \, \text{MJ/kg}$

$$E_{CNG} = m_{CNG} \times Q_{LHV} = 6.22 \times 49 = 304.8 \, \text{MJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{CNG} \approx 304.8 \, \text{MJ} \quad (\text{or equivalently } 84.7 \, \text{kWh})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Demand on a Drive Cycle

**Problem Statement:** A CNG-electric hybrid car of mass 1500 kg (including CNG cylinder and battery) is to cruise at 80 km/h on a flat road, then accelerate at 0.5 m/s¬≤ for 10 seconds. Compute the steady-state power demand during cruise and the peak power demand during acceleration. Take $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.2 \, \text{m}^2$.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Convert Speed to SI Units

$$v = 80 \, \text{km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute Rolling Resistance Force (Flat Road)

$$F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(0¬∞) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 1.0$$

$$F_{roll} = 176.6 \, \text{N}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Compute Aerodynamic Drag Force

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times \rho_{air} \times C_d \times A \times v^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (22.22)^2$$

Computing $v^2$:

$$v^2 = 493.8 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 493.8 = 199.6 \, \text{N}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Total Resistance During Cruise

$$F_{total,cruise} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = 176.6 + 199.6 = 376.2 \, \text{N}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Cruise Power Demand

$$P_{cruise} = F_{total,cruise} \times v = 376.2 \times 22.22 = 8359 \, \text{W} \approx 8.36 \, \text{kW}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Add Acceleration Resistance

$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a = 1500 \times 0.5 = 750 \, \text{N}$$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Total Resistance During Acceleration

$$F_{total,acc} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{acc} = 376.2 + 750 = 1126.2 \, \text{N}$$

### Step 8 ‚Äî Peak Power During Acceleration

$$P_{peak} = F_{total,acc} \times v = 1126.2 \times 22.22 = 25,024 \, \text{W} \approx 25.02 \, \text{kW}$$

### Step 9 ‚Äî Power Split Decision (EMS Logic)

$$\boxed{P_{cruise} \approx 8.36 \, \text{kW}; \quad P_{peak} \approx 25.02 \, \text{kW}}$$

The CNG engine (rated 40 kW) handles the cruise; the electric motor (rated 30 kW) assists during acceleration. The VCU might dispatch, e.g., 18 kW from engine and 7 kW from motor during the transient.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Battery Sizing for a Mild-Hybrid CNG-Electric Vehicle

**Problem Statement:** Design a 48 V mild-hybrid battery pack that can deliver a 10-second peak assist of 25 kW to a CNG engine, with a usable SOC swing of 30% to 70%. The pack uses Li-ion cells with nominal voltage 3.7 V and capacity 20 Ah.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Required Pack Energy

Energy delivered in 10 s at 25 kW:

$$E = P \times t = 25{,}000 \times 10 = 250{,}000 \, \text{J} = 250 \, \text{kJ} = 0.0694 \, \text{kWh}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Pack Voltage and Series Cell Count

Number of cells in series for 48 V:

$$N_{series} = \frac{48}{3.7} = 12.97 \approx 13 \, \text{cells}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Pack Usable Capacity (Ah)

Allowing 40% SOC swing:

$$Q_{usable} = Q_{rated} \times 0.40 = 20 \times 0.40 = 8 \, \text{Ah}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Usable Energy per Series String

$$E_{string} = V_{pack} \times Q_{usable} = 48 \times 8 = 384 \, \text{Wh} = 0.384 \, \text{kWh}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Parallel Strings for Power/Energy Needs

The required energy is 0.0694 kWh, so even 1 string suffices in principle. But for peak power (25 kW for 10 s requires high C-rate), we use:

Peak current per string at 48 V:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{25{,}000}{48} = 520.8 \, \text{A}$$

C-rate on a 20 Ah cell = 520.8/20 = **26 C** (impractical for most Li-ion).

### Step 6 ‚Äî Use 4 Parallel Strings

With 4 parallel strings, current per cell is 520.8/4 = 130.2 A ‚Üí C-rate = **6.5 C**, which is acceptable for power-optimized Li-ion.

### Step 7 ‚Äî Total Pack Capacity

$$Q_{pack} = 4 \times 20 = 80 \, \text{Ah}; \quad E_{pack} = 48 \times 80 = 3.84 \, \text{kWh}$$

$$\boxed{N_{series} = 13, \quad N_{parallel} = 4, \quad E_{pack} = 3.84 \, \text{kWh at 48 V}}$$

## 4.2 Power Flow Modes ‚Äî State Transition Diagram

The hybrid can operate in one of five discrete modes. The transitions are governed by the HCU's real-time rule-based logic (CD-CS with power follower).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EVmode : SOC high and low demand
    EVmode --> HybridMode : Power demand exceeds electric limit
    HybridMode --> EngineMode : SOC low and steady cruise
    EngineMode --> ChargingMode : SOC below SOCmin threshold
    ChargingMode --> EngineMode : SOC restored to SOCmax
    EngineMode --> HybridMode : Sudden acceleration pedal press
    HybridMode --> EVmode : Light load and SOC recovered
    ChargingMode --> RegenerativeMode : Brake pedal pressed
    RegenerativeMode --> EngineMode : Brake released at cruise
    note right of EVmode : Zero CNG consumption\nTailpipe emissions zero
    note right of HybridMode : CNG engine + Motor\nPeak torque assist
    note right of EngineMode : Pure CNG mode\nHighway cruise
    note right of ChargingMode : CNG engine drives\nmotor as generator
    note right of RegenerativeMode : Wheels drive motor\nas generator
```

## 4.4 Energy Flow Sankey Logic (Textual Representation)

For a **typical city drive cycle** (e.g., Indian Urban Drive Cycle, IUDC), the energy flow percentages for a CNG-electric hybrid versus a pure CNG vehicle are conceptualized as follows:

```
Pure CNG Vehicle (IUDC, 100 units input):
  CNG Tank [100] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Engine Combustion [Loss 65] + Mechanical [35]
  Mechanical [35] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Transmission [Loss 5] + Wheels [30]
  Wheels [30] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Useful KE [10] + Braking Heat [20]

CNG-Electric Hybrid (IUDC, 80 units CNG + 20 units electric input):
  CNG Tank [80] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Engine [Loss 50] + Mech [30]
  Battery [20] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Inverter [Loss 2] + Motor [18]
  Engine Mech [30] + Motor [18] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Transmission [Loss 4] + Wheels [44]
  Wheels [44] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Useful KE [20] + Regen Recovered [10] + Brake Heat [14]
  Regen Recovered [10] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ Battery [10]
```

This shows the hybrid achieves the same useful kinetic energy (20 units) with only 100 total units of primary input (80 CNG + 20 electric) versus the pure CNG needing 100 units of CNG. The 20% gain arises from regenerative braking and engine right-sizing.
