# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
Define a CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle. List any four advantages of CNG as a fuel for IC engines.

**Model Answer:**

A **CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle** is an automotive powertrain that uses **Compressed Natural Gas** as the primary fuel for the internal combustion engine and **electrical energy stored in a battery** as the secondary energy source, with the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) intelligently managing the power split between the two sources to optimize fuel economy, emissions, and drivability.

**Four advantages of CNG:**
1. **Higher Octane Rating (RON 120+)** ‚Üí allows higher compression ratio and better knock resistance.
2. **Lower CO‚ÇÇ emission per km** (~20‚Äì25% less than gasoline) due to lower carbon-to-hydrogen ratio.
3. **Virtually zero particulate matter (PM)** tailpipe emissions, making it suitable for low-emission zones.
4. **Wider flammability limits and faster flame speed** ‚Üí more complete combustion and better thermal efficiency.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
Explain the role of the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** in a CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle. What inputs does it typically process?

**Model Answer:**

The **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** is the master supervisory controller of the CNG-electric hybrid. Its primary role is to interpret the driver's torque demand and dispatch the appropriate power commands to the engine ECU and motor controller, while ensuring battery SOC remains within safe limits.

**Typical Inputs to the VCU:**
1. Accelerator pedal position sensor (APPS) ‚Äî torque demand.
2. Brake pedal position sensor ‚Äî for regenerative braking coordination.
3. Vehicle speed sensor (VSS).
4. Battery SOC from BMS.
5. Engine temperature, CNG tank pressure.
6. Drive mode selector (EV / Hybrid / Charge).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) ‚Äî 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle** and describe the functions of the major subsystems. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A CNG-Electric hybrid car has a Type-4 CNG tank of volume **80 L**, filled to **20 MPa** at 25¬∞C. The methane compressibility factor is **Z = 1.30**. Compute (i) the mass of methane stored, and (ii) the chemical energy in kWh. Take LHV of CNG = 49 MJ/kg. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution ‚Äî Question A

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Architecture Explanation (7 Marks)

The CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle architecture consists of **five major subsystems**:

**1. Energy Source Subsystem:** Comprises a **Type-4 CNG cylinder** (all-composite, 200 bar), a 48 V or 400 V **Li-ion battery pack** with BMS, and optionally a supercapacitor bank for peak power.

**2. Power Conversion Subsystem:** Includes a **CNG-fueled SI engine** (typically 1.0‚Äì2.0 L, naturally aspirated or turbocharged) and a **3-phase PMSM traction motor** with an IGBT-based inverter.

**3. Transmission Subsystem:** A **planetary gearset** (Toyota THS-style) or a **clutch-coupled parallel arrangement** that allows the engine and motor to drive the wheels either independently or in combination.

**4. Energy Management Subsystem:** The **VCU + HCU** that implements the rule-based or ECMS strategy. It receives sensor inputs and commands the engine throttle, CNG injectors, and motor torque.

**5. Auxiliary Subsystem:** The **DC-DC converter** (HV to 12 V/48 V), the **on-board charger** for plug-in variants, **regenerative braking controller**, and the **thermal management** system (radiator + chiller for battery cooling).

A complete block diagram should be drawn (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) showing arrows from driver input ‚Üí VCU ‚Üí HCU ‚Üí ECUs and from CNG tank ‚Üí engine ‚Üí transmission ‚Üí wheels, and from battery ‚Üí inverter ‚Üí motor ‚Üí transmission.

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 80 \, \text{L} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$
- $P = 20 \, \text{MPa} = 20 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$
- $T = 25¬∞C = 298 \, \text{K}$
- $Z = 1.30$
- $M_{CH_4} = 0.016 \, \text{kg/mol}$
- $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol¬∑K)}$
- $Q_{LHV} = 49 \, \text{MJ/kg}$

**Step 1: Apply Real Gas Equation**

$$P \cdot V = Z \cdot n \cdot R \cdot T$$

**Step 2: Solve for n (moles of methane)**

$$n = \frac{P \cdot V}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

$$n = \frac{(20 \times 10^6) \times (80 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.30) \times (8.314) \times (298)}$$

**Step 3: Compute Numerator**

$$P \cdot V = 20 \times 10^6 \times 0.080 = 1.6 \times 10^6 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 4: Compute Denominator**

$$Z \cdot R \cdot T = 1.30 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 3221.7 \, \text{J/mol}$$

**Step 5: Final Moles**

$$n = \frac{1.6 \times 10^6}{3221.7} = 496.6 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 6: Mass of Methane (i)**

$$m_{CH_4} = n \times M_{CH_4} = 496.6 \times 0.016 = 7.95 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 7: Chemical Energy in Joules (ii)**

$$E = m_{CH_4} \times Q_{LHV} = 7.95 \times 49 \times 10^6 = 389.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 8: Convert to kWh**

$$E = \frac{389.5 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 108.2 \, \text{kWh}$$

$$\boxed{m_{CH_4} \approx 7.95 \, \text{kg}; \quad E_{CNG} \approx 108.2 \, \text{kWh}}$$

---

### Question B (Option 2) ‚Äî 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **regenerative braking** in the context of a CNG-Electric hybrid. Derive the expression for recovered energy and the factors that limit regeneration. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A CNG-Electric hybrid bus has a 6000 kg mass. During a typical stop, it decelerates from 50 km/h to 10 km/h over 8 seconds on a flat road. The motor-generator efficiency is 90% and the regenerative braking captures 60% of the available kinetic energy difference. Compute the energy recovered. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution ‚Äî Question B

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Theory of Regenerative Braking (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Regenerative braking is the process of converting the vehicle's **kinetic energy** (or potential energy on a down-slope) into **electrical energy** by operating the traction motor as a **generator** during deceleration, and storing the recovered energy in the battery.

**Working Principle:**
- During normal braking, friction brakes dissipate kinetic energy as heat (wasted).
- In a hybrid, when the brake pedal is pressed, the HCU commands the traction motor to switch to generator mode.
- The motor applies a counter-torque that decelerates the vehicle, while generating a back-EMF that charges the battery.

**Derivation of Recovered Energy:**

Initial KE: $\frac{1}{2} m v_1^2$

Final KE: $\frac{1}{2} m v_2^2$

Recovered energy (ideal):

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

With efficiencies:

$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \Delta KE$$

**Factors limiting regeneration:**
1. **Battery SOC ceiling** ‚Äî cannot charge above ~80‚Äì90% SOC.
2. **Battery C-rate limit** ‚Äî high current can damage cells.
3. **Motor-generator power rating** ‚Äî capped at peak kW.
4. **Brake pedal position** ‚Äî regenerative is blended with friction brakes.
5. **Low SOC** or **cold battery** ‚Äî regeneration may be disabled.
6. **Vehicle speed** ‚Äî at very low speeds, motor back-EMF is insufficient to charge.

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 6000 \, \text{kg}$
- $v_1 = 50 \, \text{km/h} = 13.89 \, \text{m/s}$
- $v_2 = 10 \, \text{km/h} = 2.78 \, \text{m/s}$
- $\eta_{regen} = 60\% = 0.60$ (capture fraction)
- $\eta_{motor} = 90\% = 0.90$
- Road: flat ($\theta = 0$)

**Step 1: Compute Initial Kinetic Energy**

$$KE_1 = \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2 = 0.5 \times 6000 \times (13.89)^2$$

$$KE_1 = 3000 \times 192.9 = 578{,}800 \, \text{J} = 578.8 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2: Compute Final Kinetic Energy**

$$KE_2 = \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = 0.5 \times 6000 \times (2.78)^2$$

$$KE_2 = 3000 \times 7.73 = 23{,}180 \, \text{J} = 23.18 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3: Kinetic Energy Difference**

$$\Delta KE = 578.8 - 23.18 = 555.6 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4: Apply Combined Efficiency**

$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \Delta KE$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.60 \times 0.90 \times 555.6 = 300.0 \, \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5: Convert to kWh**

$$E_{recovered} = \frac{300{,}000}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.0833 \, \text{kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} \approx 300 \, \text{kJ} = 0.0833 \, \text{kWh} \text{ per braking event}}$$

**Interpretation:** For a city bus making 200 such stops per day, daily recovered energy = $200 \times 0.0833 = 16.66 \, \text{kWh/day}$, which is significant for auxiliary load offset.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CNG-Electric Hybrid Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently mix up bar, MPa, and Pa. Remember: 1 MPa = 10 bar = $10^6$ Pa. Marks deducted for dimensional inconsistency.
> 2. **Forgetting Compressibility Factor Z:** At 200 bar, ideal gas law (PV = nRT) is inaccurate. Always check if Z is given; if not, state assumption Z ‚âà 1.
> 3. **SOC Swing Misuse:** Battery usable energy is NOT the full rated energy. You must multiply by SOC swing (e.g., 40% for Li-ion to avoid degradation).
> 4. **Mixing CNG and Gasoline LHV:** Natural gas has LHV = 49 MJ/kg; gasoline is 44 MJ/kg. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio:** CNG is 17.2:1, NOT 14.7:1 (gasoline). Using the wrong A/F gives incorrect lambda and emissions.
> 6. **Missing Free Body Diagram in Force/Power Problems:** Always show the four force components (rolling, aero, acceleration, grade) explicitly.
> 7. **Sankey Diagram Inconsistency:** Energy in must equal energy out (with losses). A common error is unbalanced inputs and outputs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 3 ‚Äî CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle**

- [ ] CNG storage pressure standard: **20 MPa (200 bar)**; LHV = **49 MJ/kg**; A/F_stoich = **17.2:1**; RON = **120+**.
- [ ] CNG-Electric Hybrid = **Bi-Fuel HEV** with SAE J1715 classification.
- [ ] Five major subsystems: **CNG storage/delivery, Electric propulsion, Energy storage, Control (VCU/HCU), Auxiliary**.
- [ ] Two pressure regulators: **HP (200‚Üí10 bar)** and **LP (10‚Üí1.5 bar)** in the CNG delivery train.
- [ ] Battery voltage tiers: **12 V (micro), 48 V (mild), 200‚Äì400 V (full), 300‚Äì400 V (PHEV)**.
- [ ] Five operating modes: **EV, Hybrid, Engine, Charging, Regenerative**.
- [ ] Three primary EMS strategies: **Rule-based (CD-CS), ECMS, DP/ML-based**.
- [ ] Power demand equation: $P_{req} = v \times (F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{acc} + F_{grade})$.
- [ ] Real gas law: $PV = ZnRT$ ‚Äî always use Z at high pressure.
- [ ] Regenerative energy: $E_{recovered} = \eta_{regen} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$.
- [ ] Battery sizing: $N_{series} = V_{pack}/V_{cell}$; $N_{parallel}$ chosen to limit C-rate.
- [ ] Pure CNG improvement with hybridization: **+30% fuel economy, ‚Äì30% CO‚ÇÇ, ‚Äì60% NOx, ‚Äì100% PM**.
- [ ] Cost premium ~**40%**; payback ~**2.5‚Äì3.5 years** for fleet operators.
- [ ] CO‚ÇÇ emission factor for CNG: **2.75 kg CO‚ÇÇ per kg CNG**.
- [ ] Air-fuel ratio lambda equation: $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual} / (A/F)_{stoich}$.
- [ ] Energy management obeys: $P_{req} = P_{eng} + P_{motor}$ (power balance).
- [ ] Standard reference drive cycles: **NEDC, WLTP, IUDC** (Indian Urban Drive Cycle).
- [ ] CAN bus communication standard: **ISO 11898, 500 kbps** typical.
- [ ] The **VCU** is the master; the **HCU** is the energy-management strategist; **Engine ECU** and **Motor ECU** are local controllers.
- [ ] Always state assumptions: SOC swing, Z value, road grade, and ambient temperature.

---

**End of Module 3 ‚Äî CNG-Electric Hybrid Vehicle Notes**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels L1‚ÄìL5 (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Module 4 Theme: Automotive Sensors, Actuators & Embedded Control > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Automotive Sensors and Actuators* constitute the sensory and effector interface between the physical domain of a vehicle and its embedded electronic control unit (ECU). A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, position, speed, gas concentration) into a standardized electrical signal, while an **actuator** is a transducer that converts an electrical command signal from the ECU into a corresponding mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal action on the vehicle sub-system. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Human Body Analogy:** > Imagine you are driving a car with your eyes closed and hands tied. You would have no idea how fast you are going, the engine temperature, or how much fuel is left, nor would you be able to press the brake or turn the steering wheel. The vehicle is exactly in this state without its sensors and actuators. > > - **Sensors = The Five Senses of the Car:** Just like your skin feels temperature and your eyes detect light, sensors "feel" crankshaft position, "see" oxygen content in exhaust, and "hear" knock vibrations. > - **Actuators = The Muscles of the Car:** Just like your muscles move your limbs when your brain commands, actuators physically push the fuel injector, rotate the throttle blade, or engage the brake caliper. > - **ECU = The Brain of the Car:** It receives sensory data, makes decisions based on programmed maps, and sends command signals to the muscles (actuators). ### 1.3 Why This Module Matters in Modern Automobiles The modern passenger vehicle contains between **60 to 100+ sensors** and a comparable number of actuators. The proliferation of electrified powertrains, ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), and autonomous driving features has made sensor-actuator integration the cornerstone of contemporary automotive engineering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants & Ranges):** > - **Operating Temperature Range:** $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$ (under-hood) and $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+85^{\circ}\text{C}$ (cabin) > - **Supply Voltage:** Nominal **$12\text{ V}$** for conventional ICE vehicles, **$48\text{ V}$** for mild-hybrid systems, **$400\text{ V}$** or **$800\text{ V}$** for full EVs > - **Communication Speed:** CAN bus up to **$1\text{ Mbps}$**, FlexRay up to **$10\text{ Mbps}$**, Automotive Ethernet up to **$1\text{ Gbps}$** ### 1.4 Classification Overview | Domain | Sensor Examples | Actuator Examples | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Powertrain** | MAP, MAF, Crankshaft Position, Lambda ($\text{O}_2$) | Fuel Injector, Ignition Coil, EGR Valve | | **Chassis** | Wheel Speed, Steering Angle, Yaw Rate, Lateral G | Brake Caliper, Steering Rack Motor, Damper Solenoid | | **Body** | Rain/Light Sensor, Occupant Weight, Ultrasonic | Power Window Motor, Door Lock, Headlamp Leveller | | **Safety (ADAS)** | Radar, Lidar, Camera, IMU | Seatbelt Pretensioner, Airbag Squib, EPB Motor | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Generic Sensor‚ÄìActuator Closed Loop Every modern automotive sub-system follows a canonical closed-loop control architecture. Understanding this loop is essential before diving into individual device physics. **Step 1 ‚Äî Physical Stimulus Occurs:** A real-world event (e.g., driver presses accelerator pedal) creates a measurable physical quantity (pedal angle $\theta_{\text{pedal}}$). **Step 2 ‚Äî Sensing Stage:** The sensor converts the physical quantity into an analog electrical signal $V_{\text{sensor}}$, typically a voltage, current, resistance, or capacitance variation. **Step 3 ‚Äî Signal Conditioning:** The raw signal is amplified, filtered (anti-aliasing), and linearized. For digital ECUs, an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) discretizes it into a binary word $N$. **Step 4 ‚Äî ECU Decision:** The microcontroller executes control algorithms (e.g., PID, lookup-table interpolation) using the digitized measurement against a desired setpoint (e.g., driver torque demand). **Step 5 ‚Äî Actuator Command:** The ECU outputs a control signal ‚Äî Pulse Width Modulation (PWM), frequency, or analog voltage ‚Äî to the actuator driver stage. **Step 6 ‚Äî Physical Actuation:** The actuator performs mechanical work, altering the vehicle state. The new state is sensed again, completing the loop. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Loop The loop exists because **open-loop control is insufficient for automotive systems** due to: 1. **Disturbances:** Road gradient, wind, payload variation, ambient temperature. 2. **Component Aging:** Injector clogging, sensor drift, friction changes. 3. **Emissions Compliance:** Laws mandate precise air-fuel ratio control (lambda $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$). 4. **Safety:** Braking and steering systems *must* be closed-loop to guarantee determinism. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Module 4 (Sensors & Actuators):** | Symbol | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $V_{\text{out}}$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_s \left( \dfrac{R}{R + R_{\text{var}}} \right)$ | Voltage divider for resistive sensors | $\text{V}$ | | $R_T$ | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\,B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | NTC thermistor resistance vs temperature | $\Omega$ | | $V_{\text{PWM,avg}}$ | $V_{\text{avg}} = D \cdot V_{\text{supply}}$ | Average voltage from PWM duty cycle $D$ | $\text{V}$ | | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Actual AFR}}{\text{Stoichiometric AFR}}$ | Equivalence ratio for air-fuel control | dimensionless | | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60}$ | Angular velocity from rotational speed $n$ (rpm) | $\text{rad/s}$ | | $f_{\text{Hall}}$ | $f_{\text{Hall}} = \dfrac{n \cdot N_{\text{teeth}}}{60}$ | Hall-effect sensor output frequency | $\text{Hz}$ | | $\tau$ | $\tau = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + \tau_L$ | Actuator electromechanical torque balance | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ | | $V_{\text{thermocouple}}$ | $V \approx S(T) \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck effect for thermocouple voltage | $\text{V}$ | | $C_{\text{parallel}}$ | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitive position/level sensor | $\text{F}$ | | $S/N$ | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio in dB | $\text{dB}$ | **Critical Boundary Conditions:** - NTC thermistor: $B > 0$ always; $T$ and $T_0$ in **Kelvin**. - Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline: **14.7 : 1**; for diesel: **14.5 : 1**; for CNG: **17.2 : 1**. - Hall-effect sensors require $B_{\text{min}} \approx 0.05\text{ T}$ to trigger reliably. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine Control Unit (ECU):** Uses MAP + MAF + Lambda + Crank sensors to compute fueling every **$10\text{ ms}$**. - **Electronic Stability Control (ESC):** Uses steering angle + yaw rate + wheel speed sensors at **$100\text{ Hz}$** loop rate. - **Battery Management System (BMS):** Uses cell voltage + temperature sensors with **$\pm 2\text{ mV}$** accuracy. - **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Fuses radar (range) and camera (lateral position) data for longitudinal control. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of a Generic Resistive Sensor in a Voltage Divider Consider a variable resistor $R_{\text{var}}(x)$ whose resistance depends linearly on a physical quantity $x$ (e.g., displacement, force): $$R_{\text{var}}(x) = R_0 (1 + k \cdot x)$$ where $R_0$ is the nominal resistance and $k$ is the gauge factor in $\text{m}^{-1}$ (or appropriate unit). **Step 1 ‚Äî Apply the Voltage Divider Rule:** Place $R_{\text{var}}(x)$ in series with a fixed reference resistor $R$ across a stable supply $V_s$. $$V_{\text{out}}(x) = V_s \cdot \frac{R}{R + R_{\text{var}}(x)}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute the Linear Model:** Replace $R_{\text{var}}(x)$ with $R_0(1 + kx)$. $$V_{\text{out}}(x) = V_s \cdot \frac{R}{R + R_0(1 + kx)}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Define the Reference Operating Point:** At $x = 0$: $R_{\text{var}} = R_0$, and choosing $R = R_0$ (half-bridge optimum) gives: $$V_{\text{out}}(0) = V_s \cdot \frac{R_0}{R_0 + R_0} = \frac{V_s}{2}$$ **Step 4 ‚Äî Linearize About $x = 0$:** Differentiate $V_{\text{out}}(x)$ with respect to $x$ and evaluate at $x = 0$. $$\frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dx} \bigg\vert_{x=0} = -V_s \cdot \frac{R \cdot R_0 \, k}{\left(R + R_0(1 + kx)\right)^2} \bigg\vert_{x=0}$$ With $R = R_0$: $$\frac{dV_{\text{out}}}{dx} \bigg\vert_{x=0} = -V_s \cdot \frac{R_0^2 \, k}{(2R_0)^2} = -\frac{V_s \, k}{4}$$ **Step 5 ‚Äî Sensitivity Expression:** The output variation for small $x$ is therefore: $$\Delta V_{\text{out}} \approx -\frac{V_s \, k}{4} \cdot x$$ The negative sign indicates that increasing $x$ decreases the output voltage (a *decreasing* characteristic). For a Wheatstone full-bridge arrangement, this factor becomes $\dfrac{V_s k}{4} \cdot 4 = V_s k$, quadrupling the sensitivity. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: PWM Duty Cycle to Average Actuator Voltage A solenoid-driven actuator (e.g., EGR valve, variable valve timing solenoid) is driven by a PWM signal of period $T_{\text{PWM}}$ and pulse width $t_{\text{on}}$. **Step 1 ‚Äî Define Duty Cycle:** $$D = \frac{t_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{PWM}}}$$ **Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the Average Voltage (Ideal):** For an ideal switching element with zero rise/fall time: $$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{PWM}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{PWM}}} v(t) \, dt$$ The signal is $V_{\text{supply}}$ for $0 < t < t_{\text{on}}$ and $0$ otherwise: $$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{PWM}}} \left[ \int_{0}^{t_{\text{on}}} V_{\text{supply}} \, dt + \int_{t_{\text{on}}}^{T_{\text{PWM}}} 0 \, dt \right]$$ $$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}} \cdot t_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{PWM}}} = D \cdot V_{\text{supply}}$$ **Step 3 ‚Äî Actuator Transfer (First-Order Lag):** A solenoid's mechanical response is governed by: $$\tau \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \theta = K_{\text{act}} \cdot D \cdot V_{\text{supply}}$$ For steady-state ($\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = 0$): $$\theta_{\text{ss}} = K_{\text{act}} \cdot D \cdot V_{\text{supply}}$$ This shows that **duty cycle $D$ linearly commands the steady-state actuator position**, which is why PWM is the universal automotive actuator interface. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Sensor vs. Actuator | Parameter | Sensor | Actuator | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Direction of Energy** | Physical $\rightarrow$ Electrical | Electrical $\rightarrow$ Physical | | **Output Type** | Voltage, current, frequency, digital word | Force, torque, displacement, heat | | **Typical Bandwidth** | $\text{Hz}$ to $\text{kHz}$ | $\text{Hz}$ to hundreds of $\text{Hz}$ | | **Power Level** | $\mu\text{W}$ to $\text{mW}$ (passive) | $\text{W}$ to $\text{kW}$ (active) | | **Linearity Demand** | High (for accurate control) | Moderate (often used in saturated region) | | **Failure Mode** | Open/short circuit, drift | Stuck open/closed, mechanical seizure | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Generic Closed-Loop Automotive Control Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Physical Domain
Vehicle State"] -- "Physical Quantity
temp, pressure, position" --> nodeB["Sensor Stage
Transducer"] nodeB -- "Raw Analog Signal
mV, microA" --> nodeC["Signal Conditioning
Amplify + Filter"] nodeC -- "Conditioned Signal
0 to 5 V" --> nodeD["ADC
Digitisation"] nodeD -- "Digital Word
10 to 12 bit" --> nodeE["ECU
Microcontroller"] nodeE -- "Decision Algorithm
PID / LUT" --> nodeF["Actuator Driver
H-Bridge / FET"] nodeF -- "PWM Command
Duty Cycle D" --> nodeG["Actuator Stage
Mechanical Action"] nodeG -- "Alters Vehicle State
Feedback Path" --> nodeA style nodeA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD style nodeE fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style nodeG fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A ``` ### 4.2 Module 4 Topic Roadmap ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeIntro["Introduction to Sensors
and Actuators"] --> nodeA["Active vs Passive
Sensors"] nodeIntro --> nodeB["Sensor Signal
Characteristics"] nodeA --> nodeC["Temperature and Pressure

Sensors"] nodeA --> nodeE["Gas and Mass Air Flow
Sensors"] nodeC --> nodeF["Actuator
Fundamentals"] nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG["Solenoid and Relay
Actuators"] nodeF --> nodeH["Motor based
Actuators"] nodeF --> nodeI["Piezo and MEMS
Actuators"] nodeG --> nodeJ["Application in
Engine and Chassis"] nodeH --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeJ style nodeIntro fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style nodeJ fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 ``` ### 4.3 Sensor Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeRoot["Automotive Sensors"] --> nodeP["Passive Sensors
Require External Power"] nodeRoot --> nodeA["Active Sensors
Self Generating"] nodeP --> nodeP1["Thermistor
