### PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a vision-based Vehicle Information System (VIS) for an autonomous passenger car. Discuss the role of each subsystem in the Sense‚ÄìPerceive‚ÄìReason‚ÄìAct pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stereo camera rig has focal length `f = 8 mm`, baseline `B = 200 mm`, and pixel pitch `p = 5 ¬µm`. For a detected vehicle ahead, the measured disparity is `d = 40 px`.
   (i) Compute the depth `Z` of the leading vehicle. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) If the ego-vehicle is travelling at `v_1 = 90 km/h` and the leading vehicle at `v_2 = 60 km/h`, compute the TTC. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) Justify whether Forward Collision Warning (FCW) and Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) should be activated. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

A vision-based VIS for an autonomous car comprises the following subsystems:

1. **Image Acquisition Subsystem** ‚Äî front-facing stereo cameras (long range, 120¬∞ FOV), surround-view fisheye cameras (parking), rear-view camera. CMOS sensors digitize light into RGB images at **30‚Äì60 fps**.
2. **Pre-processing Subsystem** ‚Äî performs lens distortion correction (intrinsic calibration `K`, distortion `d`), noise removal, and ROI extraction to isolate relevant image regions.
3. **Feature Extraction Subsystem** ‚Äî applies Canny/Sobel edge detectors for lane markings, ORB keypoint extraction for visual odometry, and Lucas‚ÄìKanade optical flow for motion estimation.
4. **Perception Subsystem** ‚Äî Deep CNNs (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) classify objects (cars, pedestrians, cyclists, traffic signs). Semantic segmentation (U-Net) classifies every pixel for free-space detection.
5. **Localization Subsystem** ‚Äî Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) and ORB-SLAM2 fuse camera and IMU data to estimate the 6-DoF pose of the vehicle. GNSS supplements when available.
6. **Decision / Planning Subsystem** ‚Äî Behavioural planner uses TTC, lane offset, and traffic-rule interpreter to decide action (warn, brake, steer).
7. **Actuation and HMI Subsystem** ‚Äî outputs are sent over **CAN bus** to brake, steering, and powertrain ECUs; warnings are flashed on the **Head-Up Display (HUD)** and instrument cluster.

This architecture implements the **Sense ‚Üí Perceive ‚Üí Reason ‚Üí Act** paradigm and is the foundation of SAE Level 2‚Äì5 automation.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Depth `Z`:**
Convert disparity to millimetres:
$$d_{\text{mm}} = d \cdot p = 40 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2 \, \text{mm}$$
Apply stereo formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 200}{0.2} \\
&= 8000 \, \text{mm} = 8 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) TTC:**
Convert speeds:
$$v_1 = 90 \, \text{km/h} = 25 \, \text{m/s}, \quad v_2 = 60 \, \text{km/h} = 16.67 \, \text{m/s}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{rel}} &= 25 - 16.67 = 8.33 \, \text{m/s} \\
\text{TTC} &= \frac{Z}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \frac{8}{8.33} \approx 0.96 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Justification:**
Since **TTC ‚âà 0.96 s** is **less than 1.4 s**, both **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** and **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** must be activated to prevent a rear-end collision.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) algorithm as applied to a vehicle's visual perception system. Discuss the role of feature extraction, loop closure, and bundle adjustment in achieving robust localization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's front camera captures two successive frames at `Œît = 50 ms` apart. A feature at depth `Z = 40 m` shows pixel displacement `Œîd = 25 px` between frames. Camera focal length `f = 5 mm`, pixel pitch `p = 4 ¬µm`. Compute:
   (i) Optical-flow speed in pixel/s. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) Ego-vehicle speed using visual odometry. **(3 marks)**
   (iii) If TTC threshold for FCW is 2.5 s, calculate the maximum safe closing speed at `Z = 25 m`. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Visual SLAM** is the process of simultaneously **estimating a vehicle's pose** (localization) while **building a map** of the environment, using camera input.

Key stages:

1. **Feature Extraction** ‚Äî ORB keypoints are detected in each frame (rotation- and scale-invariant). These features persist across frames, enabling matching.
2. **Feature Matching and Tracking** ‚Äî successive frames are matched using descriptor similarity (Hamming distance for ORB); RANSAC filters outliers.
3. **Motion Estimation (Front-end)** ‚Äî the Essential / Fundamental matrix is computed from matched features to recover the relative rotation `R` and translation `t` between frames.
4. **Bundle Adjustment (Back-end)** ‚Äî non-linear optimization (Levenberg‚ÄìMarquardt) refines camera trajectory `T_{1:k}` and 3D map points `X_j` by minimizing the **reprojection error**:
$$\min_{T_{1:k}, X_j} \sum_{i,j} \rho\!\left(\pi(T_i, X_j) - x_{ij}\right)$$
5. **Loop Closure** ‚Äî Bag-of-Words (DBoW2) recognizes previously visited places. Once a loop is detected, a **pose-graph optimization** corrects accumulated drift globally, ensuring the map is consistent.

In a vehicle VIS, ORB-SLAM2/3 provides centimeter-level localization suitable for tunnels, urban canyons, and parking garages where GNSS is unreliable.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Optical-flow speed:**
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{flow}} &= \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta t} = \frac{25 \, \text{px}}{50 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}} \\
&= 500 \, \text{px/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Ego-vehicle speed:**
Using the visual-odometry formula derived earlier:
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{ego}} &= \frac{\Delta d \cdot p \cdot Z}{f \cdot \Delta t} \\
&= \frac{25 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 40}{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 16 \, \text{m/s} = 57.6 \, \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Maximum safe closing speed:**
At `Z = 25 m` and TTC threshold = 2.5 s:
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{rel, max}} &= \frac{Z}{\text{TTC}} = \frac{25}{2.5} = 10 \, \text{m/s} \\
&= 36 \, \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}$$
Any closing speed above **36 km/h** triggers the FCW alert at 25 m range.

### KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks ‚Äî Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of disparity** ‚Äî disparity is in **pixels** but must be converted to **millimetres** (or metres) using pixel pitch before substituting into `Z = f¬∑B/d`. Marks deducted if units are inconsistent.
> 2. **Confusing `v_rel` with `v_ego`** ‚Äî TTC uses *relative* (closing) speed, not absolute ego-speed. A stationary obstacle has `v_rel = v_ego`.
> 3. **Mixing up FOV and viewing angle** ‚Äî students often write FOV as the angle of one camera, not the total horizontal coverage.
> 4. **Skipping the diagnostic step** ‚Äî always state **"Since TTC > 2.7 s, only advisory; since TTC < 1.4 s, full AEB"** in conclusion-style questions.
> 5. **Ignoring the in-vehicle bus layer** ‚Äî KTU 2024 scheme explicitly expects mention of **CAN / Automotive Ethernet** when describing how perception data reaches the actuator.
> 6. **No diagram** ‚Äî 14-mark answers without a labelled block diagram typically lose **2‚Äì3 marks**.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Vehicle Information System (VIS)** = perception + processing + communication + HMI/actuation.
- [x] **Sense ‚Üí Perceive ‚Üí Reason ‚Üí Act** is the canonical mobile-robot vision pipeline.
- [x] **Stereo depth formula:** `Z = f¬∑B / d` ‚Äî *convert disparity to mm first*.
- [x] **Time-to-Collision:** `TTC = Z / v_rel`; thresholds: **2.7 s (advisory)**, **1.4 s (AEB)**.
- [x] **Visual Odometry speed:** `v = Œîd¬∑p¬∑Z / (f¬∑Œît)`.
- [x] **SLAM** = simultaneous localization + mapping using ORB features + bundle adjustment + loop closure.
- [x] **In-vehicle buses:** CAN (500 kbps), LIN (19.2 kbps), FlexRay (10 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbps ‚Äì 1 Gbps).
- [x] **Camera calibration:** Intrinsic matrix `K`, distortion vector `d`, principal point `(c_x, c_y)`, Zhang's checkerboard method.
- [x] **CNN architectures used:** YOLO, SSD, Faster R-CNN (detection); U-Net, DeepLab (segmentation); MobileNet (lightweight ECU deployment).
- [x] **Performance metrics:** IoU ‚â• 0.5, Detection Rate (Recall), Precision, F1, FPS, end-to-end latency < 100 ms.
- [x] **Safety standard:** ISO 26262 (ASIL A‚ÄìD); ASIL-D reserved for steering/braking.
- [x] **Industry leaders:** Mobileye (Intel), NVIDIA Drive, Tesla HW3/HW4, Bosch, Continental, Aptiv, Waymo.
- [x] **Real-time constraint:** 30 fps ‚áí 33 ms/frame; AEB/FCW systems must close the perception-loop within **100 ms**.
- [x] **Sensor fusion:** Camera + RADAR + LIDAR + IMU + GPS = robust 360¬∞ situational awareness.
- [x] **HMI outputs:** Head-Up Display (HUD), instrument cluster, infotainment, haptic steering, seat vibration.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always draw the **block diagram** before writing the answer ‚Äî it is worth a guaranteed **2‚Äì3 marks** even before the explanation begins. Mention **CAN bus** explicitly in any question about how data is communicated between subsystems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: OBJECT DETECTION IN AUTOTRONICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Autotronics (PCMRT602)**, **Object Detection** is formally defined as the computational and electronic process of identifying, localizing, and classifying physical entities (vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists, animals, road infrastructure, lane markings, and traffic signs) in the vicinity of a host vehicle using a heterogeneous suite of onboard sensors, real-time signal processing units, and machine-learning inference engines. The detected objects are subsequently tracked over time, their trajectories predicted, and the corresponding actuator commands issued to the vehicle's braking, steering, and powertrain Electronic Control Units (ECUs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCMRT602 ‚Äì Autotronics, Module 4):**
> Object detection forms the perception layer of an Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) and the broader autonomous-driving stack. It is responsible for converting raw sensory data (electromagnetic echoes, optical pixel arrays, acoustic reflections) into a structured, semantically meaningful environment model that downstream decision-making modules (path planning, collision avoidance) can consume.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Human-Eye Analogy:**
Think of object detection in a car exactly like the way a human driver perceives the road. Your two eyes are the **cameras**, your ears are the **ultrasonic sensors**, and the way you feel the wind on your face is the **radar/LiDAR**. The visual cortex in your brain combines all these inputs to instantly recognise "That is a child chasing a ball into the road." The **ECU in an autonomous system** does precisely the same thing ‚Äî but with millimetre accuracy, in microseconds, and without getting distracted by a phone call.

**The Bat Analogy (for Radar/Ultrasonic):**
A bat emits high-frequency sound waves, listens for the echo, and uses the time delay to determine how far an insect is and in what direction. Automotive radar and ultrasonic parking sensors do exactly this with electromagnetic or acoustic waves ‚Äî the **time-of-flight** tells the distance, and the **Doppler shift** tells the relative velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics in Automotive Object Detection:**
> - **Detection Range:** typically **0 m to 250 m** for automotive radar, **0 m to 100 m** for automotive LiDAR, **0 m to 50 m** for stereo cameras.
> - **Angular Resolution:** as low as **0.1¬∞** for high-resolution 4D imaging radar.
> - **Latency:** the end-to-end perception-to-actuation loop must be **‚â§ 100 ms** for highway autonomous emergency braking (AEB) per **ISO 15623** and **Euro NCAP** protocols.
> - **False Positive Rate:** must be **‚â§ 10‚Åª‚Å∂ per hour** of operation (per ASIL-D / **ISO 26262** functional safety).
> - **Operating Frequency:** 24 GHz, 77 GHz, and the emerging **79 GHz** band for automotive radar.

### 1.3 Why Object Detection is Pivotal in Autotronics

Object detection is the **sensory gateway** to all modern automotive safety and convenience features. It underpins:

1. **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** and **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)**
2. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**
3. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** and **Lane Change Assist (LCA)**
4. **Pedestrian and Cyclist Detection** (for Vulnerable Road User ‚Äî VRU ‚Äî protection)
5. **Traffic Jam Assist (TJA)** and **Highway Chauffeur (Level 3 autonomy)**
6. **Automated Parking** (perpendicular, parallel, and valet)
7. **Night Vision Systems** (infrared-camera based pedestrian highlighting)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Perspective:** Whenever a question mentions "autonomous features," "ADAS," or "collision avoidance," object detection is implicitly the first half of the answer. Always start by mentioning the **sensor**, then the **algorithm**, and finally the **actuator command**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Object Detection Pipeline

Every automotive object detection system, regardless of sensor modality, can be decomposed into the following canonical pipeline:

### Stage 1 ‚Äî Data Acquisition (Sensing)
Raw physical signals (electromagnetic echoes, photon returns, pixel intensities) are captured by the vehicle's sensor array. The sensor choice dictates the entire downstream algorithm.

### Stage 2 ‚Äî Pre-processing
- **Radar:** clutter filtering, MTI (Moving Target Indication), CFAR (Constant False Alarm Rate) detection.
- **Camera:** white balance, lens distortion correction, histogram equalization, denoising.
- **LiDAR:** ground-plane segmentation, point-cloud voxelization, ROI cropping.

### Stage 3 ‚Äî Feature Extraction
Relevant kinematic and semantic features are extracted:
- From radar: **Range $R$, Range-Rate $\dot{R}$, Azimuth $\phi$, Elevation $\theta$, Radar Cross Section (RCS) $\sigma$.**
- From camera: **edges, corners (Harris/ORB), HOG descriptors, deep convolutional feature maps.**
- From LiDAR: **point cloud clusters, surface normals, height variance.**

### Stage 4 ‚Äî Detection & Classification
- **Classical:** SVM, Random Forest, AdaBoost over hand-crafted features.
- **Deep Learning:** YOLO, SSD, Faster R-CNN, CenterPoint, PointPillars.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF), and the modern **Transformer-based fusion (TransFusion, BEVFusion).**

### Stage 5 ‚Äî Tracking & Post-processing
Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) using **SORT, DeepSORT, or Joint Probabilistic Data Association (JPDA)**. The tracker maintains object identity across frames and predicts future trajectories.

## 2.2 Sensor Modalities ‚Äî A Comparative Study

| Property | Radar (77 GHz) | LiDAR (905/1550 nm) | Camera (Mono/Stereo) | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | Up to 250 m | Up to 200 m | Up to 80 m (mono) / 60 m (stereo) | 0.1 to 5 m |
| Range Resolution | 0.1 m (FMCW) | 0.03 m (ToF) | N/A (depth inferred) | 0.01 m |
| Angular Resolution | 0.1¬∞ to 2¬∞ | 0.1¬∞ to 0.4¬∞ | sub-pixel (sub-arcminute) | coarse |
| Velocity (Doppler) | Direct measurement | Computed (multi-scan) | Computed (optical flow) | No |
| Weather Robustness | Excellent (rain, fog, dust) | Moderate (rain/snow degrades) | Poor (low light, glare) | Excellent |
| Night Operation | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (needs NIR/IR illuminator) | Excellent |
| Cost (USD) | 50 to 300 | 500 to 8000 | 20 to 200 | 5 to 15 |
| Primary Role | Long-range dynamic objects | 3D environment mapping | Classification & lane detection | Parking assist |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM FORMULAS ‚Äî Object Detection & Sensor Range Estimation**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | Time-of-Flight range (LiDAR, ultrasonic) | m |
| 2 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Monostatic radar range equation | W |
| 3 | $R_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B \, F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min}}\right)^{1/4}$ | Maximum detectable range of a radar | m |
| 4 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 \, v_r \, f_0}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift (closing target) | Hz |
| 5 | $\dot{R} = \dfrac{\lambda \, f_d}{2}$ | Radial velocity from Doppler | m/s |
| 6 | $\mathrm{TTC} = \dfrac{R}{\dot{R}} = \dfrac{R}{v_{\mathrm{rel}}}$ | Time-to-Collision (kinematic) | s |
| 7 | $\theta_{\mathrm{FOV}} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2 f}\right)$ | Pinhole-camera field of view | rad |
| 8 | $X = \dfrac{f \, x_w}{z_w},\;\; Y = \dfrac{f \, y_w}{z_w}$ | Pinhole projection (camera intrinsics) | pixels |
| 9 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | Stereo-camera depth (B = baseline) | m |
| 10 | $\mathrm{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | Intersection over Union for bounding box eval | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\mathrm{dBsm}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{1 \,\mathrm{m}^2}\right)$ | Radar Cross Section in dBsm | dB |
| 12 | $S_{\min} = k T_0 B \cdot F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min}$ | Minimum detectable radar signal | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU derivations, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \,\mathrm{m/s}$ (speed of light), $c_{sound} \approx 343 \,\mathrm{m/s}$ at 20 ¬∞C, and $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \,\mathrm{J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant). For a **77 GHz** automotive radar, $\lambda = c/f_0 = 3.905 \,\mathrm{mm}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The object detection system is the **first safety-critical link** in the Sense-Plan-Act chain. In production vehicles, it is now governed by:
- **ISO 26262** ‚Äî Functional Safety (ASIL-B for perception, ASIL-D for braking actuation).
- **ISO/SAE 21434** ‚Äî Cybersecurity Engineering for Road Vehicles.
- **UNECE R157** ‚Äî Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) regulation for Level 3 autonomy.
- **Euro NCAP 2025+** ‚Äî mandates AEB for pedestrians, cyclists, and motorcyclists; object detection is non-negotiable for a 5-star rating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Radar Maximum Range Equation

We start from the **Monostatic Radar Range Equation** (both transmit and receive antennas co-located) and solve for the maximum range at which the received signal power just equals the minimum detectable signal.

**Starting Expression:**

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Set $P_r = S_{\min}$ (minimum detectable signal):**

$$S_{\min} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R_{\max}^4}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Cross-multiply to isolate $R_{\max}^4$:**

$$R_{\max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{\min}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Expand $S_{\min}$ using the radar-receiver noise model:**

$$S_{\min} = k \, T_0 \, B \, F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute the expanded $S_{\min}$:**

$$R_{\max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B \, F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Take the fourth root on both sides:**

$$R_{\max} = \left( \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B \, F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min}} \right)^{1/4}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 4√ó increase in transmitted power yields only a $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41\times$ increase in range. Range therefore scales very weakly with $P_t$, which is why modern radars use **high antenna gain $G$** and **low-noise receivers (low Noise Figure $F$)** to extend range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Time-of-Flight Distance for LiDAR / Ultrasonic

**Physical Premise:** A pulse is emitted at $t = 0$, travels to the target at speed $v_{\mathrm{prop}}$, reflects, and returns to the receiver. The total round-trip time is $\tau$.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Round-trip distance covered by the wave:**

$$D_{\mathrm{rt}} = v_{\mathrm{prop}} \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî The wave covers the one-way distance twice:**

$$2 R = v_{\mathrm{prop}} \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for the one-way range $R$:**

$$R = \frac{v_{\mathrm{prop}} \cdot \tau}{2}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute $v_{\mathrm{prop}} = c$ (light) for LiDAR and $v_{\mathrm{prop}} = c_s$ (sound) for ultrasonic:**

$$R_{\mathrm{LiDAR}} = \frac{c \, \tau}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot \tau}{2}$$

$$R_{\mathrm{Ultrasonic}} = \frac{c_s \, \tau}{2} = \frac{343 \cdot \tau}{2}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** A LiDAR measures a round-trip time of $\tau = 666 \,\mathrm{ns}$ (i.e., $666 \times 10^{-9}$ s) to a leading vehicle. Compute the range.

$$R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 666 \times 10^{-9}}{2} = \frac{199.8}{2} = 99.9 \,\mathrm{m}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Time-to-Collision (TTC) from Radar Measurements

**Definition:** TTC is the time remaining before the host vehicle collides with the leading object, assuming both maintain their current velocities.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define the closing (relative) velocity:**

$$v_{\mathrm{rel}} = v_{\mathrm{host}} - v_{\mathrm{target}} = \dot{R}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Define the kinematic equation for closure (constant velocity assumption):**

$$R(t) = R_0 - v_{\mathrm{rel}} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Set $R(t) = 0$ at the moment of collision ($t = \mathrm{TTC}$):**

$$0 = R_0 - v_{\mathrm{rel}} \cdot \mathrm{TTC}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Solve for TTC:**

$$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{R_0}{v_{\mathrm{rel}}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Using the Doppler-shift relation $\dot{R} = \dfrac{\lambda f_d}{2}$:**

$$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{R_0 \, \lambda}{2 \, f_d}$$

**Practical Application:** AEB is typically triggered when $\mathrm{TTC} \le 2.5 \,\mathrm{s}$ for adults and $\mathrm{TTC} \le 1.4 \,\mathrm{s}$ for children (per Euro NCAP VRU protocols).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Pinhole Camera Projection

A pinhole camera projects a 3D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ onto the 2D image plane.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Similar-triangle relation in the $X$-$Z$ plane:**

$$\frac{X}{f} = \frac{X_w}{Z_w}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Solve for image coordinate $X$:**

$$X = f \cdot \frac{X_w}{Z_w}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Similarly in the $Y$-$Z$ plane:**

$$Y = f \cdot \frac{Y_w}{Z_w}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Including the pixel size $k$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$:**

$$x = k \, f \cdot \frac{X_w}{Z_w} + c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_w}{Z_w} + c_x$$

$$y = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_w}{Z_w} + c_y$$

These two equations together form the **intrinsic camera matrix** $\mathbf{K}$, fundamental to camera-based object detection.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ‚Äî Stereo Camera Depth

**Step 1 ‚Äî Disparity definition:** $d = x_{\mathrm{left}} - x_{\mathrm{right}}$

**Step 2 ‚Äî From the pinhole model, a point at depth $Z$ projects with horizontal shift $f \cdot B / Z$ between the two cameras.**

**Step 3 ‚Äî Equate and solve:**

$$d = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Numerical check:** $f = 800 \,\mathrm{px}$, $B = 120 \,\mathrm{mm} = 0.12 \,\mathrm{m}$, $d = 12 \,\mathrm{px}$:

$$Z = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{12} = 8 \,\mathrm{m}$$

This is why stereo cameras give dense depth maps for object detection but fail at long range (small disparity becomes noise-dominated).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Detection Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Speed (FPS) | mAP (COCO) | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Viola-Jones (Haar) | Classical | 15 | N/A | Face / plate detection | Weak for cars / pedestrians |
| HOG + SVM | Classical | 10 | 0.40 | Pedestrians | No scale invariance |
| YOLOv8 | One-stage DL | 160 | 0.53 | Real-time, embedded | Slightly lower accuracy |
| Faster R-CNN | Two-stage DL | 7 | 0.62 | High accuracy | Slow, GPU-bound |
| PointPillars | LiDAR DL | 60 | 0.55 | 3D detection | Requires LiDAR |
| CenterPoint | LiDAR DL | 30 | 0.65 | SOTA 3D detection | Compute intensive |
| BEVFusion | Sensor Fusion | 20 | 0.70 | Camera + LiDAR + Radar | Multi-modal calibration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Object Detection Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RADAR 77GHz Antenna Array]:::sensor --> B1[Analog Front End and ADC]
    A2[LiDAR 905nm Scanner]:::sensor --> B2[Point Cloud Processor]
    A3[Stereo Camera Pair]:::sensor --> B3[ISP and Image Rectifier]
    A4[Ultrasonic Pillar]:::sensor --> B4[ToF Echo Sampler]
    B1 --> C1[CFAR Detection and Clustering]
    B2 --> C2[Ground Plane Segmentation]
    B3 --> C3[CNN Feature Backbone]
    B4 --> C4[Range Bin Computation]
    C1 --> D[Central Sensor Fusion Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Multi Object Tracker with Kalman Filter]
    E --> F[Trajectory Predictor]
    F --> G[Decision Layer: AEB ACC LKA TJA]
    G --> H[Vehicle Actuator Bus: CAN or FlexRay or Ethernet]

```

## 4.2 Sensor Fusion Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PERCEPTION
        P1[Radar Object List] --> PF1[Kalman Filter Bank]
        P2[LiDAR 3D Bounding Boxes] --> PF1
        P3[Camera 2D Detections] --> PF2[Deep Learning Tracker]
        PF1 --> PF3[Track to Track Association]
        PF2 --> PF3
    end
    PF3 --> PRED[Trajectory Prediction: Constant Turn Rate and Acceleration]
    PRED --> RISK[Risk Assessment and TTC Estimator]
    RISK --> ACT[Actuator Command Generator]
    ACT --> BRK[Brake ECU]
    ACT --> STR[Steer ECU]
    ACT --> PWR[Powertrain ECU]
    BRK --> SAFE[(Functional Safety: ISO 26262 ASIL D)]
    STR --> SAFE
    PWR --> SAFE
```

## 4.3 Bounding Box Regression Logic (Visualisation of the 4-D Output)

