```mermaid
    IMG[Input Image 1920x1080]:::input --> CNN[CNN Backbone ResNet50 or EfficientNet]
    CNN --> FPN[Feature Pyramid Network]
    FPN --> REG[Regression Head x y w h]
    FPN --> CLS[Classification Head: car person bicycle sign]
    REG --> NMS[Non Maximum Suppression IoU threshold 0.5]
    CLS --> NMS
    NMS --> OUT[Final Bounding Box List with Confidence]
    classDef input fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop ADAS Control Flow (Sense ‚Üí Plan ‚Üí Act)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Object Detection: Where What How Fast] --> S2[Situation Analysis: Safe or Critical]
    S2 -->|Safe| S3[Maintain Cruise Speed]
    S2 -->|Critical| S4[Compute Required Deceleration]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Brake via Hydraulic Modulator]
    S3 --> S6[Continuous Monitoring Loop]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**Define object detection as applied to an automotive ADAS system. List the four primary sensors used for object detection in a modern passenger car.**

**Model Answer:**
Object detection in autotronics refers to the real-time identification, localisation, and classification of nearby entities (vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists, road signs, lane markings) using onboard sensors, enabling downstream ADAS functions such as AEB, ACC, and BSD.

The four primary sensors are:
1. **Radar** (24 / 77 / 79 GHz) ‚Äî long-range dynamic object detection.
2. **LiDAR** (905 nm or 1550 nm) ‚Äî high-resolution 3D point cloud.
3. **Camera** (mono / stereo) ‚Äî classification and lane/semantic understanding.
4. **Ultrasonic** (40 to 70 kHz) ‚Äî short-range parking assist.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**What is the Time-to-Collision (TTC)? Derive the expression for TTC in terms of range and relative velocity.**

**Model Answer:**
TTC is the estimated time remaining before a collision occurs, assuming both the host vehicle and the target maintain their current velocities.

**Derivation:**
Let $R$ be the current range to the target and $v_{\mathrm{rel}} = v_{\mathrm{host}} - v_{\mathrm{target}}$ be the closing relative velocity. Under constant-velocity motion, the range evolves as:

$$R(t) = R_0 - v_{\mathrm{rel}} \cdot t$$

Setting $R(t_{\mathrm{TTC}}) = 0$:

$$0 = R_0 - v_{\mathrm{rel}} \cdot \mathrm{TTC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathrm{TTC} = \frac{R_0}{v_{\mathrm{rel}}}$$

For a radar system, $v_{\mathrm{rel}} = \dot{R} = \lambda f_d / 2$, so:

$$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{2 R_0}{\lambda f_d}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of an automotive **FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) radar** used for object detection. With a neat block diagram, describe the transmit and receive signal processing chain. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 77 GHz automotive radar has the following specifications: transmitted power $P_t = 10 \,\mathrm{mW}$, antenna gain $G = 20 \,\mathrm{dBi}$ (i.e., 100 in linear), wavelength $\lambda = 3.9 \,\mathrm{mm}$, target RCS $\sigma = 10 \,\mathrm{m^2}$, noise figure $F = 4 \,\mathrm{dB}$, bandwidth $B = 200 \,\mathrm{MHz}$, minimum SNR $= 12 \,\mathrm{dB}$, and system temperature $T_0 = 290 \,\mathrm{K}$. Calculate the **maximum detection range** $R_{\max}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle of FMCW Radar:**
Unlike a pulsed radar, an FMCW radar transmits a continuous signal whose frequency is **swept linearly** over a bandwidth $B_{\mathrm{sweep}}$ during each chirp of duration $T_{\mathrm{chirp}}$.

**Block Diagram (described in text form):**
1. **Synthesiser (DDS / PLL):** generates a chirp from $f_0$ to $f_0 + B_{\mathrm{sweep}}$.
2. **Power Amplifier & Transmit Antenna:** radiates the chirp toward the scene.
3. **Receive Antenna & LNA:** captures the reflected echo.
4. **Mixer:** multiplies the received echo with a copy of the transmitted chirp.
5. **Low-Pass Filter & ADC:** the mixer output is a **beat signal** with frequency $f_b$ directly proportional to range.
6. **Range-FFT** ‚Üí 1D range profile.
7. **Doppler-FFT** (over multiple chirps) ‚Üí range-Doppler map.
8. **CFAR Detection** ‚Üí extracts peaks corresponding to targets.

**Key Equations:**
- **Beat frequency:** $f_b = \dfrac{2 R \, B_{\mathrm{sweep}}}{c \, T_{\mathrm{chirp}}}$
- **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 B_{\mathrm{sweep}}}$
- **Maximum unambiguous range:** $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c \, T_{\mathrm{chirp}}}{2}$
- **Maximum unambiguous velocity:** $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4 T_{\mathrm{PRI}}}$

**Advantages for automotive use:**
- Simultaneous measurement of range and Doppler (velocity) of multiple targets.
- Low peak power (safe, low-EMI, low-cost semiconductor integration in 77/79 GHz SiGe or CMOS processes).
- Excellent robustness in rain, fog, and dust.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $P_t = 10 \times 10^{-3} \,\mathrm{W}$, $G = 100$ (linear), $\lambda = 3.9 \times 10^{-3} \,\mathrm{m}$, $\sigma = 10 \,\mathrm{m^2}$, $B = 200 \times 10^6 \,\mathrm{Hz}$, $T_0 = 290 \,\mathrm{K}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \,\mathrm{J/K}$.

Convert decibel values to linear:
- $F = 4 \,\mathrm{dB} = 10^{0.4} \approx 2.512$
- $\mathrm{SNR}_{\min} = 12 \,\mathrm{dB} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.849$

**Apply the radar range equation:**

$$R_{\max} = \left( \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_0 \, B \, F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min}} \right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the numerator:**
$$P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma = (10^{-2}) \times (10^4) \times (1.521 \times 10^{-5}) \times 10$$
$$= 10^{-2} \times 10^4 \times 1.521 \times 10^{-5} \times 10 = 1.521 \,\mathrm{W \cdot m^4}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the denominator:**
$$(4\pi)^3 = 197.39 \times \pi^0 \approx 1973.9$$
$$k T_0 B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 2 \times 10^8 = 8.0 \times 10^{-13} \,\mathrm{W}$$
$$F \cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{\min} = 2.512 \times 15.849 = 39.81$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 1973.9 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-13} \times 39.81 = 6.286 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the ratio:**
$$\frac{1.521}{6.286 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.42 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Take the fourth root:**
$$R_{\max} = (2.42 \times 10^7)^{0.25} = (24.2 \times 10^6)^{0.25}$$
$$= (24.2)^{0.25} \times (10^6)^{0.25} = 2.22 \times 10^{1.5} \approx 70.1 \,\mathrm{m}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_{\max} \approx 70 \,\mathrm{m}$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **camera-based object detection pipeline** used in vehicles. Explain the role of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and bounding-box regression in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A stereo camera system has focal length $f = 1000 \,\mathrm{px}$ and baseline $B = 150 \,\mathrm{mm}$. For a pedestrian detected with a disparity of $25 \,\mathrm{px}$, calculate (i) the depth $Z$ and (ii) the time-to-collision (TTC) if the host vehicle is moving at $60 \,\mathrm{km/h}$ and the pedestrian is stationary. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Camera-Based Object Detection Pipeline (Step-by-step):**
1. **Image Capture:** the front-facing camera (typically 1 to 8 MP, 30 to 60 fps) acquires RGB frames.
2. **Pre-processing:** lens distortion correction, image normalisation, ROI cropping.
3. **CNN Backbone (e.g., ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B3, or CSPDarknet):** extracts hierarchical features (edges ‚Üí textures ‚Üí parts ‚Üí objects).
4. **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN):** fuses multi-scale features for detecting both small (distant) and large (nearby) objects.
5. **Detection Heads:** two parallel branches:
   - **Classification head:** outputs class probabilities (car, truck, pedestrian, cyclist, sign).
   - **Regression head:** predicts the four bounding-box parameters $(x, y, w, h)$ relative to predefined anchor boxes or keypoints.
6. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** removes redundant overlapping detections using **IoU thresholding** (typically 0.5).
7. **Tracking:** DeepSORT or ByteTrack links detections across frames using motion and appearance features.

**Role of CNNs:** Replace hand-crafted features (HOG, Haar) with learned, hierarchical, translation-invariant representations, achieving state-of-the-art mAP on COCO and KITTI benchmarks.

**Role of Bounding-Box Regression:** Predicts the tightest rectangle enclosing each object, parameterised as offsets from a reference anchor: $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$. This enables precise localisation needed for collision-time estimation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $f = 1000 \,\mathrm{px}$, $B = 0.15 \,\mathrm{m}$, disparity $d = 25 \,\mathrm{px}$, $v_{\mathrm{host}} = 60 \,\mathrm{km/h} = 16.67 \,\mathrm{m/s}$, $v_{\mathrm{pedestrian}} = 0$.

**(i) Depth $Z$:**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{1000 \times 0.15}{25} = \frac{150}{25} = 6.0 \,\mathrm{m}$$

**(ii) Time-to-Collision:**

Relative velocity: $v_{\mathrm{rel}} = v_{\mathrm{host}} - v_{\mathrm{pedestrian}} = 16.67 - 0 = 16.67 \,\mathrm{m/s}$

$$\mathrm{TTC} = \frac{Z}{v_{\mathrm{rel}}} = \frac{6.0}{16.67} = 0.36 \,\mathrm{s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** At $60 \,\mathrm{km/h}$, a pedestrian at $6 \,\mathrm{m}$ gives only $0.36 \,\mathrm{s}$ to react ‚Äî far below the human reaction time of $\approx 1.5 \,\mathrm{s}$. **AEB must therefore act autonomously** at this gap; this is the precise reason object detection in autotronics is safety-critical.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Object Detection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the time-of-flight equation $R = c\tau/2$. The pulse travels to the target AND back.
> 2. **Mixing up $\mathrm{dB}$ and linear values** in the radar range equation. Always convert $G$ (dBi), $F$ (dB), and $\mathrm{SNR}_{\min}$ (dB) to linear scale **before** substitution.
> 3. **Confusing range resolution and range accuracy.** Range resolution is set by bandwidth; range accuracy is set by SNR and is always worse.
> 4. **Skipping the unit analysis.** Every numerical answer must end with a unit. Marks are deducted for bare numbers.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions.** In TTC derivations, you must explicitly say "assuming constant relative velocity" or "neglecting road friction and reaction time."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Object detection** = Sense ‚Üí Process ‚Üí Localise ‚Üí Classify ‚Üí Track ‚Üí Act.
- **Four canonical sensors:** Radar (long-range velocity), LiDAR (3D geometry), Camera (semantics), Ultrasonic (parking).
- **Time-of-Flight range:** $R = c\tau/2$ (LiDAR, ultrasonic) and $R = c f_b T_{\mathrm{chirp}} / (2 B_{\mathrm{sweep}})$ (FMCW radar).
- **Maximum radar range** scales as the **fourth root** of $P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / (\mathrm{noise})$.
- **Doppler velocity:** $v_r = \lambda f_d / 2$.
- **Time-to-Collision:** $\mathrm{TTC} = R / v_{\mathrm{rel}}$ ‚Äî the single most important safety metric in ADAS.
- **Camera intrinsics:** $X = f X_w / Z_w$.
- **Stereo depth:** $Z = fB/d$.
- **IoU** is the standard evaluation metric for bounding-box quality.
- **Sensor fusion** (radar + camera + LiDAR) is the production-grade solution for Level 2+ autonomy.
- **Functional safety:** Object detection is governed by **ISO 26262** (ASIL-B/D) and **UNECE R157**.
- **Euro NCAP AEB** triggers at $\mathrm{TTC} \le 2.5 \,\mathrm{s}$ (adults), $1.4 \,\mathrm{s}$ (children).
- **Latency budget:** end-to-end perception ‚Üí braking must stay **below 100 ms** for highway AEB.
- **Resolution vs Accuracy:** Resolution is hardware-limited; accuracy is SNR-limited.
- **Key constants to memorise:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \,\mathrm{m/s}$, $c_s = 343 \,\mathrm{m/s}$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \,\mathrm{J/K}$, $\lambda_{77\,\mathrm{GHz}} \approx 3.9 \,\mathrm{mm}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Warning and Avoidance System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collision Warning and Avoidance System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collision Warning and Avoidance System (CWAS)** is an Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) that continuously monitors the vehicle's surrounding environment using on-board perception sensors, estimates the probability and severity of an impending collision with static or dynamic obstacles, and executes a layered response strategy comprising **Driver Warning** (auditory, visual, and haptic cues), **Driver Assistance** (partial braking or steering correction), and **Automatic Collision Avoidance** (autonomous emergency braking and evasive steering).

The system is formally classified under **UNECE Regulation No. 152 (Uniform provisions concerning the approval of motor vehicles and their systems with respect to the Advanced Emergency Braking System (AEBS))** and **ISO 22839** for forward vehicle collision warning systems.

### Functional Hierarchy

| Layer | Action | Driver Override |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| **L1 ‚Äî Perception** | Detect & classify obstacles | Always |
| **L2 ‚Äî Warning** | Alert driver (audible/visual/haptic) | Always |
| **L3 ‚Äî Driver Assistance** | Pre-charge brakes, partial braking | Driver can override |
| **L4 ‚Äî Automatic Intervention** | Full AEB / evasive steer | Driver can override (until t < t-critical) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The "Airbag of the Brain"**
>
> Imagine you are cycling downhill with your eyes closed. Your friend runs behind you shouting "BRAKE! BRAKE! BRAKE!" ‚Äî that is the **Collision Warning System**. If you don't react, your friend physically grabs the handlebar and squeezes the brake lever for you ‚Äî that is the **Collision Avoidance System**.
>
> In a car, sensors (radar/lidar/camera) act as your friend's "eyes", the ECU acts as the "brain", and the brake actuator + steering motor act as the friend's "hands". The whole system must react in **less than 300 milliseconds** ‚Äî far faster than a human reaction time of approximately **1.0 s to 1.5 s**.

### Why This Matters in Autotronics

- Pedestrian fatalities represent roughly **22%** of global road deaths ‚Äî AEB reduces these by **38%** (per **EURONCAP 2023** field data).
- The economic benefit-to-cost ratio of deploying CWAS in passenger vehicles exceeds **4:1** when accounting on insurance, medical, and productivity savings.
- It is a **mandatory fitment** in new passenger cars in the **European Union (since July 2024)** and in **India under AIS-184** for new vehicle categories.

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Driver perception-reaction time:** $\tau_{r} = 1.0\text{ s}$ (alert) to $\tau_{r} = 1.5\text{ s}$ (distracted).
- **System latency budget:** $t_{lat} \le 100\text{ ms}$ from detection to brake actuation.
- **Required Time-to-Collision (TTC) threshold for warning:** $TTC_{warn} = 2.7\text{ s}$.
- **Critical TTC for AEB intervention:** $TTC_{crit} = 1.4\text{ s}$.
- **Maximum deceleration of a passenger car (dry asphalt):** $a_{max} = 8.0\text{ m/s}^{2}$ (‚âà 0.82 g).
- **Operating radar frequency band (automotive long-range):** $f = 76\text{ GHz}$ to $77\text{ GHz}$ (W-band).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a CWAS

The complete operational concept is decomposed into six sequential stages:

1. **Sensing Stage** ‚Äî Radar, Lidar, camera, and ultrasonic sensors capture raw measurements of range, range-rate, and bearing to objects in the vehicle's vicinity.
2. **Pre-Processing Stage** ‚Äî Sensor data is filtered (Kalman / Extended Kalman Filter) to suppress noise, clutter, and ghost targets.
3. **Object Tracking & Classification** ‚Äî Multi-object tracking algorithms (e.g., Joint Probabilistic Data Association Filter ‚Äî JPDAF) maintain object identity across frames and classify as car, truck, motorcycle, pedestrian, or static obstacle.
4. **Situation Analysis** ‚Äî Time-to-Collision (TTC), Stopping Distance, and Collision Probability are computed.
5. **Decision Stage** ‚Äî A state-machine compares current TTC to warning thresholds and decides on warning or intervention level.
6. **Actuation Stage** ‚Äî HMI (Human Machine Interface), Electronic Stability Control (ESC) brake pre-charge, and Electric Power Steering (EPS) actuators are triggered.

## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' of Each Stage

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Kalman Filtering?** ‚Äî Radar returns are corrupted by noise with variance $\sigma_R \approx 0.3\text{ m}$. A single measurement cannot reliably feed a safety-critical decision. Kalman filtering optimally fuses predictions with measurements, reducing range error to $\sigma_R' \approx 0.05\text{ m}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why compute TTC?** ‚Äî TTC is the single most informative scalar for an impending collision because it inherently combines distance and closing velocity into a normalized physical quantity (seconds). A TTC of 1.5 s means "if nobody brakes, collision in 1.5 seconds".

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two thresholds (warn + critical)?** ‚Äî A single threshold causes driver annoyance (alarm fatigue). Tiered thresholds allow a graded escalation: visual ‚Üí auditory ‚Üí haptic brake pulse ‚Üí full AEB.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU examination numerical problems on CWAS.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Domain |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$ where $d_{rel}$ = relative distance, $v_{rel}$ = closing velocity | s | $v_{rel} > 0$ (only when closing) |
| 2 | **Inverse TTC (iTTC)** | $iTTC = \dfrac{v_{rel}}{d_{rel}} = \dfrac{1}{TTC}$ | s$^{-1}$ | Used for instant onset detection |
| 3 | **Required Deceleration** | $a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{rel}^{2}}{2 \, d_{rel}}$ | m/s$^{2}$ | $a_{req} \le a_{max}$ means AEB possible |
| 4 | **Stopping Distance (longitudinal)** | $d_{stop} = v\,\tau_{r} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2\,a_{max}}$ | m | Sum of thinking + braking distance |
| 5 | **Safety Margin Check** | $d_{rel} - d_{stop} \ge d_{safe}$ | m | $d_{safe}$ = 2 to 5 m buffer |
| 6 | **Radar Range Resolution** | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2\,B}$ | m | $c$ = speed of light, $B$ = bandwidth |
| 7 | **Radar Doppler (closing) velocity** | $v_{rel} = \dfrac{f_{D}\,\lambda}{2}$ | m/s | $f_D$ = Doppler shift |
| 8 | **FMCW Beat Frequency** | $f_{b} = \dfrac{2\,R\,S}{c}$ | Hz | $S$ = chirp slope, $R$ = range |
| 9 | **Required Brake Pressure** | $P_{brk} = \dfrac{m\,a}{A_{cyl}\,\mu_{pad}}$ | Pa | $m$ = quarter-vehicle mass |
| 10 | **Sensor Fusion Weight (Kalman)** | $K = \dfrac{P^{-}}{P^{-}+R}$ | dimensionless | $P^{-}$ = prior covariance, $R$ = measurement noise |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Segment | Application | Outcome |
|------------------|-------------|---------|
| Passenger Cars | AEB for city/interurban | Up to **38%** reduction in rear-end crashes |
| Heavy Trucks | AEB + adaptive cruise | Up to **45%** reduction in fatal truck rear-end collisions |
| Mining & Construction | Proximity detection + auto-stop | Reduces on-site fatalities by **60%** |
| Rail (CBTC) | Forward collision avoidance | Enables headway reduction to **90 s** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Time-to-Collision (TTC) for a Following Vehicle

We consider a leader vehicle traveling at constant velocity $v_L$ and a follower (host) vehicle traveling at $v_F$, both along the same longitudinal axis. Let $d(t)$ denote the bumper-to-bumper distance between the two vehicles at time $t$, and let the leader be **ahead** of the follower.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Define relative distance and relative velocity.**

The bumper-to-bumper distance evolves in time as:

$$d(t) = x_L(t) - x_F(t)$$

The relative velocity is the rate of change of the relative distance. Because both vehicles may accelerate, we write the most general form:

$$v_{rel}(t) = \dot{d}(t) = v_L(t) - v_F(t)$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Define the collision condition geometrically.**

A collision occurs when the bumper-to-bumper distance becomes zero. Integrating the relative velocity from the current instant $t_0$ to the future collision instant $t_c$:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t_c} v_{rel}(t) \, dt = -d(t_0)$$

The negative sign appears because $d$ must decrease by an amount equal to the initial gap in order to reach zero.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Specialise to constant velocities.**

If both vehicles travel at constant speed (no acceleration), $v_{rel}$ is constant, and the integral collapses to:

$$v_{rel} \, (t_c - t_0) = -d(t_0)$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Solve for the time-to-collision.**

Re-arranging for the elapsed time until collision:

$$t_c - t_0 = \dfrac{-d(t_0)}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{d(t_0)}{v_F - v_L}$$

Since the host (follower) is closing in, $v_F > v_L$ and so $v_{rel} < 0$ from the follower's frame. It is conventional to define $v_{rel}$ as the **closing velocity** $v_{rel} \equiv v_F - v_L > 0$:

$$\boxed{\,TTC = t_c - t_0 = \dfrac{d(t_0)}{v_{rel}}\,}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Sanity check at the limit.**

If $v_{rel} \rightarrow 0$ (both moving at identical speed), $TTC \rightarrow \infty$ ‚Äî no impending collision, as expected. If $d \rightarrow 0$ (already touching), $TTC \rightarrow 0$ ‚Äî imminent collision. The formula is consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Required Braking Deceleration for Collision Avoidance

A collision can be avoided if the host vehicle decelerates from its current speed $v_F$ to the leader's speed $v_L$ (or to zero, for a stationary obstacle) **before** the gap closes.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Express the closure of the gap under uniform deceleration.**

Assume the host vehicle brakes at constant deceleration $a$ and the leader continues at constant speed $v_L$. The relative motion has initial relative speed $v_{rel}$ and constant relative deceleration $a$ (because the leader does not decelerate).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the kinematic equation (3rd equation of motion).**

For the relative motion:

$$v_{rel, final}^{2} = v_{rel}^{2} - 2\,a\,d$$

The minimum condition for just-avoiding collision is $v_{rel,final} = 0$ (closing velocity vanishes exactly at the moment of gap closure). Substituting:

$$0 = v_{rel}^{2} - 2\,a_{req}\,d$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for $a_{req}$.**

$$\boxed{\,a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{rel}^{2}}{2\,d}\,}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Interpretation.**

- If $a_{req} > a_{max}$ (maximum achievable tyre-road friction-limited deceleration), then **AEB cannot avoid** the collision; it can only **mitigate** the impact by reducing closing speed.
- If $a_{req} \le a_{max}$, then **collision avoidance is physically possible** and the AEB controller requests a brake pressure to deliver exactly $a_{req}$.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Numerical example for KTU examination.**

Given: $v_{rel} = 20\text{ m/s}$ (72 km/h), $d = 40\text{ m}$, $a_{max} = 8\text{ m/s}^{2}$. Compute $a_{req}$.

$$a_{req} = \dfrac{(20)^{2}}{2 \times 40} = \dfrac{400}{80} = 5.0\text{ m/s}^{2}$$

Since $5.0 < 8.0$, avoidance is possible ‚Äî the AEB requests 5.0 m/s¬≤ deceleration. ‚úì

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Stopping Distance (Thinking Distance + Braking Distance)

The total stopping distance from initial speed $v$ under maximum braking deceleration $a_{max}$ with driver reaction time $\tau_r$ is the sum of two components.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Thinking distance.**

During the driver's reaction time $\tau_r$, the vehicle continues at constant speed $v$. Distance covered:

$$d_{think} = v \cdot \tau_{r}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Braking distance.**

Once the brakes are applied, the vehicle decelerates uniformly from $v$ to $0$. Using $v_{f}^{2} = v_{i}^{2} - 2 a d$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$d_{brake} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2\,a_{max}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Sum the two contributions.**

$$\boxed{\,d_{stop} = v\,\tau_{r} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2\,a_{max}}\,}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Numerical evaluation for KTU.**

Given: $v = 27.78\text{ m/s}$ (100 km/h), $\tau_r = 1.5\text{ s}$, $a_{max} = 7.5\text{ m/s}^{2}$ (wet road).

$$d_{think} = 27.78 \times 1.5 = 41.67\text{ m}$$

$$d_{brake} = \dfrac{(27.78)^{2}}{2 \times 7.5} = \dfrac{771.6}{15.0} = 51.44\text{ m}$$

$$d_{stop} = 41.67 + 51.44 = 93.11\text{ m}$$

This result is consistent with the widely cited rule-of-thumb that stopping distance at 100 km/h on wet road is approximately **90 m to 95 m**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Decision Logic in the State Machine

The CWAS state machine accepts the measured TTC and decides on the action level. A common KTU-asked formulation:

$$\text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{No Action}, & TTC > 2.7\text{ s} \\ \text{Visual + Audio Warning}, & 1.8 < TTC \le 2.7\text{ s} \\ \text{Haptic Brake Pulse}, & 1.4 < TTC \le 1.8\text{ s} \\ \text{Full AEB}, & TTC \le 1.4\text{ s} \end{cases}$$

This **piecewise decision function** is the heart of any CWAS controller and frequently appears in **Part B (14-mark)** questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚Äî Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Coverage Map (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The diagram below illustrates how multiple sensors of different physical principles cooperate to provide 360¬∞ perception coverage. Each sensor type is rendered as a node in a Mermaid flow diagram to comply with the autotronics board format.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Long Range Radar 76GHz Range 250m"] --> nodeECU["ADAS ECU Domain Controller"]
    node2["Mid Range Radar 76GHz Range 80m"] --> nodeECU
    node3["Front Camera 2MP Resolution"] --> nodeECU
    node4["Front Lidar 905nm ToF"] --> nodeECU
    node5["Ultrasonic Corner 12 units 5m"] --> nodeECU
    node6["Rear Corner Radar 77GHz"] --> nodeECU
    node7["Vehicle CAN Bus Speed Yaw Accel"] --> nodeECU
    nodeECU --> node8["Sensor Fusion EKF Tracker"]
    node8 --> node9["TTC Calculator and Decision Engine"]
    node9 --> node10["HMI Visual Audio Haptic"]
    node9 --> node11["ESC Actuator Brake Pre-charge"]
    node9 --> node12["EPS Actuator Steering Correction"]
    node9 --> node13["ACC Adaptive Cruise Update"]
```

## 4.2 Layered State Machine for Collision Response

The Mermaid state diagram below maps the discrete decision states through which the CWAS transitions. It is the single most exam-relevant diagram in this module.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_Nominal
    S0_Nominal --> S1_Watch: TTC less than 2.7s
    S1_Watch --> S2_Visual: TTC less than 2.2s
    S2_Visual --> S3_Audio: TTC less than 1.8s
    S3_Audio --> S4_Haptic: TTC less than 1.4s
    S4_Haptic --> S5_AEB: TTC less than 1.0s
    S5_AEB --> S6_Collision: Impact detected
    S6_Collision --> [*]
    S5_AEB --> S0_Nominal: Hazard cleared
    S4_Haptic --> S0_Nominal: Driver brakes TTC rises
    S3_Audio --> S0_Nominal: Driver brakes TTC rises
    S2_Visual --> S0_Nominal: Driver brakes TTC rises
    S1_Watch --> S0_Nominal: Driver brakes TTC rises
```

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Radar Track List Range Doppler Angle"] --> nodeE["Central Tracker EKF"]
    nodeB["Camera Object List Bounding Box Class"] --> nodeE
    nodeC["Lidar Pointcloud 3D X Y Z I"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Fused Object List ID Class Position Velocity"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Prediction Horizon 0.5s 1.0s 2.0s"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Risk Assessment Prob TTC Severity"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Decision Logic AEB ACC LKA"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Actuator Commands CAN-FD"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to the Examiner:** In the diagrams above, all sensor raw outputs feed a **centralised Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** which produces a fused object list. This list is then propagated forward in time through a **prediction horizon**, which is what enables the lookahead nature of CWAS. This architecture follows the **SAE J3061** cybersecurity-informed safety framework.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]

> **Q: Define Time-to-Collision (TTC). Why is it a critical parameter in a Collision Warning System?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TTC is defined as the time remaining before a collision occurs if both the host vehicle and the target continue at their current velocities with no change in heading. Mathematically:

$$TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$$

where $d_{rel}$ is the relative distance and $v_{rel}$ is the closing velocity. **(1 mark)**

It is critical because TTC inherently **normalises** distance and speed into a single intuitive scalar (in seconds) that matches human driver perception of urgency. **(1 mark)**

It directly drives the **decision logic** of the CWAS: warning thresholds (typically 2.7 s for caution and 1.4 s for AEB intervention) are expressed as TTC values. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]

> **Q: List any three automotive sensors used in a Collision Avoidance System and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Long-Range Radar (76 GHz)** ‚Äî Advantage: measures **closing velocity directly** via Doppler shift, robust in fog and rain. **(1 mark)**
2. **Mono / Stereo Camera** ‚Äî Advantage: provides **object classification** (pedestrian vs. vehicle) via image processing. **(1 mark)**
3. **Lidar (905 nm Time-of-Flight)** ‚Äî Advantage: provides **high-resolution 3-D point cloud** with cm-level accuracy for stationary obstacles. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B ‚Äî Choice A [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023, Adapted]

> **Q (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of a Forward Collision Warning and Avoidance System. List the functions of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of an FCW + Avoidance system comprises six functional blocks arranged in a sequential pipeline:

**Block 1 ‚Äî Perception Sensor Suite** (1 mark)
Comprises long-range radar (76 GHz, 250 m range), mid-range radar, front camera (monocular or stereo), and optionally Lidar. The radar provides precise range and Doppler velocity; the camera provides object classification.

**Block 2 ‚Äî Sensor Pre-processing** (1 mark)
Raw sensor data is filtered via a Kalman Filter or Extended Kalman Filter to remove noise, clutter, and false returns. Sensor-specific preprocessing (e.g., ego-motion compensation for radar) is performed.

**Block 3 ‚Äî Object Tracking & Classification** (1 mark)
A multi-object tracker (JPDAF or MHT) maintains object identity over consecutive frames. A classifier (CNN-based) labels each object as car / truck / pedestrian / cyclist.

**Block 4 ‚Äî Situation Analysis** (1 mark)
Computes the **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** using $TTC = d_{rel}/v_{rel}$ and the **required deceleration** $a_{req} = v_{rel}^{2}/(2 d)$. A collision probability is also evaluated by comparing $a_{req}$ with $a_{max}$.

**Block 5 ‚Äî Decision Logic / State Machine** (1 mark)
A layered state machine maps TTC to action levels: no action ‚Üí visual warning ‚Üí audio warning ‚Üí haptic brake pulse ‚Üí full AEB.

**Block 6 ‚Äî Actuation** (1 mark)
Brake actuation via the ESC modulator, steering correction via the EPS motor, and HMI warnings via the instrument cluster and head-up display. Communication occurs over **CAN-FD** at 2 Mbps.

**Block 7 ‚Äî Functional Safety Layer** (1 mark)
Implements **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** monitoring, with redundant watchdog and plausibility checks for each sensor input.

**Block Diagram (drawn on answer script):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Perception Sensors"] --> nodeB["Pre-processing"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Tracking and Classification"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Situation Analysis TTC"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Decision State Machine"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Actuation HMI ESC EPS"]
```

> **Note to student:** Always label **all six blocks** and **state one function** of each. Missing the "Actuation" block is a common cause of losing 1‚Äì2 marks.

---

> **Q (b)** A host vehicle is travelling at 90 km/h behind a leader moving at 60 km/h. The bumper-to-bumper distance is 30 m. Compute (i) the closing velocity, (ii) the TTC, (iii) the required deceleration to just avoid collision, and (iv) comment on whether AEB is sufficient, given $a_{max} = 8\text{ m/s}^2$ and $\tau_r = 1.2\text{ s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data** (1 mark):
- $v_F = 90\text{ km/h} = 25.0\text{ m/s}$
- $v_L = 60\text{ km/h} = 16.67\text{ m/s}$
- $d_{rel} = 30\text{ m}$
- $a_{max} = 8\text{ m/s}^2$, $\tau_r = 1.2\text{ s}$

**Part (i) ‚Äî Closing velocity:**

$$v_{rel} = v_F - v_L = 25.0 - 16.67 = 8.33\text{ m/s}$$

(1 mark)

**Part (ii) ‚Äî Time-to-Collision:**

$$TTC = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{30}{8.33} = 3.6\text{ s}$$

(1 mark)

**Part (iii) ‚Äî Required deceleration:**

$$a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{rel}^{2}}{2\,d_{rel}} = \dfrac{(8.33)^{2}}{2 \times 30} = \dfrac{69.39}{60.0} = 1.157\text{ m/s}^{2}$$

(1 mark)

**Part (iv) ‚Äî Sufficiency check:**

Since $a_{req} = 1.157\text{ m/s}^2 < a_{max} = 8\text{ m/s}^2$, the **AEB system can comfortably avoid the collision**. (1 mark)

Furthermore, the stopping distance at $v_F = 25\text{ m/s}$ with $\tau_r = 1.2\text{ s}$ and $a_{max} = 8\text{ m/s}^2$:

$$d_{stop} = v_F \tau_r + \dfrac{v_F^2}{2 a_{max}} = 25 \times 1.2 + \dfrac{625}{16} = 30 + 39.06 = 69.06\text{ m}$$

(1 mark)

Since $d_{stop} = 69.06\text{ m} > d_{rel} = 30\text{ m}$, if **the driver does not brake**, a collision will occur ‚Äî confirming the need for AEB intervention. However, AEB can achieve $a_{req} = 1.16\text{ m/s}^2$ and thus successfully avoid it. (1 mark)

---

### Question B ‚Äî Choice B [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024, Adapted]

> **Q (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of an Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) system. Describe the role of sensors and the decision thresholds. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle** (1 mark):
AEB continuously monitors the road ahead, computes the Time-to-Collision (TTC), and **autonomously applies the brakes** if the driver fails to respond to warnings and TTC falls below a critical threshold.

**Sensors used** (2 marks):
- **Forward-looking radar (76 GHz)** for range and velocity
- **Mono / stereo camera** for object classification (vehicle / pedestrian)
- **Optional Lidar** for 3-D localisation of stationary objects
- **Wheel-speed sensors** and **inertial measurement unit (IMU)** for ego-state feedback

**Decision Thresholds (state machine)** (2 marks):
| TTC Range | System Action |
|-----------|---------------|
| TTC > 2.7 s | No action |
| 1.8 s < TTC ‚â§ 2.7 s | Visual + audio warning |
| 1.4 s < TTC ‚â§ 1.8 s | Brake pre-charge + haptic warning |
| TTC ‚â§ 1.4 s | Full AEB intervention |

**Actuation** (1 mark):
The ESC hydraulic modulator raises brake pressure to deliver the deceleration computed by $a_{req} = v_{rel}^{2}/(2 d)$. Driver override is permitted if the brake pedal is pressed, but not if TTC < 0.6 s (last-second safety).

**Functional Safety** (1 mark):
AEB is rated **ASIL-D** under **ISO 26262** and must satisfy **SOTIF (ISO 21448)** requirements for perception failures.

---

> **Q (b)** A pedestrian steps onto the road 15 m ahead of a host vehicle travelling at 50 km/h. The driver reaction time is 1.5 s and maximum deceleration is 7.5 m/s¬≤. Compute (i) stopping distance, (ii) whether the driver alone can avoid collision, and (iii) the minimum deceleration that AEB must produce to avoid the collision. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $v_F = 50\text{ km/h} = 13.89\text{ m/s}$
- $d_{rel} = 15\text{ m}$, $\tau_r = 1.5\text{ s}$, $a_{max} = 7.5\text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (i) ‚Äî Stopping distance:** (2 marks)

$$d_{stop} = v_F \tau_r + \dfrac{v_F^{2}}{2 a_{max}} = 13.89 \times 1.5 + \dfrac{(13.89)^{2}}{2 \times 7.5}$$

$$= 20.835 + \dfrac{192.93}{15.0} = 20.835 + 12.862 = 33.70\text{ m}$$

**Part (ii) ‚Äî Can driver avoid the collision?** (2 marks)

Since $d_{stop} = 33.70\text{ m} > d_{rel} = 15\text{ m}$, the driver **alone CANNOT** avoid the collision. A pedestrian impact will occur at residual speed:

$$v_{residual}^{2} = v_F^{2} - 2 a_{max} d_{rel} = (13.89)^{2} - 2 \times 7.5 \times 15 = 192.93 - 225 = -32.07$$

Since this is negative, the car would actually stop before reaching the pedestrian if $a_{max}$ were applied from t=0 ‚Äî but the **reaction-time delay** prevents that. After 1.5 s the car has travelled $13.89 \times 1.5 = 20.83\text{ m}$, leaving only $15 - 20.83 = -5.83\text{ m}$ ‚Äî already past the pedestrian. **Collision is unavoidable by human driver.**

(1 mark for explicit comparison and conclusion)

**Part (iii) ‚Äî Minimum AEB deceleration to avoid collision:** (2 marks)

Assume AEB acts with negligible system latency (no $\tau_r$). Required deceleration:

$$a_{AEB,min} = \dfrac{v_F^{2}}{2 d_{rel}} = \dfrac{(13.89)^{2}}{2 \times 15} = \dfrac{192.93}{30} = 6.43\text{ m/s}^{2}$$

(1 mark)

Since $6.43\text{ m/s}^{2} < a_{max} = 7.5\text{ m/s}^{2}$, AEB **can avoid** the collision by producing at least **6.43 m/s¬≤** deceleration. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CWAS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion from km/h to m/s** ‚Äî Most students lose **1 mark** by writing $v_F = 90$ instead of $25\text{ m/s}$. Always show the conversion.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion in $v_{rel}$** ‚Äî Use the **closing velocity** definition $v_{rel} = v_F - v_L > 0$. Mixing it with the relative velocity $\dot d$ (which is negative when closing) loses marks.
> 3. **Stopping distance without reaction time** ‚Äî AEB asks for the *system* stopping distance, which excludes $\tau_r$ because the system has negligible latency. A *driver* stopping distance includes $\tau_r$. Examiners penalise this distinction.
> 4. **No functional-safety mention** ‚Äî Even in purely numerical questions, dropping a one-line reference to **ISO 26262 / SOTIF** in a theory part costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Incomplete state-machine answer** ‚Äî When describing TTC thresholds, you must list **all four levels** (nominal, warn, haptic, AEB), not just two.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ‚Äî Collision Warning and Avoidance System**

- [x] **CWAS = FCW + AEB** layered driver assistance system.
- [x] Core sensors: **76 GHz radar, camera, lidar, ultrasonic, IMU**.
- [x] Primary metric: **TTC = $d_{rel}/v_{rel}$** (seconds).
- [x] Required brake deceleration: **$a_{req} = v_{rel}^{2}/(2 d)$**.
- [x] Stopping distance: **$d_{stop} = v \tau_r + v^{2}/(2 a_{max})$**.
- [x] Warning thresholds (typical): **2.7 s (caution), 1.4 s (AEB)**.
- [x] System latency budget: **$\le 100$ ms** end-to-end.
- [x] Decision flow: **sense ‚Üí track ‚Üí predict ‚Üí decide ‚Üí actuate**.
- [x] Functional safety: **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**, SOTIF ISO 21448.
- [x] Regulatory: **UNECE R152** (AEBS), **AIS-184** (India).
- [x] Sensor fusion technique: **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.
- [x] Actuators: **ESC (brake), EPS (steer), HMI (warn)**.
- [x] If $a_{req} > a_{max}$ ‚Üí mitigation only; if $a_{req} \le a_{max}$ ‚Üí avoidance possible.
- [x] Always **convert km/h to m/s** (multiply by $1/3.6$) before calculation.
- [x] Distinguish **driver reaction time** ($\tau_r \approx 1.0$‚Äì$1.5$ s) from **system latency** ($\le 100$ ms).
- [x] Know the **piecewise state machine** with all four thresholds ‚Äî examiners test this directly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Tire Pressure Warning System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT602 - AUTOTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low Tire Pressure Warning System**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Low Tire Pressure Warning System (TPMS - Tire Pressure Monitoring System)** is an electronic safety subsystem in modern automobiles mandated under global safety standards (FMVSS 138 in the USA, ECE R64 in Europe, and AIS-145 in India) that continuously monitors the **inflation pressure** of pneumatic tires and alerts the driver via a **dashboard warning indicator** whenever the pressure in one or more tires falls below a manufacturer-defined safety threshold (typically **25% below the placard pressure**, as per regulatory minimums).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for AUTOTRONICS, it is classified under **Chassis Electronics & Driver Information Systems**, interfacing with the **CAN/LIN bus** of the vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Low Tire Pressure Warning System is a mandatory study item because it integrates **MEMS sensors**, **RF telemetry**, **microcontroller-based signal processing**, and **automotive networking** ‚Äî making it a perfect example of an autotronics application that merges mechanical sensing with electronic intelligence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a long journey. One of your tires slowly loses air due to a tiny puncture. You do not feel it immediately because the ride feels "almost" the same. But after an hour, the tire becomes flat and the rim bends, ruining your trip.

Now imagine the bicycle had a tiny **invisible pressure gauge** inside each tire that whispers via radio to a **smart bell on your handlebar** the moment pressure drops by even **5%**. That smart bell is exactly what a **TPMS** does for a car ‚Äî it is the car's way of "listening" to its tires continuously so the driver never gets caught off-guard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** A tire with **20% under-inflation** increases rolling resistance, reduces fuel economy by up to **3%**, decreases tire life by **15%**, and drastically increases the probability of a **blowout** at highway speeds. TPMS is, therefore, not a luxury but a **life-safety system**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Regulatory Thresholds

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Placard Pressure (Cold) | **210 kPa (30 psi)** typical passenger car |
| Warning Trigger Threshold | **25% below placard** (‚âà 158 kPa / 22.5 psi) |
| Mandatory Response Time | Within **20 minutes** of detection (ECE R64) |
| Operating Temperature Range | **‚àí40 ¬∞C to +125 ¬∞C** |
| Sensor Battery Life | **7 to 10 years** |
| RF Transmission Frequency | **315 MHz (USA/Asia)** or **433.92 MHz (Europe)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture & Operating Principle

The TPMS operates through the following structured logic steps:

- **Step 1 ‚Äî Pressure Acquisition:** A **MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor** mounted inside each tire (attached to the valve stem or strapped to the wheel rim) measures the absolute pressure of the air in the tire cavity.

- **Step 2 ‚Äî Temperature Compensation:** A co-located **NTC thermistor** measures the internal tire temperature. Pressure readings are mathematically compensated to a reference temperature (**25 ¬∞C / 298 K**) because tire pressure rises approximately **10 kPa per 10 ¬∞C** of temperature increase.

- **Step 3 ‚Äî Microcontroller Decision Logic:** An embedded 8-bit or 32-bit MCU evaluates whether the compensated pressure lies below the warning threshold stored in firmware.

- **Step 4 ‚Äî Wireless Transmission:** If a fault is detected (or periodically every **60 seconds** for status updates), the sensor module wakes its **RF transmitter** (typically using ASK or FSK modulation at 315/433 MHz) and broadcasts a uniquely coded message containing: sensor ID, pressure value, temperature, battery status, and a CRC checksum.

- **Step 5 ‚Äî Reception & Decoding:** A central **RF receiver module** (mounted near the spare wheel well or under the chassis) decodes the Manchester-encoded data stream.

- **Step 6 ‚Äî ECU-Level Decision:** The **Body Control Module (BCM)** or a dedicated TPMS receiver ECU compares decoded data against stored thresholds. If pressure < threshold, it activates the **amber tell-tale warning lamp** on the instrument cluster.

- **Step 7 ‚Äî Driver Alert & CAN Communication:** The warning is also logged as a **DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code)** on the CAN bus, accessible by OBD-II scan tools.

## 2.2 Classification of TPMS

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically distinguishes two technological implementations:

### 2.2.1 Direct TPMS (dTPMS)
- Uses **physical pressure transducers** inside each tire.
- Provides **real-time absolute pressure values** for each wheel.
- More accurate, but requires battery-powered sensors and periodic replacement.

### 2.2.2 Indirect TPMS (iTPMS)
- **No dedicated pressure sensor** in the tire.
- Uses the **ABS wheel-speed sensors** to detect a tire with low pressure by observing the **dynamic rolling radius difference** (an under-inflated tire has a smaller diameter and thus rotates faster).
- Cost-effective but **cannot detect simultaneous deflation of all four tires** and requires a re-calibration routine after tire rotation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gay-Lussac's Law (Ideal Gas) | $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ | Pressure-temperature relationship at constant volume | Compensates tire pressure to a reference temperature |
| Absolute Pressure Relation | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Converts gauge pressure to absolute | Used inside the MEMS sensor |
| Warning Threshold | $P_{warn} = 0.75 \times P_{placard}$ | 25% under-inflation trigger point | Firmware constant stored in BCM |
| Rolling Radius vs Pressure | $r_{eff} \approx r_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{P}\right)$ | Effective radius decreases as pressure drops | Indirect TPMS detection principle |
| Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | RF signal attenuation over distance | Predicts receiver signal strength inside wheel well |
| Angular Velocity Error | $\dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\omega} \approx \dfrac{\Delta r}{r}$ | Frequency ratio equals radius ratio | Indirect TPMS detection of deflation |
| Cold Inflation Correction | $P_{cold} = P_{hot} \times \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Adjusts hot reading to cold reference | Standard service-station conversion |
| Pressure Rate of Change | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = -\dfrac{k}{V} P$ | Slow leak model (first-order exponential) | Used for fast-leak vs slow-leak discrimination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Table Syntax Note:** Absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ uses the `\vert` LaTeX command to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe delimiter. Students should reproduce this correctly in exam answer sheets.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Safety:** Prevents accidents due to sudden blowouts, especially at speeds above **120 km/h**.
- **Economy:** Maintains optimal fuel efficiency (every **1 psi** drop reduces mileage by **~0.3%**).
- **Environment:** Proper inflation extends tire life, reducing rubber waste.
- **Data Logging:** Modern TPMS data is logged for **fleet management** and **predictive maintenance** using IoT gateways.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** TPMS data feeds the **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)** for predictive braking algorithms on low-grip surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Compensation of Tire Pressure

A tire is filled at a service station at **30 ¬∞C** to a cold placard pressure of **210 kPa**. After 30 minutes of highway driving, the tire temperature rises to **70 ¬∞C**. Determine the absolute pressure reading the in-tire MEMS sensor will report.

### Given:
- $T_{cold} = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$
- $T_{hot} = 70 + 273 = 343 \text{ K}$
- $P_{cold} = 210 \text{ kPa}$ (initial)

### To find: $P_{hot}$

### Step-by-step:

Using Gay-Lussac's Law for an isochoric (constant-volume) process inside the tire:

$$\dfrac{P_{cold}}{T_{cold}} = \dfrac{P_{hot}}{T_{hot}}$$

Rearranging to solve for $P_{hot}$:

$$P_{hot} = P_{cold} \times \dfrac{T_{hot}}{T_{cold}}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$P_{hot} = 210 \text{ kPa} \times \dfrac{343 \text{ K}}{303 \text{ K}}$$

$$P_{hot} = 210 \text{ kPa} \times 1.1320$$

$$\boxed{P_{hot} \approx 237.7 \text{ kPa}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation:
Although the absolute pressure has risen to **237.7 kPa** due to thermal expansion of air, the *equivalent cold pressure* has not changed. The TPMS firmware must compensate this hot reading back to the **30 ¬∞C reference** to avoid false alarms during normal driving. The compensated cold-equivalent pressure remains:

$$P_{comp} = P_{hot} \times \dfrac{T_{ref}}{T_{hot}} = 237.7 \times \dfrac{303}{343} = 210 \text{ kPa}$$

This confirms the sensor has correctly identified that **no under-inflation occurred** despite the apparent rise.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Warning Threshold & Under-Inflation Severity

A vehicle has a placard pressure of **220 kPa**. As per ECE R64, the warning lamp must illuminate when pressure falls to **75%** of placard. Determine the warning pressure and the corresponding pressure drop in psi (for a US specification report).

### Step 1 ‚Äî Compute warning threshold in kPa:

$$P_{warn} = 0.75 \times P_{placard}$$

$$P_{warn} = 0.75 \times 220 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{warn} = 165 \text{ kPa}}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Compute allowable pressure drop:

$$\Delta P_{allow} = P_{placard} - P_{warn} = 220 - 165 = 55 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Convert kPa to psi using conversion factor $1 \text{ psi} = 6.895 \text{ kPa}$:

$$\Delta P_{allow}(\text{psi}) = \dfrac{55}{6.895}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{allow} \approx 7.98 \text{ psi}}$$

### Interpretation:
A drop of just **8 psi** below the placard value must trigger the warning. This corresponds to roughly **24% under-inflation**, the threshold at which tire failure probability begins to rise sharply.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RF Link Budget for In-Tire to Chassis Receiver

A TPMS sensor transmits at **+5 dBm** at **433.92 MHz**. The receiver is located approximately **0.8 m** away (across the wheel diameter). Estimate the received power assuming free-space propagation.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):

$$FSPL (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

Converting to SI units: $d = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $f = 433.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$.

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(0.8) + 20 \log_{10}(433.92 \times 10^{6}) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right)$$

$$FSPL = 20 \times (-0.194) + 20 \times (8.638) + 20 \times (-7.077)$$

$$FSPL = -3.87 + 172.75 - 141.54$$

$$\boxed{FSPL \approx 27.34 \text{ dB}}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Received Power:

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - FSPL = +5 \text{ dBm} - 27.34 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{P_{rx} \approx -22.34 \text{ dBm}}$$

### Interpretation:
The receiver sees about **‚àí22 dBm**, well above the typical TPMS receiver sensitivity of **‚àí95 dBm**. This confirms a robust RF link even with battery aging.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Slow-Leak First-Order Model

A tire initially at **220 kPa** develops a slow leak. The leak rate constant is **k = 0.002 per day**, and the tire volume is constant. Predict pressure after **30 days**.

### Model:
$$\dfrac{dP}{dt} = -k P$$

### Solution (first-order linear ODE):

$$P(t) = P_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

### Substituting:

$$P(30) = 220 \times e^{-0.002 \times 30} = 220 \times e^{-0.06}$$

$$P(30) = 220 \times 0.9418$$

$$\boxed{P(30) \approx 207.2 \text{ kPa}}$$

### Interpretation:
After 30 days, pressure has dropped by only **12.8 kPa (‚âà 5.8%)**, still above the 75% threshold. However, the BCM firmware typically **flags a trend-based predictive warning** if it detects a monotonic decline, even before the absolute threshold is crossed.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Direct vs Indirect TPMS

| Parameter | Direct TPMS | Indirect TPMS |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ¬± **5 kPa** | ¬± **10‚Äì15 kPa** |
| Detection of All-4 Deflation | Yes | No |
| Sensor Battery Required | Yes | No (uses existing ABS sensors) |
| Re-calibration After Tire Rotation | Not required | Required |
| Cost per Wheel | High | Low (software only) |
| Real-Time Pressure Reading | Yes | No (relative estimate only) |
| Response to Spare Tire | Separate sensor needed | Not detected |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Direct TPMS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TIRE PRESSURE SENSOR] --> B[MEMS PIEZORESISTIVE TRANSDUCER]
    C[NTC THERMISTOR] --> B
    B --> D[ANALOG SIGNAL CONDITIONER]
    D --> E[8-BIT MCU]
    E --> F[RF TRANSMITTER 433 MHZ]
    F --> G[CHASSIS RF RECEIVER]
    G --> H[TPMS RECEIVER ECU]
    H --> I[CENTRAL BODY CONTROL MODULE]
    I --> J[DASHBOARD TELL-TALE LAMP]
    I --> K[CAN BUS 500 KBPS]
    K --> L[OBD II DIAGNOSTIC PORT]
    E --> M[LITHIUM ION BATTERY 3 TO 6 V]
    H --> N[PRESSURE THRESHOLD COMPARATOR]
    N --> I

    style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#f4b084,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#9bc2e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Indirect TPMS Signal Flow (ABS-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FRONT LEFT WHEEL] --> B1[ABS WHEEL SPEED SENSOR 1]
    A2[FRONT RIGHT WHEEL] --> B2[ABS WHEEL SPEED SENSOR 2]
    A3[REAR LEFT WHEEL] --> B3[ABS WHEEL SPEED SENSOR 3]
    A4[REAR RIGHT WHEEL] --> B4[ABS WHEEL SPEED SENSOR 4]
    B1 --> C1[ABS ECU SIGNAL AGGREGATOR]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[ANGULAR VELOCITY COMPARATOR]
    D1 --> E1[ROLLING RADIUS ESTIMATOR]
    E1 --> F1[UNDER INFLATION ALGORITHM]
    F1 --> G1[DASHBOARD WARNING LAMP]
    F1 --> H1[CAN BUS]

    style A1 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#f4b084,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 TPMS Communication Sequence (Time-Domain View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as TPMS Sensor
    participant T as RF Transmitter
    participant R as RF Receiver
    participant E as BCM ECU
    participant L as Dashboard Lamp

    S->>S: Measure P and T every 5 seconds
    S->>S: Temperature compensation to 25 deg C
    S->>S: Compare P with Pwarn threshold
    alt Pressure Normal
        S->>T: Broadcast status every 60 seconds
    else Pressure Below Threshold
        S->>T: Immediate broadcast with FAULT flag
        T->>R: Manchester encoded FSK frame
        R->>E: Decode sensor ID and pressure value
        E->>L: Activate amber tell tale lamp
        E->>E: Log DTC in non volatile memory
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]
**Define TPMS. List the two main categories of Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems with one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**
TPMS (Tire Pressure Monitoring System) is an embedded automotive electronic safety system that monitors the inflation pressure of each tire and alerts the driver when pressure falls below a manufacturer-defined safety threshold.

The two categories are:
1. **Direct TPMS** ‚Äî uses physical MEMS pressure sensors inside each tire to measure absolute pressure.
2. **Indirect TPMS** ‚Äî uses existing ABS wheel-speed sensors and software algorithms to estimate pressure based on rotational speed differences.

**Key difference:** Direct TPMS provides accurate real-time pressure values and can detect simultaneous deflation of all four tires, whereas Indirect TPMS cannot.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]
**State Gay-Lussac's Law and explain its significance in the context of TPMS temperature compensation.**

**Model Answer:**
**Gay-Lussac's Law:** For a fixed mass of gas at constant volume, the pressure is directly proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$$

**Significance in TPMS:** Tire temperature rises during driving (from ~30 ¬∞C ambient to ~70 ¬∞C on highways). Because pressure increases with temperature even when no air is lost, the in-tire sensor reading would falsely appear "over-inflated". The TPMS microcontroller applies Gay-Lussac's Law to mathematically compensate the hot reading back to the cold reference temperature of 25 ¬∞C (298 K), ensuring the driver receives an accurate cold-equivalent pressure reading.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a Direct Tire Pressure Monitoring System with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of each block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A tire is filled to **240 kPa** at a service station where the ambient temperature is **25 ¬∞C**. After driving on a hot day, the tire temperature rises to **75 ¬∞C**. Calculate: (i) the new absolute pressure, and (ii) whether the TPMS warning lamp will illuminate if the placard pressure is 220 kPa and the warning threshold is 75% of placard.

#### Model Solution to (a):

A Direct TPMS consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **MEMS Pressure Sensor:** A piezoresistive silicon diaphragm whose Wheatstone bridge resistance changes proportionally with applied pressure. Provides an analog voltage proportional to absolute pressure.

2. **Temperature Sensor (NTC Thermistor):** Measures the internal tire temperature for compensation of the pressure reading using Gay-Lussac's Law.

3. **Signal Conditioning Unit:** Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the analog pressure and temperature signals using a 12-bit ADC.

4. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU):** An 8-bit RISC processor that runs the TPMS firmware. It performs temperature compensation, threshold comparison, and controls the RF transmitter. Operates in low-power sleep mode and wakes periodically.

5. **RF Transmitter:** A 315/433 MHz ASK/FSK transmitter that broadcasts a Manchester-encoded data frame containing sensor ID, pressure, temperature, battery voltage, and CRC.

6. **Battery:** A 3 V coin-cell lithium battery providing 7‚Äì10 years of life. The MCU uses aggressive sleep/wake cycling to conserve energy.

7. **RF Receiver Module (in chassis):** Receives the wireless transmission, amplifies it, and decodes the Manchester-encoded data.

8. **Central ECU / BCM:** Compares decoded pressure with stored thresholds and triggers the warning lamp via the instrument cluster. Logs DTCs on CAN bus.

9. **Dashboard Tell-Tale Lamp:** An amber icon shaped like a horseshoe with an exclamation mark, illuminated when pressure < threshold.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $P_{cold} = 240 \text{ kPa}$ (at service station)
- $T_{cold} = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$
- $T_{hot} = 75 + 273 = 348 \text{ K}$
- $P_{placard} = 220 \text{ kPa}$
- Warning threshold = $75\% \times P_{placard}$

**Part (i) ‚Äî New absolute pressure using Gay-Lussac's Law:**

$$P_{hot} = P_{cold} \times \dfrac{T_{hot}}{T_{cold}}$$

$$P_{hot} = 240 \times \dfrac{348}{298}$$

$$P_{hot} = 240 \times 1.1678$$

$$\boxed{P_{hot} \approx 280.3 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Part (ii) ‚Äî TPMS compensated (cold-equivalent) reading:**

The TPMS will compensate this back to the reference temperature (298 K):

$$P_{compensated} = P_{hot} \times \dfrac{298}{348} = 280.3 \times 0.8563 \approx 240 \text{ kPa}$$

This matches the original cold pressure because **no air was lost** ‚Äî only the temperature changed.

**Comparison with warning threshold:**

$$P_{warn} = 0.75 \times 220 = 165 \text{ kPa}$$

Since $P_{compensated} = 240 \text{ kPa} > 165 \text{ kPa}$:

$$\boxed{\text{The TPMS warning lamp will NOT illuminate.}}$$

This demonstrates the importance of **temperature compensation** in TPMS firmware design.

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Direct and Indirect TPMS with respect to accuracy, cost, calibration requirement, and ability to detect simultaneous deflation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TPMS sensor transmits **+8 dBm** at **433.92 MHz**. The receiver is mounted at a distance of **1.2 m**. Calculate the free-space path loss and the received power in dBm. State whether the receiver will successfully decode the signal if its sensitivity is **‚àí90 dBm**.

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Parameter | Direct TPMS | Indirect TPMS |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Sensor** | MEMS transducer in each tire | None; uses ABS wheel-speed sensor |
| **Accuracy** | High (¬± 5 kPa) | Lower (¬± 10 to 15 kPa) |
| **Cost** | Higher (4 sensors + receiver + battery maintenance) | Lower (software-only upgrade on ABS ECU) |
| **Calibration After Tire Rotation** | Not required | Mandatory re-calibration needed |
| **All-4 Simultaneous Deflation Detection** | Yes, each tire is independently measured | No (all wheels lose pressure proportionally, no relative difference observed) |
| **Real-Time Numeric Pressure Display** | Yes | No (only relative warning) |
| **Spare Tire Monitoring** | Possible with additional sensor | Not possible |
| **Battery Maintenance** | Yes (lithium cell, 7‚Äì10 yr life) | No |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $P_{tx} = +8 \text{ dBm}$
- $f = 433.92 \text{ MHz} = 433.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- $d = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- Receiver sensitivity = $-90 \text{ dBm}$

**Free-Space Path Loss:**

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

Computing each term:

$$20 \log_{10}(1.2) = 20 \times 0.0792 = 1.584 \text{ dB}$$

$$20 \log_{10}(433.92 \times 10^{6}) = 20 \times 8.6375 = 172.75 \text{ dB}$$

$$20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) = 20 \times (-7.077) = -141.54 \text{ dB}$$

Summing:

$$FSPL = 1.584 + 172.75 - 141.54$$

$$\boxed{FSPL \approx 32.79 \text{ dB}}$$

**Received Power:**

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} - FSPL = +8 - 32.79$$

$$\boxed{P_{rx} \approx -24.79 \text{ dBm}}$$

**Decision:**

Since $P_{rx} = -24.79 \text{ dBm} \gg -90 \text{ dBm}$ (by a margin of **65.21 dB**), the receiver will **successfully decode the signal** with a high signal-to-noise ratio.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Students often forget to convert MHz to Hz before applying the FSPL formula, leading to a 100√ó error in path loss. Always check units.
> 2. Some students confuse dBm with dBW. Remember: **dBm = dB referenced to 1 milliwatt**. To convert dBm to dBW, subtract 30.
> 3. In direct vs indirect TPMS comparison questions, students often forget to mention the **re-calibration requirement after tire rotation** ‚Äî this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. When applying Gay-Lussac's Law, ALWAYS convert Celsius to Kelvin. Submitting answers in Celsius will be marked wrong.
> 5. In RF link budget problems, students frequently use the wrong formula. Memorize: **FSPL = 20log‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ(d) + 20log‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ(f) + 20log‚ÇÅ‚ÇÄ(4œÄ/c)** for dB-unit calculations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- TPMS stands for **Tire Pressure Monitoring System**, a regulatory-mandated safety system (FMVSS 138 / ECE R64 / AIS-145).
- Two categories: **Direct (dTPMS)** uses MEMS pressure sensors; **Indirect (iTPMS)** uses ABS wheel-speed signals.
- Regulatory warning threshold: **25% below placard pressure** (i.e., $P_{warn} = 0.75 \times P_{placard}$).
- Operating RF frequencies: **315 MHz** (USA/Asia) or **433.92 MHz** (Europe).
- Sensor operating temperature: **‚àí40 ¬∞C to +125 ¬∞C**.
- Key sensors inside the tire module: **MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor + NTC thermistor**.
- **Gay-Lussac's Law** $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$ is used for temperature compensation to a **25 ¬∞C reference**.
- Indirect TPMS cannot detect **simultaneous deflation of all four tires**.
- Direct TPMS requires **re-calibration only for sensor ID re-pairing**, not for tire rotation.
- Typical sensor battery life: **7‚Äì10 years**.
- Dashboard warning symbol: **amber horseshoe-shaped icon with exclamation mark**.
- TPMS data is logged on the **CAN bus** and accessible via **OBD-II** diagnostic tools.
- Pressure drop of **10 kPa ‚âà 1.45 psi** (conversion: 1 psi = 6.895 kPa).
- FSPL formula (must memorize for 14-mark derivations):
  $$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$
- Manchester encoding is used in **direct TPMS RF frames** to ensure DC-balanced data transmission.
- Modern TPMS modules integrate **SPI/ADC/MCU/RF Transmitter** on a single SoC chip.
- Always **convert ¬∞C to K** before applying any gas law equation in TPWS numerical problems.
